36Israel The making of Israeli psychoanalysis: a historical perspective

Eran J. Rolnik

The historiography of the dissemination of both popular and institutionalized psychoanalytic discourse can illuminate the diverse cultural–political spaces in which it was implanted and interpreted. Russian revolutionists, American psychiatrists, writers influenced by British Bloomsbury circle, French existentialists, and also Jewish intellectuals—they all developed their own versions of psychoanalysis and contributed in their own way to the interpretation and the evolution of the Freudian paradigm. The double wave of Jewish immigration to Palestine at the beginning of the twentieth century—the first between 1905 and 1914, the second between 1918 and 1923—contributed in a decisive way to the ideological foundations of the Jewish immigrant community of Palestine under the British mandate (1918–48). One of the chief accomplishments of those who immigrated, whose views in many respects represented those of the Russian intelligentsia, was the transformation of utopian-egalitarian ideas and socialist myths from Eastern Europe into concrete schemas for action for the Zionist movement. To be sure, the first encounter between psychoanalysis and the Yishuv1 cannot be considered a precise mirroring of the encounter between psychoanalytic theory and Russian Marxism. It did, however, bear the most prominent markers of that theory’s Russian reception. Oscillating between their admonitions in favour of sexual abstention and calls for total liberation from any “bourgeois inhibitions”, Russian sexologists, psychologists, and the first psychoanalysts stripped the human psyche of its determinist and unconscious elements. As long as it was possible to uphold the analogy between individual mechanisms of repression and those of bourgeois society, revolutionaries such as Trotsky and Joffe fashioned psychoanalysis into a scientific–ideological party instrument. Once it became clear, however, that the “Freudian Man” was devoid of any utopian yearnings, revolutionary discourse in its Russian version turned away from the subversive Freudian Unconscious, and psychoanalysis was officially removed from the party’s ideological armamentarium (see Etkind, 1997; Kloocke, 2002; Miller, 1998). The disappointment that awaited those who sought to merge the doctrines of Freud and Marx in Russia after the October Revolution did not prevent the Zionists among them from mobilizing Freudian theory for their ideological ends. As early as 1920, Ernest Jones reported to Freud of a conversation he had with Chaim Weizman, in which the Zionist leader (who would become Israel’s first president) took pride in those “poor Galician immigrants who arrive in Palestine with no clothes but holding Marx’s Capital in one hand and in the other, Freud’s Interpretation of Dreams”.2 Despite the essential conflict between the constructivist–utopian characteristics of Soviet socialism and the social pessimism in Freud’s later writings, his Zionist readers preferred to bridge the gap between these different perceptions of man by a selective reading of psychoanalytic theory. Among the images out of which the “New Jew” of the Zionist revolution was constructed, those pertaining to sexuality and masculinity played a central role (see Berkowitz, 1993; Biale, 1997; Boyarin, 1997; Bunzl, 1997; Efron, 2001; Gilman, 1993; Hart, 2000). The neurologist Max Nordau described the Jew as a sick and degenerate creature, much in need of a homeland, gymnastics, and a stable marriage. Nordau’s “Jewry of Muscle” corresponded with “Jewry of Nerves”—a prevalent semi-scientific concept that appeared under different terminology in the writings of both Jewish and non-Jewish physicians. The Zionist movement also developed an instrumental relation to the past, attempting to give its followers the impression of a collective present and future through the construction of a unitary collective–mythological past. The emerging image of the Chaluz [Pioneer], the Jewish immigrant to Palestine, itself served this purpose: the image was of a newcomer who had freed himself from the chains of an oppressive past, one presented in terms that were part historical part abstract and mythical, and who could thus henceforth determine his own fate (see Sternhell, 1986; Wistrich & Ohana, 1995).

Psychoanalytic pioneers and their discontent In the first half of the 1920s three Freudians—Siegfried Bernfeld, David Eder, and Dorian Feigenbaum—attempted to reconcile their activities in the Zionist movement with their commitment to psychoanalysis. Bernfeld repeatedly emphasized that youth are naturally endowed with moral and spiritual sensitivity and are graced with natural rebellion against injustice. His psychological–historical analysis of the problem of the split self-identity of Jewish–German youth was based on the Freudian concept of repression. When 23-year-old Meir Ya’ari, co-founder of the Viennese branch of the Zionist youth movement Ha-Shomer Ha-Zair [The Youth Guard3], arrived in Palestine in 1920 with a group of young settlers, the impact of Bernfeld’s and Freud’s influence on his social outlook was clearly evident:


I want you to become acquainted with Freud and his school in a precise fashion, I don’t know whether many of us can accurately grasp his theory, yet I hope that it will at least cleanse the charged atmosphere. I want to sanctify the drive through this experience. Please be aware of the power in you, when masculine eroticism unites you in spite of your inner resistance … I seek with all my might to penetrate into my unconscious world and expose myself. I feel this is the only way to independence, only in this manner can one grew. As you see, I am speaking in Freud’s words.4


Meir Ya’ari spoke of the pure and open-hearted generation that had arrived in Palestine, a generation that had freed itself from the hysterical nervousness of its parents as well as from the sanctimonious bourgeois corruption of the instincts. The youth movement’s role was to put an end to sexual hypocrisy and lies and to remove the creative impotence that hindered the development of young men and women—the very same erotic dullness that stereotypically characterized the Diaspora Jew. Beginning in the 1920s, as members of Hashomer Hatzair settled in agriculturally based collectives known as kibbutzim, the educational leadership of the movement argued that psychoanalytically informed education was the key to raising children free from bourgeois neuroses. They established strong ties with several European—and later also American—analysts, translated and published psychoanalytic texts, insisted that educators be analysed or, at least, psychoanalytically informed, and built a complex educational system founded on their particular understanding of Freudian insights. For them, psychoanalysis was also seen as a general prophylactic guaranteeing the mental hygiene of the community as a whole. The children in the kibbutz spent most of their waking hours in the company of nurses, educators, and teachers, while seeing their parents usually for two hours every day. This reflected the belief of kibbutz pedagogues that in this manner parental influence on the children would be far less than that of other educational figures. They believed that these conditions were sufficient to create an essential change in the “oedipal situation”, to decrease the children’s exposure to the “primal scene”, and to reshape relations between parents and children. Under the pretext of “solving oedipal conflicts” and of ensuring youth’s independence, the children’s homes in the kibbutz became in fact greenhouses in which children were trained for their full and harmonic integration into kibbutz society, which from the 1940s onwards sought to emulate Russian rather than German models in matters both ideological and practical. Thus, the child’s first encounter with the need to abandon his will in light of the group’s demands paralleled his displacement from his parents’ home, and in the context of becoming a member of a tribal group of children of his age, which would be an inseparable part of his life until he reached adulthood. In those years, the child incorporated one of the most important principles that would accompany him throughout his adult life: that the individual superego and the ideals of the group are one and the same, and the group is never wrong (see Berman, 1988; Bettelheim, 1969; Liban & Goldman, 2000; Peled, 2002; Rapaport, 1958; Rolnik, 2002; Spiro, 1958). While kibbutz pedagogues were trying to find a place for psychoanalysis in the socialist and neo-romantic admixture that informed the Zionist version of collective child upbringing, both Eder and Feigenbaum were in the midst of an attempt to bring clinical psychoanalysis to other segments in the Jewish community in Palestine. The debate on the tenets of psychoanalysis would soon take place at the watershed of two conceptions of psychoanalysis: an ideological–political conception and a scientific–therapeutic one. The division between these two facets of Freudian theory was far from clear-cut and did not necessarily represent the congruency that existed between the therapeutic and the ideological discourses in psychoanalysis. In fact, it was in the fields of pedagogy and “mental hygiene” that psychoanalysis was utilized in order to disentangle and to reconstruct the boundaries between different discourses. The psychoanalytic meetings led by Eder and Feigenbaum were frequented by a small but influential group of local intellectuals and seemed at first to be the beginning of psychoanalytic activity according to the Central European model.5 Feigenbaum’s psychoanalytic sojourn in the orient was even reported in one of the circular letters of the “Secret Committee”. Having been appointed director of the psychiatric hospital “Ezrat Nashim” in Jerusalem (the only mental institution in the country), Feigenbaum attempted to introduce the local medical community to psychoanalytic theory and delivered a number of public lectures on the interpretation of dreams and the psychopathology of everyday life. Shortly thereafter, Feigenbaum was dismissed from his position, and the first attempt to incorporate psychoanalytic theory into the public clinical framework was brought to an abrupt end (see Bergman, 1985; Hobman, 1945; Moses, 1998; Rolnik, 2002, 2007; Windhager, 2002).6 Coinciding with the untimely departure of Eder and Feigenbaum from the psychoanalytic scene in Palestine, an anonymous report in The International Journal of Psychoanalysis summed up the state of psychoanalysis in Jerusalem in unequivocal words:


In certain quarters (especially among the young immigrants) there is a tendency to introduce so-called “psychoanalysis” far too carelessly, and in a “fashionable” and vulgarized form. This, quite obviously, is doing harm, and it is most necessary that psychoanalysis should interfere in the direction of correct exposition and, above all, in checking this injurious growth. [Anon., 1924, p. 101]


The pioneering attempts by Feigenbaum and Eder to strengthen psychoanalytic theory in Palestine did not blossom into the foundation of a local psychoanalytic society that would be the centre of clinical activity, and Freud’s name at the time was mainly associated with the social and utopian aspirations of the Zionist youth movements.


Freud in Hebrew In order to draw a comparison between the individual’s sick soul and the plight of the Jewish nation that would reach beyond metaphorical boundaries, a scientific connection had to be found between private and public spheres, between the sickness of the individual and that of the collective. This pathway was revealed in Freud’s sociological essays. The diaries of Arthur Ruppin, the founder of Jewish sociology and one the main policymakers of Zionist colonization strategy, are replete with references to Freud’s essays. It appears that those entrusted with the politicization of the new Jewish psyche identified links in Freud’s social analysis, which facilitated the transformation of the psychology of the individual to one applicable to the group. Freud developed his political and social theories of liberalism–authoritarianism–patriarchalism in three works—Totem and Taboo (1912–13), Group Psychology and the Analysis of the Ego (1921c), and Moses and Monotheism (1939a)—and these are the main works through which Hebrew-language readers of the period became familiar with psychoanalysis. Apparently perplexed by the decision of the Hebrew Teachers’ Association of Palestine to choose Group Psychology as his first work to be translated into Hebrew, Freud wrote to express both his gratitude and his wish that readers of Hebrew would also be acquainted with some of his better known and more readable works.7 The teachers’ choice was anything but incidental. An essay utilizing positivist terms of drive to describe the process by which the individual becomes attached to society, Group Psychology seemed to cater to the needs of educators in Palestine. Freud’s language provided scientific underpinnings for the collectivist self-understanding of the members of the Jewish settlement. The individual was recognized only when he represented the desire to unite with the members of the group and thus to improve the group’s cohesion. A reading of this work was recommended especially for those “who took part in nationalist propaganda and in the dissemination of new ideas”, claimed one of the reviews written in honour of the translation (Strikovsky, in Rolnik, 2007, p. 252). The call to de-bourgeoisify Freudian theory characterized a number of critiques published coinciding with the Hebrew publication of Totem and Taboo. An article in one of the main organs of the labour movement called upon the Hebrew reader to “review and scrutinize the sociological psychoanalytic research and draw from it all that is important and helpful to our proletarian world-view” (in Rolnik, 2007 p. 262). Nevertheless, for Yehuda Dvir Dvossis, the translator of the work, Totem and Taboo served as a template on which an entirely different agenda could be played out: he believed that it was important to base Freud’s text on material within Jewish sources and notified Freud of his intent to add to the translation a number of comments from the Biblical and Talmudic literature in order to “strengthen and verify your claims, and to occasionally show them in a new light”.8 As may be expected, many of Freud’s critics had a fondness for the question of the relationship between the Jewish origins of the creator of psychoanalysis and his theories. Some even went so far as to claim that his concept of repression should be viewed as an acknowledgment of his faith. In 1942 the poet Ben-Shalom published an especially distraught article coinciding with the appearance of the Hebrew translation of The Psychopathology of Everyday Life (Freud, 1901b) and claiming that the “repressed Jewishness” of Freud and his contemporaries was at the basis of his theory, and that “the secret of Freud’s life and work is the secret of all the Anusim [forced converts]” (in Rolnik, 2007, p. 269). These harsh words were written at the height of the Second World War, at a time when many of the leaders and prominent thinkers in the Jewish settlement in Palestine found it difficult to renounce their ambivalent attitude towards immigrants from Central Europe. They found it necessary to express, together with their empathy and solidarity, their reproach to the assimilation of the German-speaking Jewry in the cultural life of Germany. The brunt of the rage of Freud’s readers during the war years was directed towards Moses and Monotheism (1939a), which provided the ultimate proof that the creator of psychoanalysis had an intellectual agenda that was different from that of his Hebrew readers. There were enraged responses to Freud’s historical dissertation regarding the Egyptian origins of Moses. In an open letter to Freud entitled “Sigmund Freud and ‘Made in Israel’”, the orchard owner Nachum Perlman attacked Freud’s attempt to “cast our spiritual assets into the depths of the ocean and to open our spiritual affairs before the eyes of those entirely foreign to Judaism”.9 A blend of enthusiasm and misunderstanding characterized the dispatches Freud received from his early followers in Zion. Particularly revealing was the conclusion reached by one doctor from Tel-Aviv: the analysis of the dreams of his German- and Russian-speaking patients, who had recently immigrated to Palestine, convinced him that they had an unconscious knowledge of Hebrew (Velikowsky, 1934). During the Arab riots of the 1930s a dentist from Berlin, who had recently joined a kibbutz, gave vent to his orientalistic anxieties by writing to Freud that the orchestrated nature of some of the attacks launched by the Arabs on Jewish communities can only be explained by the telepathic powers that some Arab villagers are endowed with. Freud cordially thanked the dentist for providing him with further evidence on the subject of telepathy—a secret passion that he was advised to suppress but could never really overcome.10 The failed attempts to establish clinical psychoanalysis in Mandate Palestine during the 1920s indicated that introducing Freudian theory into clinical circles and guarding it from deteriorating into a cultural and ideological curiosity would require a special brand of analyst—one whose analytic identity would not fall short of their Zionist convictions. Max Eitingon certainly epitomized this profile.


“Classic analysis”: émigré analysts in Palestine The encounter with Nazism and its murderous anti-Semitism, the forced migration to countries with different intellectual and scientific traditions, and, finally, Freud’s death in 1939 were closely intertwined during the period in which psychoanalysis entered its post-Freudian epoch (see Ash, 1991; Ash & Soellner, 1996; Fermi, 1968; Haynal, 1994; Kurzweil, 1996; Makari, 2008; Martin, 1986; Peters, 1992). During a short period, from 1932 until 1939, the majority of psychoanalysts from Central Europe emigrated to places untouched by the influence of German culture. This migratory wave had far-reaching effects both on the organizational aspect of the psychoanalytic movement and on the individual psychoanalytic societies that welcomed the immigrants into their ranks. Although Freud’s teachings gained footing in Palestine almost parallel to their inception in Vienna, the evolution of psychoanalysis as an institutionalized movement and form of treatment was markedly influenced by the arrival in 1933 of a handful of German-speaking analysts. Max Eitingon [1881–1943] had assumed a number of important administrative functions and key positions in addition to his historically most significant role as the founder of the Berlin Psychoanalytical Policlinic—the International Psychoanalytical Association’s most esteemed training facility.11 Judging by the reactions to his choice of Palestine as his new home, it appears that Eitingon’s colleagues in the IPA did not know that since his first visit to Palestine in 1910 Zionism had become his hobby-horse. In fact, the leaders of the various Zionist political, cultural, and scientific elites consulted him on matters both private and public.12 In 1933 even Albert Einstein went out of his way to dissuade Eitingon from moving to Palestine. He claimed that the large number of physicians immigrating to Palestine, the pitiable condition of the Hebrew University, and the tendency of Jewish intellectuals in Palestine to “hinder the development of others” would make it impossible for Freud’s pupil to pursue his analytic career in Jerusalem.13 Eitingon’s letters to Anna and Sigmund Freud show that he never regretted choosing Palestine as his new home and that his identification with “constructive Zionism” differed significantly from Freud’s vague solidarity with the fate of the Jews in Palestine and Einstein’s mainly spiritual and cultural vision of Zionism.14 Anna Freud wanted to know whether the future of the Jewish nation in the “Land of Israel” would also affect the state of psychoanalysis, and whether the new connection to the land and earth would cause the former Berlin analyst to suddenly become a landowner, or even a farmer. Eitingon’s idyllic descriptions of Palestine’s natural beauty echoed in Ms Freud’s heart: Jerusalem figured in one of her dreams as “a combination of the Vienna forests and Berchtesgaden”.15 A letter Eitingon sent to Freud shortly after he established the Palestine Psychoanalytic Society testified to his determination not to let historical and societal circumstances interfere too much with the dissemination of psychoanalysis:


The intensive building that characterizes this place forces us to follow our own private path and not become absorbed too early in public life, after all, it is the same people, with the same problems we had been used to dealing with, as clearly neither orthodox Jews nor Arabs are suitable in any way for psychoanalysis.16


Blinding himself to the two “others” of modern Jewish nationalism—religious orthodoxy and the indigenous Arab population—Eitingon was deploying the same strategy used by those who constructed the new Jewish self in the Holy Land. Nevertheless, he and a handful of former Berlin analysts did not count on the natural willingness of the relatively small community of German-speaking Zionists to adopt psychoanalysis, as though it were a Germanic spiritual asset, a “transitional object” that would help them retain their identity and facilitate their absorption in the orient. The fact that over 30% of the 130 analyses that took place at the Jerusalem Institute during the first seven years of its establishment were in either in Yiddish or in Hebrew demonstrates the significant success of Eitingon and his colleagues in increasing the number of persons interested in psychoanalysis (Brandt, 1941). Indeed, their repeated failed attempts to bring psychoanalysis to the Hebrew University could have served as a reminder to the analysts that the German model, on which the Hebrew University was founded and based, would hardly make Hebrew academe more receptive to Freud’s ideas, any more than German academe had been. It was only in 1977, on the occasion of the 30th IPA Congress in Jerusalem (the first congress to take place outside the borders of Europe), that the Hebrew university established a chair for psychoanalysis. In addition to serving as a “ministry of emigration” for dozens of Central-European analysts, Eitingon’s diplomatic deliberations with the representatives of the American Psychoanalytic Association during the years of the war concerned the volatile question of “lay analysis”, which threatened to alienate the US psychoanalytic establishment from its European counterpart. The few analysts who sought shelter in Palestine first had to present themselves before Eitingon, so that he might evaluate “their level of suitability for the unique conditions of this country”.17 Two renowned analysts, Wilhelm Reich and Theodore Reik, who had roamed throughout Europe during the 1930s, might well have come to Palestine, had it not been for Eitingon’s objections. He was concerned with the Communist identity of the one and the notorious rebelliousness of the other. Reik did visit Palestine in 1937 and lectured before the members of the Jerusalem group, yet his critical approach towards Jewish customs, as expressed in his books, was not only far from Eitingon’s strong personal affinity to the Jewish tradition, but might also have endangered the neutral and unobjectionable public image Eitingon attempted to foster among the new group (Rolnik, 2008b). The Palestine analysts saw themselves as the rightful heirs of the Berlin Institute, whose activities, though much diminished, had now been transferred to Jerusalem. A reading of the young Society’s statutes reveals that, in their wish to ensure that none would use the name of Freud’s theory in vain, Eitingon and his colleagues agreed to add an unusually strict clause stating that each member who desires to give a public lecture on psychoanalysis must inform the committee and receive its consent.18. The Society’s incorporation into the IPA ensued within a few months after it was established in September 1933. Given Freud’s and Jones’s scepticism vis-à-vis modern Jewish nationalism, it would be fair to assume that if it had not been for the stature of its leader or his continuing omnipresence in psychoanalytic politics, the incorporation of the Jerusalem group into the IPA would have taken years. The émigré analysts in Palestine had plenty of business even before they had spent two years in the country. Eitingon himself at the time had nine patients in analysis. Somehow the small Society managed to at least maintain an image of solidarity, even during periods in which a number of members had to face a disciplinary committee to face charges that they had strayed from “the Classic Freudian psychoanalysis”. This was an accusation often hurled by members at one another and one that helped to revive certain rituals and memories from Berlin and Vienna. Moishe Wolf, who became Eitingon’s successor as chairman of the Society, was shocked by what one training analyst wrote in 1950 in the popular newsletter for pedagogues, Mental Hygiene, and he began to suspect that she instructed the kindergarten teachers she trained to “spoil the children”. Subsequently the analyst was made to appear before a special committee and to prove that her theoretical position did not stray from those of classic Freudian analysis. Above and beyond its theoretical and technical implications, the term “classic analysis” seem to have been “hyper-cathected” by those who had been severed from their work environment, language, and culture. But regardless of how “classic” each and every analyst wished to perceive herself, in Palestine the old battles of psychoanalysis had to be fought on a daily basis. Vociferous opponents to psychoanalysis, such as Martin Buber or Gershom Scholem, had to be dealt with cautiously: not a single critique of Freud’s theory that appeared in the Hebrew press would be ignored, and pro bono professional support was given by Eitingon on a regular basis to various welfare projects.19 Yet critical voices from within the psychoanalytic society had begun to be heard: analysts who settled here are undergoing “privatization”, complained one analyst, who was also a kibbutz member, in a letter to Otto Fenichel:


Dr. Eitingon wants to continue the noble Berlin tradition and is leading the society in accordance with the principal [sic] of “touch me not”. The public can wait, he says, all we need to do is let people come and seek us. He is avoiding any active struggle, deliberately refraining from any popularization of psychoanalysis.20


What this “political Freudian” could not know is that years later Max Eitingon, who relentlessly targeted Wilhelm Reich’s political activism in Berlin, would be identified as the main secret benefactor of the Palestine Communist Party (PKK) (Dotan, 1991, pp. 385–387). The ideological and nationalist issues that were at the heart of the events of the time soon became part of the academic activities offered by the fledgling institute to its members. Eitingon’s own paper, titled “Sexuality and Dreams in the Talmud”, exemplifies the new ethnic–nationalist nuances of the scientific programme at the Institute.21 Interestingly enough, neither the encounter with murderous anti-Semitism nor the imminent Arab–Jewish conflict echoed openly or was reflected upon analytically during the formative years of the analytic Society. While inwardly the Society demonstrated much flexibility in allowing the reality in Palestine and the Nationalist Jewish sentiments to influence the lecture programme at the Institute, in the yearly reports sent to the IPA Eitingon and his successors were careful to emphasize clinical psychoanalysis as the mainstay of the Institute’s interests. This point raises the question whether the price paid by the immigrant analysts for the establishment of clinical psychoanalysis in Palestine/Israel did not entail relinquishing its critical aspects. Did the desire to ensure a wide consensus regarding the merits of Freud’s method of treatment in the collectivist atmosphere of the Yishuv not bring Eitingon and his colleagues to steer psychoanalysis onto a pronounced anti-intellectual path, leading potentially to a narrowing of the horizons of the local psychoanalytic discourse and the danger of severing its connections from the cultural–critical tradition? Modern Jewish nationalism, as radical and revolutionary as it may have been during its formative years, was not overly tolerant towards those who questioned the basic tenets of Zionism and nationalism. The amalgamation of utopian ideals with actual Zionist activities led to a renunciation of everything not directly identified with constructivist activism. “More deeds, less words” was and had always been the motto of the immigrants in Palestine who viewed philosophy, criticism, or reflection as a sort of antithesis to change and construction (Shapira, 1996). As we have seen, the critical psychoanalytic discourse of the left-wing youth movement rapidly distanced psychoanalysis from the individual and utilized it for the group’s ideological needs. Thus, a paradox is revealed: in the collectivist social reality that dominated Israeli political and intellectual culture until the late 1970s the ability of the analysts to “retreat to the clinic” and to offer their patients a space where they could recover their own private language was of primary importance. However, this very retreat to the clinic and to the seclusion of the therapy room distanced psychoanalysis from critical intellectual discourse, which could have contributed to the liberation of the individual and to the liberation of society from the utopian and near-Messianic elements it was creating and passing on to the younger generation. Those of Freud’s students who identified with the Zionist movement often found themselves in a position of a conflict of loyalty; this forced them to redefine their identification with the Central European psychoanalytic ethos.


Concluding remarks Now celebrating its 75th anniversary, the Israel Psychoanalytic Society is thriving in membership and candidates, as well as in applicants for training. It flourishes in theoretical diversity: among its members there are representatives of all major psychoanalytic orientations. If analysts in Israel seem to have given up the search for a common ground in matters of theory or technique, they certainly haven’t given up the wish to stay under the same roof, however loosely associated. Much thought has been given in recent years to developing strategies that will enhance a sense of solidarity and dialogue among Society members of different theoretical persuasions (Eifermann, 2009). The first generation of Israeli psychoanalysts was directly affected by the Holocaust or by some form of ethnic or political persecution. The second and third generation of Israeli analysts have witnessed or participated in any number of the many wars that have broken out between Israel and its neighbouring Arab states over the past 60 years. Unlike the present study, which focuses on the earlier years of the Israeli Society, no future portrait of contemporary Israeli psychoanalysis could justifiably leave out the impact of the Arab–Israeli conflict or the violent stamp of Israel’s occupation of the Palestinian population since 1967.22 If terror and persecution are notorious for their ability to probe the limits of mental and historical representation, then working analytically at the frontier of militant nationalism and religious fanaticism can at times prove challenging for those who attempt to translate their patients’ concrete horrifying situations into meaningful psychic experience and to convey the notion that reality, however harsh and painful it might be, is never dissociated from one’s inner world. Put aphoristically: no matter how one chooses to understand primary aggression in the mental life of the individual, those practising psychoanalysis in Israel are constantly reminded that they are working in a particular time and space which puts them at the very edge of the pleasure principle, if not beyond it.

Notes 1. The Jewish community in Palestine prior to the declaration of the state of Israel. 2. Letter of Ernest Jones, 7.12.1920 (in Wittenberger & Toegel, 1999, p. 202). 3. Founded in 1913 in Galicia, Ha-Shomer Ha-Zair came into being as a result of the merger of Hashomer [The Guard], a Zionist Scouting group, and Ze’irei Zion [The Youth of Zion], an ideological circle that studied Zionism, left-wing socialism, and Jewish history. By the late 1920s, there were already four kibbutzim (collective settlements) founded by Ha-Shomer Ha-Zair. The movement also formed a political party, advocating a bi-national solution in mandatory Palestine with equality between Arabs and Jews. 4. From a talk that Meir Ya’ari sent from Palestine to Vienna, most likely at the end of February or start of March 1921 (cf Mintz, 1995, 353ff). 5. The philosopher Hugo Bergman, who went into analysis with Feigenbaum, is probably the first analysand in the annals of psychoanalysis in Israel; his son, Martin Bergman, is a distinguished member of the New York Psychoanalytic Society. 6. Feigenbaum moved to the United States, became a member of the New York Psychoanalytic Society, and, together with Bertram Lewin, Gregory Zilboorg, and Frankwood Williams, established the Psychoanalytic Quarterly. 7. When the Zionist folklorist and historian Alther Druyanov sought to direct Freud’s attention to the similarities between The Interpretation of Dreams (Freud, 1900a) and the interpretations of dreams in Jewish Kabbalist and Talmudic literature, Freud replied: “Personally I find a more striking resemblance between my ideas on dreams and those of the ancient Greeks” (Freud to Druyanov 3.10.1910—Freud Museum, London). 22. For nearly five decades Israeli analysts have been studying the psychic ramifications of various forms of societal violence as well as political and religious forms of extremism (see Bunzl & Beit-Hallahmi, 2002; Erlich, Erlich-Ginor, & Beland, 2009; Klein, 2003; Kogan, 1995; Moses, 1993; Rangell & Moses-Hrushovski, 1996; Stein, 2009; Winnik, Moses, & Ostow, 1973). 26Mexico Historic relationship between the Psychoanalytic Society of Mexico (“Park Mexico”) and the IPA

Andrés Gaitán González

The origins of the Psychoanalytic Society of Mexico (PSM) can be traced to 1972, when the Mexican Psychoanalytic Association (Asociacion Psicoanalítica Mexicana: APM) experienced a split. The underlying conflicts were not of a scientific nature, since the theoretical and practical principles of the two groups remained the same. Through their resignation letters and public statements most of the members who resigned indicated that they did so because of their disagreement with the internal politics of the APM. No member complained about the prevailing psychoanalytic theory. It is important to remember that during its first 15 years the APM had only three internationally acknowledged members: Santiago Ramírez, Alfredo Namnum, and Avelino Gonzalez. The three of them resigned from the APM in 1972; the latter took on the direction of the PSM, Santiago Ramírez was appointed to honorary membership of PSM, and Alfredo Namnum created another independent group. In reference to the split in the APM, Santiago Ramírez once commented:


I will only say that this situation caused the resignation and separation of a considerable number of training analysts, full members and students…. Later on, Avelino formed his new group (which I honorably belong to), which works hard, continuously, and consistently. My congratulations and best wishes! … We have done something. In fact too much, Avelino. He would say, like Sigmund Freud at Budapest, “We are … ready … to admit the imperfections of our understanding … and to alter our methods in any way that can improve them” [Freud, 1919a (1918), p. 159]. [Sociedad Psicoanalítica de México, 1981, p. 22]


Under these circumstances, on 15 June 1972, the Mexican Group of Psychoanalytical Studies (Grupo Mexicano de Estudios Psicoanaliticos, GMEP) was organized with 12 members. By the spring of 1973, the group had 18 members: 9 training analysts and 9 full members. The name was chosen with the specific aim of obtaining IPA recognition as a study group. The IPA replied to our request by promoting the creation of an evaluation commission composed of Dr Zimmerman, Dr Guillermo Teruel, and Dr Frances Gitelson. The group requesting recognition comprised over one-third of APM members. Thus, it was hoped that it would be recognized. However, the opposition mounted by the remaining members of the APM was so fervent that the request was rejected. The effect of this rejection was reflected in the diminished support among members of the GMEP when some members resigned to pursue different paths based on their interests. Many others approached the APM. While some individuals were accepted into the group, many others were not. By October 1974, the GMEP was composed of three training analysts, three full members, one lifetime member, and two honorary members. As it was a small scientific group, it was decided to give anyone with the required skills the opportunity to receive adequate psychoanalytic training, in accordance with the standards of the IPA. Thus the group accepted other previous professional training (not necessarily medical studies). In March 1975, the Institute of Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy (IPP) was organized as a school aimed at training analysts. Though the Institute has been renamed twice, it has certified psychoanalysts for 34 years. Since 1995, the Institute has added to the traditional psychoanalysis courses Master’s courses in psychoanalysis and Doctorate studies in psychoanalytic research. In October 1976, the full members decided to rename the society the Psychoanalytic Society of Mexico. In June 1981, Avelino Gonzalez died suddenly; this triggered another split, now within PSM, but none of the original training analysts resigned. As in 1972, the name of the group was maintained by the majority. In the following years, efforts to join the IPA were unsuccessful. In 1983, when Dr Adam Limentani was President, he advised against further efforts to join the IPA because admission seemed a remote possibility. An effort undertaken at the end of the 1980s also ended in failure.

PSM–IPA Relationship In 1994, two of our training analysts, Roberto Gaitan and Rosalba Bueno, were accepted as members of the Los Angeles Institute and Society for Psychoanalytic Studies (LAISPS), and this allowed them to join the IPA as individual members. In 1999, in Santiago, Chile, they contacted the coordinator (Chair) of the IPA New Groups, who recommended that they request the IPA President, Professor Daniel Widlöcher, to initiate an evaluation of the group’s eligibility to formally join the IPA. Therefore, according to the internal procedures, the IPA Board appointed the first ad hoc committee to meet the PSM. A second ad hoc committee appointed by the IPA Board also visited “Park Mexico”—the name by which the Psychoanalytic Society of Mexico is commonly known, because its headquarters are located next to a park known by that name—in order to meet and interview each member, review their CVs, and individually evaluate their clinical material. The bylaws, procedure codes, and syllabus of Park Mexico were also reviewed. As a result, 13 members were proposed as direct members of the IPA. Later on, a Site Visit Committee was appointed to visit Park Mexico again in order to observe and participate in conferences and interview 30 eligible members of the institute. The report submitted to the IPA Board was favourable, since it was thought that, after 30 years of a psychoanalytic work including the training of several analysts in accordance with IPA standards, Park Mexico was eligible to join as a Provisional Society. Fifteen analysts joined as direct members, six of whom were recognized as training analysts. After the cancellation of the Toronto Congress due to the SARS epidemic, our case was presented and approved by the Board at the New Orleans Congress one year later, when it was deemed appropriate to enlarge the committee through the appointment of three IPA members and three PSM members, so as to integrate the Advisory Committee, with Alvaro Rey de Castro, Jorge de la Torre, and Romulo Lander representing the IPA and Rosalba Bueno, Roberto Gaitan, and Susana Velasco representing Park Mexico. Numerous visits over a period of time were required to review every aspect of the PSM. In November 2004, a formal request to be granted IPA certification as a Component Society was presented. However, its acceptance was delayed until the IPA Presidency was taken over by Dr Claudio Laks Eizirik and the new coordinating role of the New Groups Committee was taken over by Dr Marilia Aisenstein, when the Board reviewed the Provisional Society status. Simultaneously, the Advisory Committee Chair, Alvaro Rey de Castro, presented his resignation, and Dr Romualdo Romanowski took over this role. In February 2005, this modified Advisory Committee made its final visit to conduct a thorough review and drew the following conclusions:


After our last official visit in February 2005, as Advisory Committee to the Board Provisional Society of PSM–Park Mexico, and after having revised their capacities, qualities and organizational structure and their operation carefully, at both the level of the Society and of the Institute, we conclude: We are ready to express our complete satisfaction that the PSM–Park Mexico fulfills all the requirements in full compliance with the IPA’s Constitution and Bylaws. Thus we are ready to recommend that this Society, PSM–Park Mexico, be promoted to Component Society of the IPA at the next International Congress in Rio de Janeiro in July 2005. We have already sent copies of all the records and necessary documents that support this application.


Park Mexico presented one last formal request in early 2005, expecting to be accepted as Component Society in Rio de Janeiro. However, that did not take place until the summer of 2007, during the IPA Congress in Berlin. At that time, in collaboration with other Mexican societies, Mexico City was proposed to host the 47th IPA Congress: “Exploring Core Concepts: Sexuality, Dreams and the Unconscious.” Since then, the PSM has participated actively in the Congress organization, through the Local Organization Committee, to which four PSM members belong.

16Sweden The Swedish Psychoanalytical Association: early history and theoretical orientation

Bengt Warren

The idea for the Swedish Psychoanalytical Association was first considered in 1963, when Margit Norell, then the secretary of the training committee of the Swedish Psychoanalytical Society, left the Society and formed a Study Group with some of her analysands. The reasons for the break were that she and the members of the Study Group thought that the Swedish Psychoanalytical Society had become too orthodox. Mrs Norell and her followers were more interested in other psychoanalytic and psychotherapeutic practices. It might be fair to say that they were more interested in pre-oedipal phenomena, narcissism, and interactional aspects of transference and countertransference, concepts that are now accepted by the majority of psychoanalytic societies worldwide. Another more controversial aspect was that the group devalued the frequency aspect of the treatment and accepted three or even only two hours a week as psychoanalysis. Another somewhat more unclear factor concerned culture and politics: many of the originators and the early students regarded the “Holistic Society” as more leftist and politically “aware”. For its first 30 years, the name of the Society was “Svenska föreningen för holistiskt psykoterapi och psykoanalys” [Swedish Society for Holistic Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis]. When the Society eventually became more oriented towards mainstream psychoanalysis, the name was changed to “Svenska psykoanalytiska sällskapet” [Swedish Psychoanalytical Association]. The founding group was originally inspired by neo-Freudian theory. An especially important person in this connection was Harold Kelman, who visited the Society for many years, led seminars, and supervised much of the psychoanalytic work in the beginning. Dr Kelman could perhaps be described as the closest follower of Karen Horney: those two names—Horney and Kelman—were often used to describe the main line of theory and practice in the Society, and to some members they are still very important. Other important clinical and theoretical influences came from H. S. Sullivan, Frieda Fromm-Reichmann, Erich Fromm, and others. Apart from Kelman, the Society was in those days visited by Fromm, Montague Ullman, and others, all of them of a neo-Freudian, culturalist, or some kind of interpersonalist persuasion. In 1978 Margit Norell left the Society after a conflict with the other founding members. According to the tradition and the first statutes of the Society, candidates were full members of the Society once they had been accepted into the training programme. This has meant that candidates have been encouraged to take part on the Board and other administrative bodies from early on. Some of the functions on the Board—such as the office of the President—have always been reserved for graduated members, as, of course, have been all the members of the faculty.

Foundation and the IFPS The Society was formally founded in 1968 and became a member of the International Federation of Psychoanalytic Societies (IFPS) in 1972. The statutes of the IFPS require a frequency of three sessions per week for training analysis and a substantial theoretical programme lasting more than three years and built on “the psychoanalytic theory and therapy as initiated by Sigmund Freud and advanced by others, that is, the recognition and understanding of unconscious mental processes and the therapeutic use of the principles of transference and resistance”. The federation was originally neo-Freudian but has gradually changed its direction so that it now comprises many other persuasions, including mainstream Freudian groups whose clinical techniques are based on object relations theories, giving the federation a pluralistic character. The most famous member societies are the William Alanson White Society in New York and the German Psychoanalytical Society (DPG). The IFPS has 20 member societies in 11 countries in North America, South America, and Europe. Membership in the IFPS has meant a great deal to the Society, and most members appreciate the conferences and activities in the Federation. The activities consist mainly of a forum every fourth year, which is open to all interested persons and has much the same format as the International Psychoanalytical Association congresses, with lectures, group discussions, and the like. Between the forums there are scientific conferences open only to members in the federation—in a smaller format than the forums but with a similar aim. There are Nordic conferences, arranged by the Nordic psychoanalytic societies, that are much appreciated and well attended by members of Scandinavian societies. Another aspect of membership in the IFPS is that the federation’s periodical, the International Forum of Psychoanalysis (IFP) is edited in Sweden, and most of the editorial board are members of the Swedish Society. One member of the Society, Arne Jemstedt, was Deputy Secretary General of the Federation between 1989 and 1994. The criticism about the IFPS voiced in the Society is that the requirements are somewhat shaky, that societies are sometimes admitted too easily at the cost of quality, and that discussions tend at times to stray too far outside the realms of psychoanalysis. In 1991 the Society hosted the IFPS VI scientific conference, with more than 300 participants. The theme of the conference was “Male and Female Themes in the Psychoanalytic Process”.


Change in the theoretical orientation leading to an interest in the IPA In the mid-1970s many of the members in the Society shifted their interest from American neo-Freudian psychoanalysis towards the British school of psychoanalysis. This resulted in a predominance in the curriculum of the British tradition (Independent and Klein-ian), a much more thorough study of Freud’s work, and a greater emphasis on the processes between analysand and analyst in the psychoanalytic process. Together with this shift in the curriculum went a gradual shift concerning the frequency of the analysis of the candidates and of their training cases, so that from 1987 onwards the frequencies of these became a minimum of four sessions per week. We have had long-lasting and fruitful help and education from members of the Independent and Kleinian groups of the British Society, who have visited our Society for supervision, lectures, and seminars. Harold Stewart was an honorary member of the Society, and he has been very important to its development. In 1979 eight members started an organized Study Group, with Stewart as teacher and supervisor. It was mainly clinical, but also had theoretical discussions. It lasted for about three years and met for weekend seminars three or four times a year in Stockholm or in London. This group was later followed by two groups studying with Nina Coltart as teacher and supervisor, and the majority of the senior members have participated in one of these three groups. We also had much contact with Christopher Bollas, Donald Meltzer, and David Rosenfeld, as well as Haydée Faimberg, Joyce McDougall, and Michael Feldman. An important shift came around 1980, when the whole direction of the training programme was changed, as outlined above. To further the knowledge about Freud in the Society, Lars Sjögren, a leading member and training analyst in the Swedish Psychoanalytical Society—who was now also a member in the Holistic Society—led seminars about Freud for most of the analysts in the Society. His lectures were later collected in a book called Sigmund Freud Mannen och verket [Sigmund Freud, the man and the work], which was widely read and appreciated in Sweden. He was also engaged as a teacher in the ordinary training programme, giving courses, mainly on Freud, from 1978 to his death in 2008.


The role of the Society in the psychoanalytic movement The Society and its members have participated in introducing much of modern psychoanalytic theory and practice in Sweden—starting with neo-Freudian and interpersonalist theory, from the 1980s on members of the Society have introduced the British school to a wide Swedish public, notably by Ludvig Igra in several widely read books, the first presenting an overview of the British tradition and the second elaborating on Kleinian theory. In later years Arne Jemstedt translated and introduced Winnicott, and Jurgen Reeder has, through his books, given a personal interpretation of Lacan to Swedish readers. Many members have contributed in the same spirit by writing papers, lecturing, and supervising, as well as by organizing training programmes and participating as teachers in many psychoanalytic and psychotherapeutic training programmes in Sweden and the rest of Scandinavia. Many members supervise in psychiatric hospitals and other psychiatric institutions as well as in programmes for more severely mentally disturbed patients and drug addicts of different kinds. A new translation of Freud’s works (about 80% of the content in Gesammelte Werke) was edited between 1994 and 2009. This work is a cooperative project between our Society, the Swedish Psychoanalytical Society, a Society called “Psychoanalytic Forum” that edits Divan (a journal on psychoanalysis and culture), and, finally, a publishing company, Natur och Kultur, which has for many years supported psychoanalytic culture in Sweden.


Approaching the IPA We have now had good relations for many years with the Swedish Psychoanalytical Society, cooperating on administrative, scientific, and personal levels. The cooperation has materialized to a large extent in mutual efforts to get support from the political authorities for psychoanalytic training and to get public support through insurance systems to do psychoanalysis at a professional level. During recent decades we have always worked together in these matters and formed mutual bodies for negotiations and lobbying. On the scientific level we invite each other to conferences and lectures, and we have held joint clinical seminars, where members have presented clinical material. There are also several peer groups for supervision, consisting of members from both societies. Some interchange has also occurred between the internal periodicals of the two societies, which have published some of each other’s papers. After a period of intense and deep discussions we decided, in 1992, to apply for membership in the IPA. The process dragged on during the 1990s; sometimes we were hopeful, sometimes in despair. Notably we were encouraged by Professor Charles Hanly, who worked out a policy on historical anomalies. In 1997 we were visited by representatives from the New Groups Committee—a visit that ended in further complications. Finally, in 1998, we were accepted as a Study Group, and a Sponsoring Committee (composed of Heinz Henseler, Florence Guignard, and Karin Mispelblom) was appointed. The appointment of said committee represented a turning point in our contact with the IPA. Real professionals, they conducted their work with respect and in good spirits. In 2001 we were given status as a Provisional Society and finally in July 2005 we were accepted as a Component Society.

29Peru Psychoanalysis in Peru

Saúl Peña K. & Juan Manuel Yori

Professor Honorio Delgado [1892–1969] introduced psychoanalytic theory in Peru, and in Latin America as a whole, with the publication of the first book on the subject in Spanish, El psicoanálisis (Delgado, 1919). Delgado had maintained a correspondence with Freud since 1919 (Rey de Castro, 1983). However, he did not start a psychoanalytic school, and after 1930 he became an opponent of psychoanalysis. Professor Carlos Alberto Seguín (1907–95) was the most important pioneer of psychoanalysis in Peru. After a period of training in the United States, he established, in 1941, the first school of dynamic psychotherapy in the Hospital Obrero, where he recruited his disciples, including the pioneers and first Peruvian psychoanalysts, Drs Saúl Peña K., Carlos Crisanto A., and Max Hernández C.

Origins During the first three years Dr Peña worked alone; after the arrival of Carlos Crisanto (1973) and Max Hernández (1974), the Centre for the Development of Psychoanalysis in Peru was founded by this pioneer group. The most notable influences on Peruvian psychoanalysis were, in the beginning, those of Freud, Heimann, and Winnicott. Dr Peña´s analyst, Paula Heimann, had been analysed by Theodor Reik, who had been analysed by Freud. Thus, Peruvian psychoanalysis has come down in a direct line from Sigmund Freud. Peruvian psychoanalysis, through the presence of members trained abroad, was at that time much influenced by the British Psychoanalytical Society (Independent Group), and later on by the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association (APA), the Sigmund Freud Institute of Frankfurt, and the Venezuelan Psychoanalytic Association. Peruvian psychoanalysis has been and continues to be anti-dogmatic; it is open to the study of new developments in psychoanalysis and to dialogue with other disciplines. The Peru Psychoanalytic Society (SPP) has always fought for this and for its humanistic essence, integrating medical and cultural psychoanalysis, reaching out to the humanistic disciplines. The importance of the Peruvian movement was initially recognized by the Universidad Nacional Mayor de San Marcos, when it appointed Alexander Mitscherlich as Professor Honoris Causa for his social investigations from a psychoanalytical perspective.


The Centre for the Development of Psychoanalysis in Peru The Centre for the Development of Psychoanalysis in Peru was created in 1974 and included, as members, Drs Peña, Crisanto, and Hernández, as well as professionals from various disciplines. This was made possible through the interest, creative capacity, and profound motivation of those who participated in that endeavour. Many foreign analysts collaborated generously by visiting the Peruvian group. We welcomed at that time some 100 psychoanalysts, including Daniel Widlöcher, then Secretary General of the IPA, Horacio Etchegoyen, Adam Limentani, and Leo Rangell, all past Presidents of the IPA, as well as Arnaldo Rascovsky, Jacob Arlow, and many others. The Peruvian group worked for the recognition of the Peruvian movement at all of the Latin American and international congresses.


The formation of the Psychoanalytic Study Group In 1979, at the 31st Congress in New York, the IPA conferred on the group the status of Pre-Study Group. The first group of sponsors consisted of David Zimmermann (Porto Alegre) as chairman, Carlos Plata Mujica (Colombia), and Guillermo Teruel (Venezuela). Their mission was to structure an institute according to the standards of the IPA. Because of their scientific background, the three Peruvian pioneers were appointed didactic analysts. At the 32nd IPA Congress in Helsinki in 1981, the Peruvian group achieved the status of Study Group. The second Sponsors’ Committee was chaired by Serge Lebovici (past President of the IPA), and included Willy Baranger (past President of the Psychoanalytical Federation of Latin America) and Otto Kernberg. Consultants were Adam Limentani (President of the IPA), Leo Rangell (Honorary President of the IPA), and Inga Villarreal, then Associate Secretary for Latin America. During their first visit the Institute of the Peruvian Psychoanalytic Society was constituted, and the Joint Training Committee was formed by the sponsors and Drs Peña, Crisanto, and Hernández, as members of the local Sponsors’ Committee. This marked the official organization of psychoanalysis in Peru.


The Peru Psychoanalytic Society The Peru Psychoanalytic Society was officially founded on 30 January 1980, in the presence of John Bowlby. Its founding members were Saúl Peña, Carlos Crisanto, Max Hernández, Noel Altamirano, and Gustavo Delgado, and its Executive Council was composed of Saúl Peña as president, Carlos Crisanto as vice-president, and Max Hernández as didactic coordinator. The movement was reinforced by the arrival of Moisés Lemlij from England (Freudian Group) and Jaime Heresi from Venezuela. Later arrivals included Alberto Péndola, Fernando Maestre, Jaime Velasco, Pedro Morales, and Jaime Motta, all from Argentina (APA), and César Rodríguez Rabanal and Hilke Engelbrecht from the Sigmund Freud Institute in Frankfurt. The first cultural programme carried out by the SPP was in the form of “Psychoanalytic Conferences”, with the aim of presenting psychoanalytic thinking to the Peruvian academic and intellectual communities. In response to the enormous demand for training in psychotherapy among young professionals (psychologists, anthropologists, etc.), two institutions were created in 1981 by members of the Society: the Sigmund Freud Institute of Psychotherapy, Research, and Interdisciplinary Application of Psychoanalysis, and the Lima Association of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy. The first group trained a small number of psychotherapists, but then discontinued its activity. The second group has trained a large number of psychoanalytic psychotherapists and continues its operation. Several of their graduates have become candidates and members of the SPP. The Joint Training Committee appointed Dr Jaime Heresi as member of the Group of the Local Committee of Sponsors and Drs Altamirano, Delgado, Engelbrecht, Lemlij, and Péndola to the didactic function. For the period 1983–84, M. Hernández was elected president, S. Peña director of the Institute, J. Heresi vice-president, M. Lemlij scientific secretary, and A. Péndola treasurer. At the 34th Congress, in Hamburg in 1985, the IPA conferred on the group the status of Provisional Society of Psychoanalysis. At the same meeting the first psychoanalysts trained and certified in Peru—Marcos Gheiler, Matilde Ureta de Caplansky, and Fernando Alayza (analysed by Dr Peña), Juan Manuel Yori and José Cabrejos (analysed by Dr Crisanto), and Alvaro Rey de Castro (analysed by Dr Hernández)—were incorporated as members of the SPP and the IPA. At the 35th IPA Congress, in Montreal in 1987, after 17 years of intense and consistent work, and during the presidency of Dr Jaime Heresi, the society finally became a Component Society of the IPA. At the same congress Dr Max Hernández was elected vice-president of the IPA, which we considered recognition not only of his important personal merits, but also of the SPP.

Psychoanalytic programmes The first Peruvian congress, “Psychoanalysis and Identity”, was held in Lima, in 1988, during the presidency of Moisés Lemlij. The participants were Peruvian and Latin American analysts. In 1989, also during the presidency of Dr Lemlij, the “International Symposium of Universal, American, and Contemporary Myths” was held in Cuzco (Lemlij, 1990). This symposium was attended by a large number of psychoanalysts from Europe and from North and South America. In 1989, the First Congress of Candidates of the Institute, “Training and Social Crisis”, was held in Lima to study the psychological aspects of social unrest. The Peruvian Congresses of Psychoanalysis continue to be held every two years with the participation of the members of the SPP, the psychoanalytic community of Peru, and distinguished members of the IPA. As President of the Psychoanalytic Federation of Latin America, FEPAL, Dr Peña organised with FEPAL and the Peruvian Psychoanalytical Society the XXth Latin American Congress of Psychoanalysis: “Femininity and Masculinity” held in Lima in 1994. After the Congress an International Psychoanalytic Clinical Dialogue was held in Machu Picchu. During Dr Peña’s presidency, the publication of the Revista Latino-Americana de Psicanálise [Latin American Journal of Psychoanalysis] was re-started. In the same period the first Latin American Congress of Psychoanalysis of Children and Adolescents (1994) was held in Córdoba, Argentina. This was a landmark in the history of psychoanalysis in Latin America. Gustavo Delgado edited the Libro anual de psicoanálisis (1985–91) making available in Spanish the most important works published in the IJP. Other important international psychoanalytic events organized in Peru have been “At the Threshold of the Millennium” (1998) and “The End of the Battle” (2001), under the direction of Moisés Lemlij, as well as the XXVIth Congress of FEPAL: “Freud’s Legacy: 150 Years after His Birth”, followed by a clinical meeting held in Cuzco, both under the presidency of Álvaro Rey de Castro, then president of FEPAL.


Publications and outreach to the society The Peruvian Psychoanalytic Library, under the direction of Moisés Lemlij, has published numerous works covering a broad spectrum of psychoanalytic thinking. Another important group of publications have been produced in association with the Interdisciplinary Seminar of Andean Studies (SIDEA). This Seminar is dedicated to studies in history, ethnology, and anthropology from a psychoanalytic perspective. A significant contribution to the comprehension of the history of Peru has been the book Memoria del bien perdido. Conflicto, identidad y nostalgia en el Inca Garcilaso de la Vega (1993), by Dr Max Hernández. Dr Saúl Peña made an important contribution to the study of corruption in political systems from a psychoanalytic point of view in his book Psicoanálisis de la corrupción. Política y ética en el Perú contemporáneo (2003). The SPP currently has an agreement with the Fondo Editorial de la Pontificia Universidad Católica del Peru for the purpose of publishing psychoanalytic books. In 1997 the Servicio de Atención Psicoanalítica (SAP) was created to offer psychoanalytic treatment to people with lower incomes. This clinic provides an important service to the community. Its first director was Dr H. Engelbrecht, and it is now led by Dr C. Crisanto. Many of the candidates’ control cases are referred from the SAP. Two of our members, Drs Max Hernández and Moisés Lemlij, have received the Mary S. Sigourney Award for contributions to psychoanalysis. Members of the SPP have also worked to introduce psychoanalysis to the wider public. Through radio broadcasts from 1989 until today, Fernando Maestre has presented the benefits of psychoanalysis for problems relating to such subjects as psychological problems, marital difficulties, and family crises. Since 1993, Jorge Bruce has published a newspaper column dealing with clinical, cultural, social, and political issues. Many of the members of the SPP participate in radio and TV programmes, making the public familiar with psychoanalytic thinking and the benefits of psychoanalytic therapies. Members of the SPP hold significant positions concerning important issues in the country and society: Dr Max Hernández works as the Secretary General of the National Agreement, Dr Alvaro Rey de Castro is a member of the National Initiative Against Corruption, Dr Saúl Peña is a member of the Restructuring Committee of the National Intelligence Service, among many others. Many other members have also contributed to the Comisión de la Verdad y la Reconciliación. Several members contribute to the organization and development of institutions dealing with applied psychoanalysis in the fields of mental health and education. Among them we have had presidents of the Peruvian Psychiatric Association and Peruvian Psychotherapy Association. Members of the Society are also working in CEDAP (Matilde de Caplansky) and La Casa de la Familia (Bibiana Maza)—institutions dedicated to giving psychological assistance to minors in need. Dr Marcos Gheiler has played an important role in the application of psychoanalysis to education. The work done by members of the SPP in the assistance of people affected by violence as a consequence of the terrorist war in Peru is also widely recognised. Dr Luis Herrera has contributed to it in a significant way, as have many candidates of the Institute. In 2001 Max Hernández and Moisés Lemlij, in association with the Pontificia Universidad Católica del Perú, founded a Master’s programme in theoretical studies of psychoanalysis. During the presidency of Dr Juan Manuel Yori, under the coordination of Jorge Kantor and Pilar Gavilano, an annual festival of cinema and psychoanalysis was established to promote the discussion of films from a psychoanalytic perspective.


Institute Currently, there are 44 professors on the teaching staff of the Institute, and 45 candidates in training to become analysts. The training of child and adolescent psychoanalysts has recently been begun under the leadership of Dr Sara Flores. The SPP now has more than 100 members—either associates or candidates. It operates from a splendid house in Lima (acquired under the presidency of Maria del Carmen Ramos), and it is a well-recognized and important institution within the scientific and academic community. Our institution also has the “Jaime Heresi Library” (directed by Dr Francisco Otero), which contains most of the relevant psychoanalytic literature. The library is open to the public and offers membership to students and professionals in the mental health field. From 1999 until 2003, the Journal of the Society was co-directed by Dr Teresa Bolaños, Dr Luis Herrera, Dr Jorge Parodi, and Dr María del Carmen Ramos; from 2003 until 2007, the directors were Drs Teresa Ciudad and Teresa Bolaños, and from 2007 to 2009, Dr Ciudad. In 2010 Dr Augusto Escribens was appointed Director. The Society has developed important ties with psychoanalytic institutions in Latin America and abroad. Its members participate in several committees of FEPAL and IPA. Drs Hernández, Rey de Castro, and Lemlij have been vice-presidents of the IPA, Dr Lemlij also having acted as treasurer of the IPA; Dr Engelbrecht was a member of the House of Delegates and Dr De la Puente is currently a member of the Board of the IPA as a representative from Latin America. Drs Peña and Rey de Castro have been presidents of FEPAL and have participated as sponsors of psychoanalytic groups in Eastern Europe and México. Currently Dr Teresa Ciudad is the president of the SPP; one of her aims, and that of the Board, is to promote the participation of the members in the management of our institution by keeping in close contact with them through a monthly report detailing the internal discussions of the Board, the decisions taken by it, and the scientific activities, as well as contributions by the members. The SPP is now a mature society that looks with optimism to the future. It will continue working for the development of psychoanalysis.


Bibliography Delgado, H. (1919). El psicoanálisis. Lima: Ed. Sanmarti. Hernández, M. (1993). Memoria del bien perdido. Conflicto, identidad y nostalgia en el Inca Garcilaso de la Vega. Lima: IEP & Biblioteca Peruana de Psicoanálisis. Lemlij, M. (Ed.) (1990). Mitos. Universales, americanos y contemporáneos. Un enfoque multidisciplinario (3 vols). Sociedad Peruana de Psicoanálisis. Lima: Universidad San Antonio Abad del Cusco. Peña, S. (2003). Psicoanálisis de la corrupción. Política y ética en el Perú contemporáneo. Lima: Peisa. Rey de Castro, A. (1983). Freud y Honorio Delgado. Crónica de un desencuentro. Hueso Húmero (Lima), 15–16. Also in: El psicoanálisis en el Perú: notas marginales. Debates en Sociología (Lima), 11 (1985).







18United Kingdom A brief history of the British Psychoanalytical Society

Ken Robinson

When Ernest Jones set about establishing psychoanalysis in Britain, two intertwining tasks faced him: establishing the reputation of psychoanalysis as a respectable pursuit and defining an identity for it as a discipline, distinct from but related to cognate disciplines. This latter concern with identity would remain central to the development of the British Society for decades to come, though its inflection would shift as the Society sought first to mark out British psychoanalysis as having its own character within the International Psychoanalytical Association, and then to find a way of holding together warring identities within the Society.

Establishing psychoanalysis: The London Society Ernest Jones’s diary for 1913 contains the simple entry for 30 October: “Ya meeting. Psycho-med. dinner” (Archives of the British Psychoanalytical Society [hereafter Archives], P04-G-A-02). In early August he had returned to London from ignominious exile in Canada as a result of damaging accusations of inappropriate sexual conduct in relation to children. Having spent time in London and Europe the previous year, he now returned permanently, via Budapest, where from June on he had received analysis from Ferenczi. Once in London, he wasted no time in beginning practise as a psychoanalyst, seeing his first patient on 14 August (Diary, 1913, Archives), though he would soon take a brief break to participate in what would turn out to be a troublesome Munich Congress in September (for Jones’s biography generally, see Maddox, 2006). London showed a growing interest in unconscious phenomena and abnormal psychology. It had, as Freud put it, writing to Jones in 1911, “become a better soil since [Jones] left it” (Paskauskas, 1993, p. 112). By 1911 Freud had been honoured with membership of the Society for Psychical Research and in the following year “A Note on the Unconscious in Psycho-Analysis” was published for the first time in its Proceedings (Freud, 1912). In November 1912 Jones’s influential Papers on Psycho-Analysis had been published. The existing institutions in 1913, with their overlapping memberships, already catered to an extent for the burgeoning interest in psychoanalysis. As his diary entry suggests, Jones was astute enough politically to play his part in them, attending the Psycho-Medical Society dinner on the same day as the inaugural meeting of the London Society, but none was single-minded enough for Jones’s purpose. The British Psychological Society, which had been formed in 1901, was too broad-based, even though in Cambridge C. S. Myers, and in his own way W. H. Rivers, were shifting the focus of academic psychology towards clinical psychology (Forrester, 2008, p. 39). On the other hand, both the Medical Section of the Society for Psychical Research founded in 1911 and the Psycho-Medical Society were too much the province of hypnotists (Paskauskas, 1993, p. 145) and probably too closely allied to the Society for Psychical Research for Jones’s liking. He was always wary of his colleagues’ interest (including Freud’s and Ferenczi’s) in extra-sensory perception. The most likely bedfellow was the Psychiatric Society (a branch of the Royal Society of Medicine), (Paskauskas, 1993, p. 145), but Jones settled on creating a discrete Society. With “a black mark to [his] name in London” Jones was determined to establish himself as a respectable practitioner and psychoanalysis as a respectable treatment in its own right (Paskauskas, 1993, p. 196). His was the first practice in Britain dedicated to psychoanalysis. Ironically psychoanalysis had always dealt with matters that were far from respectable, but this impulsion towards establishing its respectability and that of its practitioners in Britain was one of the defining features of Jones’s vision for at least a decade and a half. There were others he could call on whose reputations were good and who were interested in psychoanalysis. One was Jones’s pupil Bernard Hart, in whose hands he had left psychoanalysis while he was in Canada (Paskauskas, 1993, p. 2); there was David Forsyth, who would later have analysis with Freud, and David Eder, who was already practising and had described a case of obsession and hysteria to the Neurological Section of the British Medical Association in 1911. In addition, there were colleagues who had some experience of hypnosis, including Douglas Bryan and Constance Long, who would become founding members of the London Psycho-Analytical Society, though Jones was keen to distinguish psychoanalysis from hypnosis. With the experience behind him of forming the American Psycho-Analytical Association in 1911, Jones wasted no time in founding the London Society. On 13 September 1913 he wrote to Freud of his plans. Six weeks later “the London Psycho-Analytical Society was duly constituted … with a membership of nine” (Paskauskas, 1993, p. 233). Another six colleagues joined to form the original membership of fifteen (Jones, 1959, p. 229). For almost half, membership was no more than an expression of interest. Only seven lived in London. Two lived elsewhere in England, another in Belfast, and one in Edinburgh; the remainder lived in Toronto, Bombay, Jubbalpore, and Beirut (Zeitschrift, 2 [1914]: 411). Jones apart, only three of the London-based members had any first-hand experience of practising anything resembling analysis. In his determination to create a Society as quickly as possible, Jones had called on old pupils, friends, and patients. In the case of the overseas members he recruited colleagues who were already members of the American Psychoanalytic Association. His haste may have been fuelled by plans first mooted in July to create a Medico-Psychological Clinic in Brunswick Square “to provide a place where treatment by psycho-therapy might be carried out, to bring this method of treatment within the reach of the poorer classes, and to provide inquirers with opportunities for study and investigation” (BMJ, 1913 [2]: 32).


Turf Wars: Jones vs the Jungians Founded less than a year before Britain entered the First World War, the London Psycho-Analytical Society’s future trajectory could not but be affected by the war. But even before Franz Ferdinand’s assassination, internal dissention was brewing within the psychoanalytic movement that would lead to a turf war in London and leave its mark on the Society. Jones was alert to danger from within psychoanalysis and determined to preserve its domain from encroachment, simplification, and dilution. He wished the London Society to be unambiguously Freudian, and, given the recent history of psychoanalysis internationally, this is not surprising. No sooner was Jones in London than he participated in two events where Jung gave papers: a meeting of the Psycho-Medical Society on 5 August and the 17th International Congress for Medicine on 6–12 August. Here Jones had heard Jung speak of the “prospective tendency” as part of his argument that psychoanalysis should be “freed from the purely sexual standpoint” (Jung, 1913, pp. 238 and 247). The psychoanalytic movement had only recently had to deal with the secession of Adler and Stekel; now it was confronted with the growing difference between Freud and Jung, whose relationship declined increasingly from May 1912 on. Faced with Jung’s divergence from Freud’s libido theory, in 1912 Jones had suggested to Freud the idea of a Secret Committee to protect the psychoanalytic movement against destructive dissent. Part of the remit that the Secret Committee gave itself was to guard against threat from within, its members monitoring each other’s writings to “coordinate [their] own unconscious aims with the demands and interests of the movement” (Paskauskas, 1993, p. 149). In this spirit Ferenczi warned Jones in December 1913, for example, that he, steeped as he was in Freud’s thinking, was close to using the concept of a “prospective tendency” in relation to dreams (Ferenczi to Jones, 16 December 1913, Archives, P04-C-C-04). In this context Jones acted quickly—“over-hastily”, he thought with hindsight (Jones, 1959, p. 239)—to found the London Society. The inaugural meeting of the London Society took place only weeks after the Munich Congress of the IPA, where differences made for “an atmosphere prickly with partisanship” (Gay, 1988, p. 239). It would prove to be Jung’s last congress: on 20 April 1914 he resigned formally as President of the IPA. Once the Society was founded, Jones wrote to Jung as President of the IPA seeking recognition as a Component Society, a move designed to strengthen the opposition to Jung within the IPA, as Ferenczi recognized (Brabant et al., 1993, p. 518), though in the event it was not wholly successful. Writing to Jones in May 1914, Ferenczi asked: “How are the scientific (Ψα) conditions shaping up in England? Did the break with Zurich have any after-effects in the London group?” (Ferenczi to Jones, 20 May 1914, Archives, P04-C-C-04). It was an apposite question. The parting of the ways between Freud and Jung led to British colleagues having to decide where their allegiances lay. Several were faced with a dilemma. For a while Jones could report that eight members regularly attended meetings, but soon he considered that meetings were wasted in trying “to reinterpret the concrete data of the unconscious into Jung’s cloudy generalities” (Jones, 1959, p. 239). After a year or so he suggested that meetings be suspended (Paskauskas, 1993, pp. 302–303).
