28Mexico The Guadalajara Psychoanalytic Group

Hugo Torres Salazar

The Guadalajara Psychoanalytic Group (GPG) consisted (as of 2007) of eight didactic analysts at Grupo Guadalajara, seven of whom were trained at the Asociación Psicoanalítica Mexicana (APM) [Mexican Psychoanalytic Association]; the eighth analyst was trained at the Instituto de la Asociación Pscioanalítica Argentina (APA). The study, practice, and promotion of psychoanalysis by this group go back to 1976, with the arrival from Argentina of Drs Norah Gramajo Galimany and Jorge Saretta Josgrinlberg. Since that time, we can identify four stages in the training and development of this group. First stage: The first stage was the formation of an informal study group in psychoanalysis, organized by Drs Gramajo and Saretta, together with other analysts in Guadalajara. This developed into a Study Group devoted to the study of Freud. This group carried out its work until July 1979. Second stage: In early 1980, because of the interest shown by the members of this Study Group, Drs Gramajo and Saretta proposed the development of a four-year course, comprising a Freudian theoretical and technical seminar, collective and individual supervision, reflection groups, and personal analysis of each member. Each student in this group attended three or four sessions per week. To popularize and develop the group’s psychoanalytic research, members were encouraged to participate in congresses and to develop collective and individual monographic projects, in which the personal and academic accomplishments of the group were demonstrated. Beginning in 1984, the first group of psychoanalytic psychotherapists graduated, and since then the training of psychoanalytic professionals has been ongoing. This has had an effect on Jalisco and nationwide, as these professionals apply their psychoanalytic knowledge and techniques. Third stage: This stage is marked by the Study Group receiving accreditation from the Public Education Administration [Secretaría de Educación Pública (SEP)]; the offering of a Master’s degree in Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy; the approval of its study plans; and the issuing of an Official Certificate to graduates of this programme by the SEP. Master’s degree studies began in August 2006 with the admission of a propedeutic group, which to this date is the first class to apply this educational modality. In August of 2007, the second class graduated, and the students currently (2010) enrolled are studying the propedeutic course. In 2007 the SEP issued the REVOE 2007 367 (Reconocimiento y Validez Oficial de Estudios [Official Recognition and Accreditation of Studies]), recognizing and giving official approval to the group’s programme of study. Fourth stage: This long history has borne fruit with the acceptance of a proposal to accept the Guadalajara Group as a Study Group of the International Psychoanalytical Association. On 25 May 2006, a site visit was carried out by Dr Carlos Valedón (Venezuela) and Dr Liliana Puauluán (Chile), who had been sent by the IPA. Upon finalizing the visit, these visitors reported that the GPG fulfils the necessary requisites to continue the process, and this report was submitted to the IPA. At the Executive Committee meeting of the IPA in Berlin in July 2006 the Guadalajara Psychoanalytic Group was accepted as a Study Group of the IPA. On 19 April 2007, Drs Carlos Valedón (Venezuela) and Alejandro Tamez (Monterrey, Mexico) visited our programme to carry out interviews and supervision of clinical cases, in order to formalize the structure of the GPG, consisting of teaching analysts, titular analysts, adjunct analysts, and analysts in training.

Principal activities of the group The following are the research, promotional and psychoanalytic practice activities of the Guadalajara Psychoanalytic Group:


the José Záyago Psychoanalysis Clinic the Norah Gramajo Psychoanalysis Library Symposium of the Americas publication of magazines and books individual and collective supervision internal Research Projects conferences to publicize and promote our work.


Group members attend congresses of the various national and international associations—IPA, APA, FEPAL, and APM—where they present research projects in theoretical and clinical areas.








42The International Training Commission, 1925–1938: efforts to standardize training

Michael Schröter

Before the First World War, psychoanalytic training, as far as it existed, was usually carried out by way of instruction given at the scientific sessions of the few local groups that formed the International Psychoanalytical Association. Membership in the IPA would serve as “a kind of guarantee” that a certain practitioner conducted psychoanalysis lege artis (Freud, 1914d, p. 43). After the war a growing need for a stricter form of training made itself felt (cf. Hale, 1995, pp. 25–37). It led, among other things, to a report of the IPA Central Executive, presented by Ernest Jones at the Berlin Congress in 1922, urging the branch societies “to demand of all candidates for membership a due standard of knowledge” (International Journal of Psychoanalysis [IJP], 1923, p. 239). This clause attained additional weight from the fact that a proposal put forward at the previous IPA Congress (The Hague, 1920) to consider issuing diplomas in psychoanalysis had been rejected by the same report, which continued to maintain “that membership of the Societies tends to constitute the present-day equivalent of a Diploma”.1 It was clear, however, even at this early date, that national discrepancies, such as between the Swiss and the German groups, concerning the amount of training required for membership, or between the New York and most other groups concerning the admission of non physicians, would constitute a serious problem as soon as a member of one Society wanted to be transferred to another. Because of the very international character of the Freudian school, some standardization of training and of the conditions of membership proved to be inevitable.

I Jones’s report of 1922 declared that the Central Executive did not feel themselves to be in a position to define the nature of training required for admission to membership “because of the difference in the opportunities of such training that are available in different countries”. This remark alluded to the situation in Berlin, where a special agency of professional training in psychoanalysis was in the course of being established, starting in 1920 with the creation of a policlinic that, on the one hand, provided free treatment for the needy and, on the other hand, served as a pool where students could find suitable patients. The decisive driving force behind this creation was Max Eitingon (Schröter, 2004a). Within three years the policlinic grew into “a training institute with an academic character and a fixed syllabus” (Schröter, 2004b, p. 172) that formally laid down its guidelines (IJP, 1924, p. 117).2 In the early 1920s the training available here was sought by many people from abroad, among them Austrians, Hungarians, and quite a few men and women from England (Makari, 2008, p. 372). The tripartite structure of the Berlin training, consisting of personal analysis, courses, and treatment under supervision, is well known (cf. Schröter, 2002b, pp. 877–879). The requirement of a training analysis, echoing an older Zurich suggestion, had met with general approval after it had been voiced at the Budapest Congress in 1918. While lectures and seminars were given in the tradition of academia, supervised analysis was an important Berlin invention. The time span needed for training was fixed at a formidable minimum of three years. Admission to the Berliner Psychoanalytische Vereinigung was restricted to analysts who had graduated. And there was a further crucial advance that is usually neglected: in Berlin, the right to train was denied to individual analysts; all authority in matters of training was transferred to a collective body, nominated by the Society: the training committee. These elements, taken together, can be said to form the Berlin system of training (nowadays fittingly dubbed the “Eitingon model”), and most of them were genuine local innovations. In 1925, at the Bad Homburg Congress, Eitingon presented this system to the IPA (IJP, 1926, pp. 130–134; cf. Schröter, 2002b, pp. 879–880). He reported that Vienna, by founding their own institute, had just followed in Berlin’s footsteps, and that London and Budapest were on the verge of doing so. At the business meeting a resolution was passed stipulating that all IPA societies should elect a training committee, and that these committees should combine to form an International Training Commission (ITC), which was to be the IPA’s “central organ” for all questions connected with training (IJP, 1926, p. 141). In the long run, this resolution was aimed at encouraging the foundation of more institutes. As Ferenczi put it, Eitingon’s initiative originated from his “aspiration to impose the brilliant example of Berlin everywhere as an obligatory model” (letter to Eitingon, 24 October 1925). Eitingon naturally took over the presidency of the new Commission. Soon he became IPA President too.


II Eitingon had already in Bad Homburg raised the question of the qualification of candidates—namely, the lay question that divided the American3 and the main European groups and caused sharp antagonism (Wallerstein, 1998, 1–49). In fact, this subject was primarily promoted by Freud, who rejected the inclusion of psychoanalysis into medical practice and wanted to establish it as an independent profession that should be open to non-physicians as well (see Freud, 1926e, 1927a). Although Eitingon’s main emphasis was on expanding the Berlin principles of “thorough training” within the IPA, he made Freud’s wish his own and launched a public discussion about the issue of lay analysis (IJP, 1927, pp. 174–283, 392–401). For some years this issue dominated, and interfered with, his own agenda. In fact, the core conflict at that time was not so much about the admission of lay candidates but, rather, about the predominance of either central regulation, to be monitored by the ITC, or local autonomy (Schröter 2002b). In 1927, the two antagonistic positions clashed at the IPA Congress held in Innsbruck. When Eitingon presented a resolution that obliged all branch societies to admit suitable non-medical candidates, he was unable to get it through. Not only the Americans and the Dutch, but Jones as well refused to place central authority above local powers. To break this impasse, the Congress charged the ITC “to draw up … a scheme of conditions of admission to training for the profession of therapeutic analysts and of the whole course of psycho-analytical training in general” (IJP, 1928, pp. 153–154). It was hoped that some universal ruling might also solve the problem of people, in particular non-physicians, being trained abroad who would be denied membership in their home groups. This had been, and continued to be, a main bone of contention between New York and Vienna/Budapest (see, e. g., Freud & Eitingon, 2004, pp. 514–515; Gay, 1989, pp. 498–499). A sub-committee appointed by Eitingon elaborated the “scheme” that had been requested at Innsbruck by drafting a body of detailed statutes, which was sent to all branch societies of the IPA to solicit their comments (Schröter, 2002a). After encountering a harsh “No” from New York, the whole draft fell flat. What provoked American resistance was the fact that the committee had been composed exclusively of Berlin members and that they had not first collected the opinions of their colleagues but had directly confronted them with their local rules, including the admission of lay candidates. This episode represented the most ambitious effort to make Berlin training regulations valid throughout the IPA. The effort having failed, the Eitingon committee declared its resignation at the Oxford Congress in 1929.


III It was expected that at Oxford the advocates of local or national autonomy would have a majority and that Jones would utilize this situation to bring about a decision in their favour. Eitingon prevented this by threatening a split of the IPA (Freud & Eitingon, 2004, pp. 634–636). Surprisingly, at a pre-congress conference a compromise was reached: if New York gave up their general rejection of non-medical analysts, the central Europeans promised not to train any foreign candidates without permission of their native group.4 At first the Oxford Agreement was implemented, with New York allowing some access to lay people. But peace ended in 1932/33, when membership in New York was again restricted to physicians.5 The Congress instituted a new ITC sub-committee to continue the attempts at designing IPA rules of training (IJP, 1929, p. 525). This time it was an international committee, with Jones as chair. The report composed by the Jones committee was passed in 1932 at the Wiesbaden Congress. In contrast to the earlier Eitingon draft, it was not a set of minute regulations but, rather, a loose framework, to be detailed in national or local legislation (IJP, 1933, pp. 157–159, 176). Some basics, however, were fixed: the responsibility of training committees, the tripartite structure of training, and its minimal duration of three years. Controversial issues, in particular the lay problem, were explicitly left open. At any rate, this was the first time a uniform regulation of psychoanalytic training was accepted by the IPA. While it marked a defeat of the central European faction with regard to lay analysis, in most respects the achievements of the “Eitingon model” had now become a common international standard. In the interval between Oxford and Wiesbaden two events took place that strongly influenced the further fate of the ITC. First, in 1931, the New York Society founded a training institute and invited Sándor Radó of Berlin, secretary of the ITC, to assume its direction (IJP, 1932, p. 274); and, second, the American Psychoanalytic Association (APsaA) was changed into a “federation” of local American societies (IJP, 1933, p. 161). Although the final constitution of the APsaA had to wait for some more years, this was a decisive organizational step that promoted American autonomy. It is little known that it was Eitingon himself, whose term as IPA president ended in Wiesbaden, who had suggested this change, because he did not want the Central Executive to be burdened with internal American struggles (Freud & Eitingon, 2004, pp. 800, 806–807; letter from Eitingon to Brill, 26 September 1929). Accordingly, he expressed some irritation when he was later suspected of opposing the independence of American psychoanalysis (e. g. letter from Eitingon to Lewin, 6 December 1937).


IV In 1934, at the Lucerne Congress, the ITC drew up its own standing rules (IJP, 1935, pp. 245–246). Here the Commission claimed formal authority to both accord recognition to, and withdraw recognition from, a training institute. In addition, it could entitle branch societies or individual members of the IPA to form a “training centre”—which was in part a reaction to the problems consequent on the massive emigration of analysts from Germany. This set of rules marked the climax of the ITC’s institutionalization and quest for power. It also marked the turning point where its decline began. The main driving force behind developing the Commission and its Council into a centre of power rivalling the IPA executive was Radó. In the eyes of the other ITC leaders his ambitions even led him to falsify his report of the Lucerne proceedings; so Anna Freud, who just had entered the Council, stood up against him and revised the report for publication. Radó resigned (Edward Bibring from Vienna becoming his successor).6 This conflict shows that Eitingon had, after his move to distant Jerusalem, lost control of the ITC, and Anna Freud had assumed a dominant role. While the Commission managed indeed to deal with problems of emigration, it soon became apparent that there would be opposition in America. A “campaign” was said to have started there early in 1935, aimed at rebutting any outside interference into national affairs (letter from Radó, 23 February 1935). That the ITC stood on shaky ground was also evident when Eitingon wished to institute a regular meeting of its members in inter-congress years and had to cancel the very first such meeting, scheduled for Paris in the summer of 1935, because of predictably sparse attendance. Even Jones called it, mockingly, a “one-man-idea”.7 At that time Anna Freud still maintained that the ITC should have the authority not only to confirm, but to license new training institutes (letter to Eitingon, 1 June 1935), and Eitingon felt that the Commission was entitled to formally approve training analysts within the IPA, while Jones wished to accord this function to local societies (letter from Jones to Eitingon, 16 October 1935). However, a few months later Anna Freud proposed a fundamental re-organization (Circular, 3 October 1935). The ITC should abandon any claim for a position of power. What power there was should be concentrated in the IPA executive. Contrary to former attempts at establishing the ITC as an agency of legislation and control, its function was now defined as that of a discussion platform—a “frame in which the contact between institutes, close communication about training matters, exchange of experiences etc. should take place” (ITC Circular, end of 1935).


V In 1936, at the Marienbad IPA Congress, this re-organization was put into effect. Eitingon furnished some examples of how the Commission’s work might continue (IJP, 1937, pp. 354–355). He suggested regular meetings between training analysts of neighbouring countries—an idea modelled on the recent Four-Countries Conference of colleagues from Vienna, Prague, Budapest, and Italy. And he found it worth while to consider whether a second analysis of qualified analysts ought to be formally included in the curriculum.8 In this way he supported a change of focus from regulation to substantial matters of training. Whatever may have been the chances of the Marienbad reform, it was never put to the test, because the Vienna group, the ITC’s current centre of force, was soon dissolved. The reform would, in any case, have been difficult. In America isolationist tendencies finally prevailed. The New York group again took the lead. In March 1936 it accepted a resolution suggesting that the ITC be dissolved and replaced by an informal training conference, without any officers or authority of their own. The idea came once again from Radó, who, in stark contrast to his earlier centralizing attitude, now advocated American autonomy. By some combination of intrigue and negligence this resolution was presented to the Marienbad meeting as Radó’s personal motion and was, as such, rejected, leading to a noisy clash between the ITC and New York.9 In retrospect this farce appears to foreshadow a more serious conflict. In December 1937 American Psychoanalytic Association had installed a committee to examine its relations with the IPA. On the basis of the committee’s report, a declaration was passed in June 1938, stating that the American societies felt strongly that “in all matters of organization, administration, training, and practice of psychoanalysis” they “are in fact, and should be, autonomous and independent of international ties”. In particular, they believed that “an international training body, entrusted with administrative and executive powers” was not necessary, and therefore they wished to terminate “their representation both on the International Training Committee and in the Central Executive”.10 The upcoming congress in Paris was stunned by this declaration of independence (see IJP, 1939, pp. 121–122), although it certainly had not come out of the blue. Under different circumstances the IPA leadership may have realized long-standing ideas of severing their organizational ties with the present American groups and of encouraging the foundation of new groups that were loyal to IPA principles. But in a situation where so many refugees from Vienna were dependent on American hospitality and help, this was not an option.11 So the Central Executive more or less agreed to dispense with any administrative power in America (letter from Glover to Kubie, 21 December 1938). A meeting between representatives of both associations, scheduled for September 1939 (letter from Jones το Eitingon, 27 July 1939), was abandoned due to the outbreak of war. When it finally took place after the war, the ITC was not reactivated.


VI Judging from this outcome, it may seem that Eitingon and the ITC had failed. As a matter of fact, this impression is only partially true. There had been enormous progress between 1925 and 1939. By the mid-1930s, the professionalized training system as it had been conceived in Berlin and then advocated by the ITC was widely established. It was an acknowledged fact that all new institutes, from Vienna to Chicago, were fashioned after the Berlin model. What proved to be illusory, however, was Eitingon’s hope that this training system could spread and be implemented by a central authority and by detailed rules this body was to issue. In terms of an institution, the ITC had, indeed, failed. Almost from the beginning its history was marked by conflicts with the American groups, mainly New York. The Commission was able to grant permission to Otto Fenichel to teach in Oslo or Prague after 1933, but it did not attain any official influence in America, which adamantly insisted on autonomy. Interestingly, it was only a short while until the American Association created its own Board on Professional Standards, exercising the same central (i.e., supra-local) powers on a national scale that it had denied the ITC internationally (cf. Mosher & Richards 2005). In this way even the structural model of the ITC survived. But while the Commission as such seems to have been of limited effect, there were other, non-organizational ways by which the “Eitingon model” of training spread. First of all, it set a standard for other groups. Even the New York opposition to ITC ruling was bound to lose credit unless the group started its own training institute. And when such institutes were founded, some of them were directed by people invited from Berlin for that task: Radó in New York, Franz Alexander in Chicago. In other places Berlin analysts played a prominent role: Hanns Sachs in Boston, Rudolf Loewenstein and René Spitz in Paris.12 And there were foreigners who took part in the Berlin training and made its principles their own, trying to achieve something similar after their return home: James and Edward Glover from London, Helene Deutsch from Vienna, Johan van Ophuijsen from Den Haag, Bertram Lewin from New York, Ives Hendrick from Boston. Arguably these personal links were the most powerful factors that secured the establishment of high common standards of training, based on the Berlin system, in most parts of the psychoanalytic world. Thus, we have the paradoxical situation that this system was a global success, even while the institution that had been designed for its dissemination and elaboration disintegrated. It is an open question whether the ITC, transformed into a forum of discussion following the Marienbad reform, could have survived if political circumstances had been less catastrophic. In the prevailing situation established national groups, whether in America, Great Britain, or France, had considerable license to take their own course in training matters. But the time came when the need for some common platforms for sharing experiences and for setting standards made itself felt again, and the IPA then had to create new instruments to fulfil the functions of the late International Training Commission.

Notes This paper originated from a contribution to the panel “Emigration from Berlin: Transfer of Theories and Institutional Regulations. Part 2” at the 45th IPA Congress in Berlin, 25–28 July 2007; see Schröter (2008) for an earlier, expanded version. If not stated otherwise, all unpublished sources quoted here are taken from the Eitingon Papers held at the Israel State Archives, Jerusalem. 1. This quotation is taken from “Report of central executive on questions concerning the desirability of granting a diploma, the conditions of membership etc.” (BPS = Archives of the British Psychoanalytical Society). 2. The guidelines were published in German in the Internationale Zeitschrift für Psychoanalyse, 10 (1924), pp. 232–333; they were not published in English. Danto (2005) does not emphasize enough the training function that had, from the beginning, characterized the Berlin policlinic. 3. According to the parlance of that time, I am using the term “America” for the United States. 4. Because it was attained behind the scenes, no traces of the “Oxford Agreement” appear in the Congress Minutes proper (Wallerstein, 1998, 35). There can be no doubt, however, of the existence of such an agreement, which is mentioned repeatedly in the relevant sources (cf., e.g., Schröter, 2004a, p. 22, n. 46; H. Sachs, quoted in letter from A. Freud to Jones, 30 January 1934 [BPS]). 5. Lay admission in New York: IJP, 1930, pp. 248, 356; 1931, p. 256. Restriction to physicians: IJP, 1935, pp. 244–245. For the date “1932/33” see the letters from Federn to Eitingon, 15 November 1932, and from Eitingon to Jones, 26 May 1933. 6. This episode is documented in a complex correspondence between Eitingon, A. Freud, Radó, and Jones. Its centrepieces are a lengthy letter of A. Freud to Radó, 9 December 1934, and his even longer answer, directed to A. Freud and Eitingon, 23 February 1935. 7. Letter from Jones to A. Freud, 11 July 1935 (BPS); cf. letter Eitingon–A. Freud, 17 July 1935. 8. For the discussions conducted at the Four-Countries Conferences about “control analysis” (today’s “supervision”) see Rath, 2008. “After-analysis” (today’s “re-analysis”) was the topic of a paper Anna Freud presented at the ITC meeting in Paris 1938 (IJP, 1939, p. 213). 9. For some detailed information about this affair see Schröter 2008, pp. 213–217. 10. Report of the Special Committee on the Relations of the American Psychoanalytic Association to the IPA, signed by L. S. Kubie, with APsaA Resolution of 3 June 1938. 11. Cf. letter from Bibring to Eitingon, 21 July 1938: “… every negative reaction [sc. to the American efforts at detaching themselves from the ITC] on our side would mean today that these people will not only leave the IPA, but also will abandon their whole relief action, endangering so many of our colleagues and candidates.” 12. Not to mention the activities of post-1933 emigrants from Berlin, as, for instance, those of Fenichel in Oslo, Prague, and Los Angeles. Bibliography Danto, E. A. (2005). 25Mexico The Mexican Psychoanalytic Association

Pablo Cuevas Corona

Psychoanalysis in Mexico is officially circa 60 years old, but even before the 1930s, Freud’s ideas were known and discussed and could be applied clinically by some medical doctors interested in the new developments in Austria and Germany, even though the medical tradition in Mexico was very closely related to France and very little with the other European countries. During the early 1940s Mexico received Spanish Civil War refugees, medical doctors among them, some of whom had been close to psychoanalytic ideas; this led to the interest of many psychiatrists in the new psychological discipline. During this time, two groups of psychiatrists in Mexico City began to look for these advancements from Europe. Since travelling to Europe was not easy at the end of the Second World War, one of these groups decided to start working with Erich Fromm, who had left the United States in the early 1950s and had settled in Mexico, where he was invited to accept a position at the National University. These physicians started their analysis with him and founded the Institute for Psychoanalysis, which has continued since then to work academically in the city and train some candidates. The other group decided to travel on their own to places where psychoanalysis was already organized in institutes and societies, mainly Argentina, the United States, and France; they were looking for a more Freudian analysis and analytic training, since they did not agree with Fromm’s “revisionist” theories. After finishing their training, they returned to Mexico and founded the Asociacion Psicoanalítica Mexicana (APM: Mexican Psychoanalytic Association), which was fully recognized by the IPA during the Paris Congress in 1957. This was an epoch when the country was retrieving and recreating its cultural assets and then accepting psychoanalysis as part of a new way to assume and integrate thinking within a liberal identity and democratic culture. From this time on, the APM has led the psychoanalytic movement in Mexico; now with more than 200 members, it is a fully active institute that has taught 34 classes of candidates, 8 classes of child analysts and 13 classes of supervisors and analytic professors. Teaching analysis in the APM has its own characteristics, with various theoretical styles inherited and then transformed over the course of time—one could say, it is a kind of combination of ideas from many points of reference, resulting in an almost eclectic way of transmitting psychoanalytic ideas from different theoretical standpoints, all integrating a rather pluralistic perspective for teaching and supervising at the Institute. For example, texts and papers may be read in Spanish—including Latin American and Spanish Literature—Portuguese, English, or French. Comprehensive and critical reading no doubt affects the way people assimilate concepts, hypothesis, and theories to the point that it seems difficult to integrate such dissimilar theoretical differences; but when it comes to the practical issues of analysing clinical material, one is given the conviction that theories converge. This replicates the IPA’s three models of teaching analysis—Eitingon’s, French, and Uruguayan—but teaching programmes at the APM are designed on the classical model. 1965 was the year when the first Cuadernos de Psicoanalisis [Notebooks of Psychoanalysis] were published as the official journal of the APM, a semi-annual publication that gathers original papers in Spanish from members, candidates, and guests. Thirty years ago a programme was launched by Association to train and supervise analysts, including a three-semester plan where full members with teaching preferences and vocation would examine the pertinent bibliography on how to examine and discuss training analysis, supervision, and teaching techniques with senior analysts. This programme has proved to enhance and upgrade the Institute’s training activities, taking into account the various current points of view in the world about training analysis. Child analysis is another programme the Institute offers to candidates who have graduated: a two-year curriculum with emphasis on the clinical supervision of child and adolescent cases, as well as theoretical and technical seminars. Stemming from regular outreach activities such as clinical conferences and workshops for non-analysts, the APM has begun to offer two psychoanalytic psychotherapy Master’s programmes on general psychotherapy and child and adolescent psychotherapy, and a doctorate programme on psychotherapy with emphasis in clinical research, fully certified by the Board of Education authority in Mexico. These alternatives have raised the number of applications to the institute since students develop a great interest in psychoanalysis in addition to their clinical abilities and research skills, which also improve the academic atmosphere in the institute. Following the new trends in education, five years ago the APM started a programme on distance learning—a system designed to take into consideration all the updates on pedagogy and instructional techniques as well as the newest technological implements to provide theoretical information (seminars) and clinical supervision to candidates who cannot attend the regular programmes. (Remote analytic techniques are of great current interest and discussion in the IPA). Evaluation of this kind of instruction is being processed, and results will be published in the near future. The Association has been involved in the creation of some new groups, such as the one in Monterrey (ARPAC), a component society of the IPA since 1993, whose first members were trained by one of the APM founders. Shortly after this, there being few analysts in Monterrey, many candidates decided to travel to Mexico City to attend training analysis and seminars—this being one of the first experiences of shuttle analysis, with sessions on Friday and Saturday mornings and evenings of each day. This increased the number of members in the nucleus, which became a study group that has developed and become an important component in the mental health milieu in that city and state in the Northeast of the country. Something similar has happened with a study group in Guadalajara, which soon will be a provisional society. Their original members were trained as psychoanalysts and training and supervising analysts in the APM. This group has an important relation with societies from Argentina, and they organize a yearly congress in the Western region of Mexico. There is a very well-organized group functioning in Guanajuato (which is now applying to the IPA to become a study group); two other groups working in Veracruz and Chiapas will soon have grown enough to let them apply to the IPA. All of them count on the support of the APM, considering that all their members graduated from its Institute. Continuing with the history, reference should be made to the formation of the two other recently recognized IPA societies in Mexico City, whose founding members were all related to the APM in one way or another. One is the Psychoanalytic Society of Mexico (“Park Mexico”), created after a split in 1973; the other, AMPIEP, was founded by the first psychoanalysts in Mexico, together with a group of psychologists looking for the development of dynamic psychotherapy in the city. Today we enthusiastically share with them two important combined projects, one in clinical research and the other as the Local Arrangements Committee for the IPA Congress in Mexico City, celebrating the first century of the IPA. During all these years of psychoanalytic endeavour, members of the APM have been involved in the application of psychoanalysis to various areas. Interest has been active on social and community issues developing a concept—Pyscho–Community—where social and family groups can benefit from psychoanalytic knowledge about development and interpersonal relations. Political issues, such as the democratic changes now present in the country’s social movements, have been analysed. Literature, art, theatre, and cinema have been the object of applied psychoanalysis articles and debates in universities and museums, with many analysts participating. A new concept on the relation of man and his environment was proposed as ecocide, meaning the way in which impulses may destroy—as they do—the natural world. On the other hand, psychoanalysts participated in emotional and psychological crisis interventions for people after the 1985 earthquake (an article was published about this in the IJP that year; see Palacios et al., 1986). Mexico is a territory whose geographical and chronological characteristics combine to locate the country “in-between” two main influences that have a deep effect on it. Geographically, Mexico is a neighbour of the United States of America and Canada to the north, and of Central and South America to the south. While physical location relates to trade and commerce (such as NAFTA—the North American Free Trade Agreement), a common language and history are the links of Mexico with Latin American countries and people sharing the common experience of Spanish colonization five centuries ago. This locates Mexico—and the APM—in a place “in-between” of two frontiers, two cultures, two economic models and levels of development, two influences in terms of psychoanalytic theories. The APM is, then, the natural mediator and transitional space between these influences. This “in-between” transitional position is also present in our relation with history, bringing together traditions between present and past and linking the ancient Aztec and Mayan civilizations to the development of a mixed culture, with influences from Spain, France, and the United States. This transitional position addresses the identity of the common Mexican citizen and the country’s institutions, as well as our psychoanalytic discipline. Psychoanalytic papers have been written (in Spanish) about how the mixing of two races resulted in a mythical origin of being “Mexican”, about the inner identity of Mexicans, and about interpreting other books and papers written by non-psychoanalysts on the same theme. Many of the expressions in art, architecture, painting, even graphic design, are clearly influenced by Aztec and Mayan forms and concepts. Such is the case of the “face/and/heart” notion in Aztec philosophy—a dual concept of man being expressed both through his face and through his heart; on one side, the visage, the image a person transmits through the face or appearance; on the other, his heart, the mood and affect articulating actions, words, preferences, work, everything a person is capable of transmitting by means of character and behaviour. Another inspiration of this kind is the Aztec goddess Tlazolteotl—a debris-eater personage who received and listened to everyone who felt like telling of his or her disturbing fears and transgressions and receiving rest and serenity in return. Because of its meaning, this icon was chosen to be the logo of the APM. The best way to end this very succinct description of the APM is by delivering, with great pride, a greeting, as an affiliate society, to the IPA on its first centennial anniversary. Bibliography Palacios, A., Cueli, J., Camacho, J., Clériga, R., Cuevas, P., Ayala, J., & Cossoff, L. (1986). The traumatic effect of mass communication in the Mexico City earthquake: Crisis intervention and preventive measures. International Review of Psycho-Analysis, 13: 279–292.







Acknowledgements This centenary history of the IPA, a four-year collective effort, provides an up-to-date history of the IPA’s last century, written by 56 contributors, in 41 countries and societies. It includes an account of the dispersal of IPA training from its beginnings in Central Europe and North America a century ago to its current expansion to all quarters of the globe. Essays by the seven living past IPA presidents appraise the accomplishments and shortcomings of their administrations and enrich the historical value of the volume. We wish to thank the dedicated IPA colleagues whose contributions appear in this volume. We acknowledge with gratitude the consistent and essential support of the two most recent IPA Presidents, Cláudio Eizirik, 2005–09, and Charles Hanly, 2009 to the present. The IPA Treasurer Henk Jan Dalewijk, the IPA Budget and Finance Committee, and the IPA Board have been steadfast in their commitment to this project. The staff of the IPA Central Office at Broomhills, London, under the benevolent oversight of IPA Interim Head of Staff Piers Pendred and IPA Publications Officer Rhoda Bawdekar-Sebastian, has coordinated this international effort with unwavering grace and professionalism. For expert translations of seven essays from the Spanish, we are indebted to Ronald G. Nibbe of UCLA. The file of the original 1910 Nurnberg Program on the frontispiece was supplied by Keith Robinson, Los Angeles. The book copy-editing and production was expertly administered by Klara and Eric King of Communication Crafts, Sussex, UK. We thank our families and our spouses, Josefine Loewenberg and Seamus Thompson, for their patient indulgence, and our children, Christopher Thompson and Jonathan Loewenberg, who tolerated parents busy with this book. Peter Loewenberg wishes readers to know that while many have contributed and worked hard, none equalled co-editor Nellie L. Thompson, who in 2011 was honoured with the first IPA Award for Recognition of Special Service for her meticulous and respectful editing of each manuscript and the authors’ notes.

20 April 2011








19Canada History of psychoanalysis in Canada

Roger Dufresne & Jacques Vigneault


The precursors Although Ernest Jones established himself in Canada in 1908 as a neuropathologist at the Toronto Lunatic Asylum and remained there until he returned to Europe in 1913, his efforts to disseminate psychoanalysis in North America were aimed exclusively at the United States, where he contributed to the foundation of the American Psychoanalytic Association (APsaA). Thus, no permanent organization was established in Canada as a result of his presence, and it would not be for another forty years, after the Second World War, before psychoanalysis gained a foothold in the country, in Montreal, the metropolis of Canada at the time. The establishment of psychoanalysis in Canada is the result of a paradox that is as strange as it is revelatory of the unique character of the country: an anti-Franco Spanish refugee, Miguel Prados, formed an alliance with a French-Canadian priest, the Dominican Noël Mailloux. Beginning in the spring of 1945, four interns from the Allan Memorial Institute of McGill University, founded by Dr Ewen Cameron in 1944 and affiliated with the Royal Victoria Hospital, began meeting at the home of Dr Prados, who had obtained a position at the Neurological Institute in the early 1940s. There they discussed clinical cases and studied what they referred to as the “Freudian doctrine”. In early 1946 they decided to form the Montreal Psychoanalytic Club. At this time Prados had only undertaken a self-analysis and was not affiliated with any psychoanalytic association. In 1948 Father Mailloux, who had founded the Institute of Psychology at the University of Montreal at the same time as Cameron was founding the Allan Memorial Institute, joined the group, which grew considerably from this time on. The number of members grew to 40, with many guests invited to meetings. Charles Fisher, George Gero, Phyllis Greenacre, Edith Jacobson, Moe Kaufman, Bertrand D. Lewin, Rudolph Loewenstein, Sándor Lorand, and René Spitz were invited from New York; Leo Bartemeier and Richard and Edith Sterba came from Detroit; Edward and Greta Bibring and Erich Lindeman travelled from Boston. Even though the Club certainly helped to spread psychoanalysis, it did not promote the training of Canadian psychoanalysts. Forced to seek training at institutes in the United States and Europe, these candidates had little inclination to return to Canada. In 1948, with the help of the Lady Davis Foundation and Father Mailloux, Professor Théo Chentrier, a member of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society [Société Psychanalytique de Paris (SPP)], became the first psychoanalyst to immigrate to Canada. He was appointed professor at the University of Montreal and joined the Montreal Psychoanalytic Club, of which he later became an enthusiastic and loyal director. Between 1948 and 1950 the Club was very active and held semi-monthly meetings, along with weekly seminars on clinical practice and theory. In 1950, Dr Eric Wittkower of the British Psycho-Analytical Society came to the Allan Memorial Institute. Then in 1951 Georges Zavitzianos, a member of the Société Psychanalytique de Paris, immigrated to Montreal. In the autumn of that same year, Dr Alastair MacLeod, of the British Society, was hired by the psychiatry department of McGill University. Finally, in September 1952, Dr Bruce Ruddick, who had just completed his training at the New York Institute, returned to Montreal.


Beginning of the Canadian Psychoanalytic Society With the arrival of these five psychoanalysts, all members of organizations recognized by the International Psychoanalytical Association, the members of the Club felt that it was time to seek official status from the IPA. Since recognition could only be granted to members who belonged to a Component Society, Théo Chentrier, Alastair MacLeod, Miguel Prados, Bruce Ruddick, Eric Wittkower, and Georges Zavitzianos formed a Study Group and applied to the IPA, hoping to be recognized at the 1951 Amsterdam Congress. As the bylaws required a recommendation from a Component Society, they turned to the Detroit Psychoanalytic Society, which was familiar with the Club, to obtain recognition as an independent organization and affiliate of the American Psychoanalytic Association (APsaA). Since the APsaA had recently withdrawn accreditation of the Detroit training programme, it was suggested to the Study Group that they contact the Boston Psychoanalytic Society for a recommendation; but this manoeuvre was not successful: at the end of September 1951 the group learned that during the IPA Congress its request had been referred to the Board of Professional Standards of the American Psychoanalytic Association. Although the Board of Professional Standards supported the request, the APsaA official response was that the time was not right: a member of the group was an analyst but not a physician, and the Canadians, mostly trained in Europe, were planning to admit lay analysts. In addition they were told, under the seal of secrecy, that North America ought to be saved from the Kleinians who might attempt to infiltrate through the Canadians. Faced with this situation, the Study Group withdrew its request to the American Association and turned to the British Psychoanalytical Society, which granted them membership after a few weeks of deliberation. As a result, in March 1952 the Canadian Society of Psychoanalysts became an affiliate of the British Society, with Chen-trier as president and MacLeod as secretary. The response from the APsaA was immediate. They let it be known that the Marienbad Agreement of the 1936 IPA Congress gave them exclusive control over all of North America. The British Society replied that since Canada was part of the British empire, it was only fair that it serve as sponsor for the Canadian group. The Americans rejected out of hand a compromise that would have involved joint sponsorship from both associations. In July 1952, after lengthy negotiations, the British Psychoanalytical Society indicated that it would not oppose an agreement with the APsaA if this solution would help to establish in Canada a psychoanalytic Society recognized by the IPA. In order to facilitate negotiations with the Americans, Chentrier, who was not a physician, gave up the presidency of the Canadian Society of Psychoanalysts in August 1952. MacLeod became president, and Ruddick secretary. But more importantly, in October 1952 Prados proposed dissolving the Montreal Psychoanalytic Club because he was convinced that the Americans were confusing it with the Canadian Society of Psychoanalysts, which consisted exclusively of IPA member analysts. These concessions turned out to be pointless, however, because the Americans never granted affiliate status to the Canadian group. On 17 October 1953, the Canadian Society of Psychoanalysts was dissolved and officially reformed as the Canadian Psychoanalytic Society. In December 1953, the group withdrew its request for membership in the APsaA and reaffirmed its membership in the British Psychoanalytical Society, which had been retained throughout this process. Because Canada is bicultural, with equal weight given to French and English, it was decided that the Society would officially be bilingual. During the summer of 1953, Dr Jean-Baptiste Boulanger, his wife Françoise, and Dr J. P. Labrecque, all of whom were trained by the Société Psychanalytique de Paris, became members of the Study Group. The following year, Dr Clifford Scott, a Canadian psychoanalyst who had become president of the British Psychoanalytical Society, Drs. Hans and Friedl Aufreiter of the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society, and André Lussier, who was completing his training at the London Institute, joined the new organization. With the sponsorship of the British Psychoanalytical Society, the Canadian Psychoanalytic Society became officially recognized as a member of the IPA on 31 July 1957, during the 20th IPA Congress in Paris.


First steps of the Canadian Institute The British Psychoanalytical Society also sponsored the training programme of the Canadian Psychoanalytic Society, which was located in a university setting, at the Allan Memorial Institute of McGill University (directed by Dr Ewen Cameron). With the help of Dr Clifford Scott, agreements were concluded in mid-1954 to initiate a training programme identical to that of the British Institute, with three training analysts. After thriving in Britain for a quarter-century, Scott, at Cameron’s request, returned to Montreal to run the programme. The way was now open for training future analysts. The first students had already begun their training in Montreal, London, Paris, or the United States. Scott wanted to help these candidates complete their training, but was opposed by Cameron, who had integrated psychoanalysis in his programme at the university so it would be under his direct control; all the analysts supported Scott. When Cameron refused to hire another training analyst, Scott and his colleagues realized they could only avoid his efforts to control training by forming their own autonomous institute. The training committee of the Canadian Psychoanalytic Society developed and introduced a teaching programme in 1958. The first seminar was held on 4 April 1959. On 1 October 1960, members of the Society ratified a proposal recommending the creation and incorporation of the Canadian Institute of Psychoanalysis, which was done on 17 March 1961, in Quebec. Jean-Baptiste Boulanger was the first director. The first training programme, in 1959, had 12 teachers for 13 students. Of the 37 candidates trained between 1959 and 1967, 11 were French speakers.


Founding of the branches of the Canadian Psychoanalytic Society In 1969, for cultural and linguistic as well as geographic reasons, a federal model was used to create different branches within the Canadian Psychoanalytic Society. A French-speaking branch—the Société Psychanalytique de Montréal (Montreal Psychoanalytic Society)—was created in Montreal; two English-speaking branches were formed: the CPS/Quebec English Branch in Montreal, and the Toronto Psychoanalytic Society in Ontario. The CPS/Quebec English Branch is composed of analysts from Montreal representing various ethnic and cultural backgrounds, but whose primary identification was with English language and culture when the branches were created. It was the most British of the Canadian branches because the majority of the founding members of the Canadian Society belonged to it. It consists of 100 members, not counting those who left to create new branches in other regions of Canada. Although the Society’s two official languages were English and French, exchanges and teaching activities took place almost exclusively in English until 1969, when the Société Psychanalytique de Montréal was established. Only after the Canadian Psychoanalytic Society created a French branch, along with other English branches, did French receive greater acceptance, and only then did simultaneous translation, though costly, become the norm in the national congresses. Mirroring the relationship between Quebec and the remainder of Canada, the Société Psychanalytique de Montréal and the English-language branches were “two solitudes” that continually question the reasons for their cohabitation. In 1969, the Société Psychanalytique de Montréal had 27 members, all French-speaking, one trained in London, two in Boston, 11 in Canada, and 13 in Paris. Confrontation was unavoidable between those trained according to the British model and more identified with psychiatry, like the Americans at the time, and those returning from Paris who wanted to introduce the French model of training and favoured lay analysts. The French model finally prevailed. Today it consists of 114 members, including many lay analysts. Psychoanalysis has also made progress in Toronto. In September 1954, Alan Parkin, who had just completed his training in London, England, arrived in the city. He was joined in 1957 by Irvine Schiffer who had trained in Boston. In 1956, Parkin created the Toronto Psychoanalytic Study Circle with a core of 11 psychiatrists with an interest in psychoanalysis. He attempted to establish a psychoanalysis training programme at the department of psychiatry of the University of Toronto and to obtain recognition for his Study Group from the Ontario Psychiatric Institute. After two years of activity the circle decided to transform itself into the psychotherapy section of the Ontario Psychiatric Society by opening its doors to all members of that association. The request was ratified on 23 January 1959, and two years later, on 20 January 1961, the psychotherapy section held its first scientific congress. In his History of Psychoanalysis in Canada, Parkin (1987) comments on the prodigious growth of this section, which in January 1970 had no fewer than 93 members. The establishment by Parkin of psychoanalysis within the context of psychiatry helped determine the medical orientation psychoanalysis assumed in Ontario. Later, the Toronto Society faced important conflicts between Freudian and Kohutian factions. A split was avoided when there emerged a third group, the post-Kleinians, whose members were partisans of object relations theory and followers of Margaret Mahler and Otto Kernberg. Their emergence prevented the complete polarization of the Society. Today the Toronto Psychoanalytic Society has 125 members. After the formation of the first three branches of the Canadian Society and the institute, other branches were created when at least five analysts belonging to the same geographic or cultural community submitted a request. In 1972, the Ottawa Psychoanalytic Society was created. At present, there are 26 members. In 1978, the CPS/Western Canadian Branch was founded, with members scattered throughout the four western provinces. Donald Watterson, in Vancouver, was the first psychoanalyst to settle in British Columbia, yet there was little growth in psychoanalysis in the province until the end of the twentieth century. Julius Guild settled in Edmonton, Alberta, followed by Perry Segal in 1971 and Hassan Azim in 1973, but there is only one analyst in Calgary and one in Edmonton. A similar number is found in Winnipeg, Manitoba. Even though the CPS Western Branch covers an area that is roughly a third of Canada, it currently (2010) has only 19 members: 16 in British Columbia, 2 in Alberta, and 1 in Manitoba. The Southwestern Ontario Psychoanalytic Society in London, Ontario, is the sixth section of the Canadian Psychoanalytic Society. It was founded on 5 June 1982 and currently has 12 members. Formed in 1988, with ten members, the Société Psychanalytique de Québec [Quebec Psychoanalytic Society] is the most recent of the seven branches of the Canadian Psychoanalytic Society. To date, there are 11 members. The senior member was Henri Richard, who began practising after psychoanalytic training in Paris from 1952 to 1959. A few years later he was joined by Noël Montgrain, who had also studied at the Institut de Psychanalyse de Paris [Paris Institute of Psychoanalysis]. There is currently no psychoanalytic presence in the maritime provinces of eastern Canada. Aside from the cultural and economic centres of Montreal and Toronto, psychoanalysis throughout Canada has grown very slowly.


Founding of the branches of the Canadian Institute of Psychoanalysis As for the Canadian Institute of Psychoanalysis, its expansion was even slower. After the creation of the three originals branches, the Canadian Institute of Psychoanalysis/Quebec English in Montreal, the Institut Psychanalytique de Montréal, and the Toronto Psychoanalytic Institute, it was only when Ottawa could count on five training analysts that the Ottawa Branch of the Institute was created in 1978. Its status was, however, suspended when the minimum number of training analysts was no longer met. Service agreements have been concluded with the CIP/Quebec English Branch so that candidates of the Ottawa Psychoanalytic Society can continue to be trained locally. In the West, the Canadian Institute had to authorize two training analysts from the Seattle Psychoanalytic Institute in the United States to administratively join the Toronto Psychoanalytic Institute to train candidates in British Columbia. With five Canadian training analysts, it recently became the Vancouver Institute of Psychoanalysis. However, candidates of the South Western Ontario Branch still undergo training at the Toronto Institute. Likewise, candidates of the Société de Québec in Quebec City must complete their training in Montreal, since the Quebec group has only two training analysts.


Diversity of views on the transmission of psychoanalysis For several years different training models have been at the core of intense confrontations in the Canadian Society and the Canadian Institute. On the one hand, there were the upholders of the Eitingon model, also called the traditional or the Anglo-American model. On the other, there were those trained in Paris and many others who elected to replace the mandatory academic short seminars with a wide range of continuous seminars that last many years and from which the candidates are free to choose according to their needs. At the same time a three-year programme of weekly seminars on Freud’s works and a one-year course on the post-Freudians authors were maintained. Still more importantly, they considered it contrary to the spirit of psychoanalysis to require a candidate to change his analyst if he or she was not a training analyst. Applicants must first have undergone several years of personal analysis and are evaluated on their capacity to reflect on their experience of an analytic process. These modalities belong to the French model, which can also be called traditional as it began over five decades ago, in the late 1950s in Paris. In order to avoid a split between the French-speaking and the English-speaking analysts, in June 1975 the Canadian Society adopted a motion allowing the Société Psychanalytique de Montréal and its Institute to authorize experienced non-training analysts to undertake the analysis of any person, including potential candidates, while the English branches retained the Eitingon model. This very “British” solution was inspired by the British Society, with its groupings of Freudian, Kleinian, and independents groups. One of us, Roger Dufresne, has previously written that the great challenge of the Canadian Society was and remains to maintain as one entity the equivalents of the British Society, the American Psychoanalytic Association, the Société de Paris, and the Association de France, without the English Channel or the Atlantic Ocean to keep them separate.


Publications For years, oral communication was the primary mode of transmission within the Canadian Psychoanalytic Society. The first generations of analysts were more absorbed with issues of foundations and transmission than in the writing of psychoanalytic works. Although a number of practitioners—such as Jean Bossé, Jean-Baptiste Boulanger, Roger Dufresne, Jean-Louis Langlois, Paul Lefebvre, Brian Robertson, Eric Wittkower, and Georges Zavitzianos, among others—wrote important articles, they spent the majority of their time training future generations of analysts. The ephemeral character of psychoanalytic publications bears witness to the phenomenon. The Canadian Psychoanalytic Review, published in 1954 and sponsored by the Canadian Psychoanalytic Society, had only one issue and was the only Canadian journal until the reappearance, nearly forty years later, in the spring of 1993, of the semi-annual bilingual Canadian Journal of Psychoanalysis/Revue Canadienne de Psychanalyse, founded and edited by Eva Lester. Julien Bigras was, however, the first to promote written communication within the psychoanalytic community with the review Interprétation, of which he was the founder and editor-in-chief from 1967 to 1971. Josette Garon, Jean Imbeault, Jacques Mauger, Lise Monette, and François Peraldi continued his efforts with the publication of Frayages from 1984 to 1987. For more than thirty years (since 1988), the Société Psychanalytique de Montréal has been publishing an internal periodical three times a year, the Bulletin de la Société Psychanalytique de Montréal. In the autumn of 1992, Dominique Scarfone published the first issue of Trans, a semi-annual, semi-thematic, interdisciplinary journal. The journal played a key role in encouraging the exchange of psychoanalytic ideas and also played an important role in the Montreal psychoanalytic community by organizing annual colloquia open to the public. Despite this success, the editorial committee decided to discontinue its publication with the tenth issue in the spring of 1999. The year 1992 also saw the introduction of the semi-annual Filigrane, financed by the publication of Santé mentale au Québec and directed at psychotherapists and professional psychoanalysts whose clinical methods were compatible with psychoanalysis. Clifford Scott, Julien Bigras, André Lussier, Patrick J. Mahony, Charles Hanly, Jean Imbeault, and Dominique Scarfone are among the first analysts to make significant contributions at an international level to a critical analysis of the psychoanalytic corpus.


Psychoanalysis “extra muros” In Canada, as elsewhere, the Canadian Psychoanalytic Society and its various branches are not the only entities involved in psychoanalysis. Alongside them, unaffiliated psychoanalysts have always been working alone or in small groups. Among the first to practise psychoanalysis outside an institutional context was Michel Dansereau, a physician who had trained with René Laforgue in Casablanca, Morocco, and was active during the 1950s. Later there were Mireille Lafortune, who was active from the late 1960s, and François Peraldi, who arrived in Montreal in 1974, both without affiliation to a Canadian psychoanalytic society. There also exist many organizations devoted to psychoanalysis. In 1986 François Peraldi established the Réseau des Cartels [Network of Cartels], composed of analysts interested in the work of Jacques Lacan. This network did not survive the loss of its founder, and today only a single cartel is still active. The Association des Psychothérapeutes Psychanalytique du Québec [Quebec Association of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapists], founded in 1985, has over 130 members in various regions of Quebec; all its clinicians treat their patients with a psychoanalytic approach. The association organizes colloquia and conferences, to which members of the Société Psychanalytique de Montréal are invited. The Groupe d’Études Psychanalytiques Interdisciplinaires [Interdisciplinary Psychoanalytic Study Group] of Quebec University in Montreal brings together professors and lecturers who include analysis in their teaching. Coming from various UQAM departments, they organize colloquia, public conferences, and MA and PhD seminars with a focus on their own teaching and on the various models of psychoanalysis. The group does not promote any particular psychoanalytic school. In the Montreal English-speaking community, the Argyle Institute, as well as the Jewish General Hospital, with the help of psychoanalysts of the Canadian Society/Quebec English Branch, have for years offered treatment, teaching, and supervision in psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Several of their respective graduates have applied for training in the Canadian Institute/Quebec English Branch. In Toronto, psychoanalytic psychologists of the Ontario Psychological Association, with the support of Division 39 of the American Psychological Association, decided, at the end of the 1980s, to found their own organization, the Toronto Society for Contemporary Psychoanalysis, and their own institute, the Toronto Institute for Contemporary Psychoanalysis. Initially, candidates training at the new institute were not physicians; although it was not a part of the IPA, psychiatrists began to apply to the organization for training. They were attracted by the diversity of approaches in its training programme and wanted to avoid the incessant conflicts between Freudian and Kohutian factions that divided the Toronto Psychoanalytic Society. In Quebec City, Father Henri Samson, who trained in France and was a contemporary of Father Mailloux, founded the Institut de Psychothérapie de Québec in the 1960s for those who wanted training in analytic psychotherapy. The Groupe Interdisciplinaire Freudien de Recherches et d’Interventions Cliniques et Culturelles [Interdisciplinary Group for Freudian Research and Clinical and Cultural Interventions], founded by Willy Apollon and cooperating with psychiatrists from the Robert-Giffard Center, has gained a considerable reputation in analytic psychotherapy based on Lacanian principles, especially in its work with psychotic patients. The Cercle Jung de Québec [Quebec Jung Circle], founded in the 1970s by Marcel Gaumont, a Jungian analyst trained at the Jung Institute in Zurich, promotes Jungian psychoanalysis in Canada through public conferences and discussions. Finally, a group of analytic psychotherapists has also been at work in British Columbia in Western Canada.


The Canadian Psychoanalytic Society and the IPA Montreal was honoured to host the 35th IPA Congress in 1987, the first and only one in Canada and the second outside Europe. The members of the Canadian Psychoanalytic Society, with a membership of over 400, have been very active since the 1980s in serving on a range of IPA committees and participating in the IPA’s central administration. Because of their large number, we will mention only the few who have held or hold elective offices, as members of Council (recently replaced by the Board of Representatives): James Naiman, vice-president from 1981 to 1983; Roger Dufresne, vice-president from 1985 to 1989; André Lussier, vice-president from 1989 to 1993; Arthur Leonoff, representative from 2007 to 2011, and Charles Hanly, vice-president from 1993 to 1995 and from 1997 to 2001, global representative from 2003 to 2007, president-elect from 2007 to 2009, and president from August 2009 on, who, therefore, has presided over the IPA centenary.

Bibliography Dufresne, R. (2005). Le modèle français de transmission de la psychanalyse à Montréal. Bulletin de la Société psychanalytique de Montréal, 17 (3): 39–45. Dufresne, R. (2008).
