32Venezuela Origin and history of the Caracas Psychoanalytic Society

Rómulo Lander & Carlos Valedón

In February 1989, after facing many difficulties within the Venezuelan Psychoanalytic Association, as mutual intolerance and deep disagreements on various scientific, teaching, and administrative issues had proved irresolvable, in spite of much effort over the preceding years to reach a shared understanding, eight full members (six training analysts) and seven associate members decided to approach the authorities of the International Psychoanalytical Association and ask for recognition as a new psychoanalytic group in Caracas. This initial group was made up of the following full members: Addys Attias de Cavallín, Fernando Batoni, Indalecio Fernández, Rómulo Lander, Serapio Marcano, Ana Teresa Torres, José Melía Alamar, and Carlos Valedón; the associate members were: Margara Alexandre de Edelman, Esther Aznar, Elvira Larrovere de Campobello, Alicia Leisse de Lustgarten, Marán Himiob de Marcano, Ana Herrera, and Dolores Salas de Torres. The group decided to send to the President of the IPA at the time, Robert Wallerstein, and all members of the Executive Committee an extensive document outlining the reasons for requesting recognition. Afterward, following the suggestion of Robert Waller-stein, a large delegation from this group attended the IPA Congress in Rome [IPAC/89] and put forward in person our proposals in greater detail. Before the trip to Rome, María Cristina Ashworth and Julio García Maldonado, both associate members of the Venezuelan Psychoanalytic Association, expressed their wish to join the initial dissident group, and they were accepted. With the aim of easily facilitating the separation from the Venezuelan Psychoanalytic Association and bringing to an end the energy being wasted by infighting, the original group of 15 dissidents decided to resign from the Venezuelan Psychoanalytic Association. The resignation was to take effect on Wednesday 31 July 1989. As a dissident group, we did not realize that with our resignation as members from the Venezuelan Psychoanalytic Association we would automatically lose our affiliation with the IPA as well and could no longer count on the services of their officers in the pursuit of our aims. Upon our arrival in Rome, we were made aware of the administrative consequences of our letter of resignation. Following the advice of some of the members of the Executive Council of the IPA, we appealed to some members of the Venezuelan Psychoanalytic Association (Board of Directors) who were present at the Congress in Rome to ask them to let us withdraw our resignation, but this appeal was rejected. Their refusal to accept our written request for re-instatement meant that at midnight on 31 July 1989 we automatically lost all affiliation with the IPA. Faced with this situation, the Executive Council of the IPA decided to submit a proposal to the General Assembly (IPA Business Meeting, 2 August 1989) to accept our group as direct members of the IPA. In the plenary session this proposal was accepted by the members present, and the 15 dissident members from Caracas who had signed the letter of resignation became direct members of the IPA. Once the members of our group regained affiliation with the IPA, we continued with our objectives. In September of that same year we sent (as requested) a formal petition to the new President of the IPA, Joseph Sandler, and to the Executive Council, asking that a Visiting Committee be sent to Caracas. In addition to this initial group of 17 members, this petition was also signed by María Teresa Aróstegui de Blanco, Bernardina Ayala, and Lía Furman de Sheinfield, associate members of the Venezuelan Psychoanalytic Association, who had joined the new Caracas Psychoanalytic Group. The visiting Committee was composed of three members: Inga Villarreal (Chairperson), Sara Zac de Filc, and Otto Kernberg. Their visit took place in December 1989. Following this visit, at the July 1990 meeting of the Executive Committee we were recognized by the Executive Committee as an Official IPA Study Group with teaching functions. The Visiting Committee became our Sponsor Committee and returned to Caracas in August 1990. At this time three new members—Doris Berlín, Carlos Rasquín, and Marisol Sandoval—joined the group, having left the Venezuelan Psychoanalytic Association. With the aim of evaluating the psychoanalytic quality of our work in the clinical, theoretical, administrative, and teaching fields, two further visits were scheduled. The first took place in December 1990 and the second in May of 1991. During these visits each member presented clinical material from present psychoanalytic cases, with corresponding theoretical, technical, and clinical commentary. The results of these visits were positive, leading to our being promoted to Provisional Society at the IPA Congress in Buenos Aires (IPAC/91). In drafting our constitution and bylaws, we sought to implement a series of new ideas in the area of psychoanalytic training and membership rights and duties. For example, the status of active member was created (or inactive, without loss of Society membership). In order to maintain an active status, the member must comply with certain minimum annual requirements. A new proposal was also established: a training analyst should be reappointed every five years contingent upon passing the same examination process initially undertaken to become a training analyst. The Child and Adolescent Department was created, as well as the Psychoanalytic Public Care Department. It was established that candidates, upon being accepted, would receive the Constitution and Bylaws of the Caracas Psychoanalytic Society along with the Regulations of the Institute of Psychoanalysis. This practice ensures that they are aware of their rights and obligations. In the selection of candidates all members are required to use psychoanalytic (not psychiatric) criteria. In this process of candidate selection, in an effort to complement the usual Bylaws requirement, a system of multiple clinical supervisions was set up. In an effort to offer different viewpoints, both full and associate members participate in the multiple clinical supervision of aspiring candidates. This clinical supervision will presumably allow us to know a little more about the capacities and abilities of the aspiring candidates to perceive and work with unconscious elements in the cases they present for supervision. In our Society candidates can and should participate in, and they are invited to, all scientific events of the Society. The training analyst does not report to the Institute, so we respect the confidentiality of a non-reporting analysis. This training analysis takes place with the same confidentiality principle that is typical of all analysis. Three candidates—Fausta Cruz Brito, Ingrid Arria, and Ziva Rosenthal—have asked to be transferred from the Venezuelan Psychoanalytic Association, and they have now completed their training in our Institute. The Sponsoring Committee participated in the process of selecting a new group of six candidates who began their training in 1992. The Caracas Society continued working intensely in all areas. In 1994 a new group of six candidates was admitted to training. In 1995 the IPA Congress was held in San Francisco (IPAC/95). At that time we were promoted to Component Society status in the IPA. In 1996 we were joined by Stella García de Rada, an ex-associate member of the Venezuelan Psychoanalytic Association. In July 1997 the following candidates graduated from the Institute, becoming associate members of our Society: Alicia Elena Díaz, Keila Graterol, Rosa Lagos, Adriana Levy, Teresa Machado, and Evelyn Pedrique. In September of 1997 a new group of eight candidates were admitted to psychoanalytic training. In December of 1997 the Society was made up of 20 full members, 12 associate member, and 14 candidates in training. The Caracas Psychoanalytic Society carries out clinical and theoretical scientific activities (round tables, panels and symposia) every Saturday morning, and theoretical courses, open to all mental health professionals are conducted on Tuesdays and Thursdays in the evening. There are many Study Groups, some exclusively for members and others open to all those interested. Almost all the members participate in university activities, such as conferences and supervision of groups and individuals. In April of every year, the Annual Caracas Psychoanalytic Encounter is held. It is open to the general public and sometimes features international psychoanalytic speakers. In October of every year the annual Child and Adolescent Meeting is held open to general public. The Society offers a Psychoanalytic Public Care Service, which offers analysis at very low cost. The theoretical orientation of our members is varied, and the training programmes are of a pluralistic nature, studying Melanie Klein, Wilfred Bion, the tenets of self psychology and of ego psychology, as well as Jacques Lacan’s works, to complement the exhaustive and detailed study of all Sigmund Freud’s works. In our Society we maintain a climate of scientific mutual respect and tolerance to the difference, which promotes, we think, the cross-fertilization of ideas. Twice a year the Society publishes a psychoanalytic journal called Tropicos, which contains the psychoanalytic work of the members and the unpublished work of international writers. The magazine began circulation in January 1991, has a run of 500 copies, averages 160 pages, and is financially supported by the Society. The Society made an unsuccessful experiment producing Volume 5 in a diskette format of 350 pages. As this digital presentation was considered difficult to read by members and the general public, we dropped the project and returned to paper production. Our Society’s publishing house, Editorial Psicoanalítica, has published 11 titles, all written by our members. Our Society actively participates through attendance and presentations at the Latin American Psychoanalytic Congress (FEPAL) and the IPA Congresses (IPAC). Our members are fully devoted to the practice of psychoanalysis and contribute their time and energy to many administrative tasks of the Latin American Federation of Psychoanalysis (FEPAL) and the IPA. We believe that we are seriously committed to the spread, support, and development of psychoanalysis in Latin America. Throughout all the years up to 2009 our Society has maintained its intense teaching, administrative, clinical, and cultural activities. In 1998 a regular Film Psychoanalytic Forum, open to the general public, was begun. It runs on Saturday mornings once a month. One recent film is projected in a regular public cinema house. One analyst and one non-analyst present their views of the film, and then a free discussion is open to the public. This programme has been very successful, and it always runs with a full house. So far it has continued until today. In 1999 serious political turmoil begins to shatter all levels of our once peaceful Venezuelan community. Since then, social and political turbulence and violence have permeated our psychoanalytic community and practice. Both local IPA psychoanalytic societies and other psychoanalytic non-IPA groups and societies have raised their voices locally and internationally, denouncing government abuse of power, abuses of human rights, a serious erosion of freedom of speech, and the evident absence of all democratic foundations. The clinical practice (private) of psychoanalysis has been in jeopardy. Analysand free association and the rules of analyst neutrality and analyst abstinence have been difficult to keep under these circumstances of duress. Economic survival is also in danger. Several (eight) analysts from our own Society, as well as analysts from other societies, have been forced to migrate elsewhere; they feel neither safe nor productive under these dangerous circumstances. Despite the danger and economic obstacles, we continue with our work, offering psychoanalytic training and carrying on with our theoretical, clinical, and cultural activities. Sometimes these activities have to be postponed or cancelled, due to severe street violence and political marches. At other times we do our work as usual, as if nothing were happening. We have gained new insights. For example, even within our Society, we realize we are afraid to speak clearly of our future plans. We begin not to trust our friends and colleagues, and we develop extra-careful ways of speaking and behaving. Depending on the political news of the day and on new threats imposed on the community, the level of fear on any given day will be high or low. Persecution and political repression are on the rise. Anti-Semitism is common, as seen in the savage attack on the synagogue and Jewish schools. All these local events have led to ever greater difficulties in successfully inviting overseas analyst speakers. The increase in street assaults and express kidnappings1 has made it very difficult for guest speakers to come to Caracas. They postpone all invitations. In addition, this particular situation has made it impossible to organize and offer Caracas as a venue for a Latin American International Congress (FEPAL Congress); the Congress had to be moved to Guadalajara (Mexico) because of the impossibility of holding it in the dangerous conditions of Caracas. Despite all these difficult socio-political circumstances, our Society has been growing during all these years. At the present time (2010) we have a total of 45 active members (eight have emigrated but retain their Society affiliation) and we have 11 candidates. We offer openings for psychoanalytic training every two years. Our selection of candidates maintains high standards. We keep our training analyst re-confirmation tradition, so every five years we have to reconfirm our functions as training analysts. Our commitment to the cause of Freud and to the support and spread of psychoanalysis is maintained in very high spirits. Note 1. Express kidnappings refers to a special kind of kidnapping, whereby people are kidnapped in their own cars for a few hours in order to collect a ransom (usually US$3,000–6,000). This kind of kidnapping may happen at any time during the day or night. Bibliography Bylaws of the Psychoanalytic Society of Caracas. Document printed by the SPC Publishing House. Updated yearly. Available upon request. Present Situation of Psychoanalysis in Venezuela (2006). Document written by Caracas P. S. and circulated in Latin America (FEPAL). Available upon request: ralander@viptel.com Up-date of the Present situation of Psychoanalysis in Venezuela (2007): Document written by Caracas P. S. and circulated in Latin America (FEPAL). Available upon request: ralander@viptel.com Valedón, C. (1991): Why? About the origins of the Psychoanalytic Society of Caracas. Tropicos Psychoanalytic Journal, 2 (No. 1). SPC Publishing House.







Part IEurope







Part IIILatin America







20United States American exceptionalism: a challenge to an international movement

Judith M. Hughes

Psychoanalysis was created by Sigmund Freud, a Viennese Jew, who had an outpatient practice with a large number of upper-middle-class hysterical women—also mainly Jewish. These bare facts prompted critics to claim that psychoanalysis was, first, a Jewish science and, second, applicable only to late-Victorian ladies from a particular social milieu. In short, so the argument ran, psychoanalysis had grown up in a very specific and special cultural niche—and outside that niche it could hardly hope to survive, let alone prosper. Freud, of course, insisted on the universality of his findings and had every intention of exporting them and indeed establishing an international movement. (To that end, he engineered the founding of the International Psychoanalytical Association in 1910.) But his confidence, not only in his theory, but in the possibility of its being transplanted, was sorely tried when he thought of the United States. His anti-Americanism is notorious. As early as 1902, he commented to his great friend Wilhelm Fliess that the “old world is ruled by authority, … the new … by the dollar” (Freud to Fliess, 11 March 1902, in Masson, 1985, p. 457). Nothing that occurred on his one trip across the Atlantic in 1909 changed his mind. And whenever his adherents visited the United States, his anti-Americanism emerged with renewed virulence. When, for example, Sándor Ferenczi spent the academic year 1926–27 lecturing in New York, Freud feared that he would lose his disciple “to the land of the dollar barbarians” (Freud to Ferenczi, 19 September 1926, in Falzeder & Brabant, 2000, p. 278). The United States appeared to him as alluring and rich, a seductive rival to an austere Europe. Or, as he confided to Ernest Jones, “Yes, America is gigantic, but a gigantic mistake” (Jones, 1959, p. 181). So how, then, did psychoanalysis take root in the United States? The story is full of twists and turns, and here it can be sketched only in bare outline. It falls into three parts or periods: the first, from the formation of the American Psychoanalytic Association in 1911 to roughly 1938, was marked by a struggle for professional identity and recognition; the second, from 1938—or the arrival of a signifi-cant number of émigré psychoanalysts—to the late 1970s, witnessed the consolidation of that identity coupled with a dominant position within psychiatry; the third, from the late 1970s, was a time of unremitting attacks on American psychoanalysis that left it once again uncertain of its footing. * * * In a letter to Jones dated 12 April 1921, Freud offered the following description of the American scene:


The Americans are really “too bad.” I would not give a judgment why they are so without better opportunity for observation, I think that competition is much more pungent with them, not succeeding means civil death to every one, and they have no private resources apart from their profession, no hobby, games, love or other interests of a cultured person. And success means money. Can an American live in opposition to the public opinion, as we are prepared to do? [in Paskauskas, 1993, p. 419]


Freud’s remarks were astute. Still, something more needs to be said about the social position professionals occupied in early twentieth-century America. Historians—starting with Richard Hofstadter—have written at length about “an upheaval in status” that took place during the closing decades of the nineteenth century and early decades of the twentieth. Up to about 1870, the United States was a nation with “a rather broad diffusion of wealth, status, and power, in which the man of moderate means, especially in the many small communities, could command deference and exert much influence”. After the Civil War all this changed. “The rapid development of the railroads, the emergence of the corporation as the dominant form of enterprise, transformed the old society and revolutionized the distribution of power and prestige” (Hofstadter, 1955, pp. 135, 136). Walter Weyl, a leading Progressive journalist, commented:


that in the still competitive arena of prestige derived from conspicuous consumption and the style of life, the new plutocracy had set standards of such extravagance and such notoriety that everyone else felt humbled by comparison…. Although lawyers, doctors, engineers, architects, and professional men generally, make larger salaries than before, the earning of one hundred thousand dollars a year by one lawyer impoverishes by comparison the thousands of lawyers who scrape along on a thousand a year. The widening of the competitive field has widened the contrast between success and failure, with resulting inequality and discontent…. [A]ll groups with claims to learning and skill shared a common sense of humiliation and common grievance against the plutocracy. [Weyl, 1912, pp. 242–248, quoted in Hofstadter, 1955, pp. 147, 149]


Still more, doctors faced a special challenge to substantiate their claims to skill and learning. In 1910 Abraham Flexner, in a report published under the aegis of the Carnegie Foundation, threw down the gauntlet. In America, he noted, “we have … medical practitioners not inferior to the best elsewhere; but there is probably no other country in the world in which there is so great a distance and so fatal a distance between the best, the average, and the worst”. He called for “the development of the requisite number of properly supported institutions and for the speedy demise of all others”. Physicians met the challenge: by 1930s, Flexner’s model of a laboratory- and hospital-based research medical university had become standard (Flexner, 1910, quoted in Beck, 2004). Flexner promoted educational reform as a public health measure; physicians used it to ensure, indeed to increase, professional status as well. Given these status anxieties, it is no wonder that American psychoanalysts did not take kindly to the idea of lay analysis. It is no wonder that this became the greatest bone of contention between the American Psychoanalytic Association and the IPA, that this issue dominated relations between the two organizations for more than half a century. Among the participants to the ongoing dispute, Freud played a—if not the—leading role. In 1926 in response to legal proceedings brought against Theodor Reik, a prominent nonmedical member of the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society—Reik stood accused of violating an Austrian law against quackery—Freud fired off a pamphlet entitled The Question of Lay Analysis. (Reik’s case never came to trial: after a preliminary investigation, the charges were dropped.) In sounding the clarion, Freud staked out an extreme position, or rather a succession of extreme positions. On the most obvious matter, whether psychoanalysis should be considered a specialized branch of medicine, he was adamant: he did not want to see it “swallowed up by medicine”; he did not want it “to find its last resting place in a text-book of psychiatry under the heading ‘Methods of Treatment.’” “The possibility of its application to medical purposes”, he argued, should not lead one “astray. Electricity and radiology also” had “their medical application, but the science to which they both” belonged was “none the less physics”. If psychoanalysis did not belong to medicine, of what was it a part? Psychology was the answer Freud gave, “not … the psychology of the morbid processes, but simply … psychology”. Moreover, “as a ‘depth-psychology,’ as a theory of the mental unconscious”, it could “become indispensable to all sciences … concerned with the evolution of human civilization and its major institutions such as art, religion and the social order”. Freud obviously did not regard medical education as obligatory for the future psychoanalyst. Did he consider it at least desirable? He sounded dubious: it offered much that was “indispensable” to him; but it burdened “him with too much else” of which he could “never make any use”, and there was “a danger of its diverting his interest and his whole mode of thought from the understanding of psychical phenomena”. What Freud dreamed of instead was a curriculum that included “elements from the mental sciences, from psychology, the history of civilization and sociology, as well as from anatomy, biology and the study of evolution” (Freud, 1926e, pp. 248, 252). Freud occupied one end of the spectrum; his followers in New York occupied the other. In fact, nowhere was opposition to his stand as united as in the American metropolis: Berlin and Vienna were divided; New York spoke with one voice. And that voice insisted on limiting “the practice of psycho-analysis for therapeutic purposes … to physicians” who were “graduates of recognized medical schools” (Resolutions of the New York Psycho-Analytical Society; IPA, 1927, p. 283) (In 1926 this regulation became New York State law.) In the opinion of the New Yorkers, too many of their countrymen travelled to Europe, received perfunctory training, returned home, and “degenerated into quacks”—or, at best, became “second-raters” (A. A. Brill, in IPA, 1927, p. 221). Then there were those with no training at all: the novice who presumed “to call himself an analyst when surfeited with the boredom of social functions, or the scamp” who saw an opportunity for financial gain through charlatanry—“such as the correspondence school psychoanalyst” (C. P. Oberndorf, in IPA, 1927, p. 206). Against types of this sort, so the argument ran, the honest and the reputable needed the protection that medical education—backed up by the law—provided. In this matter, would the Americans go their own way? For the next decade the question of American autonomy figured prominently at international gatherings. In 1929 Freud suggested that a “friendly separation be arranged between the American and European Societies” (Jones, 1957, p. 298)—but Jones and others were appalled at this prospect. Instead, in 1932, they acquiesced to American demands: the IPA resolved that “the rule for the selection for candidates, including lay candidates, should be left to the discretion of each individual Society”. And further: “[B]efore any foreign candidate is accepted for training approval of his Home Training Committee be obtained” (quoted in Jones, 1957, p. 299). In other words, a New Yorker—presumably someone without a medical degree—could not undertake psychoanalytic training in Vienna without the prior consent of the New York Society. But events in Europe—that is, the Nazi seizure of power in Germany—required some modification. In 1934, the IPA carried a proposal to grant “direct membership” in the Association to those forced to leave their own Society for political reasons. A number of analysts emigrating to the United States, including lay analysts, were granted this new status. The Americans did not respond with grace or sympathy: they did little to disguise their resentment at what they regarded as foreign interference. The worsening political situation in Europe did nothing to temper American intransigence. In 1938 the American Psychoanalytic Association (APsaA) created new rules, stating:


… that only physicians who had completed a psychiatric residency at an approved institution could become members. The Americans … attempted to enforce their professional policies, including the requirement of a medical degree, on the European immigrants. In addition to warning incoming analysts that practicing psychoanalysis without a medical license was illegal, they tried to steer lay analysts or laymen with aspiration to practice into other professions, such as social work or teaching. They also insisted that no European analyst, no matter how exalted his status, train others without authorization of an existing institute. [Hale, 1995, p. 128]


Only one concession did they make:


The Americans did agree … that already trained lay analysts who were members of the International Association could continue to practice but that thenceforth no other laymen could be trained to do therapy. [Hale, 1995, p. 128]


At the same time the Americans prepared for a showdown with the IPA. They announced that the American Psychoanalytic Association, which comprised all the branch societies in the United States,


would be willing to consider “affiliation” to the International Association on three conditions: that the International Training Commission, which they maintained was a superfluous institution that interfered with internal affairs in America, be abolished; that the “free floating membership” be withdrawn from analysts settling in America; and that the International Association should meet for scientific purposes only and be deprived of all administrative functions. [Jones, 1957, p. 300]


In the event, the dispute was referred to two committees, one American, one international. The outbreak of the Second World War altered the entire situation, and neither committee took action. The unity of the IPA was preserved, but at the cost of postponing “the still unsolved problem of the status of lay analysts” (Jones, 1957, p. 300). As Freud saw it, the opposition to lay analysis constituted “the last mask of resistance to analysis”—and “the most dangerous for the future” (Freud to Ferenczi, 27 April 1929, in Falzeder & Brabant, 2000, p. 366). In 1938, the Americans would have dismissed Freud’s view out of hand. But over the next five decades Freud proved prescient. Years later, Americans would pay a heavy price for the gender bias that hostility to lay analysis entailed. For where lay analysis flourished, so too did female analysts—and, one might add, the analysis of children. The Americans would also pay heavily for having thrown in their lot with medical practitioners, particularly for their success (albeit temporary) in capturing departments of psychiatry. * * * When the war was over, Jones wrote, “little was left of the psychoanalytic movement on the continent of Europe, and the Americans, now constituting the large majority of analysts in the world, … lost their former apprehension of the International Association” (Jones, 1957, p. 300). Despite—or perhaps because of—their international dominance, the Americans became parochial and complacent. For roughly three decades they regarded psychoanalysis and American psychoanalysis as one and the same. Not only were American psychoanalysts riding high internationally; they were simultaneously doing extremely well at home. The status anxiety of the early years, the economic insecurity of the Depression, were things of the past. Psychoanalysts had plenty of patients and scant competition for them. In those post-war years there was very little besides talk therapy on offer to the mentally ill, and there was very little in the way of talk therapy apart from psychoanalysis. This was a golden age that would not come again. But this fleetingness—the special circumstances of the mid-century—was taken for granted rather than considered a rare (and missed) opportunity. By the 1970s American psychoanalysis found itself, thanks to its continued insistence on a medical degree and its hostility to lay analysis, in a professional cul-de-sac; it found itself in an intellectual cul-de-sac as well. If the first can be understood as largely the result of earlier trends and attitudes persisting, indeed intensifying, in the post-war years, the second represents something new and paradoxical. The paradox is this: émigré psychoanalysts quickly became the intellectual leaders, really the intellectual face, of American psychoanalysis; they articulated a research programme much admired at home but little appreciated or emulated abroad. It was as if American-born psychoanalysts outsourced the establishment of intellectual credentials to European émigrés—except that the émigrés were no longer outsiders. In psychoanalytic institutes across the country, they had become the ultimate insiders. No one was more the insider nor had greater intellectual prestige than Heinz Hartmann. More than a decade before he came to the United States, he had announced his project: to substantiate the claim of psychoanalysis to being a natural science (see Hartmann, 1964). In his Ego Psychology and the Problem of Adaptation (1939), he declared that psychoanalysis could “claim to be a general psychology in the broadest sense of the word”. Above all, he was determined to make ego autonomy, and, along with it, what he called the conflict-free sphere, the focus of study. How did he intend to fill those boxes, those large, loose categories of the autonomous and the conflict-free? With the subject matter of academic psychology, it would seem—now introduced as specific ego functions. It should, however, be remembered that in 1939 Hartmann was merely making a programmatic statement: he recognized full well that to pursue his programme required “detailed empirical investigations” (Hartmann, 1939, pp. 4, 108; emphasis in the original). The empirical investigations were never carried out. Instead, Hartmann’s work after 1939, and particularly after 1941, when he finally settled in New York, remained confined to the theoretical aspects of psychoanalysis. Yet Hartmann appeared to flourish. His aspirations for psychoanalysis fitted his new environment nicely: “Both psychoanalysis and American psychology aimed at a scientific psychology which, in [Ernest] Hilgard’s words, ‘would be pleasing to Helmholtz—a deterministic world of energy-interchanges, a lawful world in which such principles as conservation of energy,’ would hold” (Jahoda, 1969, p. 435). On the personal level as well, Hartmann found the environment congenial; he quickly established himself both in private practice and in the New York Psychoanalytic Institute, and before long a position of primacy was more or less thrust upon him. Perhaps his very success was his undoing: he became entrenched within the psychoanalytic community and isolated from the academic psychologists who should have figured among his regular interlocutors. As a consequence Hartmann’s own research programme was reduced in scale: the nub of the matter now became reformulating and synchronizing psychoanalytic metatheory to accommodate the autonomous ego. What followed became the staple fare of psychoanalytic education in the United States. Neutralization of sexual and aggressive energies, primary and secondary autonomy, as well as the ego’s centralization of functional control, all became standard items—that is, until the reaction set in. In the 1970s complaints began to ring out against the proliferation of energies and cathexes. In the 1960s and 1970s Americans turned, not en masse, but in significant numbers to the work of another émigré from Vienna—Heinz Kohut. Like Hart-mann, Kohut laid claim to a European cultural heritage, and though his bona fides were less impressive or convincing than Hartmann’s, his English prose was equally tortured. Kohut settled in Chicago in 1940. Almost twenty years Hartmann’s junior—the older man was born in 1894, the younger in 1913—Kohut had barely managed to obtain his medical degree before being forced to flee the Nazi onslaught. In the Midwestern metropolis he completed his internship and residency, and there too, after an initial rejection, he received his psychoanalytic training, graduating from the Chicago Institute in 1950. For the next two decades, during which he served a term as President of the American Psychoanalytic Association (but, unlike Hartmann, he never held the presidency of the IPA), Kohut considered himself to be very much part of “mainstream” psychoanalysis in the United States—by which he meant psychoanalytic ego psychology, with Hartmann acknowledged as pre-eminent in matters theoretical. Kohut’s early publications were all written as if he were looking over his shoulder at Hartmann for recognition and approval; he employed the same technical language or jargon and the same ponderous tone. Indeed, when Hartmann died in 1970, so John Gedo reported, Kohut telephoned him at 2:30 in the morning:


With great urgency, he announced that Hartmann was dead. I said I was sorry to hear that, but that I did not understand the reason for the urgent … call. Kohut replied, half in exasperation, half in despair, ‘Heinz Hartmann is dead!’ I am sure I was supposed to say that, from then on, Kohut would be the premier theoretician of psychoanalysis. In my exasperation …, the idea came to me in the nastiest form imaginable: “The Heinz is dead, long live the Heinz!” [Gedo, 1997, p. 152; emphasis in the original]


In the last decade of his life—he died in 1980 after a long struggle with cancer—Kohut charted a new course and “to an extraordinary degree … closed out other thinkers”. That decade saw the publication of The Analysis of the Self (1971) and The Restoration of the Self (1977). It saw the crystallization of Kohut’s theoretical and technical approach to narcissism; it saw self psychology take shape as a movement outside, and threatening to, the American psychoanalytic establishment. Convinced that each generation had “to find its own conceptual voice”, Kohut became persuaded that his was “the new voice for psychoanalysis” for his generation, “that he was the new Freud in a new land” (Strozier, 2001, p. 266). Kohut saw himself as founding a psychology of the self; in fact, he had simply latched on to one component—narcissism—of Freud’s legacy and had bracketed or ignored much of the rest. Over time his chosen item loomed larger and larger until, in his eyes at least, other conceptual components sank into insignificance. His theory building, however faulty it may have been, had a serious (if unintended) consequence: it broke the stranglehold that psychoanalytic ego psychology had exercised over psychoanalysis in the United States. Note a painful irony. Just as American psychoanalysis was breaking out of its post-war conceptual rigidities—self psychology was but one manifestation of intellectual effervescence and a new theoretical pluralism—its glory days as a profession were coming to an end. In the 1970s and 1980s it paid the price for having bet the house on (male) psychiatrists. * * * The year 1970: the publication of Kate Millett’s Sexual Politics, and 1980: the publication of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual–III (APA, 1980)—two dates and two books on which to fasten comments about the deteriorating fortunes of American psychoanalysis. The first—in the name of consciousness-raising—implicitly attacked the psychoanalytic concept of unconscious motivation; the second—a neo-Kraepelinian classification system—attacked the very notion of the mental and/or psychological. In the 1970s and 1980s American psychoanalysts felt increasingly beleaguered and marginalized. In sharp contrast to their academic compatriots interested in psychoanalysis, American clinicians—once the émigré generation had left the scene—evinced little appreciation that they were part of an international movement. Of the many feminist critiques of Freud to appear in and around 1970, Kate Millett’s ranks as the most important.1 Her discussion of Freud was both more extensive and more incisive than those of other feminist exposés. From it Freud emerged as one of the architects of modern sexism, as a central figure in the counterrevolution against modern feminism. In short, Millett saw his theories as lending scientific respectability to age-old prejudices. What feminists found most objectionable was the theory of penis envy—the notion that women’s psychology is based on a sense of genital inadequacy, from which follow noxious consequences: passivity, narcissism, and masochism. Millett in particular granted that the sense of inferiority was real enough, but she insisted that Freud had mistakenly attributed it to the little girl’s envious perception of her brother’s genitals, when in truth female inferiority was a social and historical creation, fully explicable in terms of the concrete arrangements that kept women in a subordinate position. The unfortunate element in Freud’s theory, Millett argued, was that it anchored that sense of inferiority in the immutable facts of anatomy. It made women’s lesser station into a biological inevitability, since—as the famous or infamous phrase goes—anatomy is destiny. Still more—or worse—Freud claimed that women’s anatomical defect had serious cultural (in addition to psychological) repercussions. Because of the less traumatic (and hence less complete) resolution of the Oedipus complex, so Freud’s argument ran, women do not develop the powerful superegos that might equip them to be the bearers of civilization. They remain ethically as well as physically inferior to men. In the two decades following its publication, Millett’s attack became an intellectual fixture of second-wave feminism. At the same time the feminist critique broadened to include, along with Freud’s theory, his sometimes high-handed treatment of his female patients. The case of Dora turned out to be a goldmine in this regard. In it feminists found Freud browbeating an 18-year-old girl for failing to respond to the advances of the middle-aged husband of her father’s mistress. In similar vein, the feminist critique allied itself with the growing body of writings on child abuse, incest, and rape. Here Jeffrey Masson, Kurt Eissler’s erstwhile aide-de-camp at the Freud Archives, entered the lists.2 In The Assault on Truth: Freud’s Suppression of the Seduction Theory (1984), he argued that Freud had been ahead of his time: when he propounded the seduction theory he had recognized as profound truth, the abuse of women and children; when he abandoned that theory, he fell into falsehood. And in substituting the notion of unconscious fantasy for a historical event, he made children responsible for the passion that the earlier theory had correctly located in adults. Above all, Masson, in his apostasy, disavowed the Freudian vision of the self as a site of internal tension and conflict; he disavowed what is central to the psychoanalytic enterprise: the exploration of the unconscious mind. Masson wanted to return to a pre-Freudian world of childhood innocence; academic psychiatrists were eager to restore—after decades of psychoanalytic or psychodynamic dominance—the reign of the somatic or biomedical. The 1980 adoption of DMS–III by the American Psychiatric Association signalled the growing influence of those who hungered for diagnostic precision and a somatic approach. It constituted the triumph of those who insisted that a classification system should be based on standardized, reliable criteria that would generate testable hypotheses. (And it also testified to the unwavering—and unwarranted—belief that such a system was ready at hand.) Psychiatrists thus praised the new manual as an indication of a rein-vigorated commitment to scientific method. As for psychoanalysts, long identified with medicine and psychiatry, they now found themselves judged—and judged adversely—by changed measures of “scientific” confirmation. Otto Kernberg, for one, regarded the manual as a “disaster. It is a straitjacket and a powerful weapon in the hands of people whose ideas are very clear, very publicly known, and the guns are pointed at us” (quoted in Hale, 1995, p. 239). The psychiatric profession was not the only one pointing guns: psychologists were as well—in the form of a lawsuit against the American Psychoanalytic Association and, secondarily, against the IPA. In this instance what looked like an attack and, at the time, felt like an attack produced a positive result: it resolved the status of lay analysis; it settled an issue that had bedevilled relations between American psychoanalysts and the international movement for more than half a century. * * * In October 1988 the lawsuit, initiated under the Sherman Anti-Trust Act, “by four clinical psychologists, as a class action suit on behalf of all PhD clinical psychologists against the American and two of its Affiliate Societies, New York and Columbia, for allegedly illegally depriving qualified psychologists of equal access to psychoanalysis as a profession and a source of livelihood, and against the IPA as a co-defendant, for allowing its American component organization to do these improper things” (Wallerstein, 1998a) was finally settled. In the course of the litigation that dragged on for three and a half years, APsaA passed the so-called “Gaskill amendment”. For the first time, APsaA set down the conditions under which “qualified psychologists and other non-physicians could be admitted to the Institutes of the American” for full clinical training (Wallerstein, 1998a; see also Simons, 2003; Wallerstein, 1998b). At long last—and only under duress—APsaA relaxed its guild-like rigidity. At long last, APsaA fell into line with the IPA. Recall that in 1938, the Americans, then roughly 20% of the IPA, had come to the Paris Congress insisting on the right to bar non-physicians from psychoanalytic training in the United States (and also to bar European-trained lay analysts—should they immigrate to America—from membership in the American Psychoanalytic Association). And they had backed up their demands with a threat of secession. In the hope of finding a compromise, Jones, then IPA president, put off a vote. The next congress was to have been held in 1940, but by then Europe was engulfed in war, and the IPA was unable to function as an international body. After an 11-year gap, a congress was once again held in 1949. Prior to this meeting in Zürich, Jones, still president, made another effort to reach a settlement with the Americans. The understanding arrived at gave the Americans what they wanted: the IPA agreed to act as if the American demands of 1938 had been adopted—“in effect accepting as de jure what the Americans had created de facto in 1938”. This decision, dubbed the “1938 rule” and taken without a vote at the 1949 Congress, “was later formalized as the Regional Association status of the American, giving the American complete control over its training policies and standards (not subject at all to IPA supervision) and an ‘exclusive franchise’ for the establishment of new IPA affiliated institutes and societies in the U.S.” (Wallerstein, 2002). The lawsuit called this arrangement into question. The two-thirds of the IPA membership that was not in the American—most of whom were hostile to the American’s exclusionary practices—resented the use of their dues to help defray the costs of the lawsuit. Preparing for the 1987 IPA Congress in Montreal, the British Society notified the IPA that it intended to introduce a resolution “stating that if any IPA component organization got into difficulties with the laws of its own country, the IPA would be held blameless and would be fully indemnified by that component organization if it incurred any penalty or expenses because of the litigation”. Were this resolution to have been put to a vote—it would have undoubtedly have found Europeans and Latin Americans joining hands in opposition to APsaA—the IPA would have faced the prospect of splintering, the very danger that Jones had managed to finesse in 1938. Now Robert Wallerstein, the IPA president, picked up the torch—and with no sleight of hand this time.


To avert a possible splitting of the IPA in Montreal, and to simultaneously indicate to the plaintiffs in the lawsuit the willingness of organized psychoanalysis to work toward resolution and settlement of the entire controversy over nonmedical psychoanalysis, the IPA asked the American to relinquish at least one of its prerogatives under the Regional Association agreement, either the complete internal autonomy on training matters or the “exclusive franchise” in the U.S.


APsaA chose to give way on the second item.


The officers of the American pledged to bring a resolution to Montreal relinquishing the “exclusive franchise.” … This was duly announced and subsequently carried through; in response the British Society withdrew its resolution and the IPA’s integrity as an organization was not brought into jeopardy. [Wallerstein, 2002, p. 641]


Resolving the thorny issue of lay analysis and revising the regional association agreement, taken together, opened the way for much greater involvement of the APsaA—and Americans—in the IPA. That involvement, in turn, helped breach the intellectual isolation of American psychoanalysts. Where earlier members of APsaA rarely came into dialogue with competing psychoanalytic theories—the Kleinian and the object-relational in Britain and the Lacanian in France were prime examples in the 1950s and 1960s—now they, or a younger generation, are far more abreast of diverse theoretical currents. As the IPA celebrates its 100th anniversary, it can feel vindicated in its claim that, both institutionally and intellectually, it represents a movement of international scope. Notes 1. On the feminist critique of psychoanalysis, see Robinson (1987). For a feminist defence, see Hughes (1999), especially ch. 5. 2. For Masson’s relations with Eissler, see Malcolm (1984). Part VInternational survey







37Japan Globalization and the importation of psychoanalysis into Japan

Shigeyuki Mori

How is the importation of psychoanalysis into Japan related to globalization around the world? This was the question in my mind as I began to work on this chapter. To begin, I quote Blowers and Chi, who compared the impact of psychoanalysis in China and Japan: “Whereas in China this knowledge got little further than providing rhetoric for social reformers, in Japan it evolved as a practice with training institutes, and a journal, and, for its pioneers, some meetings with Freud and his immediate circle in Vienna” (Blowers & Chi, 1997, p. 115). In fact, the importation of psychoanalysis was already underway when Japan was in the early stages of modernization and psychoanalysis itself was at the beginning of its development.1 From the start, the development of psychoanalysis in Japan has rested on a paradox: the need to obtain formal approval and acknowledgement from Freud himself while simultaneously adapting some of the central tenets of psychoanalytic theory to the demands of Japanese culture. The process of introducing psychoanalysis into Japan was a twofold process: to be faithful to its tenets while at the same time assimilating them within Japanese culture. This process might be viewed as one common to all instances of foreign ideas being imported into a culture. My intention here, however, is to present the process of the importation of psychoanalysis in Japan rather as an amalgamation of multidimensional and diverse phenomena in order to shed light on an aspect of the globalization of psychoanalysis. The early stages of Japan’s modernization were not simple. They were initiated by the Meiji Restoration, which forced Japan to shift from an age of the sword, governed by samurai, to an age of parliamentary democracy. The Meiji government prescribed the introduction of Western knowledge as an urgent task: it promoted the invitation to Japan of foreign scholars as well as giving support for Japanese scholars to study abroad and for the translation of Western literature. The translation by Nishi Amane of Haven’s Mental Philosophy Including Intellect, Sensibilities, and Will (1869) introduced concepts such as sensation, memory, and imagination as scientific terms, and Nishi’s translation of these terms prepared the ground for future academic discussions. The Japanese translation of the title, sinri-gagu, was adopted about a decade later as the translation for the term “psychology”. Western medicine was introduced to Japan by the German professor Erwin von Bälz at Tokyo University, who is said to have introduced hypnosis into Japan by lecturing (between 1876 and 1902) on hysteria and hypnotherapy. The psychiatry professor who succeeded him, Kure Syūzo, introduced Kraepelin’s diagnostic system in Japan and laid the foundation for Japanese psychiatry. His paper on psychotherapy was the first of its kind. The first Japanese psychologist was Motora Yūjiro, who was in the United States from 1883 to 1888; initially he studied philosophy, but, after meeting G. Stanley Hall at Johns Hopkins University, he specialized in psychology. Hall, having returned from visiting Wundt’s laboratory, had opened his own laboratory; Motora, following Hall’s example, opened the first psychological laboratory in Japan at Tokyo University in 1890. Like Hall, he developed an interest in child psychology and introduced intelligence tests as a pedagogical tool. An associate professor in the psychology department, Fukurai Tomokichi, who had also studied abroad under Hall and was in deep sympathy with William James, obtained a doctorate in hypnotic studies and wrote two volumes on the subject (Fukurai, 1905, 1906). Hypnosis had already become popular in Japan, even experiencing a boom around 1900, resulting in the emergence of many folk hypnotists. Fukurai was a leading scholar in the field and a strong candidate to be the next professor. Western knowledge, then, was being introduced into Japan at the same time as Freud was beginning his career. Therefore, as a matter of course, psychoanalysis soon entered academic discourse. The first literature that refers to Freud may be an essay on Western medicine published in 1902 by a medical doctor Mori Ōgai, who is also widely known as a novelist (Mori, 1902–3). Mori, who had written a novel based on King Ludwig of Bavaria’s tragic suicide, which occurred during his stay at Munich Medical University, had been in a position to have encountered Freud’s name in German medical literature. But his references to Freud are only fragmentary. The photograph taken on the occasion of Freud’s visit to America with Jung and Ferenczi is well known. Perhaps many people will be surprised to find two Japanese scholars in the photograph. They are a visiting psychologist, Kakise Hikozo, and a biologist, Kanda Sakyo, who were studying at Clark University. They had both studied at Tokyo University under Professor Motora and were present on this historical occasion.2 Kakise gave a lecture after returning to Japan in 1911 and devoted a large part of it to introducing psychoanalysis—probably the first time that psychoanalysis became the object of public attention. Through the Hall–Tokyo University connection, psychoanalysis gradually became known to academics and intellectuals. Overlapping this early stage of the introduction of psychoanalysis, a serious course of events was unfolding in the department of psychology—that is, a scandal caused by the associate professor, Fukurai Tomokichi. Though it does not appear that he knew Freud’s theory, his far-reaching knowledge of the French school of hypnotism and his use of the concept of latent consciousness strongly suggests that he would soon become familiar with psychoanalysis. Nevertheless, in 1910, just before Kakise’s return, Fukurai became involved in the study of two female clairvoyants who were garnering public attention. A para-psychological experiment he planned and demonstrated in front of prominent professors in various fields created a sensation. But a fatal flaw1 in the experiment ended up bringing disgrace to Fukurai as a scientist; the scandal was further exacerbated by the sudden death of both of the clairvoyants: one committed suicide, the other succumbed to the flu. The scandal forced Fukurai to resign from his position at the university, and the subjects he had been teaching, such as hypnotism and abnormal psychology, disappeared from the curriculum. The coincidental tragedy that his senior professor, Motora, died of cancer must have further aggravated Fukurai’s isolation. The various interests of the psychological department, which had developed following exposure to G. Stanley Hall’s influence and interests, were suddenly restricted, and, as Tokyo University was the leading academic institution, the study of the Unconscious was excluded from Japanese psychology as a whole. Though the relationship between psychoanalysis and academic institutions in Europe was not positive, there was from the beginning the possibility of integrating the study of psychoanalysis within academic institutions in Japan, where there was no history of discrimination against Jewish people. The Fukurai scandal happened at the very beginning of the importation of psychoanalysis; it was a traumatic event for Japanese depth psychology, and it cast a shadow over Japanese clinical psychology right up to the 1980s. In my view, this continues even today, because it has biased the faculties of universities against psychoanalysis and depth psychology. Fukurai, however, continued his para-psychological studies until his death; he established a private research institution, and he himself voiced no regrets over his resignation. Nonetheless, in 1912, “Psychology of Forgetting” and “Psychology of Making Mistakes” by Ōtzuki Kaison, as well as “On Psychoanalysis” by Kimura Hisaichi were published in a newly established journal, Shinrikenkyū [Psychological study]. These papers were the first examples of psychoanalysis appearing in the literature. Ōtzuki Kaison was a psychology scholar who had studied at Tokyo University, but he later gave up psychology. Another important figure was Kubo Ryōei, who, after participating in Fukurai’s experiment as a student, studied abroad, also under Hall’s tutelage; he studied psychoanalytic theory even more thoroughly than his precursors. His book Seisinbunsekihou [Psychoanalysis],4 published in 1917, was the first comprehensive text on psychoanalysis in the Japanese language. In the Foreword he wrote: “I owe my encounter with Freud’s theory as well as all my understanding of its true virtue to my teacher Dr. Stanley Hall. His lecture given twice a week had hardly ever ended without mentioning the names of Freud and his opponent, Adler.” Hall had been devoting his energies to introducing psychoanalysis to America even after Freud’s visit. We can catch a glimpse of it in Freud’s letter to Ferenczi on 8 February 1917:



Dear friend,

Today I can finally forward to you the long-awaited news from Worcester that your article on dreams arrived a long time ago, has been translated by Mr Chase, the translator of my lectures, and is in press. Stanley Hall adds that he asked Brill, whom he saw in New York, to write him a paper “which should do for your work on the actions of daily life what Ferenczi attempts to do for Traumdeutung.” The old man is certainly very commendable. [Falzeder & Brabant, 1996, p. 133]


After returning to Tokyo University in 1916 after his stay at Clark University, Kubo found no place to pursue his interests. He did not leave psychology and, perhaps as a compromise, decided to study child psychology to obtain a position as a lecturer in Hiroshima. He continued to read Freud’s work and in 1932 published a more comprehensive and systematic text on psychoanalysis, Seisinbunsekigaku [Psychoanalytic theory], which included Freud’s later theory of Jenseits des Lustprinzips [Beyond the Pleasure Principle: Freud, 1920g] and Das Ich und das Es [The Ego and the Id: Freud, 1923b]. Kubo was the last generation to be exposed to psychoanalysis through the Freud–Hall–Tokyo University link that was so important in introducing psychoanalysis to Japanese scholars and psychologists. Although this link was severed, as I have described above, the knowledge of psychoanalysis introduced through this link was very probably disseminated among intellectuals. Ōtzuki Kenji, a man of letters, Yabe Yaekichi, a psychologist, and Hasegawa Seiya, a journalist, who had all become independently interested in psychoanalysis, joined forces and established the Tokyo Institute of Psychoanalysis in 1928, and the number of people who were interested in psychoanalysis outside the universities was increasing. Along with these developments, psychoanalysis was also becoming known to medical scientists through the enthusiastic activities of Marui Kiyoyasu, who had encountered psychoanalysis rather incidentally. Having studied medicine under Professor Kure Syūzo, Marui planned to study abroad. Europe was in the midst of the First World War, so he decided to go to Johns Hopkins University in the United States, where he studied under Adolf Meyer’s tutorship until 1919. Meyer, who had himself attended Freud lectures, was one of the supporters of psychoanalysis. Marui himself became an enthusiastic supporter of psychoanalysis and, after visiting Vienna and London, established the Sendai Branch of the IPA with Jones’s approval in 1934. Marui, together with his students, including Kozawa Heisaku, Kaketa Katzumi, and Yamamura Michio, developed their own interest in psychoanalysis and became the founders of psychoanalysis in psychiatry. The importation of psychoanalysis increased in pace during the 1920s and 1930s. The translation of Freud’s work was an essential reason for this development. The first translation was Vorlesungen zur Einführung in die Psychoanalyse, by Yasuda Tokutarou, in 1926. Then two groups, one headed by Ōtzuki Kenji in Tokyo and the other by Marui in Sendai, planned to translate the collected works of Freud and, in fact, began to translate his major works. Studying in Europe also became a significant source for bringing knowledge of psychoanalysis to Japan. Yabe and Kozawa, via independent routes, visited Europe to deepen their understanding of psychoanalysis. A psychologist, Yabe had had personal analysis with Glover, attended Jones’s lecture in London, and met Freud in 1930 in Vienna. Kozawa, a psychiatrist, visited the Vienna Institute of Psychoanalysis and met Freud in 1932. He had personal analysis with Richard Sterba and supervision with Paul Federn from 1932 to 1933. He is famous for his psychoanalytic concept of the Ajase complex, which stresses the role of the mother–child relationship in human psychological development in terms of the child’s anger at mother’s betrayal and its repentance at the encounter with mother’s love. (Kozawa originally did not stress cultural differences, but, in later developments by other scholars, the concept was also used to understand Japanese characteristics.) Their stay was rather short for training, but Yabe was registered as a psychoanalyst by the IPA and Kozawa was recognized by followers as a person who had undergone training to the same level as Yabe, though there was a conflict between the two to some extent over their legitimation as psychoanalysts (Myōki & Anzai, 2004). Of the two, Kozawa, who survived the Second World War, had a much stronger influence on his followers. Practitioners who had personal analysis with him became main members of the psychoanalytic movement during the post-war period. Kozawa’s teacher, Marui, presented a paper at the conference of the Vienna Society of Psychoanalysis in 1933 and met Freud, Anna Freud, and Federn, and then Jones and Glover in London. With Jones’s permission, in 1934 he established the Sendai branch of the International Society of Psychoanalysis. This resulted in the coexistence of two societies, one in Tokyo and one in Sendai, in part caused by Marui’s refusal to join the Tokyo Society, which was composed of lay practitioners. While the Tokyo group and the Sendai group published their own journals, they also tried to maintain a cooperative relationship. They had a meeting in Tokyo on 30 January 1938 to find a way to form an integrated group of psychoanalysis in Japan (Kitayama, 2004a). (There are no records of other meetings.) Granting that there was no manifest consensus on a Japanese way of psychoanalysis, the foundation for the later development of Japanese psychoanalysis had already been laid by around 1940, and we can identify a few of its characteristics as follows:


Buddhism and psychoanalysis. Both Yabe and Kozawa sought the applicability of psychoanalysis to Buddhist culture. Following Freud, who found the roots of the human psyche in the Greek tradition, Kozawa searched for similar roots in Buddhist literature. Japanese psychoanalysis is of great interest because, as in India, it is a case of psychoanalysis being accepted and practised in a non-Christian society. Furthermore, it is interesting when we take into account the fact that the interrelation between Buddhism and psychoanalysis is now frequently discussed; I need hardly refer to the pioneering work by Erich Fromm, Zen Buddhism and Psychoanalysis (Suzuki, Fromm, & De Martino, 1960). The cooperation of scholars outside academic institutions. Psychoanalytic training within medical education and activities outside universities together contributed to the cultivation of psychoanalytic thinking. This cooperation took shape after the war as the concentric structure, as it were, of two psychoanalytic organizations established on the same day in 1955—that is, the Japan Psychoanalytic Association (JPA) of all scholars interested in psychoanalysis, and the Japan Psychoanalytic Society (JPS) of registered psychoanalysts only. The previously existing groups in Tokyo and Sendai were integrated into one organization, and the conflict between the two practically disappeared. Practical modification. The practice of standard psychoanalysis encountered difficulties in Japan, so one or two sessions a week has become normative practice. This style is supposed to have originated with Kozawa.


Needless to say, the process of the importation of Western sciences and culture was blocked from the late 1930s until 1945. The situation was the same for psychoanalysis. It stagnated even during the post-war period due to the difficulty of studying abroad. The first to go abroad after the war was Doi Takeo, in the early 1950s. He is famous for his amae theory, another effort to apply psychoanalysis to Japanese culture, and he remained one of the leading figures in Japanese psychoanalysis until his death in 2009. I will not treat amae theory in detail here, but his position as a Christian made his work more complex as well as stimulating. Probably the most epoch-making event after the war was the establishment of the JPA and the JPS Kozawa. This structure has influenced the subsequent process of the importation of psychoanalysis to a great extent, so that the membership of the JPA has greatly increased (to more than 2,000 members today), while the number of analysts has not grown much (it still remains under 30). This structure, according to Kitayama, was not only organizational but also psychological—a result of the resistance of Japanese society to psychoanalysis. The resistance left its trace not only in the structure but in the character of the popularity of psychoanalysis in Japan. While psychoanalysis as a theory has gained as much popularity in Japan as in Western societies, or even more, its practice is still limited to a small circle. The unpopularity of training analysis is one result. As a matter of fact, due to the geographical distances and because the number of analysts was too limited to establish a standardized system, what had been called psychoanalysis in Japan is practised over only one or two sessions a week—a fact that was brought to international attention and criticized at the Amsterdam IPA Congress in 1993. A revision of the rule was made after the conference: it was prescribed that only a practice with more than four sessions should be called psychoanalysis. The incident is called the “Amsterdam shock” by Japanese analysts. Interestingly enough, however, standard analysis is still rare, and there is no sign that this will change. This practice has always been a source of a sense of inferiority for Japanese analysts and has been taken as a sign of their being behind; but, given the worldwide difficulty of obtaining analysands for standard analysis, I must agree with Kitayama that Japan has been facing this difficulty in advance and is even in a position to present a model for future psychoanalysis. Returning to the process of bringing psychoanalysis to Japan, a considerable number of medical doctors and psychologists have studied abroad in the course of the post-war development of psychoanalysis. In Europe they mainly chose the United Kingdom and France, due to the post-war situation in Germany and Austria. This was the period of the development of schools of psychoanalysis, such as the Kleinian and Lacanian schools in Europe or ego psychology, self psychology, and interpersonal theory in the United States, so that students trained in one school brought it back to Japan. Therefore the controversies between schools of psychoanalysis were also imported, to the extent that scholars and practitioners took the role of missionaries, thereby intensifying the controversies. This overview of the events that preceded and accompanied the introduction of psychoanalysis into Japan has emphasized the multidimensional factors that have influenced the development of psychoanalysis in Japan rather than exploring what is distinctive about Japanese psychoanalysis. If I may still point out a feature of the process, it would be that Japan’s import of psychoanalysis has been incurring a trade gap, though it has nothing to do with today’s recession. Therefore, I need to ask myself how we could start exporting psychoanalysis or contributing to world psychoanalysis. Even though psychoanalytic practice is of universal value, it is a matter of course that psychoanalysis has been developing with a certain degree of diversity, and, I believe, the multiplicity or the diversity itself is of great value to the creativity of psychoanalysis. If that is the case, then Japanese psychoanalysis can also contribute to world psychoanalysis by increasing its diversity and multiplicity. And, of course, the diversity of psychoanalysis within Japan must also be preserved. Among other things, the cooperation of multiple schools (Kleinian and Independent Groups in the UK, Lacanian, the Interpersonal School, Self Psychology, and so on) within a small group in Japan will be able to contribute to the future international cooperation of schools. The Japanese experience of “importing” psychoanalysis without a strong leadership by particular Western psychoanalysts can also contribute to developing a benign form of an export/import process, with the independent work of introjection of the importing and without too strong identification with “a master”. In the process of modernization, the world has been becoming more and more homogeneous. Psychoanalysis, laying the axiom, Wo Es war, soll Ich werden [Where Id was, there shall Ego be], at the centre of its practice, also shared the values of modernization, even as it was practised as a tool to analyse that process critically. Facing today the violence of homogenization and the destruction of diversity, I would stress, instead, at the end of this chapter, another possibility that we have also learnt from psychoanalysis—that is, the possibility of mutual and creative dialogue with others with diverse value systems. Notes This paper is a revised version of a paper that was presented at Jahrestagung der DPG (Deutsche Psychoanalytische Gesellschaft) in Munich, May 2008. 1. Other sources of information for this chapter are the work of Kawada Akira (1977), Kitayama Osamu (2004), Myōki Hiroyuki & Anzai Junko (2004), and Sato Tatuya & Mizoguchi Gen (1997). 2. This was also the first occasion when Japanese psychologists met Ferenczi and Jung, though there is no document left about their encounter. 3. The clairvoyant was given one of several sealed lead pipes, each containing a sheet of paper, and asked to read the letters on the sheet in another room prepared for her to concentrate in. When she came back and gave an answer and the pipe was cut by a professor, her answer and the letters on the sheet were identical. The experiment seemed to be successful, but the professor who had prepared the material claimed that he had not put the letters in any of the pipes. Her testimony was that she, not being able to see through the given pipe, read another sealed pipe that Dr Fukurai had given her for practice on the previous day. Dr Fukurai claimed that she succeeded in seeing through the lead in any case, but the suspicion that the experiment was a magic trick could not be dismissed. 4. The book also contained pictures of Freud and Jung, which was the first encounter with their portraits for Japanese readers. Bibliography Blowers, G. H., & Chi, S. Y. H. (1997). (1906). Saimin Sinrigaku [Hypno-psychology]. Tokyo: Seibidou Syuppan. Haven, J. (1869). Mental Philosophy Including Intellect, Sensibilities, and Will. Boston, MA: Gould. Kawada, A. (1977). Sinrikenkyū, 1 (4): 1–40. Ōtzuki, K. (1912b). Yarisokonai no Sinri [Psychology of making mistakes]. Sinrikenkyū, 2 (1): 16–45. Sato, T., & Mizoguchi, G. (1997).
