7Hungary On psychoanalysis in Budapest

André Haynal

Around 1900, when Hungary was still part of the Habsburg monarchy, it was the first among the non-German-speaking countries where psychoanalysis attracted a considerable following. The “Budapest School” is characterized by a number of seminal ideas and independent features, which are epitomized in the personality and the activities of the outstanding figure of Sándor Ferenczi [1873–1933] and some of his followers. Ferenczi was representative of the typical Judeo–Hungarian urban culture in Budapest. His father had Magyarized the name from Fraenkel to Ferenczi. He came from a family of immigrants from the then eastern provinces of Austria (Galicia). His father was the proprietor of a bookstore and publishing house in Miskolc, an industrial town in the north of Hungary. Ferenczi spoke Yiddish with his mother and German during his medical studies at the University of Vienna, which meant that his cultural background was partly German. According to Freud, Ferenczi’s works “have made all analysts into his pupils” (1933c, p. 228); he added that it would be “impossible to believe that the history of our science will ever forget him” (p. 229). As a matter of fact, Ferenczi strongly influenced the entire psychoanalytic movement, especially the “technique” of therapeutic treatment. In Freud’s view, however, “[t]he need to cure and to help had become overpowering in him” (p. 229; translation modified). Nevertheless, Ferenczi opened up new vistas, the importance of which is increasingly being recognized. It is because of his influence that the original analytic model of analysis as a series of dream analyses changed to a view of analysis as the process of the development of a relationship in the analytic situation, from the inner perspective of an encounter and the mutual, interactive relationship between analysand and analyst. Under Ferenczi’s influence, psychoanalysis changed from a model rooted in association psychology and interpretation of symbols to the experience of an intellectual and affective process and the encounter between an empathetic analyst and his or her patients, rather than a merely rhetorical exchange between the two (cf. Haynal, 2002). On an institutional level, in accordance with Freud’s views and convictions, Ferenczi was one of the founders of the International Psychoanalytical Association (Jones, 1957, p. 173, writes even “actual founder”). Although he had recognized the pathology of such associations—for example, he wrote that “in most political, social and scientific organizations childish megalomania, vanity, admiration of empty formalities, blind obedience, or personal egoism prevail instead of quiet, honest work in the general interest” (Ferenczi, 1911, p. 302)—he hoped, nevertheless, that psychoanalytically trained individuals would be best adapted to found an association that would combine the greatest possible personal liberty with the advantages of family organization. For Ferenczi,


it would be a family in which the father enjoyed no dogmatic authority, but only that to which he was entitled by reason of his abilities and labours. His pronouncements would not be followed blindly, as if they were divine revelations, but, like everything else, would be subject to thoroughgoing criticism, which he would accept, not with the absurd superiority of the pater familias, but with the attention that it deserved. [Ferenczi, 1911, p. 303]


Eighteen years later, recalling that it was on his initiative that the IPA was founded, Ferenczi wrote:


It groups all those who are interested in psychoanalysis and who do their best to preserve the purity of psychoanalysis according to Freud and develop it as a separate scientific discipline. On establishing this association I had decided in principle to admit as members only those people who adhered to the fundamental thesis of psychoanalysis [today, personal analysis is also a part of the entrance conditions]. I believed and still believe that a productive discussion is only possible between people who think in the same way. Those who have adopted other basic principles as a starting point would do just as well in having their own centre of activity. [Ferenczi, 1928, p. 428]


Ferenczi was elected President of the IPA in 1918, but the collapse of postal and telephone communications in Central Europe forced him to resign from this position. Also, Ferenczi having launched the first issue of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis, the journal was taken over by Ernest Jones [1879–1958], his former analysand and future rival. Ferenczi explained:


I have therefore asked … Dr. Ernest Jones, who from his central geographical position and knowledge of the conditions … seemed the most suitable person, to undertake this task, and he has consented to do so, as also to act for me as President of the “International Psycho-Analytical Association”. [Ferenczi, 1920, pp. 1–2]


The question of the possibility of his re-election as President of the IPA was raised in the correspondence between Ferenczi and Freud at the end of the 1920s and the early 1930s (letter dated 18 September 1931, and passim, in Falzeder, Brabant, Giampieri-Deutsch, & Haynal, 2000), an attempt on Freud’s part to bring Ferenczi closer again to himself and his colleagues and to get him to give up his technical experimentations in his treatment of deeply regressed patients. Ferenczi refused, and Freud opted for Jones (Paskauskas, 1993). Moreover, Ferenczi, like Freud, was deeply convinced that psychoanalytic training should also be open to non-physicians, an idea that met with strong resistance, especially in North America. In 1913 Ferenczi founded the Hungarian Psychoanalytic Association in Budapest. Its first secretary was István [Stephen] Hollós [1872–1957], who specialized in the care of psychotics (Hollós, 1926) and became director of the Lipótmezö [Leopold meadow] asylum. Among the original figures in the early psychoanalytic group in Budapest was Melanie Klein [1882–1960], who, because of her depressive moods, had been in therapeutic analysis with Ferenczi from 1916 to 1920 (Grosskurth, 1986). Encouraged by the latter, she began, while still in Budapest, to develop her technique of analysing children’s play. She belonged first to the Budapest—and then to the Berlin—school of psychoanalysts, until she eventually found a new home in 1926 in London. Klein was also greatly influenced by the outstanding figure of Karl Abraham—disciplined, exact, respectable, and scientifically oriented, a good organizer with a clear mind—who exerted a fundamental influence on her theories, including her concept of the “depressive position”. Klein was in analysis with Abraham from early 1924 until his premature death in 1925. On the other hand, Ferenczi—who was less reserved and was a charming, charismatic personality, interested in the arts and literature and full of ideas—impressed her with his therapeutic style of work. She was also influenced by the emphasis he put on the mechanisms of introjection: identification and projection in a relational closeness between the two protagonists of the psychoanalytic encounter. Starting with Ferenczi and the analysts around Klein (P. Heimann, H. Racker), the analyst’s role and sensitivity and the question of countertransference came increasingly into focus (M. Balint, T. Benedek, etc.). Moreover, Klein postulated a very early and differentiated fantasy life in the infant, a position that was to lead to intense controversies (King & Steiner, 1991). Possibly under the influence of Ferenczi—and his personal problem which made him complain about his mother and what he felt as her lack of affection for him—the study of the role of the mother became important in his analytic orientation. This trend of thinking was followed by many of his successors. Perhaps the Budapest culture in general may have been, though under the cloak of a patriarchal system, more a “matriarchal” one. The list of analysts of the Budapest school who were interested in children, mothers, pre-genitality, and direct infant observation is a long one. For example, Alice and Michael Balint, Géza Roheim, and others looked beyond oedipal manifestations and investigated earlier experiences. Of the pioneers with a deep interest in the development of children, let us mention Alice [1898–1939] and Michael [1896–1970] Balint and Sándor Radó [1890–1972]; furthermore, for the initiation of direct infant observation, we need to add the Hungarian emigrants René A. Spitz [1887–1974] and Margaret Mahler [1897–1985]. A late literary testimonial, a reflection and a gleam of this sensibility, are shown in the moving autobiographical description of the extreme trauma from Nazi thugs suffered during his adolescence by Nobel Prize winner Imre Kertész (Kertész, 1975). The investigation of depressive moods was further deepened by Radó and by Robert Bak [1898–1974], among others. Let us return to the historical development after the end of the First World War. The tragedy of the post-Habsburg years in Hungary begins with a short-lived Communist post-war period. The so-called Republic of Councils of Béla Kun was followed by the White Terror, whose victims are estimated to have numbered 5,000, among them about 3,000 Jews (Fischer, 1988). Ferenczi writes in this context:


After the unbearable “Red Terror”, which lay heavy on one’s spirit like a nightmare, we now have the White one. For a short time it seemed as if they would succeed in moderating the parties toward a just compromise, but in the end the ruthless clerical–anti-Semitic spirit seems to have eked out a victory. If everything does not deceive, we Hungarian Jews are now facing a period of brutal persecution of Jews, They will, I think, have cured us in a very short time of the illusion with which we were brought up, namely, that we are “Hungarian of Jewish faith”. I picture Hungarian anti-Semitism—commensurate with the national character—to be more brutal than the petty-hateful type of the Austrians. It will very soon become evident how one can live and work here. It is naturally the best thing for Ψa. to continue working in complete withdrawal and without noise. [Ferenczi to Freud, 28 August 1919, Letter 819, in Falzeder & Brabant, 1996 p. 365]


Imre Hermann [1889–1984] and Michael Balint were persecuted, and Ferenczi was subjected to retaliatory measures, particularly expulsion from the Budapest Society of Physicians. Moreover, he also lost his professorship at the university. Due to the political climate in Hungary, many intellectuals, including a number of psychoanalysts, were forced to emigrate: Charlotte Balkányi, Edit Ludowyk-Gyömröi, and Lilla Vészy-Wagner went to London; George Devereux and Béla Grunberger to Paris; Robert Bak, Sándor Feldmann, George Gero, Sándor Lorand, Endre (Andrew) Petö, Sándor Radó, Rene Spitz, and Géza Roheim to New York City, David Rapaport, after a short stay in New York, went to the Menninger Clinic in Topeka, Kansas, and from there to Austen Riggs in Stockbridge, Massachusetts; Franz Alexander and Therese Benedek settled in Chicago. The reestablishment of fascism in Hungary reached its climax on 19 March 1944 with the German occupation. It is generally estimated that two thirds (that is, some 550,000) of the approximately 800,000 Jews in Hungary were victims of National Socialism. The Hungarian Psychoanalytic Society mourned the loss of 6 of its 26 members, and of 2 of its 11 candidates (Nemes, 1985). Due to the advance of the Soviet Army in 1944 and the siege of Budapest, the deportation of Jews could no longer be continued. Members of the fascist Arrow Cross Party forced Jewish citizens out of their houses, which were marked with the yellow star; even those under the protection of foreign embassies were often rounded up and shot at night on the lower steps of the Danube embankment. Hollós and/or his wife would have been killed, too, if—for some unknown reason, according to some sources perhaps through the intervention of the Swedish diplomat Raoul Wallenberg—the killing had not miraculously been stopped. In a letter to Paul Federn dated 17 February 1946, Hollós writes that “Of the approximately 200 barefoot and half-naked people some 60 had already been forced into the Danube”. Hollós goes on to recount this experience and his immediate reaction to this traumatic situation:


We had to leave the last pieces of our clothing in the house of the Arrow Cross men, stand near the river, and wait until they had finished with those before us. These were clear and unmistakable signs of what awaited us. And despite all that, it did not enter my consciousness that something would happen to me and to my wife. A great alteration of the ego1 had to take place within myself, by which the utmost danger lost its significance…. Through this alteration of the ego, our judgement was so distorted that we believed those henchmen; for they told us they would bring us to the ghetto…. It lasted a second, and it lasted an eternity. In this sudden fall of my ego I was simultaneously numbed and clairvoyant; I took a sudden, but fundamental look at my life, taking stock. [Hollós, 1999]


These were unbelievable times, with inconceivable incidents, and incredible gestures—for example, the story of how the Budapest psychoanalyst Ilona Felszeghy crossed the frozen Danube from Pest to Buda on a provisional pontoon bridge to rescue Ferenczi’s diary from the ruins, and then succeeded in sending it to the West. A new wave of difficult times followed. After the Communists’ rise to power in 1948–49 and under the influence of Stalinism and the repressive cultural policy of Zhdanov, psychoanalysts—or at least those who stooped to doing so—first had to condemn psychoanalysis as a “bourgeois ideology” and then had to renounce it altogether in the developing people’s democracies. This was also the case in other countries—for instance, in France, where such declarations were issued by analysts such as S. Lebovici, F. Kestemberg, and J. Kestemberg (Bonnafé et al., 1949). During this campaign against psychoanalytic “ideology”, the Analytic Society in Hungary decided to disband on 8 February 1949 (Mészáros, 2008, p. 11). Hermann and Lily Hajdú, former and acting presidents, respectively, were in favour of voluntary dissolution. They shared the opinion of those who thought that it would be dangerous in this new era to have an independent society with close ties to the West. In any case, about a month later all other non-Communist societies and organizations, from boy scouts to chess clubs, were disbanded. With the dissolution of the Hungarian Psychoanalytical Society, the connection with the IPA was also severed. The truly heartbreaking history of cultural life in Hungary is connected to the deeper and even more tragic history of psychoanalysis under the influence of individuals like George (György) Lukács [1885–1971]. The son of a banker, a widely admired—particularly in France—Olympian, and a Marxist-Leninist philosopher, he contributed to the atmosphere of ideological denunciation. His personal drama was that he wished to be more a Marxist than a Leninist. After the fall of the revolutionary government of Imre Nagy, in which he served as minister of education and culture, he was deported by the Soviets to Romania. Unlike Nagy himself, Lukács was not hanged; in 1958, having made a narrow escape, he could then lecture on the happy future promised by Leninism. This was the same G. Lukács who, in 1919, had signed Ferenczi’s appointment as a professor in psychoanalysis, but in 1948 wrote that psychoanalysis could no longer be tolerated. Following the dissolution of the Psychoanalytic Society, a number of psychoanalysts were arrested. It seems that there were several reasons why psychoanalysis in particular was so vehemently attacked:


Anything to do with the psyche and human behaviour was to be viewed from an ideological standpoint that combined Pavlovianism and the ideology of class struggle. Psychoanalysis (like religion) was perceived as a dangerous ideological rival and was consequently denounced as “bourgeois and decadent”. Investigations of social phenomena and attitudes—for example, the roots of anti-Semitism, as explored in the post-war book by Imre Hermann—were dismissed as decidedly anti-Marxist and thus classified as “unscientific”. Those who worked with children of the Holocaust, and thus came into contact with Zionists, were viewed as traitors. Many were imprisoned for years for their alleged conspiracy with a hostile foreign movement, among them Székács-Schönberger and András József (in 1953). When the latter was released after years of imprisonment, he was a human wreck and died shortly thereafter. To the end, he refused to talk about anything that he had endured in prison. As early as 1947, Schönberger tried to analyse in a detailed monograph the connections between the works of Freud and Pavlov—naturally, to no avail. Soon afterwards the works of Freud and others were destroyed.


There were even two Stekelian analysts—who had previously not been accepted as candidates by the Hungarian Society—who collaborated with the secret police in the torture of political prisoners (Bonnard, 1954; Mészáros, 2008; Tabajdi & Ungváry, 2008). Male analysts, such as Hermann, Rajka, and others, took positions in communal outpatient clinics so as not to be denounced as “work-shy elements”. Married women were able, given the secure income of their husbands, to conduct a few analyses, mainly with children (Mészáros, 2008). The history of psychoanalysis in Hungary has been called a “history of horrors”. The fate of the Gimes family, for instance, is a tragic drama of Shakespearean proportions. Miklós Gimes Sr died in the deportations of 1944. In 1945 his wife, the analyst, Lilly Hajdú-Gimes, following her son and her daughter, enthusiastically joined the Communist party. From 1953 until 1956 she was the Director of the aforementioned Lipótmezö asylum. After the events of 1956, she had to step down from her position as director of the psychiatric clinic. Her illusions of the Communist ideals were shattered, and so was her courage to face life. In the course of the regime’s retaliatory measures after 1956, her son, who after the war had been a Communist journalist but had sided with Imre Nagy and the reform Communists, was sentenced to death as one of those accused in Imre Nagy’s trial and was executed in 1958. Her daughter and her family (Gimes’s son-in-law and four grandchildren) managed to escape; they fled to Zürich, Switzerland. Lilly Hajdú-Gimes, who suffered from a severe arthropathy, was not allowed visit her daughter and her grandchildren in Switzerland. Three times her request was rejected, the last rejection being irrevocable. In 1960, deprived of her last hope, sick and lonely, she committed suicide (Schiess, 1999). After the last emigrations around 1948–49, the year of the Communist takeover, those who were left behind were also left alone in their mourning. Deprived of their oxygen supply from the outside and effectively cut off from contacts beyond the Iron Curtain, they fell victim to their fears and the horrors of loneliness. During the period from 1948 to about 1980 psychoanalysis essentially went underground. This situation inevitably implied a certain complicity between analyst and analysand concerning the values of decent human ethics, rational thinking, and anti-totalitarian attitude, which could have been seen by the authorities as an anti-regime conspiracy. It did, in turn, modify inevitably the shape of the setting of analysis. Was it possible under such circumstances to afford the luxury of playing à la Winnicott? Hungarian psychoanalysis became a serious, sometimes rigid, affair, good for the morale of those involved. How the state of the larger society may have influenced the practice of analysis, we will illustrate through one of Hermann’s cases. Imre Hermann [1889–1984], who wrote his most important works before the Second World War, is one of the most important figures of this epoch. In the 1950s and 1960s his 1934 book Psychoanalysis as a Method was one of the basic analytic textbooks in Central Europe. His theory (Hermann, 1926) of the instincts of clinging and of going-in-search, based on observations of animals, exerted a great influence and was considered by many a valuable amendment to the original psychoanalytic drive theory; it was further developed by John Bowlby in his attachment theory and also influenced the work of René Spitz. As Hermann became more and more closely involved with the Communists, his life after the war was also influenced by politics. One of his patients was among those who were sentenced to death and executed in the Rajk trial, and suddenly he, too, was in danger. We may assume that, had that analysand mentioned Hermann’s name to the secret police, he would have been harassed, questioned, or arrested (which, however, was not the case). Hermann can rightly be seen as someone who was instrumental in maintaining the continuity of analytic teaching and research, despite the formal dissolution of the Society, despite ideological attacks, despite economic reprisals, even despite some life-threatening situations. Perhaps because Imre Hermann reached a very great age, he allowed himself to be bogged down by details and petty compromises and lived in bitterly felt isolation. He was offended by the loss of his contacts in the IPA, and he experienced this as a rejection. In contrast to those who—like Ferenczi—had died young and were remembered nostalgically, and those sons and daughters—like Balint—who had emigrated to other countries and successfully established themselves in their new psychoanalytic homes, Hermann’s life seems to have remained unfulfilled. It was only in the 1970s that it was possible to bring about a rapprochement with the IPA, which eventually led to the (re-)admission of the Hungarian Psychoanalytical Society (through negotiations conducted by György Hidas), first in 1975 as a Study Group and then, in 1989 as a Component Society of the IPA. From the 1980s on, the Hungarian Society re-established the structures of education, the analytic treatments, and research. Slowly it retrieved its historical roots and traditions. Moreover, a Ferenczi Society was founded to develop further contacts with the cultural environment, and for setting up a museum relating to the history of the Budapest School in a house formerly owned by Ferenczi. In order to support these projects, the Sigourney Award was generously granted to this Ferenczi Society in December 2008. Hungarian psychoanalysis, in spite of its brilliant, influential legacy and burdened by its tragic history, is nowadays struggling for a new life in the middle of a cultural crisis and a ruined economy left by 40 years of Communist regime. Note 1. Freud had used the concept of “alteration of the ego” [Ich-Veränderung] to describe interpretative delusions in paranoia (S.E., 3, p. 185; also already mentioned in Draft K of the Fliess correspondence). 100 Years of the IPA








31Venezuela History of the Venezuelan Psychoanalytic Association

Lucia Morabito Gómez

The history of the Venezuelan Psychoanalytic Association (Asociación Venezolana de Psicoanálisis, ASOVEP), in common with many other Latin American psychoanalytic societies, is a challenging history of individuals, convinced that the struggle to first become psychoanalysts, and then to establish an accredited Society and Institute, is a worthy endeavour. Achieving this has required many individuals to travel abroad. In contrast to the situation in England, France, the United States, or Argentina, psychoanalysts who, together with Freud, left Vienna, Berlin, Budapest, and Zurich in the late 1930s did not choose to emigrate to Venezuela. Therefore, Venezuelans had to leave Venezuela in order to receive psychoanalytic training. Moreover, only a few individuals were fortunate enough to have the opportunity to do so. As Fuenmayor (2003) has pointed out, however, the growth of psychoanalysis in Venezuela has also depended on individuals whose knowledge of psychoanalysis was superficial and inexact being willing to take advantage of the psychoanalytic expertise of the returning founders. By the end of the 1950s, in the immediate aftermath of the period of a terrible dictatorship, the first two psychoanalysts trained abroad, Dr Hernán Quijada in France and Dr Guillermo Teruel in England, arrived in Caracas. Dr Quijada’s important position in the Ministry of Health facilitated state support for psychoanalysis. The first Psychoanalytic Study Group was formed in 1963, following the arrival in 1961 of Dr Manuel Kizer from Argentina, Dr William Hobaica from France, and Drs Antonio Garcia, Fernando Acuña, and J. Araujo from Chile. Although not yet accredited by the IPA, they all worked very hard to bring psychoanalysts from abroad to lecture and teach, in order to strengthen their application to the IPA for Study Group status. Their work was economically supported by the group (A. García, personal communication 2010). Soon after the formation of the original study group, new members were added: Drs Cesar Ottalgano, Julio Aray, Hugo Dominguez, and Hans Voss from Argentina, and Dr Nicola Cupello from England. Some of the new members continued to travel back and forth until they had concluded their seminars; others, like Drs Hugo Dominguez and Julio Aray, who had finished their training in 1959 and 1962, remained in Argentina for several more years to continue their clinical practice. The arrival of these new members meant, however, that the IPA requirements to be recognized as a Study Group had finally been met (Olivares, 1984). By 1967 this first group of psychoanalysts finally achieved formal recognition as a Study Group by the IPA at the Copenhagen Congress. An IPA committee then worked at improving the group’s training by revising the theory curriculum and conducting group supervisions (López-Corvo, 2002). Between 1966 and 1969, Venezuelans who had travelled abroad for training finally settled in Caracas and joined the study group: Drs Hugo Dominguez, Julio Aray, and Juan Antonio Olivares came from Argentina, and Dr Antonio Briceño from Mexico. Dr Guillermo Teruel, who had trained in London, was designated after his return as the first training analyst by the IPA’s International Committee. He thereby became the first Director of the Venezuelan Institute. By the end of this period, Drs Kizer, Dominguez, and García were also appointed by the IPA as training analysts; and Drs Olivares and Aray were promoted to full membership. The appointment of several training analysts and the promotion to full membership of the others helped the group to gain recognition as an IPA Provisional Society. On 29 February 1968, the formal and legal constitution of the Psychoanalytic Study Group of Venezuela was passed. By 1969 the first group of candidates started their psychoanalytic training. During this period our Study Group was visited by the IPA president, Dr Peter Van Der Leeuw, and many COPAL members also came for training interchanges. But it was not until 1971, at the Vienna Congress, that the hard work of these Venezuelan psychoanalysts was finally realized when the group was recognized as an IPA Component Society, which would then become the Venezuelan Psychoanalytic Association. By 1973, the first group of candidates had graduated from our Institute (Olivares, 1984) Some time later Miriam Lopez, the first child psychoanalyst in the Venezuelan Society, and Dr Nicola Cupello finished their training in London, and Dr Hans Voss finished his training in Argentina. They all returned to Caracas and joined the newly recognized Psychoanalytic Association. Since most of the study group members came from Argentina, Argentinian psychoanalysts were enormously important not only for the creation of psychoanalysis in Venezuela, but also for its consolidation. During the 1970s, once the Venezuelan Association had been accredited by the IPA, many of them found our country a welcome refuge from the dictatorship in Argentina: the time had come when the Venezuelan analysts who had been trained in Argentina could extend their hospitality to help their colleagues from Argentina. In May 1975, power struggles and exclusion anxieties gave rise to conflicts within the Association between the old analysts and the new arrivals. Two groups were formed, necessitating the intervention of the IPA at the London Congress in 1975. Thanks to their intervention, the dissentions were smoothed over, and a joint agreement was signed in 1977 (López-Corvo, 2002). However, according to Gisbert (2002), the students whose training had been interrupted by the conflict—the third class—suffered the most and sought the collaboration of the Colombian Institute in order to provide the seminars disrupted by the conflicts within the Association. In 1983 Dr Kizer, one of the founding members, left ASOVEP to create a Lacanian group. In 1989, after more quarrelling, 15 other members decided to break away and create a separate group, the Caracas Psychoanalytic Society, which was soon given IPA accreditation as a Provisional Study group. In 1995, at the San Francisco Congress, the group received their final accreditation as a Component Society (López-Corvo, 2002). Although today there are still visible traces of past quarrels, the preponderance of new members who did not experience such difficult moments has ensured our institutional stability. The reputation of the Venezuelan Psychoanalytic Association, the oldest Institute in our country, is that of a serious, solid group (Gisbert, 2002). Many of our psychoanalysts are or have been professors at Venezuelan universities, teaching at both the undergraduate and graduate level. Their teaching has contributed to the spread of psychoanalytic knowledge and stimulated new generations of candidates. From 1973 until today, 15 classes of psychoanalysts have graduated at the ASOVEP Institute, and currently a new group is being trained. ASOVEP was also a member of COPAL, and was one of the Latin American societies present in 1983 to sign the Constitution of FEPAL. Many of our members have held important positions at the FEPAL’s board of directors in the past. As Gisbert (2002) pointed out, at the beginning our Institute was predominantly influenced by Kleinians. This was due to the fact that our founders had been trained primarily in England and Argentina. The tendency today, however is to integrate diverse theoretical perspectives. At the beginning, only medical doctors were accepted in the Institute, but today we welcome clinical psychologists and are moving towards accepting professionals from other fields as well. This broadening of our membership has enriched our clinical practice and made it more creative. Since 1999, a two-year psychoanalytic psychotherapy programme has been established and is offered to clinical psychologists and psychiatrists who want to improve their technical expertise. This programme is accredited by the Venezuelan Psychotherapy Association and by the World Council for Psychotherapy. Many of our members belong to these two groups, notably Dr Guillermo Garrido, who is a founding member and was the first President of the Venezuelan Psychotherapy Association. This fact has encouraged our members to become actively involved with other groups and participate in conferences with different clinical perspectives. These scientific exchanges have enabled us share our psychoanalytic perspective with others as well as promote the contributions of our members. By 2003, 12 of our members had achieved, through the grandfather clause, recognition by the IPA as child and adolescent psychoanalysts. Today, the ASOVEP is the only society in Venezuela with an accredited Child and Adolescent Department authorized to train child and adolescent psychoanalysts. In addition to being a component society of the IPA and FEPAL, thus guaranteeing our identity through proper vigilance of our analytic training and practice, ASOVEP members have worked freely and very hard to share our knowledge with students of the other helping professions in Venezuela. Among these activities, the most important are the following:


Annual Ad Honore Teaching and Clinical Supervisions. During the last two decades, over 25 psychoanalysts have given theoretical and technical seminars and yearly provide 720 hours of clinical supervision to clinical psychology training institutes. Low-fee psychoanalytic treatments and psychoanalytic psychotherapy training programmes. During the last decade, over 25 of our members have provided psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic psychotherapy, individual and group clinical supervisions, and seminars in psychoanalytic psychotherapy programmes, as well as teaching seminars on individual psychoanalytic authors. Disseminating psychoanalysis. Annual two-day Sigmund Freud Conferences for professionals and the general public have been held for over 30 years; other activities have been parental counselling in schools and in children’s hospitals; delivering talks at mental health conferences; and offering psychoanalytic forums at various cultural events for the past two decades. Ten members have served on the faculties of different university programmes and psychiatric hospitals.


The ASOVEP Review of Psychoanalysis, which used to appear at irregular intervals, is now in a transitional period; it is yet to be decided whether to continue publishing in print or to move the Review to electronic publishing. The most noteworthy contributions include Dr Teruel’s work on the interaction between couples and the death instinct; Dr Dominguez’s study of the dynamics of communication; Dr Aray’s work on fetal psychism and abortion, and his wide-ranging psychoanalytic study of a Latin American sociological condition such as illegitimate children; Dr Gisbert’s work on the identity of the psychoanalyst and on creative processes; Dr López-Corvo’s study on femininity, addictions, self-envy, a dictionary on the work of Wilfred Bion, and other clinical applications of Bion’s ideas; Dr García’s work on instincts, teaching psychoanalysis, and the study of Wilfred Bion’s contributions. Together they come to more than 24 published books, with 4 more, including Dr Fuenmayor’s work on the origins of sanity and madness, in the pipeline. Today, four decades after its formal accreditation by the IPA, ASOVEP is a respected institution, with more than 50 active members in Venezuela. However, due to the sociopolitical situation in our country, some 5 members have emigrated to Canada, Spain, the United States, and Panama over the last eight years. Bibliography Fuenmayor, J. (2003). Palabras en el 30 aniversario de la primera promoción de psicoanalistas de la ASOVEP. In: Auxi Scarano (Ed.), El Psicoanalista en Venezuela. Caracas: Silabaria Producción Editorial, 2009. Gisbert, A. (2002). Venezuela, and psychoanalysis. In: Edward Erwin (Ed.), The Freud Encyclopedia: Theory, Therapy and Culture. New York: Routledge. López-Corvo, R. E. (2002). Venezuela. In Alain de Mijolla (Ed.), International Dictionary of Psychoanalysis (3 vols.). New York: Macmillan Library Reference. Olivares, J. A. (1984). Breve reseña histórica de la Asociación Venezolana de Psicoanálisis. In: Auxi Scarano (Ed.), El Psicoanalista en Venezuela. Caracas: Silabaria Producción Editorial, 2009.







13Spain A brief history of the Spanish Psychoanalytical Society

Antonio Pérez-Sánchez & María Blanca Anguera Domenjó

The Spanish Psychoanalytical Society (SEP) is celebrating its 50th anniversary as a member of the International Psychoanalytical Association. In 1959 the IPA recognized, for the first time, a psychoanalytic Society on the Iberian peninsula, under the name of Lusitanian-Spanish Psychoanalytical Society, embracing analysts from Lisbon, Madrid and Barcelona. This society later evolved into the SEP. How did psychoanalysis come to Spain? Freud became news in Spain very early. In February 1883 “The Mental Mechanism of Hysterical Phenomena” was published in the Barcelona Journal of Medical Science, and the same translation also appeared, in the same year, in the Granada Medical Gazette1 (Bermejo, 1993). But according to Carles, Muñoz, Llor, and Marset (2000), these publications did not awaken any special interest. From that year until just before 1922, there were very few publications about psychoanalysis, and these were frequently written to oppose psychoanalysis. From 1918, thanks to the interest of pedagogues such as L. Luzuriaga and D. Barnés, from the Pedagogic Museum, and J. Peinado and J. Jaén, jurists such as C. Camargo and L. Jiménez de Asúa, and literary and artistic figures such as the novelists Azorín, I. Sánchez Mejías, and J. J. Domenchina, and the poets Antonio and Manuel Machado, who were receptive to new ideas, together with the new generation of progressive physicians and psychiatrists (such as G. R. Lafora, S. Banús, C. Juarros, and E. Mira) a more positive attitude towards psychoanalysis was developed (Carles, Muñoz, Llor, & Marset, 2000; Carpintero, 1984). The II Spanish Republic witnessed a cultural and scientific regeneration, which necessarily brought psychoanalysis to debate and brought with it a wider acceptance among the progressive sectors. And all of this in spite of the influence of the great scientist S. Ramón y Cajal, who stimulated a fervour for neuropathology among psychiatrists, to say nothing of the strong influence of German Krapelinian psychiatry, which, substituting for the previous French influence, was more sensitive to psychological content. Nevertheless, among young psychiatrists there existed a concern for scientific rigour and new ideas. This social and scientific atmosphere was responsible for the success, in 1922, of the project to begin publishing Freud’s writings, recommended by Spanish philosopher Ortega y Gasset, who, in spite of an ambivalent attitude towards psychoanalysis, sensed its importance. The Biblioteca Nueva publishing house entrusted the translation from German to L. López Ballesteros, who was congratulated on it by Freud.2 In three years, from 1922 to 1925, ten volumes were published; by 1934, the complete 17-volume work had appeared. From 1922 until the Spanish Civil War, knowledge of psychoanalytic theory carried on growing. From several journals of the time we can see an acceptance of the hypothesis of the unconscious, of the concepts of repression and censorship, and of the psychoanalytic interpretation of dreams, with childhood sexuality causing the greatest controversy (Carles et al., 2000). As for psychoanalytic practice, it was accepted as an exploratory rather than a therapeutic method. Even though there was a general knowledge about psychoanalysis, there was a marked polarization between, on the one hand, progressive psychiatrists receptive to psychoanalysis, although to varying degrees (such as, S. Banús, G. R. Lafora, C. Juarros, E. Mira i López, and A. Garma), and, on the other hand, prestigious psychiatrists of conservative ideology, who were opposed, although also to varying degrees, ranging from absolute (like J. M. Villaverde) to partial (J. L. López Ibor, Vallejo Nágera etc.) rejection (Carles et al., 2000, p. 304). During this period, A Garma, a Basque psychiatrist, a disciple of the prestigious neurologist S. Ramón y Cajal, travelled to Berlin in 1927, contacted Eitingon, was analysed by T. Reik, and was eventually accepted as a member of the German Psychoanalytical Society (GPS) in 1930. He returned to Spain as the first IPA psychoanalyst, with the idea of developing psychoanalysis here. A little earlier, in 1925, R. Sarró, a Catalan psychiatrist, had gone to Vienna, where he met Freud and began a personal analysis with H. Deutsch; however, he interrupted this and returned disappointed to Barcelona in 1927. In spite of his ambivalent attitude towards psychoanalysis, as professor of psychiatry at Barcelona University, Sarró accepted the collaboration of the first psychoanalysts, who would go on to found the Spanish Psychoanalytical Society belonging to the IPA. After the civil war, discussion on psychoanalysis between these opposite positions was no longer possible: most of the progressive psychiatrists had to leave the country. For example, E. Mira y López, the first professor of Psychiatry in Spain at the Independent University [Universitat Autònoma] of Barcelona, went to Latin America. A. Garma emigrated to Argentina in 1936, where he contributed to the foundation of the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association. M. Prados, another psychiatrist and Garma’s pupil, fled to Canada in 1939, where he later founded the Canadian Psychoanalytic Society.3 The psychiatric scene came, therefore, to dominated by the psychiatrist followers of the new dictatorial government; culture, science, and also psychiatry were ideologically determined by the military and a particular version of Catholic religious principles that was opposed to psychoanalytic ideas. But at the end of the 1940s, two independent and differentiated groups emerged, one in Madrid and the other in Barcelona. In Barcelona, in 1947, a number of psychiatrists, physicians, and writers created a cultural society, “The Erasmus Center for Anthropological and Humanistic Studies”, in the context of which they studied Freud. Among them we find the pioneers who would later officially enter the international psychoanalytic movement: Pere Bofill Tauler4 [1920–2009], Pere Folch Mateu5 and Júlia Corominas Vigneaux.6 In Madrid, two of Garma’s ex-pupils, R. Portillo and J. Molina, travelled to Berlin in 1949 and contacted the new German Psychoanalytical Association.7 Margarita Steinbach, a training member of the German Society, came to Madrid, and, between 1951 and 1953 she started 12 training analyses, one of which was with J. Corominas, from Barcelona.8 M. Steinbach died suddenly in 1954. In 1950, Bofill, Folch, and Corominas attended the World Conference on Psychiatry in Paris and held meetings with members of the Training Committee of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society,9 and they were accepted as candidates. But Bofill went to Switzerland (from 1952 to 1956) were he received training analysis, first with Odier, and later with Kraft and Reding. Folch also did his analysis with Odier. After her analyst’s death, J. Corominas had to restart a new analysis with M. Bovet (from 1955 to May 1957)10 in Paris, and in the 1960s she received a third period of personal analysis, this time with S. Resnik,11 initially in Barcelona. Then, when her analyst left Barcelona, she made twice monthly trips to London from 1965 until 1970 to continue her analysis with him. N. Abelló and T. Eske-linen,12 also from Barcelona, initiated their training in Switzerland. At that time (1950) in Geneva there was also another of the peninsula’s pioneers in psychoanalysis, F. Alvim from Lisbon, who was later joined by J. Rallo and J. Pertejo from Madrid and by P. Luzes, also from Lisbon. In 1955, the IPA recognized the existence of two groups in Madrid and Barcelona and suggested that they should follow separate training; the Barcelona group with the support of the Paris and the Swiss psychoanalytic societies, and the Madrid group under Argentinian analysts, led by the Spanish A. Garma. P. Bofill, P. Folch, N Abelló, P. Luzes, Alvim, J. Rallo, and J. Pertejo (already members of the Switzerland Society) and C. Zamora and J. Corominas (already members of the Paris Society) applied for recognition by the IPA. In 1957, at the IPA Congress in Paris, the group was recognized as a Study Group and in 1959 as an IPA Component Society under the name Lusitanian-Spanish Psychoanalytical Society [Sociedad Luso-Española de Psicoanálisis (SLEP)]. After years of work and perseverance, this small group of pioneers accomplished their goal of being recognized by the IPA. This is how two protagonists of that time remember the moment. In 2009 Pere Folch, confronting the death of his friend and colleague, Pere Bofill, recalled:


Pere Bofill did not become discouraged by the interminable obstacle course that the organization and legalization of the psychoanalytic society on the peninsula and during the dictatorship implied, at a time when psychoanalysis was suspect by the inquisition-like politics of the time. [Folch, 2009, p. 144]

Júlia Corominas also recalled that time:


The pioneers from Lisbon, Madrid and Barcelona, who had put so much effort into founding the study group, had been looking forward to this moment of recognition with great excitement. Important papers which have since become classics were presented at that conference, but what I most remember was our feverish activity, directed by Dr. Pere Bofill, in preparation for the Business Meeting, and our satisfaction at the full recognition which was granted to us. I will only say one thing about the party that evening: we were all packed in the car—the Diatkines, the Bofills and myself—our happiness in sharp contrast to the grey sea that was close by us. [Corominas, 1990, p. 13]


They deserved happiness after all of the difficulties they had faced, because in Spain political power was in the hands of people who detested change and freedom and, with the exception of very few individuals, in the 1950s and 1960s official psychiatry was contemptuous of psychoanalysis. On 26 September 1959 the Society met in Barcelona with the permission of the “governing authority”, as it was then called, and the Executive was designated: Pere Bofill as president, J. Corominas as vice-president, P. Folch as secretary, and Carolina Zamora as treasurer. The minutes mention a motion of appreciation to P. Bofill for all of the effort he had put in for the new Society to be organized, created, and admitted to the IPA.13 In 1958 M. T. Ruiz and R. Portillo applied for admittance to the SLEP.14 Before that, psychoanalysis in Spain had been present through two important events in Barcelona. In 1956, Prof. Sarró was celebrated at the University the I Ibero-American Congress. P. Bofill came from Geneva and was very active; he discussed Garmas’s ideas of the direct interpretation of the symbolism of psychosomatic symptoms (Carles et al., 2000, p. 259). The other event was the IV World Conference on Psychotherapy, also in Barcelona, in which a good number of foreign psychoanalysts, including F. Alexander from the United States and R. Diatkine and J. Lacan from France, took part. J. Corominas and P. Bofill presented a paper. Shortly afterwards, in 1962, the XXIII Conference of Romance Language Psychoanalysts met in Barcelona, and Bofill and Folch presented one of the main papers on “Clinical and Technical Problems in Countertransference”. But, as J. Corominas (1990) observed, the Barcelonan founders of the SEP continued to be interested in broadening their psychoanalytic training. Some of them met British analysts in 1961, at the 22nd IPA Congress in Edinburgh, and made contact with H. Segal and B. Joseph (M. Pérez-Sánchez, 1984). They decided to start a reanalysis. Bofill’s analysis was with H. Segal, for seven years, beginning in 1962.15 Folch also went to London later, to be analysed by D. Meltzer. N. Abelló’s analysis was with E. Jaques, and T. Eskelinen’s with B. Joseph. In order to broaden their own knowledge and that of the new candidates, foreign analysts—H. Segal, H. Rosenfeld, D. Meltzer, M. Harris, B. Joseph, R. Riesenberg, and I. Menzies, all themselves from the British Society and belonging to the Kleinian Group of London—came to visit Barcelona to give seminars and supervisions. R. Diatkine and L. Grinberg also gave seminars. So it is clear that the psychoanalytic orientation predominating in the Barcelona group was Kleinian. Later, the Lusitanian-Spanish Psychoanalytic Society evolved into three societies on the peninsula. In 1966 our Portuguese colleagues decided to form a separate Study Group; since then, the SLEP (Sociedad Luso-Española de Psicoanálisis) has become the Spanish Psychoanalytical Society (Sociedad Española de Psicoanálisis—[SEP]), made up of members from Madrid and Barcelona.16 In 1973 the members from Madrid decided to form their own Study Group, which was accepted by the IPA, and in 1981, at the 32nd IPA Congress in Helsinki, the two new societies—the Psychoanalytic Association of Madrid and the Portuguese Psychoanalytical Society in Lisbon—were recognized as Component Societies of the IPA. Nevertheless, the relationship between the three societies was maintained through frequent scientific meetings, which eventually led, in 1989, to the Iberian Congress, which has been held biannually to this day. Also, in 1983, the 33rd IPA Congress was held in Madrid, with the collaboration of the Madrid Psychoanalytical Association and the Spanish Psychoanalytical Society.17 The 1960s was a decade for growth of the Society. Between 1966 and 1969, seven new members joined the Society.18 Dr Campo, an Argentinean analyst who worked in research in Geneva, came to Barcelona to work and joined the SEP in 1971; he was a member until the end of his life. Our Society has contributed to other international events. In 1987 the European Psychoanalytical Federation and our Society organized the 7th EPF Conference in Barcelona, the main theme being “Negative Transference”. In 1997 the SEP organized the 40th IPA Congress on “Psychoanalysis and Sexuality”. Terttu Eskelinen, a member of the SEP, was president of the EPF from 1991 to 1995.19 In 2007 the EPF Conference took place in Barcelona on the theme of “Time and Timelessness”. In 1971 the Institute of Psychoanalysis of Barcelona opened to continue the training of psychoanalysis; its library subscribes to most of the psychoanalytic journals in existence. In 1973, the SEP, through the creation of the Commission for Psychoanalytic Relations and Applications, held interdisciplinary meetings and made contact with other institutions. Members of the Barcelona Institute started to collaborate in general hospitals and psychiatric hospitals, and also created a group for the teaching of psychoanalytic psychotherapy outside the Institute. For the last two years, there has been a signed arrangement with Barcelona University to offer a Master’s degree in Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy in the Public Mental Health in our Society. Nowadays we can say that the proportion of our members’ time dedicated to psychoanalysis proper (on-the-couch) and to institutional and public jobs (not only collaborations) weighs in favour of the latter. The SEP has two journals: Revista Catalana de Psicoanàlisi (Catalan Journal of Psychoanalysis), in the Catalan language, published for the first time in 1984; since then 25 volumes have appeared, in many years with two issues per volume. In 1996 another journal—Temas de Psicoanálisis (Themes of Psychoanalysis)20—was published in Spanish and directed at Spanish speakers all over Spain and Latin America. Since 1986, the Catalan Journal of Psychoanalysis has organized an annual Conference day in Barcelona, dealing with a great variety of subjects and attracting considerable public (not only psychoanalytic) participation. Also, since 1994, every year a Conference of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy is held in Andalusia organized by our Society. In 1991, the first candidates living in Andalusia (Seville)21 were accepted.22 In 1994 the Society created the SEP-Andalusia Group. Currently (2010), there are seven members and five candidates living and working in Seville. At present, the Spanish Psychoanalytical Society has 92 analysts and 50 candidates. Although the traditional main line of psychoanalytic thought is the Kleinian and post-Kleinian one and a large number of its members continue thinking and working from this position, other psychoanalytic lines of thought have developed among other colleagues, based on theories such as those of Winnicott, Ferenczi, Kohut, and others close to an object relations theory, in a wide sense. After three years it broke up, and three of its members, these two plus C Zamora, entered the SLEP. 15. “I worked nine days per week: I received analysis and seminars in London from Monday to Thursday, and I worked in Barcelona from Thursday to Sunday” (P.
