Inicis del moviment psicoanalític de Barcelona. Revista Catalana de Psicoanàlisi, 1 (1): 229–246.







Introduction

Peter Loewenberg & Nellie Thompson

The history of the spread of both popular and institutionalized psychoanalytic discourse can illuminate those particular cultural–political spaces in which it took root and interpreted clinical and cultural phenomena. The essays in this volume demonstrate the consistent way the psychoanalytic culture responds to and reflects the social and political culture of its host countries. American psychiatrists, British writers, members of the Bloomsbury Circle, French existentialists, and émigrés from Europe to Latin America each developed their own version of psychoanalysis and uniquely contributed to the interpretation and further evolution of the insights and praxis of Sigmund Freud. This volume offers not only a description of the history of the International Psychoanalytical Association in its constituent countries and cultures, but also what our authors are uniquely able to do, the presentation of assessments, interpretations, and evaluations of conscious and unconscious developments based on an intimate “local knowledge” of events. In the words of Clifford Geertz, we have aimed for


a continuous dialectical tacking between the most local of local detail and the most global of global structure in such a way as to bring them into simultaneous view…. Hopping back and forth between the whole conceived through the parts that actualize it and the parts conceived through the whole that motivates them, we seek to turn them, by a sort of intellectual perpetual motion, into explications of one another. [Geertz, 1983, p. 69]


The birthplace of the dynamic unconscious, Freud’s Vienna was situated at the crossroads of several frontiers. The Habsburg Empire stood at the frontier between the Occident and the Orient, between Max Weber’s bureaucratic rationality of Central Europe and the personal rule of Asian potentates. Vienna itself stood at the frontier between the Enlightenment quest for knowledge and righteousness and neo-romanticism, no less than at the frontier of modernity; it pulsated with the conflict between egalitarian social democracy and virulent popular anti-Semitism. Sexuality and eroticism furnished the means by which the prevailing culture of repression and suffocating tradition was fatally undermined by the generation who came of age in the early twentieth century. Socializing analysts sometimes play a fantasy game: Where would you like to have been present as an observer at an historic encounter, to what resonant event would you wish to have listened? German analysts often dream of being present when Goethe and Schiller created, conversed, and administered in Weimar; French analysts think of the Committee of Public Safety during the Revolution; North Americans would wish to have witnessed Freud’s visit in 1909 to Clark University. For contemporary analysts celebrating the IPA centenary, to have been present in 1910 at the founding Congress in Nuremberg and hear Freud deliver his paper on “The Future Prospects of Psycho-Analytic Therapy”, to be part of an audience that included Karl Abraham of Berlin, who spoke on fetishism; Sándor Ferenczi of Budapest, who argued for the necessity of an international organization; Carl Gustav Jung of Zürich, who reported on the previous year’s trip to America; Alfred Adler of Vienna, who presented a paper on psychic hermaphroditism; and Wilhelm Stekel of Vienna, who lectured on dream symbolism, would have been the memorable adventure of a lifetime. Freud’s paper addressed the importance of the analyst’s countertransference:


We have noticed that no psycho-analyst goes further than his own complexes and internal resistances permit; and we consequently require that he shall begin his activity with a self analysis and continually carry it deeper while he is making his observations on his patients. [Freud, 1910d, p. 145]


Freud also spoke about the need for modifications in technique to enhance and strengthen the educational function of the analyst for analysands. He spoke of helping them “by interpreting their unconscious to them until they can make up their minds to do without the protection of their phobia and expose themselves to a now greatly mitigated anxiety” (p. 145). This book examines the interrelatedness and tension in the reception of psychoanalysis with the different surrounds in each land and culture. The Canadian Psychoanalytic Society contains Anglo-phone and Francophone institutes, as Canada is bilingual and federal. The psychoanalytic institutions of Switzerland alternate the presidency between a German- and French-speaking analyst, just as the presidency of the Federal Council is rotated annually in the Swiss Confederation. Today the IPA recognizes three psychoanalytic training models, each of which is evident in these essays. The Eitingon model, originating in Central Europe and now in use throughout the world, is a tripartite structure. Sigmund Freud outlined the essentials of this training model in 1926:


The candidates themselves are taken into analysis, receive theoretical instruction by lectures on all subjects that are important for them, and enjoy the supervision of older and more experienced analysts when they are allowed to make their first trials with comparatively slight cases…. What is still needed must be acquired by practice and by an exchange of ideas in the psycho-analytical societies in which young and old members meet together. Preparation for analytic activity is by no means so easy and simple. The work is hard, the responsibility is great. But anyone who has passed through such a course of instruction, who has been analysed himself, who has mastered what can be taught today of the psychology of the unconscious, who is at home in the science of sexual life, who has learned the delicate technique of psycho-analysis, the art of interpretation, of fighting resistances and of handling the transference—anyone who has accomplished all this is no longer a layman in the field of psycho-analysis. He is capable of undertaking the treatment of neurotic disorders, and will be able in time to achieve in that field whatever can be required from this form of therapy. [Freud, 1926e, p. 228]

The French system separates training analyses from institute seminars. The decentralized Uruguayan model is especially worthy of our interest because of its unique adaptation to the political culture of Uruguay. The Uruguayan Psychoanalytical Association has a collective leadership, as does the polity of the country. Uruguayan psychoanalysis is decentralized, separating the personal analysis of the candidate from institutional considerations through the policy of non-intervention of the analyst in the Association at any point in the training process. Entrance into the Association does not depend on the candidate’s analyst. The aspiring student is evaluated for the Association through an interview by a commission which is set up for that purpose. Teaching takes place in Working Groups, each of which researches theoretical questions in order to gain a deeper understanding of the practical and organizational problems inherent to its specific area. The expectation is that each such group will design its own area of research, admit and train new members, exchange experiences, and maintain coherence as individuals work together in the group. We quote a passage from the chapter in this book by two members highlighting the specific anti-institutional convictions of the Uruguayan psychoanalysts:


The UPA Institute does not exist in harmonic relation with psychoanalysis. The existence of the Institute does not facilitate the needs of psychoanalysis, even if an analytic association defines itself as psychoanalytic—but not “of psychoanalysts”. Fantasies that exercise strong influence on the atmosphere of the Institute can result in a powerful resistance to the process of psychoanalytic discovery. [Capo & García, chapter 30, this volume, p. 328]


A consistent theme in these essays is how commitment to the values of the IPA transcends national frictions and political difficulties. The IPA has served as an arbiter of local and international disputes, and even conflicts within local psychoanalytic societies due to personal clashes and competing theoretical orientations. For example, at a time when international tension between Greece and Turkey was high, the Hellenic Psycho-Analytical Society, led by Peter Hartocollis, welcomed Turkish candidates for training. As Vamık Volkan, a pioneer of Turkish analysis, writes in this volume, “He was most gracious and opened the way for three Turks to receive their training analysis in Athens, analysing two of them himself” (Volkan, chapter 41, this volume, pp. 430–431). A major function of the IPA is fostering the highest quality of psychoanalytic training in various parts of our shrinking global world. The largest and most successful effort is the Han Groen-Prakken Psychoanalytic Institute for Eastern Europe (PIEE), which has nurtured the revival of psychoanalytic culture in the former Soviet bloc: Russia, Estonia, Latvia, Moldova, Ukraine, Belarus, Georgia, Armenia, Kazakhstan, Bulgaria, Rumania, Czech Republic, Slovakia, Serbia, Croatia, and Poland. Currently, China is the focus of a major IPA New Groups effort. For decades German, Norwegian, American, Argentine, British, and Australian IPA analysts have been engaged with developing and teaching psychodynamic psychotherapy in China. Building on this foundation, in 2007 the IPA launched psychoanalytic training in Beijing, and in October 2010 the IPA sponsored an IPA Asian Centennial Congress in Beijing. In 2011 the IPA is initiating psychoanalytic training in Shanghai. This volume is a proud record of our struggles, evolution, and growth in the past century and a marker for the continued development of our psychoanalytic heritage for the next century. Bibliography Freud, S. (1910d). The future prospects of psycho-analytic therapy. Standard Edition, 11: 139–151. Freud, S. (1926e). The Question of Lay Analysis. Standard Edition, 20: 177. Geertz, C. (1983). Local Knowledge: Further Essays in Interpretive Anthropology. New York: Basic Books.







4Germany The history of psychoanalysis in Germany up to 1950 and its relationship to the IPA

Ludger M. Hermanns

The history of psychoanalysis in Germany is not a story of continuous growth and stable development: rather, the founding phase and the subsequent flourishing period in the 1920s is followed by ruptures, discontinuities, persecution, professional conformity, and self-dissolution, during the Nazi period. Thus 1945 marked the starting point of the reconstruction of psychoanalysis in Germany and the attempt to recreate the almost vanished tradition of psychoanalysis as it had existed before 1933. For Anna Freud, in 1970, five decades after the founding of the Berlin Psychoanalytical Institute, it was “especially satisfying to know that the birth, rise, and demise of the Institute are now followed by a rebirth” (A. Freud, 1970). At the same time, the history of psychoanalysis in Germany was at several turning points extremely closely tied to the politics of the International Psychoanalytical Association, more so than to any of its constituent organizations—a close relationship that is described in this chapter. At the Burghölzli in Zurich, Karl Abraham, the first German psychoanalyst, working under C. G. Jung and Eugen Bleuler, had become familiar with their treatment of psychoses and, through them, had come to know Freud’s work. Towards the end of 1907, Abraham had his own practice as a neurologist and an expert witness at court in Berlin; but he soon became one of the first professional analysts after Freud. Beginning in August 1908, he gathered together a group of Freud’s adherents, and by the end of March 1910 this group constituted the first local branch of the newly founded IPA. Scientifically, up to the end of the First World War, psychoanalysis remained mostly Abraham’s domain. His research on psychosis, in particular, explored the uncharted territory of pre-oedipal psycho-dynamics, but he was unable to conquer “the promised land of psychiatry” for Freud. Instead, psychoanalysis enjoyed success, especially in Berlin and Munich, among expressionist artists and writers. In consequence of the “apostasy” of Adler, Stekel, and Jung, in the early years of the IPA its local groups were preoccupied with their internal consolidation. In comparison with Vienna and Zurich, few of the members quit the “Berlin Psychoanalytical Association”. When it seemed that the first IPA president, C. G. Jung, would not voluntarily resign his office despite his theoretical “deviations”, Freud wanted to bring Abraham and the Berlin group into his camp. The organization of three IPA congresses on German soil—in Nuremberg (1910), Weimar (1911), and Munich (1913) (a fourth in Dresden in 1914 fell victim to the World War)—documents the dedication of the German group. After Jung’s resignation in 1914, Abraham assumed the acting chairmanship of the IPA and kept it throughout the war. He also belonged to the “Secret Committee” whose members formed an informal leadership structure in the psychoanalytic movement that would one day be Freud’s collective successor. Edith Kurzweil, in her history comparing psychoanalysis in different countries, made the claim that, consciously or unconsciously, each country gives rise to the form of psychoanalysis it needs (Kurzweil, 1989). After 1918, it became clear what kind of psychoanalysis war-buffeted Germany needed following its defeat and the abdication of the Emperor: a “psychoanalysis of the masses” (Simmel, 1919). The impoverished strata of society, profoundly affected by the war’s consequences, faced the new era without any orientation and were neurotically decompensated. It is for this reason that at the 1918 Budapest Congress Freud’s demand for free treatment for the poor (Freud, 1919a) was forcefully taken up in Berlin. After a financial donation from its wealthy member, Max Eitingon, in February 1920, a polyclinic was established for this purpose. There, the social application of psychoanalysis for broader segments of the population (than could be treated in private practice) was carried out daily. Psychoanalysis in Germany remained true to this task of providing for the population, even throughout the years of National Socialist dictatorship and particularly after the latter’s military defeat by the Allies. Fulfilling this task, however, required an adequate number of competent analysts, for whom a training programme had to be created at the polyclinic. Step by step, its components took shape—training analysis, theoretical seminars, and—a Berlin innovation—control analysis: that is, an experienced colleague’s supervision of the candidate’s first treatments. The Institute Director, Eitingon, was simultaneously Chairman of the International Training Commission (see Schröter, chapter 35, this volume), and so this tripartite model, tested in practice in Berlin, became the obligatory, internationally mandated basis of training guidelines. That this training model strove to ensure a thorough, well-structured training and to bestow the seal of approval only on those psychoanalysts who had gone through “the whole training” can be regarded as another characteristic of psychoanalysis in Germany. The position of “training analyst” was created especially for Hanns Sachs, Freud’s associate, who was brought from Vienna to fill the post. Many individuals from abroad came to the Berlin Psychoanalytical Association because of its curriculum and, upon returning to their home country, implemented this model. Among them are such prominent figures as Bertram Lewin, Ives Hendrick, Abram Kar-diner, Gregory Zilboorg, James and Edward Glover, Sylvia Payne, Alix Strachey, Heinz Hartmann, Helene Deutsch, Marianne Kris, Medard Boss, and Michael Balint (see the extensive list in Hermanns, May, & Müller, 2007, p. 10) Despite criticism—for example, from Bernfeld, who decades later reproached the model as being overly structured in an all-too-Prussian fashion and thought it was detrimental to creativity (Bernfeld, 1962)—the Berlin training, under the rubric of the “Eitingon model”, has lasted until today, Following Freud’s linkage of treatment and research, the records of patients of the Berlin Polyclinic were statistically evaluated. Fenichel used them as the basis for his various tenets on neurosis, and the clinic’s staff members and lecturers scientifically processed them in clinical and theoretical investigations. Ulrike May recently advanced the hypothesis “that one could speak of a thorough-going Berlin ego psychology, that is, of Berlin contributions to psychoanalytic theory oriented towards Freud’s structural model” (May, 2008, p. 18). In her view, the terms “structural model” and “structural aspect” had already been coined by Alexander, Radó, and Fenichel around 1925; the ego psychology in Freud’s latest works (Freud, 1920g, 1923b) was also applied and differentiated long before Heinz Hartmann and Anna Freud. She argues that Fenichel, in particular, had produced a unique synthesis of Freud’s structural theory and Abraham’s emphasis on pre-oedipal development (May, 2008, p. 17). Ever vigilant for deviations in psychoanalytic teachings, Abraham occasionally came into conflict with Freud and was criticized by him, when, for example, he questioned Otto Rank’s theory of birth trauma and the technical modifications he and Ferenczi advocated (cf. Falzeder & Hermanns, 2009). As long as Abraham was firmly in control of the Berlin Association—which was not called the “Deutsche Psychoanalytische Gesellschaft” (DPG) [German Psychoanalytical Society (GPS)] until 1926—the Society and the Institute were stable organizations in which Max Eitingon, Ernst Simmel, and Hanns Sachs held crucial positions in the polyclinic and the training institute. After Abraham’s early death in 1925 and Eitingon’s declining to be his successor, the old unity was over, and the GPS was fractured by the competition between individuals with divergent interests. Ideological worldview debates (Dahmer, 1973; May, 2008, p. 20; Schröter, 2009) and political factional struggles came to characterize psychoanalysis in the Weimar Republic. Beginning in 1924, a so-called “children seminar” was organized outside the Institute, with Otto Fenichel and Harald Schultz-Hencke as outstanding protagonists. While some participated in the “Physician and Counsellor” group or in the general practitioners’ psychotherapy movement, others oriented themselves towards the class interests of the workers’ movement and joined the “Association of Socialist Physicians” or formed a Marxist faction around Wilhelm Reich and Otto Fenichel. In exile members of this group were recipients of Fenichel’s legendary “circular letters” (Fenichel, 1998). Psychotherapeutic care or healing was central in Georg Grod-deck’s sanatorium in Baden-Baden, in Frieda Fromm-Reichmann’s so-called “Thorapeutikum” in Heidelberg, and at Ernst Simmel’s psychoanalytic clinic Schloß Tegel. In 1930 the founding of the Psychoanalytical Institute of Frankfurt at the university as a guest of the Institute for Social Research indicated another direction: the promotion of the application of psychoanalysis in the humanities (Brecht, Friedrich, Hermanns, Kaminer, & Juelich, 1993, pp. 26ff). It was there, in 1930, that Freud—and hence psychoanalysis—received the highest recognition German society can award, the Goethe Prize of the city of Frankfurt. This is a cultural award previously given to Stefan George and Albert Schweitzer and not in the area of psychotherapy or academic psychiatry, where psychoanalytic influences were noticeable at most in the sense of a gradual “pénétration pacifique” (Hirschmüller & Kimmig, 1996). The Berlin analysts had been united in the struggle to gain ground against psychiatrists and psychotherapists and had even attempted a “bit of psychoanalytical treatment of public opinion itself” (Simmel, 1930, p. 12, quoted by Turnheim, 1993, p. 131). But the Depression and increasing political radicalization increasingly led to fissures among the colleagues. After Abraham’s early death, his student Melanie Klein was invited to London by Jones, and the intellectual leaders of the Institute—Alexander, Radó, Sachs, and Horney—accepted offers to lead training programmes at institutes in the United States years before the Nazis seized power in Germany. It remains an open question whether their decision to leave the Berlin Institute had to do with the “increasing political tension” that Hilda Abraham reported, which led to an “inner tension of anxiety … that, possibly initially unconsciously, impeded collaboration between Christian and Jewish psychoanalysts” (Abraham, 1971, p. 24). In 1932, at the Wiesbaden Congress—the last psychoanalytic congress in Germany for more than 50 years—the writing was already on the wall. Six months later all Jewish analysts and those politically on the left were endangered by the omnipresent anti-Semitism and anti-Communism of Hitler’s National Socialist Party, which also contributed to the creeping demise of the leadership structures at the Institute and Society. Politically persecuted analysts were the first to have to flee; other Jewish members at first lost their leading positions, then their permission to treat patients and their teaching duties at the Institute, and, finally, their membership. As a result, the “Aryans” Felix Boehm and Carl Müller-Braunschweig, long-time second-string members, supplanted the previous Executive Board members Eitingon, Simmel, and Fenichel and led the psychoanalytic group during the 12 years of the “Third Reich”. Boehm as Chairman repeatedly sought direct contact with Sigmund and Anna Freud and with the IPA Chairman Ernest Jones, with whom he had several consultations in Germany, Holland, and Switzerland. Initially persecution focused primarily on Hitler’s political opponents, and therefore Wilhelm Reich, Ernst Simmel, and Otto Fenichel had to flee the country, but gradually all Jewish colleagues were hindered in and ultimately completely prevented from practising their profession. This process lasted for several years and created a deep gulf between the Jewish majority and the Christian minority. Freud and Jones were primarily interested in preserving psychoanalysis in Germany, even if Jews could no longer practise it. In point of fact, Boehm and Müller-Braunschweig did everything they could to maintain the Institute and the Society; the price they paid was to negotiate with functionaries of the Nazi Party and the government administration and to write memoranda, petitions, and magazine articles to present psychoanalysis as being useful to the system and the new challenges of the times. From today’s standpoint, the tone and the content of the resulting pamphlets are difficult to tolerate because they can be viewed as acts of collaboration with the Nazi system in their willingness to curry favour and their anti-Semitic colouration. On the other hand, their position vis-à-vis the Nazis was compatible with the position of the IPA Chairman Ernest Jones, who wanted to preserve psychoanalysis in Germany at whatever price, if only to create a core that could become a starting point for a new generation of psychoanalysts after Hitler’s defeat. This “path of compromise with the new power-holders” was anathema to the Institute’s founder, Max Eitingon, and could not be trodden by him. He regarded the Palestinian Institute for Psychoanalysis, which he founded in 1934, as the legitimate successor to the decimated and corrupt Berlin Institute (Schröter, 2009, p. 1120). In October 1935, when the GPS member Edith Jacobson was arrested and sentenced to prison for supporting a socialist resistance group, Boehm suppressed international declarations of solidarity in order to avoid endangering the existence of the GPS (May & Mühlleitner, 2005). The final step in this direction, and the historical nadir of the GPS, was the membership meeting on 1 December 1935, at which Jones was present, when all the Jewish members remaining after the first great wave of emigration in 1933/34 were forced to leave the GPS. Twenty members resigned, and whereas in 1932 there had been 56 members, now only 14 remained, including two lecturers. Henceforth the GPS led a rump existence. At the beginning of 1936, when no more teaching concessions were granted to the Psychoanalytical Institute by the health authorities, Boehm conferred with Anna Freud in Brünn on the next steps to be taken. Afterwards, she reported Boehm’s lucid self-observation: “The worst thing about life there is that today it already seems natural to one, while last year one’s hair would have stood on end” (letter from Anna Freud to Ernest Jones, 10 March 1936, quoted after Lockot, 1994, p. 47). In this predicament, in April 1936 the Berlin Psychoanalytical Institute merged with other psychotherapy schools in the “German Institute for Psychological Research and Psychotherapy”, under the directorship of Prof. M. H. Göring. The humiliating dependency of the GPS was deepened by its subsequent declaration, withdrawing from the IPA. When the responsible Nazi Party officials were told about the GPS’s withdrawal, it was forcefully instructed to correct this hasty decision:


Precisely because it has moved into opposition to Freud’s teachings, the German group must under all circumstances remain in the International Psychoanalytical Association. Otherwise it will have no opportunity anywhere to refute Freud’s teachings before an international forum and thereby to stand up to the Jewish spirit of psychoanalysis. We must not avoid the possibilities of struggle; on the contrary, we must seize every opportunity to face the opponent. [Archives of the German Psychoanalytical Society, K4–2]


And indeed the GPS thereafter attempted to rescind its withdrawal (19 September 1936), though its announcement had already appeared in the IPA newsletter. The ensuing acts of adaptation and submission cannot be individually traced here; they are documented in Lockot (1985), Brecht et al. (1993), May (2000), and in the newly evaluated files from the Eitingon Archive and the recently discovered Boehm depository in the GPS Archive (cf. Schröter, 2009). The leading analysts of the wartime GPS—Boehm, Müller-Braun-schweig, Kemper, Schultz-Hencke, and Kalau vom Hofe—held positions of responsibility in the Göring Institute. They contributed to developing the concept of a “new German study of the mind”. Beyond treating patients, they gave courses and lectures and trained the small group of remaining young students. Müller-Braunschweig’s letter of 14 July 1937 to Bibring, the editor of the Internationale Zeitschrift für Psychoanalyse in Vienna, illustrates the spirit with which these things were done:


It would be pleasing if, on the other hand, psychoanalytic circles would realize more than up to now that the preservation of a psychoanalytic centre in Germany is not only a matter for the German analysts, but that one would serve the idea of psychoanalytic research in general by letting interested parties know that psychoanalytic science is still being carried out in Germany and that one can still be trained as a proper psychoanalyst in Germany. [Müller-Braunschweig, 1937]


Müller-Braunschweig seriously believed that candidates from abroad would apply for training at the “rump” GPS and did not appear to grasp that psychoanalytic science practised in this environment was fatally compromised. Less than a year later, a true psychoanalytic centre in Central Europe, the Wiener Psychoanalytische Vereinigung [Vienna Psychoanalytical Association(WPV)], vanished when Hitler’s Germany annexed Austria, and Freud and almost all Viennese analysts emigrated. An attempt launched from Berlin by Müller-Braunschweig to rescue the publishing house and the organization (details in Rothländer, 2008) failed, and the GPS was forced to dissolve. For the next seven years the psychoanalysts of the GPS led only a shadow existence as Working Group A at Göring’s Institute in Berlin. One of its members, the head of the polyclinic John Rittmeister, joined an anti-fascist resistance group and was arrested in September 1942. He was sentenced to death in February 1943, and executed by guillotine in Berlin’s Plötzensee prison on 13 May 1943 (Hermanns, in Brecht et al., 1993, pp. 186ff) After Rittmeister’s murder, all members of the Institute were urgently enjoined to denounce patients who were opponents of the regime. As well, Working Group A lost its name and only under supervision in the Institute’s rooms allowed to hold its “lecture evenings for casuistics and therapy”. Some analysts retained their directing and research positions, whereas Müller-Braunschweig was forbidden to teach or publish. The majority of the members concealed their clinical work and attempted to continue to do psychoanalysis as best they could, if they were not drafted into military service as doctors or required to perform other work (Dräger, 1972). Germany’s capitulation on 8 May 1945 produced a déjà vu of the social and political situation shortly before the end of the First World War, when the Budapest Congress had taken place in 1918. A few days later, the remaining psychoanalytic functionaries of the Göring Institute, especially two dynamic representatives of the younger generation, Werner Kemper and Harald Schultz-Hencke, installed themselves as leaders in an informal working group and, with tremendous effort, prepared to provide Berlin’s population with comprehensive psychotherapeutic care, founded various institutions, contacted health insurance companies, and began setting up an institute for psychotherapeutic treatment; similar activity took place in Munich and later in other cities as well. The dire psychological straits of the populace led to the rapid establishment of a psychotherapeutic job description that bridged the various schools; it generously included “lay persons” (the foundations for this had already been laid at the Göring Institute). By negotiating funding from the health insurance companies it made analysts dependent on referrals from the “Insurance Institute”. These socio-political efforts thereby also served to secure “the foundations of a livelihood” (Kemper, see Lockot, 1994, p. 262) for the psychotherapists who, after “de-Nazification”, were able to work again in their old profession. Some of them also taught future colleagues at the eclectic “Institute for Psychotherapy”, founded two years later as a successor to the Göring Institute. What remained of psychoanalysis in Germany? It reappeared as an institution as early as in the autumn of 1945 as the newly re-founded GPS with its old charter of 1931. Did this mean that the minimum goal that Anna Freud had outlined in a letter on 10 March 1936 to E. Jones of the continued survival of a “rump German Psychoanalytical Society” had been realized? Then she wrote: “If he [Boehm] rescues a small working group into another era, then that is good” (after Lockot, 1994, p. 47). Restoration of contact with the former colleagues in their countries of emigration proceeded laboriously; not every case of personal contact via letters and care packages was as successful as that between Gerhart Scheunert and his earlier training analyst, Therese Benedek, now settled in Chicago (May, 2000), or that between Müller-Braunschweig and his student Edith Weigert, in Chevy Chase, Maryland (Holmes, 2007). The English colleague John Rickman’s reports on his interviews with five important members of the GPS (including both of its Chairmen, Müller-Braunschweig and Boehm) found reason to be dubious as to whether the two chairmen had been able to preserve both their psychoanalytic identity and their moral integrity (King, 1989; Lockot, 1994, pp. 188–202; Brecht et al., 1993, pp. 234–239). We learn, for example, from Boehm’s memo dated 7 August 1945 that he had always suffered from the preponderance of Jews in the old GPS in the 1920s (Lockot, 1994, p. 338). It is hardly astonishing then that no émigré psychoanalyst was willing to return to Berlin—quite apart from the unattractive living conditions in early post-war Germany. Aside from attempts by analysts who had remained in Germany to re-establish old professional contacts with their émigré colleagues, the IPA entered the picture again. Its president, Jones, would have preferred to let the Germans’ membership continue without comment, thereby casting a mantle of silence over its earlier withdrawal from the IPA under pressure and the problematical development in the GPS (Lockot, 1994, p. 211). In line with this, Anna Freud called on Central Secretary Müller-Braunschweig to pay back the membership dues that were in arrears since 1939. Additionally, she had written to him on 31 May 1946, “Jones and I, who have been familiar with the circumstances in Germany, know how much everyone who lived there was a victim of these circumstances and that it would be senseless to let that lead to difficulties for further work” (Lockot, 1994, 181). But this time businesslike dealings with the wondrously resurrected GPS did not work out, for two reasons: first, the scientific and human conflict between the protagonists of Schultz-Hencke’s neo-analysis and Müller-Braun-schweig’s defence of classical analysis sharpened intensely. There ensued an uproar at the first post-war IPA Congress in Zurich in 1949, in front of the members and officers from all over the world, who were gathered together for the first time in years. Müller-Braunschweig unexpectedly held a lecture on the same topic as a lecture announced by Schultz-Hencke, thereby provoking the latter’s anger. Second, after a controversial discussion in the business meeting, a majority rejected the readmission of the GPS to the IPA and stipulated that future admission would depend on the GPS expelling Schultz-Hencke and establishing its own independent training institute. Jones, as President, had not taken the émigrés into account in his efforts to reintegrate the GPS into the IPA. In the following two years, Jones’s earlier prediction of 13 March 1936 (letter to Anna Freud after Boehm had announced the impending withdrawal from the IPA) proved to be true:


When better days come we shall in any case find that there is a nucleus left over from which we can again grow, and I hope we shall be wise enough when that day comes to start with a small nucleus and realise how much purification will be needed in the region around it. [Lockot, 1994, p. 48]

In June 1950, Müller-Braunschweig, understanding the situation correctly, secretly founded, with his adherents, the “Deutsche Psychoanalytische Vereinigung (DPV)” [German Psychoanalytical Association (GPA)]. On 3 December 1950 he and his little group left the general assembly of the GPS, whose chairman he had been up to that point. He had failed to persuade Schultz-Hencke to resign from the GPS and thought that psychoanalysis could not be adequately represented in the “Institute for Psychotherapy”. All of these steps were taken in agreement with the new IPA President, Leo Bartemeier, ensuring that the acceptance of the newly founded German Psychoanalytical Association and the simultaneous rejection of the GPS were mere matters of form at the next congress. The IPA was thus not only the presiding godmother of the founding of the GPA; it had also from behind the scenes commissioned and participated in its creation. Over the last 25 years, German psychoanalysts have conducted a passionate debate on how the schism—that is, the founding of the IPA-sanctioned German Psychoanalytical Association—should be understood psychologically, politically, and in terms of group dynamics based on the documents presented by Lockot (1985, 1994) and Brecht et al. (1993). Was it merely a clever move by the old strategist Carl Müller-Braunschweig, who, after meticulously following the IPA’s Zurich requirements, was immediately rewarded at the next congress in Amsterdam by having his newly founded GPA accepted? Did Müller-Braunschweig, as various interpreters have suspected (Beese, 1987; Dührssen, 1994; Lockot, 1995; Ermann, 1996), thereby avoid drawing attention to his opportunistic behaviour during the Nazi period by shifting the IPA’s focus to one of theoretical controversy and combating the revisionist tendency in Schultz-Hencke that Freud had already remarked on? Had a schism in fact occurred much earlier, with the dissolution of the original GPS and the emergence of a psychotherapeutic movement in the Third Reich? Could the founding of the GPA be seen as an attempt at repairing it (Eickhoff, 1995; Hermanns, 2008, p. 20)? Was there an “illusionary” pattern of group identity in the GPA’s founding myth? “Thus the heroic separation from the all-powerful Schultz-Hencke group made it possible to shake off eclectic psychoanalytical schools, re-establish a connection to the old Berlin Psychoanalytical Institute, and thereby recover the original psychoanalytical identity intact …” (Beland, 2007; Hermanns, 2008, p. 19). After Amsterdam, the little GPA began laborious efforts lasting many years to integrate itself into the IPA, described in the following contribution by Werner Bohleber (chapter 5). In the minutes of a GPS business meeting on 28 February 1950, Boehm wrote: “Boehm does not want to belong to any Society which is under such pressure. He is resigning before he is forced out, because resentment against the Germans will last another half-century” (Brecht et al., 1993, p. 210). That, and why in fact it took so long for the GPS to successfully gain reacceptance in the IPA, is another story. Bibliography Abraham, H. (1971). 10The Netherlands The Dutch psychoanalytic movement and the IPA

Eddy de Klerk & Harry Stroeken


Early beginnings The Dutch Psychoanalytical Society was founded on 24 March 1917, as the seventh member Society of the International Psychoanalytical Association (Groen-Prakken & De Nobel, 1992, p. 225; Stroeken, 1997, pp. 26–27). Freud sent his congratulations and best wishes for fruitful endeavours. How to characterize the founding members of the Society? They were 13 in number, all men, all medical doctors, most of them specialists in psychiatry. Socially, the psychoanalysts belonged to the upper reaches of society. They stood for social change, but within limits. Strong roots in the medical profession were a valuable asset in the struggle to attract income and clients. The birth of the Society was not a sudden development. It had been preceded by several events, notably the first International Congress on Psychiatry and Neurology, Psychology and the Nursing of the Insane, held in Amsterdam in September 1907. At this congress Jung read an extensive paper on Freudian theory and practice, which received a great deal of publicity and caused a huge stir among psychiatrists and laymen alike. In fact, some books and articles by Freud had already been translated into Dutch.1 Then in 1914 G. Jelgersma [1859–1942], a professor of psychiatry, gave his famous rectorial address for the Dies Natalis of the University of Leiden, which spoke about the unconscious and Freud’s theory of dreams. For many people in the Netherlands, this was the first time they had heard of Freud. Freud was quite pleased with this address (Bulhof, 1981, 1982, 1983). Starting in 1913, a number of Dutch psychoanalysts in contact with Freud held informal gatherings at the home of A. Van der Chijs [1874–1926] in Amsterdam. In “On the History of the Psycho-Analytic Movement” (1914d), Freud mentions J. E. G. van Emden [1868–1950], J. M. van Ophuijsen [1882–1950], A. W. van Renterghem [1845–1939], A. Stärcke [1880–1954], and J. Stärcke [1882–1917]. Van Emden underwent an analysis with Freud, comprised of a total of 245 sessions spread over several periods (May, 2008). The first paper presented to the newly founded Dutch Psychoanalytical Society was by J. H. van der Hoop [1887–1950] and published in the Nederlands Tijdschrift voor Geneeskunde [Dutch Review of Medicine]. It gave rise to considerable debate inside and outside the Society. The members considered the paper to be too Jungian, and Jung’s disagreements with Freud split the membership. Only 6 of the 13 founding members had undergone a training analysis, and 5 of them had done so with Jung; 2 of the 5—Van Ophuijsen and Van der Hoop (S. J. R. de Monchy [1893–1969] as well, in 1936)—later did some more personal training in Freudian circles in Berlin and Vienna. Van der Hoop would never shake off his Jungian stigma: because of it, he remained suspect all his life.


Developments and conflicts in the first thirty years Very early in its history the Society experienced a high point: in September 1920, the 6th IPA Congress was held in The Hague, which was then the centre of psychoanalytic life in the Netherlands. As the Netherlands had been neutral during the First World War, former enemies could meet there; and it was prosperous enough to stage the event. Both Jelgersma and August Stärcke read papers: Jelgersma on “The Theory of Emotions” [des Gefühls] and Stärcke on “The Castration Complex” and on “The Relations between Neurosis and Psychosis” (Anonymous, 1920). It was the only time that Jelgersma met Freud; he wanted to safeguard his independence. The main organizer was Johan van Ophuijsen; his wife received Freud, Anna Freud, and a number of others in their house for dinner. The Society’s membership grew slowly during its first thirty years. In 1931 the Society had 21 members and 5 extraordinary members; by the end of the Second World War in 1946 there were 33 members and 6 associate members. Following the war, there was a steady and increasing growth in the Society’s membership. From its founding, two subjects caused conflict within the Society. The first was the issue of lay analysis. The majority wished to keep psychoanalysis within the framework of psychiatry. They also opposed the obligatory training analysis: this was the second issue of controversy. Jelgersma thought that it would only attract psychopaths, that people would become too attached to their training analyst, and that it would form a barrier to the membership of valuable but non-analysed psychiatrists. There were widely divergent opinions on what the Society ought to be: an organization for well-trained and practising psychoanalysts, according to a minority, or a Society of psychiatrists with an interest in psychoanalysis, according to the majority. The requirements of the IPA prescribed lay analysis and training analysis in 1925. The arrival in the Netherlands after Hitler took power in January 1933 of four German Jewish psychoanalysts—Th. Reik [1888–1969], K. Landauer [1887–1945], who died in Bergen Belsen, Max Levy-Sühl, who died in September 1947, and A. Waterman, who died in a German concentration camp—aggravated these smouldering conflicts and made them acute. Should these men become full members of the Dutch Society? Reik was not a medical doctor, and Dutch law did not recognize the other three as doctors either. These difficulties had a financial dimension, due to the fact that the world was in the midst of an economic crisis: some feared that if immigrants were to start practising in the Netherlands, it would take patients away from their Dutch colleagues. Van Ophuijsen—who held an executive function in the IPA for almost ten years—saw a great opportunity in the arrival of the German colleagues: he wanted to revitalize his analytic institute in The Hague, which had fallen into decline, by making it the exclusive training centre. Karl Landauer seemed particularly well suited as a training analyst. Van Ophuijsen, president of the Society, tried to force the issue, against the advice of Ernest Jones. A majority of the Society signed a motion of no confidence against him in September 1933; in response, Van Ophuijsen left the Society and founded a new one: the Society of Psychoanalysts in the Netherlands. This tiny, newly established Society pursued the course set out by the IPA and was immediately recognized by it. Apparently there was no objective professional reason for this split: it was personal tensions that played a major part. A committee was appointed to bring about reunification, and unity was restored in 1938. But there were significant losses in the intervening period. Van Ophuijsen left the country for the United States in 1934 (Stroeken, 2009). Westerman Holstijn [1891–1980]—one of the main opponents of van Ophuijsen as well as his former analysand—left the Society altogether, fed up with all the fighting, even though he had been an active participant.


German occupation and the founding of the Dutch Psychoanalytical Association During the war the Amsterdam Psychoanalytic Working Group—the Amsterdam chapter of the Society—was more important than the Society as a whole. Until then, the Society had mainly been concentrated around The Hague and Leiden. During and after the war the centre shifted towards Amsterdam. Officially this working group had dissolved itself when the German occupying forces demanded that the Jewish members leave the Society, but in practice it continued to work very hard. Jeanne Lampl-de Groot [1889–1987] and her husband Hans Lampl [1889–1958] had settled in the Netherlands in 1938, following the Anschluss of Austria. The Amsterdam Psychoanalytic Working Group set up the field of psychoanalysis in accordance with the regulations of the IPA, just as a few had wanted in the 1930s. The measures necessary for this were adopted on 19 April 1943 at a meeting of the working group held in the Lampls’ home. On the same occasion it was decided to have only two training analysts, Lampl-de Groot and R. Le Coultre [1897–1987]. After the war, the Working Group took over the Society completely, and the requirements of the IPA came to apply to the Society as a whole. Not everyone was pleased with the actions of the Amsterdam Working Group. J. H. van der Hoop was deeply aggrieved by his exclusion as a training analyst. He was a well-known Dutch psychiatrist who held important positions in the world of mental health, among them assistant professor at the University of Amsterdam, where he lectured on psychoanalysis. A founding father of the Society in 1917, he had been doing training analyses for decades and was suddenly no longer allowed to do so. He lodged a protest, in vain, against the decision of the working group. A. J. Westerman Holstijn had left the Society a few years before the German occupation. He was opposed to the “moguls” who headed the Society. Van der Hoop and Westerman Holstijn—altogether very different personalities—started their own psychoanalytic training. Their attempt to work together with the Society was unsuccessful, and their group developed into the Dutch Psychoanalytical Association. Although officially founded on 1 January 1947, its activities can be traced back to 1945.


After the Second World War After the war the Dutch Psychoanalytical Society enjoyed several decades of great prosperity. The Dutch Psychoanalytical Association did well too, although it needed many years to establish itself. The relationship between the Society and the Association was very troubled for years. The Association was blamed for everything that had gone wrong during the 1930s. The Society regarded itself as the continuation of everything that had gone right, even though most of its members had originally been against lay analysis and training analysis and had not viewed the inclusion of the German immigrants as a thing to be taken for granted. In 1946 the Society founded the Psycho-Analytic Institute in Amsterdam. In 1947 Jeanne Lampl-de Groot, P. J. Van der Leeuw [1909–85], an analysand of Jeanne Lampl-de Groot, and H. G. van der Waals2 [1894–1974] organized a conference of European psychoanalysts in Amsterdam. Ernest Jones presided over this European conference, which was an attempt at reconciliation and cooperation, drawing more than 100 participants from 11 countries. He said, and afterwards wrote, that the Dutch Society was very likely the most active one on the continent. Van der Leeuw helped to organize the Mitteleuropäische Arbeitstagung [Central European Working Group] in 1958,3 which became a bi-annual event for German-speaking countries. In 1986, a new journal—Zeitschrift für psychonalytische Theorie und Praxis [Journal of psychoanalytic theory and practice]—resulted from these encounters. Dutch analysts helped to revive psychoanalysis in Germany.4 Van der Leeuw also helped the Swiss Raymond de Saussure to found the European Psychoanalytical Federation (EPF) in 1966. By far the most influential Dutch psychoanalyst after the Second World War was Jeanne Lampl-de Groot. She had been in analysis with Freud and became an intimate friend of the Freud family, especially of Anna. She served as vice-president of the IPA in 1947, and was honorary vice-president from 1965 till her death in 1987. Shortly after the war, she supported the admission of the new Deutsche Psychoanalytische Verein to the IPA and the rejection of the old Deutsche Psychoanalytische Gesellschaft. In 1953, as a member of an IPA enquiring committee, she was against the recognition of the newly founded Société Française de Psychanalyse, of which Lagache and Lacan were the principal leaders. It was the beginning of a ten-year process that finally led to Lacan’s expulsion.5 Jeanne Lampl-de Groot and her circle presided over what some have called “The Golden Age of Dutch Psychoanalysis”, from 1960 to 1980. The Society grew rapidly, and there was a great deal of international exchange. Many analysts held professorships in psychiatry, psychology, psychotherapy, and sexology, resulting in frequent publications. But in the long run, her lifelong influence on the Society, supported by her IPA status, formed a limitation to the development of psychoanalytic thought in the Society. Because of her close ties to Anna Freud and the Hampstead Clinic, the atmosphere was definitely anti-Kleinian; Lacan was unknown. In contrast, many psychoanalysts within the Association were familiar with Klein and Lacan. However, the Association was not recognized by the IPA, because the rules then in force demanded the assent of the Society, which it refused in the late 1970s. Only after the IPA had abolished this rule did recognition become possible—in 2001, more than 50 years after the Association’s founding. Going back in time, the 17th IPA Congress in 1951, the second to be held in the Netherlands, took place in Amsterdam, which had replaced The Hague as the centre of psychoanalysis. In 1965 the IPA held a third Congress, its 24th, in the Netherlands, in Amsterdam again. There, Van der Leeuw was elected President of the IPA (1965 to 1969) and M. M. Montessori [1921–93] its secretary; after his term Montessori became vice-president (1969 to 1975). In 1966 the first training in child analysis was set up in Leiden by S. Teuns; in the same year the Department for Children and Adolescents of the Psycho-Analytic Institute in Amsterdam was opened. The Hampstead Clinic in London lent its support by sending instructors for teaching, technical seminars, and supervision. Jeanne Lampl-de Groot, who had worked together with Anna Freud and others in Vienna at a centre for destitute children, stimulated this endeavour. Elisabeth Frijling-Schreuder [1908–2003], another analysand of Jeanne Lampl-de Groot, had been director of a child guidance clinic and became the first professor of child psychiatry in Amsterdam (1965). She was a great source of inspiration for the child analytic movement in the Netherlands. In 1969 the Dutch Society asked the IPA to recognize child analysis as a regular form of analysis; at the same time the Society supported Anna Freud in her fight for recognition of the Hampstead Training Clinic. This proposal was rejected after an emotional discussion in the IPA business meeting (Groen-Prakken & De Nobel, 1992, p. 225). Frijling-Schreuder attributed this rejection to the fact that many child analysts were psychologists, and the Americans did not want lay analysts (Stroeken, 1997, p. 157).


Recent developments Even before the fall of the Iron Curtain, Han Groen-Prakken [1927–2003], then president of the EPF (from 1987 to 1991), had become active in Eastern Europe. Many members of the Society joined her. In 1999 Groen-Prakken started the “Amsterdam School of Psychoanalysis”, a training programme that allowed Eastern Europeans to become direct members of the IPA. It continued for ten years, and during this time many Dutch psychoanalysts provided training, shuttle analysis, and hospitality to their East European colleagues. In a way, the work done in Germany after the Second World War was repeated. The Psychoanalytic Institute for Eastern Europe (PIEE) was named the Han Groen-Prakken Institute. In 1993 the IPA held its Congress, the 38th, in the Netherlands for the fourth time, once again a high point in the existence of the Dutch Society. With the increasing interest in biological psychiatry, the rise of alternative forms of therapy, and cuts in governmental financial support to psychoanalytic treatment, a lively discussion began in the 1990s about the place and the financing of psychoanalysis. The number of candidates who sought psychoanalytic training, either in the Society or in the Association, dwindled. The financial base became fragile. Because the very survival of psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic training was at stake, the Society and the Association sought to work together, with the Dutch Society of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy as a third partner. The latter Society had been established in 1979 as a joint effort between the Society and the Association. The plan was to pool financial and teaching resources for part of the theoretical curriculum. However, at about the same time a conflict erupted within the Dutch Psychoanalytical Society in connection with a remark by a training analyst in the Mededelingenblad [Bulletin] of the Society, which was deemed by many members to be anti-Semitic and in need of retraction, by other members much less so. At the same time, the position of training analyst was separated from that of supervising analyst, and the functions of the training committee were redefined. A small group in the Society, comprising a relatively large proportion of training analysts, dissented and sharply criticized the new joint training programme. Rumours began to reach the IPA board, which thereupon threatened the Society with suspension of the training programme. A visiting IPA committee led by A.-M. Sandler came to the Netherlands (2005) but failed to heal the looming split. This was not surprising since this committee could not be perceived as neutral—not by the Society, because it was under pressure by the IPA’s threat to end its training programme, and not by the dissenting minority, which felt encouraged by the stalemate. Following the visit, in 2005 the minority—about one-tenth of the Society—organized itself into the Dutch Psycho-Analytic Group. After the split, a second IPA committee, consisting of I. Szecsödy and R. Lander, visited the Society (2006) and found its new regulations and training programme perfectly in line with existing IPA standards, with the exception of a few details, which were readily resolved. The Dutch Psycho-Analytic Group was recognized by the IPA in 2007. In retrospect, we might wonder whether the split could have been avoided if the IPA and the Society had first ironed out their apparent differences of opinion. Meanwhile, the Association, only recently (2001) recognized by the IPA, had watched the conflict within the Society and the reaction of the IPA board with growing concern. Shortly thereafter the Association withdrew from the three-way cooperation; it feared that the IPA would disapprove of the joint training. But ambivalence towards the Society, which had for decades opposed the Association’s admission to the IPA, may also have played a role in its withdrawal. The result of these events was ironic: at the beginning of the attempt to work together, two organizations existed in the Netherlands that provided training in psychoanalysis. Now there are three, all of them recognized by the IPA. All three organizations have to date only a handful of new candidates each year, for a total of around ten—sometimes more, sometimes less. Together, the Society, the Association, and the Group have 406 members, including associate members, candidates, and extraordinary members. Dutch psychoanalysts continue to be active internationally. For instance, Duveken Engels has served two terms as treasurer of the EPF, and in that capacity was liaison officer for the Psychoanalytic Institute for Eastern Europe (PIEE), which is sponsored by both the EPF and the IPA. Henk Jan Dalewijk was vice-president of the EPF, its treasurer, and EPF representative to the board of the IPA; he currently (2010) serves as IPA treasurer. Members of the three organizations continue to work together under the umbrella of the Netherlands Psychoanalytic Institute, a merger of the Psycho-Analytic Institute in Amsterdam and the one founded in 1983 by the Association. Unlike similar institutes in other countries, it is incorporated in the government-regulated and -financed field of mental health care. In addition to offering patient care, the Institute has devoted its energies to research into the effectiveness of long-term therapy (i.e., psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic psychotherapy). The government and health insurance companies will only continue their financing if the effectiveness of long-term psychoanalytic treatment is proved beyond doubt. Another area of productive cooperation since 1995 has been the journal Tijdschrift voor Psychoanalyse, which is supported by seven psychoanalytic societies in the Netherlands and Belgium. Cooperation exists also in groups such as Psychoanalyse en Cultuur and Psychoanalyse en Film and the website http://www.psychoanalytischwoordenboek.nl Evidence of the fact that Freud’s thinking continues to be important in the Netherlands is the publication of his complete works in a new Dutch translation entitled Werken (Boom, Amsterdam), which appeared on the occasion of Freud’s 150th birthday in 2006. This edition—the translation as well as all the accompanying registers—compares favourably with the existing German and English editions. In summary, the history of the psychoanalytic movement in the Netherlands in relation to the IPA can be characterized in three ways: impassioned internal controversies reflecting international ones, practical cooperation whenever necessary and possible, and some hard work in the international arena, both in professional and in administrative functions. * * * We note with sadness the premature death of Eddy de Klerk, the co-author of this chapter.
