12Portugal The Portuguese Psychoanalytical Society: historical note

João Seabra Diniz

Egas Moniz [1874–1955], who was awarded the Nobel Prize for Medicine for his work on angiography and cerebral leucotomy, first introduced Freud’s works in Portugal. His first paper on psychoanalysis is dated 1915. It was the initial lesson within the structure of a neurology course, the title of which was “As Bases da Psicanálise” [The fundamentals of psychoanalysis]. Six years later he published an article with the title “O Conflito Sexual” [Sexual conflict], in which we can see Egas Moniz using the analytic method in the analysis of two clinical cases, making use of the couch, free association, and dream analysis. He makes reference to the great results achieved and the use of the analytic method in other cases. He also published some psychoanalytic studies of works of literature. His psychoanalytic publications ceased in 1925. During the pre-history of Portuguese psychoanalysis we should mention the name of Sobral Cid, a professor of psychiatry, who oriented Portuguese psychiatry towards psychopathology. He used to say that his views were very close to the hypothesis inspired by Freud’s psychological postulates and that it was possible to treat the psychoses with psychoanalytic methods. Although the work of these and others that followed were of unquestionable importance, none of them led to a systematic therapeutic approach in the psychoanalytic field. Moreover, the authors had no contact with the representatives of the psychoanalytic movement, which would have been necessary for real psychoanalytic activity in Portugal. Only during the decade of the 1950s was a proper psychoanalytic movement created in Portugal. Even though this kind of activity was not well received by Salazar’s political regime, which was in power at the time, some Portuguese physicians, among them Francisco Alvim and Pedro Luzes, went to Switzerland to be trained as psychoanalysts with the Geneva group led by Raymond de Saussure. They became full members of the Swiss Psychoanalytical Society. In Geneva, the Portuguese analysts made contact with the Spanish analysts P. Boill and P. Folch, who were also in training in the same Society, and the four of them agreed to create the Lusitanian–Spanish Psychoanalytical Society [Sociedad Luso-Española de Psicoanálisis (SLEP)]. At the Paris Congress in 1957, the International Psychoanalytical Association recognized the Lusitanian–Spanish Psychoanalytical Society as a Study Group. Two years later, in 1959, at the Copenhagen Congress, this Study Group was accepted as an IPA Component Society. After this acknowledgment the Portuguese group was enlarged when João dos Santos, a Lisbon physician who had received his analytic training in Paris, joined it. The Lusitanian–Spanish Psychoanalytical Society continued to grow; at first members were analysts whose training had been concluded abroad; eventually, facilities for analysts’ training were gradually established in Portugal and Spain. In 1963, Francisco Alvim was the first analyst to start a clinical practice in Portugal, followed by João dos Santos and Pedro Luzes. João dos Santos had worked in Paris with some members of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society who were practising child psychoanalysis. He became responsible for the introduction and development of the psychoanalytic work with children and adolescents in Portugal. It can be said that he founded the modern Portuguese pedopsychiatry, based on an extensive psychodynamic comprehension of the symptoms and a philosophy of psychotherapeutic intervention. Under the influence of João dos Santos and his followers, mental health services for children, with a multidisciplinary orientation, were created in the three main cities of the country—Lisbon, Oporto, and Coimbra. Due to his activity the training of the professionals in this area was highly influenced by psychoanalytic concepts and the work of psychoanalysts. João dos Santos demonstrated a rich and multi-faceted intervention that significantly contributed to the prestige of psychoanalysis in the cultural scene of our country. In 1966, because of distance and language problems and some disagreements concerning the selection and training of candidates, the Iberian organization split, and the Grupo de Estudos Português [Portuguese Study Group] was created. In 1968, the organization of the 29th “Congrès des Psychanalystes de Langue Française des Pays Romans” was assigned to the Portuguese Group. This congress was held in Lisbon, and Pedro Luzes presented a report, “Les troubles de la pensée en clinique psychanalytique” [Thinking disorders in clinical psychoanalysis], in which, for the first time outside England, the theory of Wilfred Bion was examined in the context of its theoretical and clinical perspectives. The congress was a great success for the Portuguese group, and the report had massive repercussions at the international level. In Portugal, the local use of “Psychoanalytical Society” appeared in 1971. In 1973 the Education Minister approved the constitution of the Portuguese Psychoanalytical Society, and Francisco Alvim became its first president. The Salazar dictatorship had raised numerous obstructions to the acceptance of the Psychoanalytical Society as a scientific organization during its time. The new possibilities following the political shift on 25 April 1974 enabled Portuguese psychoanalysts to develop their work widely outside the private clinic, in public social institutions and in academic training. During his long dictatorship Salazar had established strong control of the media. “New ideas”—every new form of thought, even in scientific fields—were looked upon as suspect and dangerous, especially if they came from abroad. That was the case with psychoanalysis. With the end of the regime, many exiled intellectuals returned and brought a significant change in university teaching. From 1974 to 1984 Pierre Luquet, who was at the time a training analyst of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society and scientific secretary of the Congresses of French-Language Psychoanalysts, travelled to Lisbon frequently—at least four times a year—to conduct theoretical–clinical seminars of two and a half days each. The Lisbon Psychoanalysis Institute was created in 1975 with a training field to prepare future psychoanalysts and a clinical field of psychoanalytic therapy from then on until the present. In 1977 the Portuguese group became an IPA Provisional Society. In 1978 the Second Conference of the European Psychoanalytical Federation, organized by the Portuguese group, was held in Estoril (near Lisbon). The subject was “The Narcissism of the Psychoanalyst”. In 1980, also in Estoril, the First World Congress on Infant Psychiatry was held in memory of René Spitz. The topics and participants of this congress were almost entirely from the psychoanalytic field. At the 32nd IPA Congress of 1981 in Helsinki, Finland, the Provisional Portuguese Psychoanalytical Society became an IPA Component Society. In 1984 the 44th Congress of French-Language Psychoanalysts—whose presidents were jointly the Portuguese Pedro Luzes and the French Michel Fain—took place in Lisbon. The main theme of the congress was “From Anxiety to Hysteria”. João dos Santos presented a report on “A Neurose da Angústia” [Anxiety neurosis]. Pedro Luzes, Francisco Alvim, and António Coimbra de Matos also contributed to this congress with works. A second report, entitled “L’hystérie. Unité et diversité” [Hysteria: Unity and diversity] was presented by Augustin Jeanneau. In 1985 the biannual Portuguese Journal of Psychoanalysis [Revista Portuguesa de Psicanálise] was first published. The editorial quality of this journal was officially recognized when, on 28 June 2007, Volume 26 (2006), Number 2 was included in the LATINDEX Catalogue. Since 1985, scientific reunions, called, alternately, “Symposium” or “Conference”, have been organized, at a rate of two per year, without interruption until today. The Symposium has a greater clinical weight, and the Conference a cultural one. Their objective is fostering the public awareness about the presence of psychoanalysis in Portugal. These reunions are also open to scientific contributions of professionals who are not analysts. They have drawn the participation of a wide professional public, including physicians, psychologists, professors, historians, scientists, philosophers, artists, and university students. In 1987, the year of the death of João dos Santos, the Society began regular seminars, each lasting two days, for young analysts and candidates. Five to six seminars, led by foreign psychoanalysts, are held each year, to promote the training of child psychoanalysts in Portugal. Analysts such as Florence Guignard, Jean Bégoin, Annie Anzieu, Didier Houzel, Donald Meltzer, Antonino Ferro, Dominique Arnoux, and Bianca Lechevalier have participated in these seminars. Since the year 2000, these seminars have been held less frequently—mainly for two reasons. On the one hand, it was becoming more and more expensive for foreign colleagues to come. On the other hand, with the creation of the Committee on Child and Adolescent Psychoanalysis (COCAP/IPA), many Portuguese analysts qualified as child and adolescent psychoanalysts. Currently (2010), with the collaboration of the COCAP, a regular training in child and adolescent analysis is being organized in our Institutes. Since 1989, the Iberian Congresses of Psychoanalysis, comprising the Portuguese Psychoanalytical Society, the Spanish Psychoanalytical Society, and the Madrid Psychoanalytical Association, have been taking place biannually. An Iberian journal of psychoanalysis in the Castilian language, entitled Anuário Ibérico de Psicoanalisis, is published by these societies. In 1994 the 54th Congress of French-Language Psychoanalysts of the Roman Countries, organized by the Portuguese Psychoanalytical Society, convened in Lisbon under the supervision of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society. Carlos Amaral Dias and Manuela Fleming presented a report entitled “La Fonction Contenant de L’Analyste” [The containing function of the analyst], and Louise de Urtubey presented one with the title “Le Travail de Contre-Transfert” [The work of countertransference]. In Oporto, the second city of the country, the psychoanalytic movement has developed under the leadership of Jaime Milheiro and Celeste Malpique. Both had come into contact with psychoanalysis in Paris and Geneva in the 1960s, when training in psychiatry and pedopsychiatry. Later they were trained as psychoanalysts in Portugal. Always in close collaboration with the Portuguese Psychoanalytical Society, beginning in 1980, they progressively organized clinical, training, and cultural activities that led in 1997 to the foundation of the Oporto Psychoanalytic Institute, with functions similar to those of the Lisbon Institute. In 2006 the 66th Congress of French-Language Psychoanalysts was organized in Lisbon, with the subject “Relations d’Objet et Modèle de la Pulsion” [Object relations and instinct model]. Reports were presented by Bernard Brusset, “Metapsychologie du lien et ‘troisième topique’?”, and Gérard Lucas, “Clinique de la relation d’objet et du registre pulsionnel dans la psychanalyse de l’enfant”. We are related to Brazil by history, language, and culture. In 2006 the first Lusitanian–Brazilian Congress of Psychoanalysis took place in Lisbon. In 2007, the second meeting took place in Brazil, under the title II Luso-Brazilian Congress of Psychoanalysis. Pedro Luzes, one of the first three Portuguese analysts, who had had Kleinian training in Switzerland, introduced Melanie Klein’s ideas in Portugal. Some differences of opinion started to arise between him and the other two analysts (Francisco Alvim and João dos Santos). However, a split never happened in the Society, mainly because of the good personal relations between all of them. As a result of Pedro Luzes’s report at the Congress of Romance-Language Psychoanalysts in Lisbon in 1968, the Portuguese analysts became acquainted with Bion’s ideas. Today the majority of the Portuguese Psychoanalytical Society members follow a mixed orientation, with roots in a diversity of perspectives and open to new revisions. Note Some of the information in this chapter is based on Pedro Luzes, Cem anos de psicanálise [One hundred years of psychoanalysis] (Lisbon: ISPA, 1997). Bibliography Moniz, E. (1915). As bases da psico-análise [The fundamentals of psychoanalysis]. Lição do Curso de Neurologia. A Medicina Contemporânea, 33: 337. Moniz, E. (1921). O conflicto sexual [Sexual conflict] [Conferência plenária Luso-Espanhola das Associações para o Progresso das Ciências, Portugal]. Médico, 6: 385.








100 Years of the IPA








41Turkey Psychoanalysis, Turkey, and the IPA

Vamık D. Volkan

As I write this in 2010, there are 16 psychoanalysts who are members of the International Psychoanalytical Association and two IPA-approved training centres in Turkey, both in Istanbul. Turkey is a secular country, with a population of about 73 million persons, of whom 99% are Muslims. Other analysts with Muslim backgrounds who are members of the IPA have been practising in the United States, Europe, and elsewhere for many decades. As illustrated in Salman Akhtar’s book, The Crescent and the Couch: Cross-Currents between Islam and Psychoanalysis (2008), some of these analysts have contributed greatly to psychoanalytic theory and technique. Even so, the IPA’s initial involvement in a country with a Muslim population was groundbreaking. This chapter tells this story. In order to have a clearer picture of how psychoanalysis evolved in Turkey, however, I will first go far back in history and provide a bird’s eye view of how the Turks dealt with mental health issues under the influence of Islam, and then fast-forward to the 1930s to illustrate the initial reception of Freud’s ideas in Turkey. I will describe how one German analyst escaping from the Third Reich played an especially significant role in introducing psychoanalysis in the new Republic of Turkey, which was founded in 1923 from the ashes of the Ottoman Empire.

Turks and the Islamic tradition for caring for persons with mental problems The Turks created two large empires in Central Asia before their Western migration during the ninth and tenth centuries. They filled the vacuum left by the fall of the Arabic Islamic Empire and adopted the faith of Islam and eventually established the Ottoman Empire, which lasted from 1299 to 1923. After its collapse at the end of the First World War, the Republic of Turkey was born under the leadership of Mustafa Kemal, who later took the surname Atatürk. Let us go back to a book entitled Kutadgu-Bilig, which was written in Turkish in 1069. Passages in this book describe the efsuncu—people who warded off jinns [demons] and occupied a role just below hekim [medical men] in the social hierarchy. The same book gives an account of disagreements between efsuncus who treated their patients with “suggestions” and those medical men who scorned such methods (Gökay, 1969; Ünver, 1943; Volkan, 1975). When Yusuf Hasi Hacib wrote Kutadgu-Bilig, Avicenna (also known as Ibni Sina), the great Islamic physician, had been dead for 32 years. It is believed that he was a Persian, but in Turkey many scholars dispute his ethnic origin and often refer to him as a Turkish healer who, like other scholars of his time, wrote in Persian (Gökay, 1969). Avicenna was aware of mania and depression and the importance of good child-rearing practices in preventing mental problems. Before the Ottomans, Seljuk Turks had established an empire in Anatolia. They were great builders, and their principal innovation was the madrassa [medrese] (Cahen, 1968). Today we associate madrassas in Afghanistan, Pakistan, and elsewhere with locations where future extreme fundamentalist Islamic terrorists are brainwashed and trained (Volkan, 1997; Volkan & Kayatekin, 2006), but during the Seljuk period the madrassa was an establishment for the teaching of the religio–judicial sciences (Cahen, 1968). Urban communities with schools, libraries, fountains, baths, and hospitals were established around madrassas, including several hospitals built between 1205 and 1308 that had sections for the care of the mentally ill. Besides these hospitals, tekkes [houses of sufitic beliefs] were erected throughout the country. Tekkes were religious and philosophical establishments that also offered humane treatment for people with mental problems, reflecting the traditional understanding of mental illness that grew from the belief that all goodness and all suffering comes from God, and the reasons God inflicts pain on mankind are beyond human understanding (Volkan, 1975). When the Ottoman Empire came to power following the disappearance of the Seljuk Empire, the Ottomans continued building madrassas and hospitals and erecting tekkes, and in most towns there were also separate hospitals for “infidels”. In 1470 they opened a hospital in Istanbul for the exclusive use of persons with mental problems. This followed many similar hospitals in various locations in the Empire. Although Muslim Arabs had built institutions for the mentally ill before the Turks did, it was the latter who, by becoming the champions of Islam, not only carried out the Islamic tradition of benevolent treatment of persons with mental problems, but made further improvements in their care (Adasal, 1964; Erkoç, 2004; Öztürk & Volkan, 1971). For example, the hospital in Edirne that was built during the reign of Beyazid II (1481–1512) had individual rooms for the patients, with an open dome and a soothing fountain in the middle; at one of the dais musicians played to calm and distract the residents (Lewis, 1971). During the final three centuries of the Ottoman Empire, every aspect of medical care declined, including that for the mentally troubled. Sultan Abdülhamid II, whose reign spanned the years between 1876 and 1909, was afraid of the “insane”. Although this story might be exaggerated, we know that during his reign Topkapı Hospital, the main hospital in Istanbul for those with mental illness, became a virtual prison for those confined there. The patient death rate there was high, and at one time the Sultan’s permission was required to visit the place. When Emil Kraepelin visited Istanbul and asked to see the hospital, he was unable to obtain permission to do so (Adasal, 1964). There were also attempts in the Ottoman Empire for modernization. In 1898 the first modern neuropsychiatric clinic was opened by a Turkish physician named Raşit Tahsin, who had studied with Kraepelin. Following Raşit Tahsin, prominent Turkish physicians dealing with mental illness became followers of Kraepelin, among them Mahzar Osman, who had also studied with him. During the First World War the Allies occupied many parts of the Ottoman Empire, including parts of Anatolia, the heartland of the Turks. The Empire collapsed, and the Caliphate ended. Following a war of independence under Mustafa Kemal’s leadership, the Turkish Republic was born. Even though Western powers were the enemies of the Turks, Mustafa Kemal Atatürk, as the first president of Turkey, led in westernizing and modernizing the new republic. His political/cultural revolution included the establishment of a parliamentary government, language and educational reforms, the closing of tekkes [Sufic houses], the emancipation of women, changing the dress style, allowing painting and erecting human figures (previously not allowed according to the Muslim tradition), creating the Turkish opera, and, most importantly, establishing secularism. In Atatürk’s Turkey, interest in psychoanalysis began in Ankara, then a dusty little city that had become the capital of the new republic, rather than the capital of the now-vanished Ottoman Empire, Istanbul. The followers of Kraepelin in Istanbul, led by Mahzar Osman, dominated the psychiatric scene.


Mustafa Kemal Atatürk’s Turkey and psychoanalysis When Norman Itzkowitz and I (Volkan & Itzkowitz, 1984) were writing a psychobiography of Mustafa Kemal Atatürk, we learned that when he was Turkey’s first president, he sometimes had severe problems with his teeth, and he had a special relationship with his dentist, Sami (Samuel) Gunzberg, a Turkish citizen of Jewish origin. We know that Gunzberg gave a great deal of information about Nazi Germany to the Turkish leader. Mustafa Kemal as a young man studied French and German and, prior to his becoming a national hero, had visited France and Germany. Ever since his days at the military college in Istanbul, he had been interested in reading the works of Western thinkers. He was a “reparative leader” (Volkan, 2009). Although we cannot attribute his aversion towards what was happening in Nazi Germany to his relationship with his dentist, nevertheless we believe that Gunzberg was influential. Some years after we published our psychobiography of the Turkish leader, during a trip to Jerusalem, I learned from Hebrew University historian Professor Gavriel Cohen that Sami Gunzberg was the leader of a group of Jewish persons helping other Jews to flee from Germany to Turkey. A letter written by Albert Einstein on 17 September 1933 to the President of the Cabinet of Ministers of the Republic of Turkey was discovered in October 2006. In 1933, Einstein lived in France and was the honorary president of OZE (Organization for the Protection of the Jewish Population). In his letter Einstein asks the president of the Cabinet of Ministers


to allow forty professors and doctors from Germany to continue their scientific and medical work in Turkey. The above mentioned cannot practice further in Germany on account of the laws governing there now. The majority of these men possesses vast experience, knowledge and scientific merits and could prove very useful when settling in a new country.


OZE would pay their salaries during the first year of their stay in Turkey. The letter ends in the following way:


In supporting this application, I take the liberty to express my hope, that in granting this request your Government will not only perform an act of high humanity, but will also bring profit to your own country.


On the document there is a handwritten instruction by İsmet İnönü, who was, at that time, the Prime Minister and Mustafa Kemal’s right-hand man. This instruction sent Einstein’s request to the Turkish Ministry of Education. The Ministry rejected this proposal on the grounds that there were no existing conditions to accept such an unusual request. But Einstein’s request was accepted when Mustafa Kemal intervened. We have no document that illustrates Sami Gunzberg’s role in Einstein’s writing the letter and in Mustafa Kemal’s positive response to it. We know only that the 10-year-old, very poor Turkish Republic had allowed some German–Jewish scholars to come to Turkey even before Einstein’s request (Günergün, 2006). In 1933, 30 German–Jewish scholars and their families came to Turkey. Eventually 190 intellectuals and their families arrived, ultimately saving over 1,000 lives (Reisman, 2006). Among the German–Jewish intellectuals who fled to Turkey was Professor Oscar Weigert. He would become a consultant to Atatürk in changing the attitude of Turkish men towards labour, bringing labour out of its feudal state and introducing such ideas as the eight-hour day, regular vacations for workers, workers’ compensation, and the idea of organizing Turkish labour unions (Weigert, 1937). Professor Weigert’s wife was an ethnic German, psychoanalyst Edith Vowinckel Weigert. She accompanied her husband to Turkey in 1935 and spent over three years in Ankara before she and her husband went to the United States (Usak-Sahin, 2009a, 2009b; Volkan & Itzkowitz, 1984). When I was accepted as a psychoanalytic candidate at the Washington Psychoanalytic Institute in the mid-1960s, because I was a Turk who had migrated to the United States a few years earlier, I was asked to start my training analysis with Edith Weigert, with the idea that she was familiar with the Turkish culture. I did not become Edith Weigert’s analysand because at that time she had broken a bone and had to recuperate. However, during my last year of psychoanalytic training she functioned as one of my supervisors, and in the late 1970s, when Norman Itzkowitz and I began to write our psychobiography of Atatürk, she was helpful by describing her life in Ankara, her perception of Atatürk, and her activities as a psychoanalyst in Turkey. Edith Vowinckel Weigert was born in 1894 in Düsseldorf and settled in Berlin, where she was trained as a psychiatrist and psychoanalyst. She became a member of the German Psychoanalytical Society (DPG) and served on the board of the DPG as a training analyst as the Nazi regime began. The Bulletin of the International Psychoanalytical Association includes her name in the list of members of the IPA and gives her address as Ankara from 1935 to 1938. A report in the same Bulletin in 1938, written by Edward Bibring, states:


In Ankara Dr. Weigert Vowinckel has given lectures and taken part in discussions on psycho-analysis in private circles; and as a psychiatrist, who is at present still in Training Analysis, has acquired a thorough theoretical knowledge of the subject and has translated Freud’s writings into Turkish. [Bibring, 1938, p. 280]


In the 1939 Bulletin Edith Weigert Vowinckel is listed as a member of the Washington–Baltimore Psychoanalytic Society. There is some ambiguity in Bibring’s report since it states that Edith Weigert was still in training analysis. No one in Turkey was training her. Perhaps this statement refers to her continuing study of psychoanalysis. The report’s reference to her giving lectures and discussing psychoanalysis in private circles is correct. Edith Weigert met Atatürk on several occasions, but during our interviews she did not say that Atatürk had been present when she was discussing psychoanalysis in private circles. Nevertheless, we have information on Atatürk’s and some of his colleagues’ interest in Freud. They were intent on modernizing Turkey, and knowing about Freud and psychoanalysis in general was of interest to them. For example, the second president of Turkey, İsmet İnönü, would, like Atatürk had done, sometimes gather Turkish intellectuals at the presidential palace for discussions. We know that in one such gathering Freud and psychoanalysis was the topic of discussion. When İsmet İnönü asked “Was Freud correct?” one intellectual—Hasan Ali Yücel, who was the Minister of Education—responded by praising Freud’s findings and then added that Freud sometimes “went too far”. Upon hearing this, İsmet İnönü replied, “I wish I could be like Freud and speak about the truth and go too far” (Küçükkaya, 2008, p. 129). With Atatürk’s instruction, Edith Weigert was given a license to practise psychoanalysis in Turkey—thereby becoming the first formally trained psychoanalyst to practise there. Before and after her death in 1982, her Turkish license was exhibited at the Washington Psychoanalytic Association building to honour her. While she was in Ankara, all of Edith Weigert’s analysands with the exception of one were foreigners, most of them working at various embassies. The Turk whom she “analysed” was a physician named Ahmet İzeddin İadan Biril. İzeddin İadan was born in 1893 and, after graduating as a physician in Istanbul in 1924, left for France in 1927 for three years of further training. In addition to French, he also spoke English, and later he would write three books, one titled, Psikanaliz [Psychoanalysis], as well as dozens of articles.1 Certainly, İzeddin İadan was someone who attempted to bring psychoanalytic theory and technique to the attention of Turkish psychiatrists and intellectuals even before he had met Edith Weigert. The parts of his memoirs that I have read mostly illustrate his struggles with better known psychiatrists in Istanbul and the arguments between these psychiatrists, most whom were influenced by Kraepelin. Mahzar Osman and others hesitated to be openly associated with Freudianism because in their minds it dealt with sexuality—a perception that would not be acceptable in their Muslim environment. İzeddin İadan (under the name of A. Izeddin) studied a Mohammedan legend and published his studies in German in Imago (Izeddin, 1932). In this paper he basically deals with Sufi traditions and finds reflections of oedipal situations and castration anxiety in them. In 1948 his letter to Psychoanalytic Quarterly was published (Izeddin, 1948), stating that on account of the war he had lost contact with psychoanalytic centres and thus had failed until recently to read Freud’s (1939a) Moses and Monotheism. Then, referring to his German paper mentioned above, he declared that he had written the first psychoanalytic study on monotheism, and that Freud’s analysis post-dated his by six years. I recall that when I spoke with Edith Weigert, I got the impression that İzeddin İadan did not undergo a proper psychoanalytic process. Reading the Turkish translation of his paper and parts of his memoirs, I came to the conclusion that İzeddin İadan’s approach to psychoanalysis was highly intellectualized. Furthermore, the nature of his personality organization, which made him known as an argumentative individual, is, in my estimation, what kept him from becoming a Turkish pioneer in initiating a deep interest in psychoanalysis in Turkey. Soon he was forgotten. Interest in psychoanalysis in Turkey grew because of Atatürk’s and his followers’ intense efforts to modernize. After the Turkish Republic was born, the Turkish Ministry of Education had all major works of Western philosophers, thinkers, and novelists translated, one by one. Freud’s writings began to be translated into Turkish in the late 1920s. During Edith Weigert’s stay in Ankara, and because she was known to the Turkish authorities and diplomats mainly because of her husband’s position, her discussion of psychoanalysis at private parties encouraged an interest in Freud’s writings. There were not many Turks at these parties, but Turkish authorities and intellectuals intent on cultural revolution were influenced by them. Elsewhere, I wrote about my childhood and one of the reasons I became a psychoanalyst (Volkan, 1985). My father, a Cypriot Turk who was an elementary school teacher, used a huge black wooden box in my parents’ bedroom in our house in Cyprus as his personal library. As a youngster, I was intensely interested in what he kept in this box: the box, with its obvious symbolism, became an object of my Oedipal curiosity. When I examined it, I found Freud’s (1905) Three Essays on the Theory of Sexuality in it. To learn about Freud was to learn about my Oedipal father, as well as to identify with him. Modernization in Turkey and Edith Weigert’s activities in Ankara had reached my Cypriot Turkish home. Once I had established myself as a psychoanalyst, I would try to do my best to bring the IPA to Turkey.


Preparing for IPA activities in Turkey Atatürk died in 1938, but his influence and modernization continued in Turkey. The growing city of Ankara, with its medical school at Ankara University, remained a more fertile ground for the acceptance of psychoanalysis than did the medical school of Istanbul University, where followers of Kraepelin continued to dominate the psychiatric scene. When I went to Ankara in 1950 and started my medical education, some of the Jewish professors who had escaped the Nazis 15–20 years earlier were still teaching there, and there were other such professors at Istanbul University. None of them, however, taught psychiatry. In 1950 the Turkish large-group identity had already greatly changed from what it had been during the Ottoman period. For example, one third of my medical school class were women. Seven years later, when I came to America, only a few female students could be found in any of the American medical school classes. Our psychiatry chairperson at Ankara University’s Medical School was Rasim Adasal. He called himself “Turkish Freud”, even though he had no formal training in psychoanalysis. Adasal wrote books with references to psychoanalytic thinking. He was very charismatic and was well known by the public because of his interest in sports and sports medicine. Thus Freud was popularized in Turkey. In 1958 Donald Winnicott and Anna Freud were asked to write papers addressed to the Turkish audience on psychoanalysis and its role in medicine. I have not yet discovered who initiated this request, but both responded, and their papers, in Turkish translation, were published. Winnicott described some key points of psychoanalysis and stated that interest in psychoanalysis varies from one civilization to another. He added:


When a medical journal in Turkey asked me to write a paper on psychoanalysis I realized that there is yet another country that wants to place the investigation of the human psyche within the broad spectrum of science and that wishes to investigate the psyche, as we investigate the body, through a scientific method. [Winnicott, 1958, p. 18]


He suggested that were those in charge of medicine in Turkey to decide to establish a psychoanalytic institute, they should find ways to send some bright Turkish doctors to foreign places, such as the London Psychoanalytic Institute, for at least four years. He then stated that when the time comes to start a psychoanalytic institute in Turkey, it should be established by both men and women. Anna Freud’s paper is didactic and deals with the psychological processes in children when they experience physical illness or undergo surgery. It explains the importance of the mother–child relationship and a child’s response to the loss of the mother (A. Freud, 1958). Soon after I graduated from medical school and moved to the United States, another medical school, called Hacettepe Medical School, was opened in Ankara. Orhan Öztürk, who is still living and well respected, also studied in the United States before returning to Turkey and becoming the chairperson of the Department of Psychiatry of this hospital. While he was in the United States, he worked for some years at the Austen Riggs Center in Stockbridge, Massachusetts, while Erik Erikson was also there, and he was influenced by Erikson. Öztürk did not go through a formal psychoanalytic training, but when he became a key figure in psychiatry in Turkey, he also became a key figure in expanding psychodynamic views in psychiatric practice, and, following Erikson, he especially focused on interactions between individuals and their societies. In 1974, as a psychoanalyst, I returned to Ankara University as a visiting professor of psychiatry for a little over a year. Also there during the same time period as a visiting professor of psychiatry was Celal Odağ, who had been trained as a psychoanalyst in Germany. We joined forces and taught psychoanalytic concepts in a most intensive way. In 1994 Celal Odağ opened a training institution in Izmir called the Halime Odağ Foundation, named after her late mother. While this foundation did not offer traditional psychoanalytic training, it functioned as a forerunner of the IPA-sponsored schools that would later be established in Istanbul. I am grateful to psychoanalysts in Israel, primarily to the late Rafael Moses and the late Rena Moses, and, in the United States, to Salman Akhtar, Ira Brenner, and David Sachs—as well as other colleagues from Germany—who accepted our invitations and lectured at Halime Odağ Foundation, provided supervision for young therapists, and helped with the Foundation’s evolution. Through the activities of the Halime Odağ Foundation, psychodynamic training and interest in psychoanalysis spread in Turkey, especially among young psychiatrists and psychologists. The Foundation’s annual scientific meeting, where analysts from different countries spoke, was always packed with young audiences. Starting in the mid-1990s, I began meeting one to three times a year, for several days each time, with 21 psychiatrists from Istanbul, who established themselves as a “psychoanalytic club” for the intensive study of psychoanalytic principles. I did this without any financial remuneration as part of my own desire to build a foundation so that Turkey could become a place where, in the future, formal psychoanalytic training would be available. Today some of these members, having gone through their formal education in psychoanalysis, are already IPA members, and others are close to becoming IPA members. Others from Istanbul began training in France. Among them, Talat Parman would take the leadership role in creating the cornerstone of one of the IPA-sponsored training facilities in Istanbul. Still another group, again in Istanbul, established connections with the Chicago Institute of Psychoanalysis. This led to visits to Turkey by Arnold Goldberg and Allen Siegel and phone seminars that lasted for years. The emphasis of this group was on Kohutian self psychology (Siegel, 2003). Different groups interested in psychoanalysis in Istanbul did not have much communication among themselves. Nevertheless, the time was becoming ripe to start formal IPA involvement in Turkey. While all this was taking place in Izmir and Istanbul, the IPA created a Middle East Committee, chaired by Antoine Hani from the Washington Psychoanalytic Society. Antoine Hani’s personal connections to Lebanon and his knowledge of the Middle East were considered helpful to the Committee’s work. I, too, was appointed a member. Our task was to study psychoanalytic interest in the Middle East to try to see if there would be openings for formal IPA involvement and IPA-sponsored training facilities in this part of the world.


The story of IPA involvement in Turkey The IPA was already well connected with the psychoanalytic scene in Israel. Accordingly, the IPA’s Middle East Committee would be dealing only with countries in the Middle East where the majority of the population was Muslim. We learned that there were 16 psychoanalysts in Lebanon, but they were not trained in IPA-approved institutions. Antoine Hani’s work with them apparently went nowhere, as they did not want further scrutiny or education and did not wish to become IPA members. Neither could we begin an IPA process in Egypt. Turkey remained the only country for our focus. We knew that those Turks who were being trained in France would be members of the IPA once their training was completed. Many other Turks in Turkey who wanted such training could not afford to be educated in France or did not know French, so our main problem in Turkey was that no training analysts were located there. We entertained the idea of finding training analysts from foreign countries who would settle in Istanbul and earn a good living because they would find many analysands. Attempts to bring this idea to fruition did not work out. Talat Parman’s and others’ graduations were perceived as a major opening for the IPA’s further work in Turkey. However, unfortunately a split occurred in this group: Bella Habip, who, like Talat Parman, had French training and, like Talat Parman, had graduated in 2000, separated herself from her French-trained colleagues and began to have her own followers. Antoine Hani’s efforts to bring the Parman and Habip groups together failed. In 1987 talks on the creation of a psychoanalytic training programme for Eastern Europe began in Hungary. Eventually the Han Groen-Prakken Psychoanalytic Institute for Eastern Europe was established under the auspices of the IPA as well as of the European Psychoanalytical Federation (EPF), but education in this institute was not offered to Turkish candidates. The IPA accepted what became known as “shuttle analysis” (Szönyi & Štajner-Popoviİ, 2008) as part of psychoanalytic training. Eastern European candidates could have their training analyses by travelling long distances. Instead of seeing their analysts four times a week, they would lie on the couch many times, in what is known as “concentrated analysis”, whenever they travelled to the locations where their analysts lived. The IPA’s acceptance of “shuttle analysis” opened a way to train other Turks who did not know French but spoke English or who could not afford to be trained in France. There were three individuals in Izmir who were willing to travel to a foreign country for IPA-approved training analysis. All of them were already very close to Rafael Moses and Rena Moses and could have gone for training analysis to Israel, but Greece was closer. This was a time of political tensions between Turkey and Greece. I contacted Peter Hartocollis, who had left the Unites States and settled in his home country, Greece. He was most gracious and opened the way for three Turks to receive their training analysis in Athens, analysing two of them himself. I am indebted to Peter Hartocollis and other Greek colleagues. Two persons who had gone to Greece for their training analyses are today members of the IPA. David Sachs, from the Philadelphia Psychoanalytic Society, matched Peter Hartocollis in going out of his way to help the Turks. He was the chairperson of the IPA’s International New Groups (ING) Committee. He had earlier participated in a Halime Odağ Foundation annual scientific meeting and had been exposed to the enthusiasm of many young Turks for psychoanalytic training. We prepared an official proposal to the IPA to start an IPA-approved school in Turkey. In January 2003 David Sachs and I were asked to go to Miami, where the IPA’s Executive Council was meeting. We met with the Council and presented data to show why it was time to start a training facility in Turkey, and how it could begin. At that time we considered a modified Han Groen-Prakken Psychoanalytic model for Turkey until such time as some analysts in Turkey were ready to become training analysts. Our proposal that the IPA start a process of eventually establishing a training facility in Turkey was accepted, and then IPA president Daniel Widlöcher appointed an Exploratory Committee under Chairwoman Abigail Golomb of Israel. At that time, Abigail Golomb and I were members of the IPA’s Terror and Terrorism Committee, and she had been to Turkey when this committee met in Izmir. She consulted me and others who knew about the situation in Turkey and prepared herself well. Later, in 2005, Abigail Golomb would become the chairperson of the IPA Turkey Supervisory Committee. Nikolas Kouretas from Greece and Marie-France Dispaux from Belgium, both of whom know Turkish, also serve on the Supervisory Committee. The other two members are Betty Denzler from Switzerland and Milagros Cid Sanz from Spain. Abigail Golomb and her colleagues had to find ways to deal with the split between the Talat Parman and Bella Habip groups in Istanbul. In the end it was decided that two schools would be opened in Istanbul, one under the leadership of Talat Parman and the other under that of Bella Habip. Those who had formed a “psychoanalytic club”, those who had received education from Chicago Psychoanalytic Society earlier, and those who had their training analyses in Greece joined the Bella Habip group. Many analysts from Europe and the United States were involved in training the Turkish candidates. The dedication of Maurice Apprey from the Virginia Psychoanalytic Society and Ilany Kogan from Israel in educating and supervising the trainees in the Bella Habip-led school needs to be acknowledged. Two years ago the Talat Parman group, which had been guided by Bernard Penot in France and Yolanda Gampel in Israel, reached IPA Study Group status. This year the other group also reached the same status. Both schools already have graduates who have become direct members of the IPA. This year a new member of the IPA, Ayla Yazıcı, took the leadership role of the group that had originally been led by Bella Habip. At the present time, IPA activities in Turkey exist only in Istanbul. There is only one IPA member in Ankara, Ü. Elif Gürışık, who was trained in London long ago, and one in Izmir, Işıl Vahip. In 2010 Işıl Vahip was elected to become a Fellow at the American College of Psychoanalysts, an organization that honours accomplished psychoanalysts with a medical background. There are dozens of young mental health professionals in Ankara and Izmir eagerly awaiting IPA-sponsored psychoanalytic training facilities to develop in their cities as well. In Istanbul, the split between the two psychoanalytic schools continues, and it has also played a role in creating some discomfort within the IPA and a “competition” between French or American “styles” of psychoanalytic training or thinking. Nevertheless, the IPA’s involvement in Turkey has been successful, and one hopes that new graduates will, in the near future, bring an end to the split. I will finish this brief story of IPA involvement in Turkey by recalling Donald Winnicott’s 1958 advice when he stated that both men and women should work together in establishing a psychoanalytic institute in Turkey. He would have been very pleased to know that most candidates in both Turkish schools are women, the number of the IPA Turkish women who are members at the present time exceeds that of their male counterparts, and, while one school is led by a man, the other has been and is led by women.

Note 1. I am grateful to Dr İahap Erkoç, a serious scholar of the history of psychiatry in Turkey, for providing me with a copy of parts of İzeddin İadan’s memoirs, and Hale Uşak-İahin for sharing her research on this man’s role in introducing psychoanalysis to Turkey (Usak-Sahin, 2009a, 2009b). (1984). The Immortal Atatürk: A Psychobiography. Chicago: University of Chicago Press. Volkan, V. D., & Kayatekin, S. (2006). Extreme religious fundamentalism and violence: Some psychoanalytic and psychopolitical thoughts. Psyche & Geloof, 17: 71–91. Weigert, O. (1937). New Turkish Labour Code. International Labor Review, 35: 753–774. Winnicott, D. W. (1958). Psikanalizde ilerlemeler [Advances in psychoanalysis]. Tıpta Yenilikler, 4: 17–19.







43A history of new groups

David M. Sachs

The spread of psychoanalysis can be divided into three roughly demarcated periods. The preceptor or “guru” early stage occurred when the individual followers of Freud obtained an analysis from him or one of his close collaborators, were exposed to discussions about psychoanalysis, and discussed their cases with the same or other members of the founding fathers. Groups formed around these individuals as they moved to different cities. Similarly, when analysts moved to Latin America and the United States, this pattern was followed. The “guru” stage lasted until the IPA took over the establishment of societies following an agreed-upon training programme in the new area that was modelled on the training in the Component Society. The formation of a new society followed a sequence of stages. The first stage occurred when a sufficient number of training analysts, faculty members, and applicants were available to form a Study Group. The IPA President appointed a Sponsoring Committee of experienced analysts to monitor it. As this embryonic society grew, it became a Provisional Society that was also monitored by a Liaison Committee. When all functions of a Component Society were in place and functioning well, the Provisional Society became a Component Society. Throughout this process reports were made to the International New Groups Committee for its consideration. In short, all training was done within the structure of a group of experienced IPA analysts. When the United States became a Regional Society responsible for training, it formed new groups following a similar pattern. One other route to a new society could occur when a Component Society split and one group of members withdrew from the other. At first splits were handled in different ways. For example, both groups could be accepted as Component Societies. Or, only the group that did not leave the Component Society would retain that status, and the split-off group would have to re-apply. Procedures for handling split societies have had too many variations over the years to be described here. This path to the formation of a new group was discouraged when possible. Because all these methods required a critical mass of trained analysts at all stages so that training could occur within an established institute, it is not surprising that the spread of psychoanalytic training centres was slow. There was no pathway to IPA membership for individuals interested in becoming psychoanalysts who satisfied the educational requirements outside an IPA institute structure. All of these methods of forming new training centres are impractical in areas of the world without any trained analysts and where trained analysts were unwilling to move. Two seemingly unrelated events—the development of the Equivalency Procedure in the 1990s, and the fall of Communism in Russia in 1989—led to the Direct Member route to IPA membership. This was an entirely new development that was necessary for the special circumstances in Eastern Europe and in many other parts of the world. The Equivalency Procedure provided the means to qualify an applicant for IPA membership, and the existence of a large pool of qualified applicants in Eastern Europe provided the opportunity. To understand how these two events became linked to the expansion of psychoanalytic training, it is necessary to place the development of the Equivalency Procedure in its historical context. A lawsuit was filed by four psychologists against the American Psychoanalytic Association (APsaA) in the 1980s, claiming that it is a violation of the laws governing Restraint of Trade to exclude psychologists from training. Although the exclusion of non-medical applicants was not a policy of the IPA outside the United States, it was a policy of the APsaA, the only Regional Association of the IPA responsible for training. The lawsuit was settled in 1989 in favour of the psychologists. Among the permanent consequences of this legal decision, the APsaA could not exclude non-medical applicants from its training, and the IPA was allowed for the first time to train psychoanalysts in the United States. At the time the lawsuit was settled, there were a number of psychoanalytic training programmes in existence in the United States that had similar training requirements and standards as did the IPA and APsaA societies. Although the IPA had not approved of these training programmes, their graduates were now entitled to apply to the IPA for membership. The IPA was obligated to evaluate them under the terms of the settlement of the lawsuit. If it did not, it would be guilty of Restraint of Trade. Four such societies did apply to have their training programmes approved by the IPA and to have their members—if they chose to do so—considered on a case-by-case basis for IPA membership. These circumstances created a unique challenge for the IPA. Hitherto, all IPA members had been trained in IPA-approved programmes, and therefore it did not have a procedure to evaluate applicants based on their claiming to have had equivalent training. But the terms of the settlement of the lawsuit made it necessary to consider applicants who had been excluded from psychoanalytic training. For this reason, the IPA Executive Council asked Charles Hanly, chair of the International New Groups Committee (ING), and David Sachs, chair of the IPA New Groups Committee in North America, to write an Equivalency Procedure that would enable the IPA to evaluate those parallel-trained analysts who chose to apply. This procedure, approved by the Executive Council, was strictly limited in its application to the United States. The Equivalency Procedure was immediately applied to the members of the four applicant societies, and it proved to work very well. Some applicants could be approved directly, and others were required to complete aspects of their training that were below IPA requirements in order to meet IPA standards. As a result the IPA gained many new well-qualified members and four new societies that became qualified to train. The Executive Council believed that a unique situation had been successfully resolved and that the Equivalency Procedure would not be needed again. The expectation of the Executive Council was that the establishment of new groups would continue as it had before 1990 by the use of the methods described at the beginning of this chapter. However, this would prove not to be the case. The second unexpected event, the collapse of Communism in Russia toward the end of the 1980s, greatly affected how new groups were formed and changed psychoanalytic education. Psychoanalysis had existed in Russia in the 1920s and early 1930s, but it was prohibited after 1936. When Russia broke up, the suppressed interest in psychoanalysis re-appeared in many places in Eastern Europe. The IPA was confronted with a unique situation. Individuals wanted to be trained who lived in widely scattered cities in which there were no or very few trained analysts and which were remote from Western European training centres. At the same time there were many psychoanalysts in Western Europe who felt a deep bond of sympathy for those individuals who were deprived of the opportunity to become psychoanalysts due to the repressive forces of the Communist state. These people were willing to make many sacrifices to help the Eastern Europeans become trained, and they met with many dedicated individuals who were equally willing to sacrifice in order to be trained. To meet the challenge presented by qualified individuals seeking psychoanalytic training, the IPA responded by permitting the ING to utilize many innovative methods of training and to abandon the requirement that all training had to be accomplished within some version of an institute. It established a subcommittee on Eastern Europe under the brilliant leadership of Han Groen-Prakken and John Kafka, and it worked in cooperation with many individual analysts in the European Psychoanalytic Federation (EPF) who had a deep interest in helping Eastern Europe develop psychoanalysis. Both organizations provided financial and educational support to this project, as the applicants had very little money to support their training. And analysts from the United States and Canada were also drawn into helping start analysis where it had not existed and re-start psychoanalysis where it had. The IPA Executive Council and the ING recognized that the spontaneous response of IPA analysts to Eastern Europeans interested in training to become psychoanalysts required a way to evaluate many idiosyncratic paths to clinical competence, all of which fulfilled tripartite training requirements—personal analysis, supervision, and didactic courses. It became apparent to many psychoanalytic educators that the Equivalency Procedure had provided a means to evaluate individuals whose training was similar, but not the same as, training within IPA institutes. All that was needed was to modify it so that it could apply to training outside the United States. This task was assigned to David Sachs by the Executive Council because he had become chair of the ING and had experience with writing the original procedure. This new Equivalency Procedure was adopted by the Executive Council in the mid-1990s and became the procedure by which individual Eastern Europeans could become Direct Members of the PA before belonging to a society. It was expected by the ING that when there were a sufficient number of Direct Members in a city, a Study Group would form and would grow into a Provisional Society. At least four years later, Component Society status could be achieved. This programme was so successful that soon there were enough individuals and groups in various stages of development that the ING needed administrative help. Accordingly, the Psychoanalytic Institute for Eastern Europe (PIEE) was formed to monitor the many individuals who were trying to become Direct Members. The ING reentered the development at the point when forming a Study Group was possible and the usual procedures to follow new groups could be employed. Once the PIEE was established, it became possible to organize a summer school programme to which people would come from all over Eastern Europe. A more complete understanding of the effect of the Equivalency Procedure on psychoanalytic education requires the consideration of a partial list of its effect on the tripartite system of requirements. The most difficult hurdle was to obtain an analysis from a training analyst. One solution was to undertake a “shuttle analysis”. This is a form of concentrated analysis (four sessions per week over two or three days) used when the candidate had to travel a long distance to the training analyst. Some variations of this were also used—for example, interrupted, concentrated analysis. In this procedure the applicant who could not maintain the analytic schedule continuously until the analysis was completed was allowed to interrupt the analysis from time to time. Some candidates travelled to a Western centre, remained for a year or more with a training analyst, interrupted, and then returned for more analysis. In the opinion of the training analysts from many societies, all of these methods could result in a completed analysis that was equivalent to one done without concentrated sessions and without interruption; however, this is not to imply that all such ventures were successful. Of great importance for psychoanalytic educators is the fact that these variations in analysis placed the determination of a successful analysis on an evaluation of the ability of the analysand to conduct an analysis. In particular, the ability to resolve countertransference enactments was carefully considered. In regard to supervision, many variations of traditional education were tried. For example, sessions by telephone (or Skype), in combination with face-to-face sessions, became routine. To accomplish face-to-face supervision, some supervisors who lived very far from their supervisees were willing to do many supervisory sessions over a weekend—concentrated supervision. All of these methods were considered as successful as the usual weekly face-to-face sessions. Providing didactic courses proved to offer little difficulty. Experienced analysts visited and gave courses. Candidates travelled to meetings and to summer schools. If the range of material covered was not balanced, recommendations were made to the candidate to find courses to remedy the deficiency. Although the courses were somewhat haphazard and not in lock-step levels of difficulty, as in many societies, the end result proved to be as effective in providing the background necessary for clinical competency. All applicants for direct membership were evaluated frequently, because the IPA required these individualized programmes to educate the Direct Members to be as rigorous as those from established institutes. The goal was to have these candidates meet the same standards the IPA required from institute-trained candidates. In some regards the assessment process for Direct Members is even more difficult than that for candidates in established institutes. For example, they are required to present a case to a panel of three IPA training analysts from three different societies from two or three different countries. The introduction of the concept of IPA Direct Member was made possible by the application of the Equivalency Procedure, and this route to IPA membership became the major source of growth in the IPA. There is no reason to believe that it will not continue inasmuch as it has been applied to the development of psychoanalysis in Korea (now a Study Group) and in Latin and Central America through the creation of an analogue of PIEE, the Latin American Institute of Psychoanalysis (ILAP). More recently, the Direct Member path to IPA membership is underway in China. This overview of the routes to IPA membership is intended to provide a window through which the complex history of the ING can be appreciated. It is not a complete history but may serve as an introduction to the remarkable achievement that was accomplished by adapting psychoanalytic training to a wide range of external circumstances. It is not an easy task to design programmes that meet the educational requirements that are needed to become a psychoanalyst and to maintain equivalent standards of competency achieved by the different routes of training. Finally, no history of this achievement would be complete if the driving force that made this possible is not acknowledged. It could not have been accomplished without the energy and devotion of all the members of the ING to the maintenance of IPA standards, and without acknowledging the incredible ability of the candidates to maintain their enthusiasm and interest in psychoanalysis despite the most daunting challenges the training imposed on them.







11Norway The history of the Norwegian Psychoanalytic Society

Håvard Friis Nilsen

From its beginning, psychoanalysis in Norway was linked to the university, unlike most other countries, where it did not gain a similar academic foothold. In Freud’s 1914 exposition of the history of psychoanalysis, he noted that “the first textbook of psychiatry to refer to psychoanalysis was written in Norwegian” (1914d).1 The author, psychiatry professor Ragnar Vogt [1870–1943] of the University of Oslo clinic, remained sceptical towards the scientific merits of psychoanalysis, however, and continued to keep it at arm’s length. Central in establishing psychoanalysis as a profession in Norway was another university professor, Harald Krabbe Schjelderup [1895–1974]. An academic prodigy, Schjelderup earned a professorship in philosophy in 1922, when he was only 27 years old, then the youngest professor ever appointed in Norway. Unlike Vogt, Schjelderup had high hopes for psychoanalysis as a new science. Originally educated in physics, he was a pupil of Lars Vegard (who was mentioned twice in Niels Bohr’s lecture upon receiving the Nobel Prize in Physics). Schjelderup (1923) was also one of the first in the world to write on the epistemological implications of Einstein’s relativity theory, a publication that was reviewed extensively in the distinguished journal Nature. Schjelderup gradually switched from physics to philosophy and experimental psychology. At the time, this was not unusual, since most philosophers and academic psychologists had a background in physics or the experimental sciences. Schjelderup submitted a psychophysical PhD dissertation based on laboratory work at Alfred Lehmann’s experimental laboratory in Copenhagen. Thus when Schjelderup went to Vienna in 1925 for a training analysis with Eduard Hitschmann, and later for one with Oskar Pfister in Zürich, he was one of very few analysts with a professorship and an established scientific reputation. Pfister wrote to Freud about the “brilliant 32-year old professor” and praised Schjelderup’s textbook in psychology, “the first psychology textbook including psychoanalysis”. Freud congratulated Pfister on his successful analysis of Schjelderup, as “the resistance against analysis among these Scandinavians is particularly deep-rooted”. Beginning in 1928, Schjelderup was on the editorial board of the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis. Teaching a philosophy and psychology course for all new students at the University of Oslo, Schjelderup introduced generations of new students in Norway to psychoanalysis, which he included in the curriculum. Some of these medical students would become central figures in the first Norwegian generation of analysts: Karl Evang, later Surgeon General, Nic Waal [1905–60], a pioneer of child psychiatry, and Trygve Braatøy [1904–53], who became Head of the Psychiatric Department of Oslo Municipal Hospital. Between 21 and 23 August 1931, Scandinavian analysts met at the Swedish analyst Alfhild Tamm’s apartment in Stockholm, to discuss establishing a Scandinavian Society of Psychoanalysis. Attending from Norway were Ola Raknes, Harald Schjelderup, and J. I. Strømme, as well as Danish, Swedish, and Finnish analysts. The meeting received a telegram with cordial greetings from the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society and returned the compliment. Ola Raknes [1887–1975], who was a member of the Berlin Society, described the plans for a new Society in a letter to the German Psychoanalytical Society (DPG) leader, Max Eitingon, on 20 August 1931, and pointed to the problem of Strømme, a former student of Eugen Bleuler and a Jung-oriented non-IPA member, whom they considered to be a “wild analyst”. Max Eitingon agreed that Strømme was a problem, but he was very positive about the idea of a Scandinavian group, and would become an important supporter of the Norwegians in future International Psychoanalytical Association discussions on the question of “die Skandinavische oder die Norwegische Gruppe” [The Scandinavian or the Norwegian group]. In November 1933, two years later, when all ties to Strømme and his students had been severed, Ola Raknes and Alfhild Tamm sent the first formal proposal to Ernest Jones, as President of the IPA, requesting recognition of a Scandinavian Psychoanalytic Society, with Alfhild Tamm as President, Schjelderup as Vice-president, and Ola Raknes as Secretary. By then, the group consisted mostly of Norwegians—Schjelderup and his brother Kristian [1894–1980], Nic Hoel [1905–60], Ola Raknes [1887–1975], and Johannes Landmark—from Sweden there was Alfhild Tamm [1876–1959], and from Finland Yrjo Kulovesi [1887–1943]. The Society lacked a Danish member, but in a further letter to Jones dated 21 November 1933 Alfhild Tamm suggested a prominent member: Princess Marie Bonaparte of Greece, who by marriage was also Princess of Denmark and a Danish citizen. She wanted to become a member as long as she did not have to quit her membership in the French group. As several of the Swiss members were also members of the French Society, precedence for a dual membership in the IPA seemed to exist. The IPA Central Executive Committee was divided regarding the proposed Scandinavian Society. While Max Eitingon was positive, Anna Freud and Ernest Jones were predominantly negative. Anna Freud thought the group could represent trouble for the IPA, and a larger bloc would be more difficult to control than individual small countries. She also feared that the strong personality of Wilhelm Reich, who was living in Denmark at the time and shortly thereafter moved to Norway, might cause problems if he became a member. On 12 December 1933, Ernest Jones replied to Raknes, thanked him for “the interesting application for a Scandinavian Union”, and requested information about whom they had all had as training analysts. To Eitingon, Jones explained that this move was designed to check Reich’s possible influence on the group.2 Reich had not analysed any of the Norwegians at that time, however. Eitingon told Jones he did not have any problems with the proposed Scandinavian Union. He observed that such a model might prevent the Narzissismus der kleinsten Differenzen [The narcissism of minor differences], which, Freud once had said, characterized the Scandinavian countries. Eitingon described Raknes and Schjelderup as charming personalities and skilful analysts, and noted Schjelderup’s generosity in letting Alfhild Tamm have the presidency, even though he was a professor. Jones wrote to Anna Freud and proposed reconsidering the Scandinavian union in light of Eitingon’s arguments, but she was not appeased. She sent a circular letter to the committee, in which she wrote: “We are all in agreement that the Scandinavian group in its suggested form represent an unnecessary problem and possible danger for the IPA, while small local groups in the Nordic countries are just an advantage.”3 Eitingon did not back off immediately. He wrote his own circular letter, in which he argued that he could not see any danger with the Scandinavian group, that they could have good reasons for their model, and that it could harm the goodwill of psychoanalysis in the Northern countries if the Central Committee overruled their proposal.4 They agreed on settling the question at the upcoming congress in Lucerne while remaining wary of Reich’s future membership. Jones and Anna Freud wanted to exclude him from the IPA for political reasons: he was a Communist. In their view, this fact jeopardized the future of psychoanalysis in the German-speaking world. At the Lucerne Congress, a compromise was reached between the Scandinavian group and the IPA. Two groups (rather than four) were created: one a Danish–Norwegian group and a Swedish–Finnish one. Within a year the name of the first was changed to Norwegian–Danish; Schjelderup was elected President, and Otto Fenichel Secretary. Reich was excluded from the German Society, and also from the IPA. At first it was taken for granted that in order to participate Reich could simply join the Nordic group. However, the Central Executive Board of the IPA demanded that the Norwegians end all contact with Reich and his pupils in order to be officially recognized (N. Hoel, 1938). On principle, Harald Schjelderup, Ola Raknes, and Nic Hoel could not accept this demand. Since first meeting Reich in Berlin, they had been fascinated by his ideas of the importance of the body in analysis (cf. Hoel, 1957) and were enraged by the way Reich was treated. Reich, for his part, was especially happy about Harald Schjelderup’s support at the Lucern Congress, since the latter did not share his political convictions but nonetheless, in Reich’s view, defended him more than did his Marxist colleagues (cf. Reichmayr & Muehlleitner, 1998). Harald Schjelderup did indeed have high regard for Reich because of the technical and theoretical contributions of his 1933 book Charakteranalyse. To Schjelderup, Reich’s work represented the most important contribution to making psychoanalysis scientifically acceptable, a problem that, as a university professor, was always on Schjelderup’s mind. He saw Reich’s new principles of interpreting in therapy character attitudes and bodily postures—the patient’s behaviour—as a way out of the notoriously criticized “introspective” level of psychoanalysis. It effectively offered a way of bridging the gap between psychoanalysis and academic psychology, for example, behaviourism. He also liked Reich’s writings on the autonomic nervous system, arguing that the sympathetic and the para-sympathetic system corresponded to the psychoanalytic concepts of anxiety and sexuality (thus providing a medical antithesis at the core of psychoanalysis, as an alternative to the libido–death instinct duality) (cf. Reich, 1927). At an international psychiatry conference in 1936, Schjelderup presented a paper on character changes through psychoanalytic treatment, and described Reich’s character technique as “the most important progress in psychotherapy since Freud” (Schjelderup, 1936). The collaboration between Schjelderup and Reich led to an ambitious research programme to investigate psychoanalytic tenets in a variety of scientific fields—for example, physiology and cellular biology—at the University of Oslo. Reich was probably partly inspired by Sándor Ferenczi’s Thalassa, with its proposal for bio-analysis as a logical counterpart to psychoanalysis. That this research would be severely criticized was to be expected, but it was certainly a unique project in the history of science. Psychoanalysts generally regard this move as a complete break with psychoanalysis,5 and the experimentalism was taken as a sign of someone out of his element. But Reich was now in academia, and academic psychology had been experimental since the 1870s. Reich’s so-called bion experiments and his later concept of the orgone, which he coined in Oslo, were dismissed scientifically. Reich left Norway in 1939, having by then fallen out with Schjelderup, but his influence led Norwegian psychoanalysis to combine standard therapy with an eye for body language, muscular tensions, facial expressions, tone of speech, the way of breathing, and in general all traits of sympathetic dominance in the autonomic nervous system. In this respect, Reich made a lasting impact on Norwegian psychiatry, and books like Trygve Braatøy’s Fundamentals of Psychoanalytic Technique (1954) acknowledged this fact, while rejecting his later phase. Reich’s ideas led to exchanges between medical psychotherapists and physiotherapists in Norway, for instance between Trygve Braatøy and Aadel Bulow-Hansen, who together developed a therapy system. When Germany occupied Norway, the psychoanalytic society was disbanded. Most of the analysts participated in the resistance movement and took great risks. Arrested as the leader of the resistance movement at the university, Schjelderup was sent to the “Grini” concentration camp. Some analysts fled to Sweden; Johannes Landmark died in a German bombardment, and Paul Bernstein (who had married Lotte Liebeck from Berlin a few years earlier) died in a concentration camp in Germany. After the war, the Norwegian analysts realized that their status as a society had been withdrawn by the IPA, and they all believed this to have been because of their former association with Wilhelm Reich. The President of the IPA in 1953, Heinz Hartmann, noted that there were “a few analysts among the Norwegians who practised a new technique, which made a recommendation from the Central Executive impossible” (Hartmann, 1954). Several attempts at renewed membership were unsuccessful. In 1961, at the IPA Congress in Edinburgh, the Norwegian Society was granted status as a Study Group, but that status was almost immediately withdrawn. The reason given for the withdrawal, communicated by Dr Thorkil Vangaard of Copenhagen acting as “deputy” for Dr Maxwell Gitelson of the IPA, was “unsatisfactory training analysis by Professor Schjelderup”.6 Disillusioned by what he perceived as the ultra-orthodoxy of the IPA of this period, Schjelderup nevertheless continued his efforts to prove the scientific and therapeutic value of psychoanalysis, exemplified by his pioneering longitudinal study of the effects of psychoanalytic treatment, published in Psychiatry (Schjelderup, 1955). A long struggle for recognition ensued, with applications made at various congresses. In 1971, the Norwegian Society was given status as a Study Group and finally gained status as a Component Society in 1975. Today (2010), the Norwegian Society has 86 members and 43 candidates. An active child-analytic group and a group working with psychoanalytic research form important parts of the Society’s activities. The main trend is a broad object-relational approach with emphasis on analysis of character, but also drawing inspiration from ego psychology. The university tradition continues through Professor Siri Gullestad, today Head of the Psychology Department in “Harald Schjelderup’s House”, at the University of Oslo. Notes 1. The book, Psykiatrens grundtraek, by Ragnar Vogt, was actually published in 1905, not, as Freud wrote, in 1907. 2. Reviewed in the article “Relativity and Theory of Knowledge” (Anonymous, 1923). 3. For two influential works in this respect, see, e.g., Charles Rycroft (1972), or Philip Rieff’s chapter on Reich in Rieff (1966). 6.
