38Japan The “Japanese ways” in psychoanalysis

Osamu Kitayama

The two pioneers of psychoanalysis in Japan, Yaekichi Yabe and Heisaku Kozawa, underwent training in Europe around 1930. Since then, Japan has had two academic societies related to psychoanalysis. At present, these are the Japan Psychoanalytical Association (JPA) and the Japan Psychoanalytical Society (JPS), both with a 55-year history. The organization that is a member of the International Psychoanalytical Association (IPA) is the JPS. A total of 36 qualified psychoanalysts belong to the JPS. Members conduct training that complies with IPA standards. At present, the association has 20 candidates, and a majority of its regular members are psychiatrists. In the light of the Japanese population having exceeded 100 million people and the JPS having a history spanning 80 long years, this small number of members may reflect the fact that there are very few Japanese who have the time and the money to undergo sessions four times a week. There is no doubt, however, that this organization is playing a solid role in fostering and educating Japan’s leaders in psychoanalysis. In contrast, the other organization, the JPA, has members with diverse occupations. At present, there are 2,800 members, of whom 900 are physicians and 1,600 are psychologists. The JPA provides no training that complies with the standards required by the IPA. Most members often perform psychoanalytic psychotherapy at a rate of once a week. Still, the number of members is increasing rapidly. This format, where a society representing a particular field of science is divided into an international organization and a domestic organization, is commonly seen in other countries. However, the huge gap seen in the number of members may be uniquely Japanese. Moreover, the two organizations get along well. For example, former JPA presidents have long been appointed JPS presidents. I, too, served as president of the JPA until 2009, and am currently vice-president of the JPS. In addition, all the 36 JPS psychoanalysts are members of the JPA as well. Needless to say, the duality of the two organizations—the JPS that actively adapts itself to demands imposed by the IPA, and the JPA that does not—may represent “Japan’s resistance” towards psychoanalysis. However, this situation is not something that should be simply criticized as being a “double standard”. Our wish now is to remain true to this duality. Although there have inevitably been conflicts between the two, both the JPS and the JPA have accumulated extensive track records for the last eight decades. I believe we can say that, because we have these two organizations, we are creative and productive in our own way. These are the Japanese ways of psychoanalysis.







1Austria The three histories of the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society

Andrea Bronner

The Vienna Psychoanalytic Society [Wiener Psychoanalytische Vereinigung (WPV)] has not one but three histories: the Society is not merely the cradle of psychoanalysis, the “mother of all psychoanalytic societies” (Jones, 1939), which in its first history underwent an incredible development from its founding in 1908 to 1938; in its second history, it had to endure its own brutal destruction (1938–45); and in its third history, beginning in 1946, it experienced a difficult and slow but steady rehabilitation. The founding of the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society by Freud and his disciples in 1908 was only the formal beginning of the WPV. In reality, the WPV was the logical consequence of a development that had begun much earlier: namely, in the autumn of 1902. It was at this time that Sigmund Freud, together with four doctors—Wilhelm Stekel, Alfred Adler, Rudolf Reitler, and Max Kahane—created the “Psychological Wednesday Society” to discuss Freud’s work. The gatherings took place every Wednesday at 8:30 pm in Freud’s apartment at Berggasse 19 in Vienna’s 9th district. By this time, Freud had written, together with Breuer, Studies on Hysteria (1895d) and The Psychopathology of Everyday Life (1901b). Freud was subjected to increasing isolation in the medical and academic world at this time because of his unsettling discovery of the unconscious, and especially following the publication of The Interpretation of Dreams (1900a), which contained many intimate and, at times, humiliating details of his self-analysis. Freud was sharply criticized by countless colleagues, many of whom—even former friends—avoided his company. About this difficult time, Freud once said that “people took care to handle him like a freshly painted wall: nobody dared to touch him” (Minutes of the WPV [Nunberg & Federn, 2008], p. XXI). Even his appointment to a professorship in 1902 did not change much. Nevertheless, he was then permitted to give lectures, and some interested listeners did, in fact, come to hear him on Saturday evenings: at first doctors, and then other educated people who were interested in psychology and—dissatisfied with the current doctrines—were on the lookout for new ideas. In them, Freud found people with whom he could exchange views. At this time he published, among other works, Three Essays on the Theory of Sexuality (1905d). One of those who attended his lectures—Wilhelm Stekel, a doctor whom Freud had treated successfully and who was especially gifted at understanding symbols—had the idea to meet weekly. They would discuss psychological problems and interpret each other’s dreams: this was the birth of the Wednesday evening gatherings. Soon other interested people began to attend the gatherings, such as Paul Federn in 1903 and Eduard Hitschmann in 1905. Federn remained both a loyal friend and tarot partner of Freud’s for his entire life and was Vice President of the WPV from 1924 to 1938. Hitschmann became head of the newly founded outpatient clinic in 1923, a position he held until 1938, when the Nazis dissolved the WPV. Beginning in October 1906, the group’s secretary—Otto Rank—took minutes at the gatherings. Until the quarrel with Freud in 1924, Rank was Freud’s favourite student and was slated to become his successor. The group had appointed Rank secretary so that he could thereby finance his study of philosophy. The minutes reveal that the members of this group had a wide-ranging conception of what psychoanalysis should concern itself with—people who were ill were not alone at the centre of their attention—so were issues of literature, religion, philosophy, sociology, and pedagogy: “To this circle of friends, which together undertook the task of trying out Freudian ideas in different domains, I gave a speech about Richard Wagner’s Flying Dutchman”, wrote Max Graf, a professor at the Viennese Academy of Music who was also a member of the WPV and the father of “Little Hans” (from the Foreword to Graf, 1911; cited in the Minutes of the WPV [Nunberg & Federn, 2008], p. XXXIV). In 1907, foreign guests began to attend these Viennese gatherings. The first was Max Eitingon, later the founder and patron of the Psychoanalytic Institute and Outpatient Clinic in Berlin as well as the founder of the Psychoanalytic Society in Palestine. Eitingon came to Vienna to consult Freud on a particularly difficult case. Shortly thereafter, Carl Gustav Jung and Ludwig Binswanger first attended. For a time Jung played a very important role in Freud’s life, and Binswanger, who in 1910 became the first president of the local group in Zurich, was a lifelong friend of Freud’s despite their many scholarly differences. All of them came from the Sanatorium Burghölzli in Zurich, where Eugen Bleuler—a highly regarded professor of psychiatry and one of Freud’s very few sympathizers in academia—taught and practised. At the beginning of April 1908, as previously mentioned, the WPV was established for the “nurture and advancement of the psychoanalytic science founded by Prof. Dr Sigmund Freud in Vienna”, as the statutes still read today. Shortly thereafter, the first congress of the International Psychoanalytical Association was held in Salzburg, with twenty-six Austrian, six Swiss, five German, two English, two Hungarian, and one American participants. It was also in 1908 that the first psychoanalytic periodical—the Jahrbuch für psychoanalytische und psychopathologische Forschungen [Yearbook for Psychoanalytic and Psychopathological Research]—was founded; Sigmund Freud and Eugen Bleuler were its publishers and C. G. Jung its editor. The first volume appeared in 1909. Bleuler (who never became a psychoanalyst) and Jung played important roles. To a certain extent, it was Bleuler who opened the academic and medical world to Freud. Jung, as the first non-Jewish psychoanalyst, helped to free psychoanalysis from its reputation—especially damaging at the time—as a “Jewish” science. It is also likely that this is the reason Freud appointed Jung the first president of the IPA, which was founded in 1910. But Freud also felt a deep professional and personal connection with Jung, making him publisher of the Yearbook and designating him as his successor. It is not difficult to comprehend why Freud’s decision deeply offended his original Viennese followers: they felt overlooked. Freud tried to compensate by lending his support to Alfred Adler, who in 1910 became chairman of the WPV, with Stekel as his deputy. But the peace did not last long: Adler’s theory about the inferiority of the organs and the implications of this for the psyche was increasingly in opposition with Freud’s theories. In Freud’s view, Adler’s psychology was a theory of consciousness, not of the unconscious. Freud ultimately perceived a threat to the very foundations of his teachings; this came to a head in 1911, and after intense discussions, Adler and some of his followers resigned from the Society. Adler went on to found his own school of “Individual Psychology”. The second serious fracture followed in 1914, with C. G. Jung, an incident that deeply hurt Freud. Jung subsequently also established his own school. In 1913, to protect both Freud from defectors and psychoanalysis from disintegration, Ernest Jones came up with the idea of creating an unofficial executive committee of the IPA. The “Secret Committee”—composed at first of Freud, Ferenczi, Rank, Sachs, Abraham, and Jones—was intended to help Freud in future differences of opinion. Eitingon joined the Committee in 1919, and Anna Freud in 1924. In 1910, in consequence of an application by one of Vienna’s first female doctors, Dr Margarethe Hilferding, a discussion ensued about whether women should be admitted as members. A few men objected as a matter of principle, particularly Isidor Sadger, who had made important contributions to the understanding of perversions and homosexuality. Sadger’s nephew, Fritz Wittels, a writer and fierce adversary of Karl Kraus, also vehemently opposed the idea of women joining. Freud, by contrast, viewed it as inconsequential. Eventually, a clear majority favoured allowing women to join, and in April 1910 Margarethe Hilferding became the first female member. In 1911, however, she left the WPV together with Adler and, as one of the “individual psychologists”, dealt with women’s rights and birth control. In the meantime, membership had expanded so much that Freud’s apartment was no longer large enough for the Wednesday evening gatherings, and so these had to be moved elsewhere. In 1912, the magazine Imago was founded. Slowly, psychoanalysis established itself not only in Vienna but in other countries, as evidenced by the founding of local groups and societies in Berlin and Zurich (1910), New York, Russia, and Munich (1911), and Budapest and London (1913). It was during these years that narcissism assumed an increasingly important role in Freud’s thinking, and in 1914 his paper “On Narcissism: An Introduction” was published. And then the First World War broke out, halting for the time being the previously uninterrupted development of psychoanalysis. More than a few members were subject to military conscription, and it was no longer possible to maintain international contacts. The number of patients fell, and consequently so did the incomes of psychoanalysts. For a while Freud was able to continue working, first using the experience of the atmosphere in the War to write “Thoughts for the Times on War and Death” (1915b), which dealt with aggression, death, and ambivalence towards loved persons, then making an important contribution to metapsychology with “Mourning and Melancholia” (1917e [1915]). But eventually the destitution all around him led to the slowing of his scientific work. The end of the war and the large number of shell-shocked soldiers, however, led to a gradual revival of the psychoanalytic movement: in the autumn of 1918, the 5th IPA Congress took place in Budapest. Among those present were official representatives of Central European governments who wanted to hear what psychoanalysis had to say about shell shock, which had in part been very successfully treated by psychoanalysts. A few fundamental themes were addressed. Herman Nunberg, a psychiatrist who had completed his training under Paul Federn, petitioned for the introduction of mandatory training, and he had Freud’s approval for this, but the motion was not accepted until 1925, at the IPA Congress in Bad Homburg. In 1918, the idea of an international psychoanalytic publishing house was put forward; a year later one was founded, with Vienna as its hub. The money for the publishing house was supplied by Anton von Freund, a brewer from Budapest who was first a patient of Freud’s and later a friend and patron. The first head of the publishing house was Otto Rank, who was followed in 1925, after his falling out with Freud, by A. J. Storfer. Storfer, according to Alfred Polgar, was “a real Bohemian” and “an original” of the Viennese literary scene. From 1932 until March 1938, the publishing house was led by (Jean) Martin Freud, a lawyer and banker and Sigmund Freud’s oldest son, who had been named after Jean Martin Charcot. Martin Freud’s primary task was rescuing the publishing house, which was deep in debt. Though it was an important instrument of scientific communication, the publishing house encountered financial difficulties from the very beginning. With the outbreak, in 1931, of an economic crisis in Central Europe, the publishing house slid into a precarious position and urgently needed to be put back on its feet. The WPV stepped in and contributed a sum that was third only to funds given by Germany and the United States, which is evidence of just how powerful and important the WPV was at this time (Huber, 1977, p. 23). In 1922, Felix Deutsch—a university lecturer in internal medicine and a co-founder of psychoanalytic–psychosomatic medicine—established, with his wife Helene and Eduard Hitschmann, an outpatient clinic in Vienna. Like the Psychoanalytic Institute and Outpatient Clinic in Berlin, this outpatient clinic was intended to make an education in psychoanalysis possible as well as to ensure treatment of the poor. It would be maintained by private donations, and members who were medical doctors pledged to take on at least one patient free of charge. Colleagues agreed to submit to regular supervision by an experienced psychoanalyst and also had to participate in additional seminars and training evenings. Wilhelm Reich—appointed as the first resident physician in November 1923—established a therapeutic–technical seminar that he himself led from 1924 to 1930, and to which he would also occasionally introduce difficult cases from his own practice. He concentrated especially on the analysis of character and resistance as well as on the negative therapeutic transference. But because only trained medical doctors were permitted to treat patients who were ill whereas analysis by lay people was meant to continue as before, a line had to be drawn between treatment and training. A training institute was thus established in October 1924, and by its opening at the start of 1925 it had 15 students (including a few from abroad). Its first head was Dr Helene Deutsch, a specialist in psychiatry who had completed her training analysis under Freud and who was a pioneer in researching female psychology. Her husband, Felix Deutsch, gave lectures in English, together with Paul Federn, at this time. The atmosphere in the training institute was open and tolerant and was marked by scientific curiosity. Students and faculty were often friends with one another and met together regularly in their free time—to play cards at night, for instance—where “problem patients” were frequently reported on and keenly discussed (Reichmayr, 2004, pp. 147–148). It was primarily the young people associated with the psychoanalytic movement who viewed themselves as revolutionaries. Pedagogical and sociopolitical questions—e.g. school reform, sexual education, and adult education—were of particular concern to many psychoanalysts, especially those on the left, such as Siegfried Bernfeld, Otto Fenichel, and Anni and Wilhelm Reich. As a result, there was a close relationship with the Social Democratic Party in Vienna—especially with famous city councilman for health (and professor of anatomy) Julius Tandler. The WPV itself was, however, not politically active, and Freud himself was a liberal of the old school: “There was … a small [liberal] party, which once or twice put up a candidate in Freud’s district; when that happened, Freud would vote for him” (Jones, 1955, p. 435). Nevertheless, education was of great concern to many psychoanalysts. Anna Freud pioneered the field of child psychoanalysis and psychoanalytically influenced pedagogy. Together with Dorothy Burlingham and a few other psychoanalysts, she offered courses and seminars on child psychoanalysis. In 1936, they opened an experimental kindergarten run with a psychoanalytic point of view in mind, for toddlers at risk of neglect: the Jackson Nursery. It was closed in 1938, with the arrival of the Nazis. August Aichhorn, one of the most active psychoanalytic educators and a pioneer in social work with neglected youth, offered very popular courses for educators and social workers. He was, incidentally, one of the few members of the WPV associated with the Christian Socialist Party. In 1923 the first parenting support centre was established as part of the outpatient clinic; its first head was Hermine von Hug-Hellmuth. Children and young people from poor families were the beneficiaries of this new WPV facility. It was in this fertile atmosphere for psychoanalysis that Freud further developed his science of the unconscious: in 1920 he wrote Beyond the Pleasure Principle, in which he introduced the concepts of “repetition compulsion” and the “death drive” as contrasted with the “life drive” (libido). Group Psychology and the Analysis of the Ego followed in 1921 and, in 1923, The Ego and the Id, a work in which Freud introduced the concepts of the ego, the superego, and the id as the three parts of the psychic apparatus. In 1925, a propaganda committee was created to popularize psychoanalytic ideas; in 1927, it was expanded through the formation of an action committee. The goal of this committee was—through lectures and publications in medical magazines—to enlighten and win favour among those in the medical community who were sceptical of psychoanalysis. In March 1929, a department dedicated to treating psychotics was opened in the outpatient clinic. Its first head was Paul Schilder, who had been appointed as a professor of psychiatry in 1925. Schilder was the first to try to integrate psychiatry and psychoanalysis. He emigrated from Austria to the United States later in 1939 and was succeeded by Edward Bibring, treasurer of the WPV from 1928 to 1938. Furthermore, a second parenting support centre was built in 1925 as part of the outpatient clinic led by August Aichhorn, which he then also ran. At the start of 1932, parenting support was expanded and moved into its own rooms. In addition to Aichhorn, Anna Freud, Willi Hoffer, and Editha Sterba worked there. Yet at the start of the 1930s, the position of psychoanalysis began slowly to deteriorate. In 1933, the National Socialists came to power in Germany, and many German psychoanalysts immigrated to Austria. In March 1933, Austrian Chancellor Dollfuss curtailed all civil rights and turned what had been a democratically elected Christian Socialist government into an authoritarian corporate regime. In February 1934 a blue-collar riot was suppressed, but not before blood had been spilled. Freud had the feeling that he had to be increasingly careful in order to protect psychoanalysis from persecution, because there were also psychoanalysts among those arrested. For example, Josef Karl Friedjung—a socialist member of the town council as well as a paediatrician and freemason—was interned in the Wöllersdorf detention camp in 1934; in 1938, however, he was able to emigrate to Palestine. Martin Pappenheim, head of the psychiatric department in the Lainz hospital, was only able to avoid arrest as an active Social Democrat because he never returned from a series of lectures he had been giving in Palestine. Siegfried Bernfeld, founder and leader of the youth movement, went to France in 1934. Edith Buxbaum, a politically engaged secondary school teacher, was arrested in 1935 for her political activities and spent four weeks in jail. Felix and Helene Deutsch left Austria in 1935 for the United States, in part because they wanted to protect their son, who was actively resisting the corporate state [Ständestaat]. Edith Buxbaum’s arrest and the brief detention in Vienna of Marie Langer, who was undergoing analysis at the time—coupled with Edith Jacobson’s arrest in Berlin by the Gestapo for refusing to disclose details about a politically active patient of hers—led the WPV to tighten its so-called “abstinence commandment”: psychoanalysts and those in their care were forbidden to engage in political resistance, and psychoanalysts were not allowed to treat politically active patients. Thus, while individual psychoanalysts remained politically active, the WPV exercised restraint in its official politics. In this way, it was believed that the survival of psychoanalysis could be ensured. On this point, the President of the IPA, Ernest Jones, spoke out at the 13th IPA Congress in Lucerne in 1934. (It was at this convention, incidentally, that Wilhelm Reich was banned from the IPA.) Jones believed that it was useless, if not downright harmful, to protest against the fascists’ and National Socialists’ interference with psychoanalysis. Of this situation, Reich said sarcastically, “One is being beaten indeed—and yet remains genteel” (Reich, 1935, p. 59). Freud did not publish Moses and Monotheism (1939a [1937–39]) until he was safely in England, out of fear that he would lose the Catholic Church’s protection because of the arguments he advanced in the book: “and would conjure up a prohibition upon the work of the adherents and students of psycho-analysis in Austria” (Freud, 1939a [1937–39], p. 56). On the other hand, a common enemy brought the psychoanalysts together. Jeanne Lampl-de Groot, who had come to Vienna in 1933 with her husband, Hans Lampl, said:


… and I knew that it was stupid from a political side, and I always had the feeling when Hitler is in Germany he will come to Austria too. But it was then, at that time, that the analysis was so wonderful because then there were the Hartmanns, Bibrings, Waelders, Deutsches, the Krises, Maenchen, so we decided to go to Vienna. And that was the nicest time there analytically, you see…. Of course politically it was terrible. [Lampl-de Groot, 1973, p. 13; quoted in Reichmayr, 2004, p. 148]


Many psychoanalysts were of two minds about this: as private individuals, they faced political repression and restrictions; as psychoanalysts, however, they did not. Marie Langer, about the period:


The institute was filled at the time with Jewish colleagues who had fled, the majority of whom had come from Berlin. Everyone, from Freud on down, had denied the danger. To this day I do not understand … how they could shut their eyes to the obvious. Even Jewish colleagues who had to flee … appeared not at all to heed the ominous political developments in Austria. [Langer, 1986, pp. 91–92; quoted in Reichmayr, 2004, 149]


Viennese psychoanalysts were thus advised to forego all political activity; as a result, they withdrew more and more into a scientific cocoon; in this way, they hoped to avoid standing out in an authoritarian, fascist state, any more than they already did because of their status as “Jewish” psychoanalysts. Things were rather different in the sovereign nation of Czechoslovakia. In the sister city of Prague, there were regular discussions about how psychoanalysis could shed light on political and societal changes. And it was also in Prague that a psychoanalytic consortium was founded in 1933, which became affiliated with the WPV one year later. It bears mentioning that it was the politically active Social Democratic and Communist psychoanalysts in particular who went to Prague, because they ran the constant danger of being arrested in Austria: Steffi Bornstein (the last chairperson in Prague before Hitler’s invasion), Frances Deri, Otto Fenichel (who had already fled to Oslo in 1933 and headed for Prague in 1935), and Anni Reich. At the same time, the Viennese Institute was growing in the 1930s due to the influx of psychoanalysts who had fled from Berlin to Vienna, although the number of domestic candidates was reduced compared to those from other countries because the scholarships that had previously gone to poor candidates dried up with the economic crisis of 1931. In total, however, the WPV grew, and the old rooms in the general hospital’s cardiology unit were too small. A new meeting place had to be found: Berggasse 7, not far from Freud’s apartment, was settled upon. For the first time, it was possible to have the Society, the outpatient clinic, the training institute, and the publishing house under one roof. There was a large assembly hall for 50 people, a library, several treatment rooms, and a kitchen. Richard Sterba said, “We had the feeling that we’d arrived” (Sterba, 1985, p. 158). For Freud’s 80th birthday in 1936, newly refurbished rooms were unveiled. This was the final apex in the first history of the WPV. Thomas Mann came to Vienna to deliver a laudatory speech entitled “Freud and the Future”, and psychoanalysts from various countries also arrived, including Ludwig Binswanger from Switzerland, who presented an address on “Freud’s Conception of Man in the Light of Anthropology”—but no one came from Germany. The situation became increasingly dangerous, and, after Hitler’s Anschluss of Austria on 12 March 1938, it was unbearable, especially for Jews: they were attacked on the street, forced to scrub the streets, knocked down, robbed. Apartments and stores were plundered and destroyed, while those who resisted were brutally abused; people were carried off and deported. The oppression was so enormous that hundreds of people committed suicide. Jews were robbed and persecuted as rapidly as possible; Jewish fortunes were quickly, systematically, and efficiently appropriated. The Vienna Psychoanalytic Society, the psychoanalytic outpatient clinic, and the international psychoanalytic publishing house were also affected: just two days after Hitler’s annexation of Austria, the meeting place in Berggasse 7 was searched and seized. The publishing house was no longer active, though it was in possession of bonds, cash, currencies, savings, and 15 carloads of books valued at 3 million Reichmarks. (Later on, a “race institute” moved into the WPV’s former quarters in the Berggasse.) Dr Anton Sauerwald, a chemist who was not so negatively disposed towards Freud, was appointed the interim head of the psychoanalytic organization. On Sunday, 13 March, Anna Freud called a board meeting in Freud’s apartment, at which it was decided that everyone who had the opportunity would flee the country, and that the Society’s headquarters would be wherever Sigmund Freud made his new home. The WPV met for a final time on 20 March. Among the participants were Anton Sauerwald, Ernest Jones, Princess Marie of Greece, Anna Freud, Carl Müller-Braunschweig, and several Viennese psychoanalysts. It was decided that the German Psychoanalytical Society (GPS) would hold the Society’s funds, rights, and obligations in trust. However, shortly thereafter the GPS was dissolved. The Göring Institute in Germany was its successor, and the trust had to be given over to the state medical board; the WPV’s assets were thereby lost in their entirety. On 1 September 1938, the WPV was officially dissolved and its facilities closed. On account of a good international network and the swift help of foreign candidates, the majority of Viennese psychoanalysts were able to leave Austria between May and July 1938. Of the many who helped, Mary O’Neill Hawkins—who remained to help out in Vienna until all psychoanalysts had left—and Edith Jackson—who made available a small fortune—are representative (Pappenheim, 2004). Only August Aichhorn, Alfred von Winterstein, and Richard Nepalleck remained in Vienna. Sterba was one of the first to emigrate, even though Jones had been unwilling to help him as he hoped he would stay in Vienna as “a memory of psychoanalysis for a happier future” (Sterba, 1985, p. 168). A few days after Sterba’s departure, Jones exclaimed, “Oy, our Shabbes goi is gone” (Sterba, 1985, 170). Else Pappenheim, then a candidate of the WPV, bitterly draws attention to the fact that the extraordinary assistance was offered only to members, not to candidates (Pappenheim, 2004). Also, loved ones who were not members of the immediate family could not count on help—even four of Freud’s sisters, all very advanced in years, were eventually deported and murdered. Due to international pressure and with the help of Marie Bonaparte and Ernest Jones, Freud was able to leave Vienna for London on 4 June 1938. London thus became the centre of psychoanalysis, even though the majority of psychoanalysts (52 of them) had fled to the United States. And yet not all were able to save themselves. Many psychoanalysts and former members of the WPV were murdered or died as a result of their persecution: Alfred Bass was deported to the Łódź ghetto in 1941 and killed there; Alfred Deutsch was killed in Theresienstadt in 1943; Margarethe Hilferding was killed in 1942 while in transit from Theresienstadt to Maly Trostinec; Salomea Kempner was killed in the Warsaw ghetto; Karl Landauer was killed in 1945, shortly before Bergen-Belsen was liberated; Alfred Meisl was killed in 1942 in Theresienstadt; David Ernst Oppenheim was killed in 1943 in Theresienstadt; Isidor Isaak Sadger was killed in 1942 in Theresienstadt; Sabine Spielrein, together with her two daughters, was shot to death in 1941 in Rostov-on-Don; and Nikola Sugar was killed in 1945 in Theresienstadt. Gustav Grüner died in 1941 while fleeing to New York; Ernst Paul Hoffmann died in 1944 as a result of his internment by the Nazis; Adolf Josef Storfer perished in 1944 in Melbourne; and Rosa Walk died after jumping out of a window in Paris to avoid arrest by the Nazis. Among the psychoanalysts who had remained in Vienna, only August Aichhorn—who had for years been in close contact with the Freud family—continued to work in the field. In 1938, he became a member of the German Göring Institute. Aichhorn “inherited” the few remaining candidates of the Society. Between 1938 and 1944 he led fourteen training analyses and six control analyses. He offered courses and seminars that were partly recognized by the Göring Institute as educational, and he also arranged pertinent evening discussions. Additionally, he had a private practice as a psychoanalyst. In the summer of 1944, Aichhorn closed his practice and left Vienna for fear that his secret activities would endanger himself or his patients. Indeed, there were actually a handful of people in his circle who were active in the underground resistance and who tried to help Jews. Kurt and Ella Lingens and Karl von Montesiczky, a grandson of Anna von Lieben (who gained fame as “Cäcilie M” in Freud’s Studies on Hysteria), were arrested. Ella Lingens and Karl von Montesiczky were deported to Auschwitz; he died there of typhus in 1943, while she was lucky enough to survive. Her husband, Kurt Lingens, was sent to the Eastern front as a punishment, where he sustained severe injuries but survived the war. The group that had gathered around August Aichhorn to complete their psychoanalytic training—under, one imagines, very trying circumstances—was also the group that brought the WPV back to life in 1946. Aichhorn assumed the presidency. Alfred von Winterstein—who had been a WPV member prior to 1938 but who, because of partial Jewish ancestry, had withdrawn during the Nazi regime—was elected to the executive committee. The goal was to rebuild and preserve Freud’s damaged legacy—no small task. The greatest challenge facing the new Society was, in Aichhorn’s opinion, providing in-depth training to new psychoanalysts. He wanted some of the psychoanalysts who had been persecuted to return to Vienna, at least temporarily, to help him with this task, but only Otto Fleischmann in Budapest and Robert Jokl in France answered his call. On 10 April 1946, a celebratory session was held to present the new WPV to the public. In the same year, the WPV was recognized—provisionally, at first—as a member organization of the IPA. But the initial euphoria could not mask the fact that the Zeitgeist in Austria was not conducive to psychoanalysis. The rebuilding process was laborious, and neither the mood nor the financial situation of the psychoanalysts were good. It is no wonder, then, that some psychoanalysts—including Fleischmann and Jokl—left Vienna again. In a letter to Aichhorn, Jokl wrote: “Two and a half years after my return I had no apartment, no adequate income … it was impossible to continue living in this way” (letter to August Aichhorn dated 17 February 1949; quoted in Huber, 1977, p. 81). By 1950, the Society had only a single candidate in training. Psychoanalysis in Vienna found itself in a crisis, having well-nigh reached a stalemate, also regarding the further development of psychoanalytic work. Finally, Winterstein, at the time President of the WPV, thought “it will soon be no longer possible to keep the Society alive” (in a letter to Carl Müller-Braunschweig, dated 22 August 1950; quoted in Huber, 1977, p. 85). With incredible effort, however, a training institute was re-established and the number of candidates increased. During the tenure of President Wilhelm Solms-Rödelheim, a Central European Convention was set up towards the end of the 1950s. In November 1961, the WPV opened up a department for child therapy that remained in existence for many years. And yet the still conservative Zeitgeist in Austria hindered the spread of psychoanalytic ideas and concepts, and psychoanalysis found scant societal recognition. With the onset of student activism in the late 1960s and the burgeoning of a liberal, cosmopolitan, intellectual current in the early 1970s, the general public in Austria became more interested in psychoanalysis. In this new dawn, the 27th IPA Congress was held in Vienna in 1971, which for Anna Freud marked the first time she returned to the city after her expulsion. (It was also, incidentally, the first time the IPA Congress was held in Vienna.) This climax in the Society’s post-war history had the important effect of strengthening its self-worth, and it also witnessed a noticeable increase—from 16 to 37—in the number of people who registered for psychoanalytic training (Huber, 1977, p. 94). Psychoanalysis began to open itself up more to society, and a sign of this increased openness was the fact that, for the first time, journalists were allowed to attend a congress of the IPA. But society also increasingly opened itself up to psychoanalysis as well: the Sigmund Freud Society was established in 1968 with support from the conservative government then in power, and, because students were especially interested in psychoanalysis, Austria’s first professorship and institute for depth psychology and psychotherapy were created. A WPV member, Hans Strotzka, became the institute’s head. Under President Harald Leupold-Löwenthal, basic regulations for training in child analysis were set. Beginning in the 1970s, there were also occasional debates about the Nazi past of the WPV that resulted in profound conflicts and the breakdown of friendships. The wounds were slow to scar over. In 1999, after a 61-year break, candidates took the initiative to reopen the Vienna Psychoanalytic Outpatient Clinic. As it had before the war, the outpatient clinic served those with few or no financial means and provided candidates with patients on whom to conduct control analyses. At present, an area for child therapy and analysis is being set up in the outpatient clinic. In 2006, under President Christine Diercks’s leadership, the WPV established—together with the Vienna Provisional Society of Psychoanalysis (which for a number of years has been a Study Group of the IPA)—the Viennese Psychoanalytic Academy. The academy sees itself as a publicly accessible centre for psychoanalysis and its application in theory and practice. The Sigmund Freud lectures coordinated by the academy have hit upon keen interest among the public. The WPV now has 98 members as well as 81 candidates—representing all the important internationally recognized streams of psychoanalysis. Otto Kernberg’s congratulations to the WPV on “facing up to the challenges involved in overcoming the discontinuation with the past” (Kernberg quoted in Bronner, 2008, p. 7) are testimony to the fact that this has not always been easy; eventually, however, the third history has become a successful one. Thoughts for the times on war and death. Standard Edition, 14. Freud, S. (1920g). Beyond the Pleasure Principle. Standard Edition, 18. Freud, S. (1921c). Group Psychology and the Analysis of the Ego. Erinnerungen eines Wiener Psychoanalytikers. Frankfurt am Main: Fischer Taschenbuchverlag.







2Belgium What if Freud had stopped over in Brussels?

Serge Frisch

It is very difficult for psychoanalytic associations to analyse the primal scenes of their own founding because these are linked to derivatives in the unconscious of interwoven transference and countertransference residues, often unresolved—and therefore passionate and/or symbiotic. The complex nature of such a situation, the meaning of which we often fail to grasp, must not prevent those who are responsible for the institution from maintaining it, although in doing so they may find themselves ignoring any residual trauma or defects in functioning. Vienna and London are two cities inextricably linked in the collective memory of psychoanalysts since Freud, fleeing the Nazi tyranny of Vienna, sought refuge in London. The European Psychoanalytical Federation (EPF) Conference took place in 2008 in Vienna, and the 2010 conference was held in London. In moving from Vienna to London, the EPF Conference stopped, in 2009, in Brussels. That led me to wonder: what if Freud, too, had stopped over in Brussels on his way from Vienna to London? As we read in a letter he wrote to his fiancée on 13 October 1885, Freud the tourist did stop in Brussels for three and a half hours in 1885 (Jones, 1953–1957, Vol. I, pp. 182–183). He wrote: “Brussels was wonderfully beautiful, an enormous town with splendid buildings” (p. 182). Freud the psychoanalyst, however, never returned to Brussels: in 1910, he refused an invitation to participate in a congress in Brussels, preferring instead to send Jones and Seif (Cod-dens, 2008). Freud the psychoanalyst, then, never stayed in Brussels. Had he done so, then perhaps he would have written a paper on Using different forms of unconscious communication,1 thus revolutionizing his conception of the unconscious, a topic that we will be analysing in some detail. Freud, then, did not bring the plague to Belgium. What would psychoanalysis be like in Belgium nowadays if we had come into that inheritance? Would the “Freud effect” have given us a boost, so that we might not have remained for quite so long one of the smallest and most discreet psychoanalytic societies in the whole of Europe? In other words, had Freud stopped over in Brussels, would psychoanalysis have had a different impact in modern-day Belgium? When we discuss our psychoanalytic society, we cannot avoid evoking the institutional primal scene and family romance. If Freud the psychoanalyst had stopped over in Brussels, our primal scene fantasies would probably have been different. However, if the great man himself had been a background object, would Belgian psychoanalysts have developed more confidently? Or, conversely, have Belgian psychoanalysts, quietly developing in the shadow of the great European societies, given more weight to their own modest, non-grandiose sense of identity—what I would call a kind of psychoanalytic Belgeness, a kind of psychoanalytic humility, which means that Belgian analysts do not think of themselves as the centre of the universe—at least of the psychoanalytic universe. When I became President of the Belgian Psychoanalytical Society (BPS) in June 2008, I wanted to know something about the genealogy of which I was now a part: who were the presidents who preceded me? To my astonishment, I discovered that there is no official register in which I could find the names of the former presidents of our Society. It was not possible to find such a list in the secretariat of the Society—yet the Belgian Society is a relatively young one, having been founded on Christmas Eve, 24 December 1946, by three lay analysts: Maurice Dugautiez, Fernand Lechat, and Camille Lechat. Dugautiez, who was an electrician by trade, had always been interested in hypnosis, spiritualism, and other kinds of esoteric ideas before he discovered psychoanalysis. Fernand Lechat worked in insurance, and Camille Lechat was a primary school teacher (Luminet, 1962). My desire to find the list of my predecessors bears witness to my curiosity as regards the origins of the Society and to my wish to take my place within that genealogical and historical dimension. According to Freud, the destiny of each individual depends on his relationships with others (Freud, 1933a [1932]); I would interpret that as meaning that the destiny of each individual analyst depends on his or her relationships with other analysts—that is, with past and present colleagues, with his or her own analyst or analysts, and with the analytic institution to which he or she belongs. Kaës reminds us that “Our origins are precisely what elude us, from which we are irredeemably absent, that which we cannot grasp in the very movement by which we come into being in and through the desire of another, and moreover of another who precedes us” (Kaës, 1993, p. 3). Or, as Jacques André wrote (2008, p. 13): “The unconscious … always brings us closer to the origins of the universe: that uncanny factor is the most secretly familiar of all.” We are beings of the unconscious, shaped by our own inheritance. The unconscious handing down of that inheritance unfolds via the highly complex network of our origins and our relationships, not only familial, but also group-based. That unconscious is shaped by the life and past history of the institutions—as well as by the footnotes of that history—to which we belong, institutions that have wanted us, thought about us, waited for us, in which we have grown up under the watchful eye of our predecessors and with the expectation that others will follow us. This is what Kaës (2003, p. viii) has called primal violence or anticipatory violence, which, quoting Piera Aulagnier, “is born of every anticipatory project because it is that which, in the mind, imposes itself from the outside, at the cost of a primary violation of a space and an activity that obeys laws that are at variance with the I and with discourse”. As psychoanalysts, we are permeated by the unconscious, influenced by the unconscious of the institution to which we belong, which existed long before we became psychoanalysts. In that sense, our analytic societies are the locus in which unconscious elements are produced. We may also become the depositories of elements of the group unconscious, some of which have remained unprocessed by the group—they have remained encysted “raw material” handed down from one generation to the next. In a retroactive movement, each of us—and each generation—has to reflect on and reshape those unconscious impregnations and reconstruct the sequence of generations. The legacy that Freud left to the psychoanalytic societies was the task of handing down psychoanalysis from one generation to the next: not simply psychoanalytic theory and knowledge, but, in a more unconscious way, the founding father also imposed on psychoanalytic institutions a certain obligation to pass on the psychoanalytic tradition. We could surmise that the obligation to transmit psychoanalysis binds together all the members of a society around an unconscious pact—one to which we all adhered when we became members of the International Psychoanalytical Association. What, then, were the unconscious pacts that formed the Belgian Society? How did it come about that there is no documentary record, not only of the presidents of the Society but also of the various administrative committees of the BPS and its members over the past 60 years? With those questions in mind and the feeling of astonishment to which they gave rise in me, I began to search for concrete evidence of the primal scenes of the Belgian Society, its foundation and founding members, in an attempt to uncover my own family romance of the BPS. I therefore went back to the original members of our Society: the presidents from 1946 to 1960, Maurice Dugautiez and Fernand Lechat, who died within a few months of each other, in 1959 and 1960, respectively. I then remembered that one of my own supervisors had spoken to me about Ernst Paul Hoffmann, a Viennese psychoanalyst who had moved to Belgium after the Anschluss in Austria. Hoffmann was a Jewish analyst of Romanian origin, who had been analysed by Paul Federn. My supervisor had told me that Hoffmann was a pupil of Ferenczi’s, the enfant terrible of psychoanalysis. With a malicious twinkle in her eye, she added that it was perhaps for that reason that the Belgian Society is so open to and tolerant of new ideas. It would indeed be interesting to analyse Ferenczi’s unconscious influence on our Society. After some active research with colleagues in Vienna, Paris, and Switzerland, I was able to discover traces of Hoffmann and to learn more about his life. It is interesting to note that, as far as I am aware, the name Hoffmann appears on only one official document of the Belgian Society. His surname is mentioned—misspelt, as it happens—but there is no record of his given names. What, then, might all that mean for our Society—the complete absence of any mention of Hoffmann in our institutional memory, the misspelling of his name, the vagueness about how he died? Could this amount to some kind of repression as regards our origins? Could this be evidence of an unease felt with respect to this first psychoanalyst to have settled in Belgium,2 of a lack of enthusiasm at the idea of being indebted to a foreigner who was just passing through in those troubled times, even though he belonged to a circle of people quite close to Freud himself? Ernst Paul Hoffmann lived in Brussels from 1938 to 1940. It was thanks to him that Dugautiez and Lechat began their analysis, although they had set themselves up as psychoanalysts in 1934, supervised by Marie Bonaparte. Hoffmann was arrested in 1940 and deported to the south of France, where he was interned in the St Cyprien and Gurs camps. He escaped and fled to Switzerland, where he died after a surgical operation in 1944. Thus the origins of psychoanalysis in Belgium were immediately accompanied by disappearance and death. Hoffmann was the analyst of the three founding members of the Belgian Society: no doubt he passed on to them something of the atmosphere of psychoanalytic Vienna as it then was, with its direct reference to Freud. I can easily imagine that, through Hoffmann, Freud the psychoanalyst did indeed stop over in Brussels. It is probable that, without those tragic historical events, Hoffmann would have become the first president of the Belgian Society … if, of course, he had settled in Brussels. I would argue that here there is a condensation of origins and death, between presidency and death. Hoffmann’s arrest, deportation, and death occurred in a historical context that was emotionally and professionally very difficult for the pioneers of Belgian psychoanalysis. We can imagine how hard it must have been to process the work of mourning and reach adequate symbolization after Hoffmann’s death. The arrest and death of Hoffmann have been left outside the BPS and have not been integrated into the history of our Society. This death has not been “historicized”, transformed by the different generations of analysts in the BPS. With his arrest, deportation and death, the origins of our psychoanalytic history disappeared. What is interesting is the fact that, some years later, we will come across a repetition of the disappearance of our past history. After Lechat’s death in 1959 and Dugautiez’s in 1960, the Belgian Society went through an institutional crisis: no one was prepared to accept the presidency. In the end, Dr Thérèse Jacobs, trained in Paris, agreed, at her colleagues’ insistence, to take on the role of President. She was to be the first of a series of women presidents, all of them medically trained, with the result that for many years the BPS had the quite erroneous reputation of being a medical and matriarchal society (Roudinesco, 1997). In 1960, the Association of Belgian Psychoanalysts changed its statues and its name, becoming the Belgian Psychoanalytical Society. As Danièle Flagey (2008) points out, some reshaping of the Society was absolutely necessary if the quality of training offered to candidates was to be improved. All the same, it could well be that the act of changing the statutes and the name of the Society by its members was an enactment that enabled the group to avoid any true mourning for the rapid and sudden loss of its male founding members. Twice over, the founding members suddenly died; as a result, twice over, “the act of foundation was to some extent de-symbolized and taken over by a repetition of the murderous scenario of its origins … condensing death-wishes against every possible image of a founding father” (Kaës, 2007, p. 56). During the following two decades, the Society closed in on itself; to an outside observer, it appeared somewhat immobile and fixed in its ways (Coddens, 2008). We could perhaps hypothesize that the Belgian Society was going through a depressive phase, because the young analysts who had to take over the destiny of the institution had lost their founding members too early. If we think of Totem and Taboo (Freud, 1912–13), we could say that the children were depressed after the murder of the founding fathers. For the next 20 years, the same analyst presided over the training committee. This could be understood as an indication at that time of the fragile nature of the very structure of the Belgian Society—its survival hung by a thread. Yet, according to some, in spite of that precarious situation, the training committee, “terrorized whole generations of trainees”. After the sons had killed the founding fathers, the mothers, like Gorgons, devoured their potential offspring. We could view this situation as an expression of the unease experienced by that young generation of analysts, worried about the future of their Society, because the trainees—who do not always correspond to one’s ideals and expectations—were unable to allay their anxiety about death as regards the future of the analytic institution itself. Perhaps those 20 years were necessary for transforming the murderous fantasies of the primal horde and detoxifying the anxiety related to them. It was only thereafter that that primal horde, now transformed, could be seen as a group of analysts able to work within an institutional context and to discover their creative independence. During the two decades of withdrawal into itself, the institutional structures—which have lasted to this day—were gradually worked out. In 1975 the Extended Committee was set up. A think tank in the true sense of the word, it has no powers of decision; at present it has more than 30 members, and it fulfils a necessary “third-party” role with respect to the traditional committees of every psychoanalytic association: executive committee, training committee, ethics committee, and so on. During this period, the same small number of analysts were appointed to these committees. The creation of the third-party structure, the Extended Committee, enabled the BPS to move from being bogged down in depression, from its unprocessed mourning after the death of two of its founding members. Once the Extended Committee was set up, the more private realm encompassing psychoanalytic relationships full of unresolved transferences and the social and institutional spheres could be more clearly differentiated from one another so that institutional life could begin to function. It is certainly not by chance that shortly afterwards, beginning in 1980, some of our members began to participate in international organizations—first the EPF, then the IPA. I am thinking here particularly of Annette Watillon, Jacqueline Godfrind, Maurice Haber, André Alsteens, and Jim Innes-Smith. But history was once again to repeat itself: 20 years later, in 2001, our Society went through another wrenching crisis, fraught with passion and unaccustomed violence. There is no need to go into any detail about the reasons, major or minor, that sparked off the crisis; but when I look back on it from the point that I have chosen here, I can see that it had the same ingredients as previous crises (Dispaux, 2008). With hindsight, we could say that certain trends in the institutional unconscious brought about this new crisis in the Society. Let us take a look at the context in which that crisis burst upon us like a storm on an otherwise clear day. In August 1998, André Alsteens died, and in January 1999, Jim Innes-Smith died. Once again, two of our most eminent colleagues, who held important institutional appointments, died within a few months of each other. Both had been presidents of our Society some years earlier, and Jim Innes-Smith was, at the time of his death, president-elect of the EPF. Those colleagues died just as the baton was about to be passed on from one generation to the next. In the crisis that followed, the suffering of the members of the BPS inhibited our thinking, including our capacity to reflect calmly on the institutional issues facing our Society in today’s world. It should not be forgotten that the crisis broke out at a time when, once again, major structural changes were being envisaged, among them the abolition of the specific role of training analysts. These structural changes were then put on hold for several years. The traumatic history, so difficult to process, was repeated once more. As in 1960, no one from the following generation wanted to take over, so that once again three former presidents, all of them women—Jacqueline Godfrind, Nicole Minazio, and Marie-France Dispaux—took up the challenge in their stead. Once again we can see the traumatic impact of the link between the deaths of former presidents who played a major institutional role, structural changes, and—this is a hypothesis—the unconscious re-surfacing of insufficiently worked through mourning. “When death occurs suddenly, taking over an inheritance is a violent act, experienced as theft, a forcible and dangerous appropriation” (Kaës, 2007, p. 66). According to Kaës (2003, p. ix), destructive violence is the outcome of a failure to symbolize the primal or anticipatory violence that is expressed when the functions of a structure designed for creating symbols are attacked or found to be deficient. Repetition involves the distant past, the most archaic of object relations, and, when all is said and done, has to do with the very foundations of an institution, its establishment, its identity, and the relationships between its members. I would argue that those two crises, in 1960 and in 2001, both of which had to do with the death of male presidents, re-evoked an unprocessed trauma that repeated the initial crisis, which followed the disappearance of that very first psychoanalyst, Ernst Paul Hoff-mann. In each of those crises, our institution and its members had to deal with disappearance, death, mourning, and the pitfalls of handing down psychic life and death from one generation to the next. What could not be processed was simply deposited in the following generations. It is important to acknowledge the fact that when the 2001 crisis broke, the office-holders of the Society, with a great deal of courage, asked Gilles Amado, a psychoanalyst external to the Society, to undertake an analysis of the institutional elements involved. In my view, that institutional analysis—which, surprisingly, had little to say about issues relating to mourning—nevertheless helped us to break free of a certain number of projections and deep splittings. This, in turn, enabled us to recover our composure in dealing with the work of our institution. Our internal cohesion and the confidence we had in our ability to deal with internal conflicts were stronger as a result of those trials and tribulations. We could perhaps wonder how much space was devoted to memory in the above struggles. As I have mentioned, our Society did not—at least as far as I am aware—devote a single paper to Hoffmann, whether in the Revue or in our internal newsletter. No trace of him survives, hence a loss of part of our collective memory. I was also astonished to discover, in the course of my researches, that the archives of our Society are not kept in one place but are scattered about in the cellars of several members, including my own. More recently, I learned that an important part of the archives relating to the very early years of our Society are in the keeping of a Lacanian analyst who has never in fact been a member of our Society but who—at least until now—has steadfastly refused to return them to us. Part of our history, therefore, no longer belongs to us. Let us hope that that part is simply repressed and therefore capable of being recovered, rather than permanently and absolutely lost, as though it were split-off. When, as I have described, the fantasy of death is roaming about, with no adequate symbolization, we have to ask ourselves what might be the consequences for the scientific life of our institution and for the scientific ability and creativeness of its members. In its early days, the Belgian Society had been an offshoot of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society. At the 11th Congress of French-speaking psychoanalysts, held in Brussels during Whitsun 1948, Germaine Guex presented a paper, “Aggressiveness as a Reaction to Anxiety about Being Abandoned”, which could not have left the young Belgian Society indifferent; it was just two years old at the time, had just lost Hoffmann, and depended entirely on Paris for the training of its future members! In such a context, autonomy is difficult to achieve. A major turning-point occurred in 1962–63, when the Paris Psychoanalytical Society (PPS) decided to impose “an obligatory seminar that was to be financed by” those Belgian analysts who wanted to become full members of the Belgian Society. The Belgians agreed to the principle behind the project, but showed their hostility towards it by responding with the utmost passivity, first by ignoring the French request and then by always finding problems with the meeting dates suggested by the French. Thus the Belgians deprecated the French idea. For me, that was the first and very hesitant step towards our Society gaining its independence from its French counterpart. The road, however, was to prove long and difficult before our Society could go from being “mothered” by the PPS to its present state of being, in Dispaux’s words (2008), the latter’s “younger sister”—a long and difficult road perhaps not only for the Belgian Society. It was only in 1972, more than 25 years after its founding, that for the first time a Belgian psychoanalyst presented a Scientific Report to the Congress of French-speaking psychoanalysts held that year in Brussels. Danièle Flagey had the honour of presenting that Scientific Report; her topic was … intellectual inhibition. I do not know whether Danièle Flagey—who has had a major influence on our Society on both scientific and human levels—would agree with me, but her choice of such a topic—itself an interesting subject, and her development of it was brilliant—would seem in all probability to have had something to do with what was happening in the unconscious of our Society at the time. Danièle Flagey has authorized me to share the following anecdote.3 Whenever, as a young analyst, she wanted to say something spontaneously during international congresses, the—female—analyst who had taken over the presidency of our Society after the death of its founding fathers instructed her to keep quiet, surmising that her wish to take the floor was, in the President’s eyes, suspect and probably indicated that she needed a further spell of analysis. This little illustration clearly bears witness to the exaggerated hierarchy and obeisance to authority that reigned in our Society at the time. Similarly, it is interesting to note that almost all of our members who held university posts (N. Dopchie, C. Bloch, J. Flament, P. van Reeth, F. Duyckaerts, etc.) left the Analytical Society—as if there were some kind of insuperable incompatibility of thinking in the BPS between being a psychoanalyst and being a university lecturer. Ever since Freud’s day, psychoanalysts have produced a considerable quantity of scientific writings. Our internal newsletter, however, just happened to cease publication in 1960; it would not again be published until 1977—that is, immediately after the setting up of the Extended Committee. The Revue Belge de Psychanalyse was first published in 1982 thanks to the sheer determination and creative energy of Maurice Haber, in spite of all the opposition he confronted. I am particularly pleased to announce that the Revue Belge de Psychanalyse will be the first French-language journal to be featured on the Psychoanalytic Electronic Publishing (PEP) website. As a result, Belgian ideas about psychoanalysis will soon be readily available to all. I may be mistaken here, but I think that it was only in the 1990s that our members—Léon Vanek, Jacqueline Godfrind, and, later, Michèle van Lysebeth, Danièle Flagey, and others—first dared to publish their books: Recently, however, there has been a remarkable output of books written or co-edited by many of our colleagues and, indeed, trainees. I hope that this is an indication that the younger generations are not to the same extent the depositories of the encystments of their elders and that the intellectual inhibition that comes from that inheritance has been lifted, so that the younger generations can express their own creativeness. In much the same way as Brussels is the capital of Europe and the meeting-point of different cultures, so the Belgian Psychoanalytical Society has been marked by various psychoanalytic schools and currents of thought. Of course, given our history, we feel close to French psychoanalysis, and the training model that we have adopted is based on the French one. We have also, nonetheless, in our own way incorporated the various contributions of Klein, Winnicott, and Bion, to name but a few. If there exists an institutional unconscious in our psychoanalytic societies, then, that being the case, they will have to deal with crises, and their institutional functioning will inevitably and regularly be called into question. Human beings do not control their unconscious, nor can psychoanalytic societies do so. It is true also that, when psychoanalytic societies generate derivatives of the unconscious, they do not know to any great extent how to deal with the manifestations of these derivatives within their structures; this is all the more so since, as Jacques André (2008, p. 28) writes: “There is no reason for the unconscious to undertake an analysis, it is quite against such a step.” Yet it is thanks to that unconscious, rebellious and yet familiar, intolerable and yet heimlich, that the specific nature of psychoanalysis will always be relevant in the modern world. Notes 1. “Using Different Forms of Unconscious Communication” was the title of the EPF Conference held in Brussels in 2009. 2. The first Belgian psychoanalyst was in fact Julien Varendonck, but he had no connection with the BPS. He was a member of the Dutch Psychoanalytical Society. He died suddenly in 1924, without having trained any followers (Coddens, 2008; Labbé, 1955). 3. I am extremely grateful to Danièle Flagey for her permission to use this very telling and valuable anecdote. Bibliography André, J. (2008). L’événement et la temporalité.
