21Argentina Psychoanalysis in Argentina

Gilda Sabsay Foks

In 1910, the psychiatrist Fernando Greve gave a talk, entitled “Psychology and Psychotherapy of Certain Anguished States”, in the Neurology–Psychiatry–Anthropology–Legal Medicine section of the International Congress of Medicine and Hygiene in Buenos Aires. This lecture marked the first time that Freud’s work was discussed in Argentina. Greve described it as a clinical practice based on free association, which emphasized the importance of sexuality in the genesis of neurosis. In 1926, 16 years later, another psychiatrist, Enrique Mouchet, wrote on the meaning of psychoanalysis. Three years later a distinguished Argentine psychiatrist, Gregorio Berman, met with Freud, who noted in his diary Berman’s paper, “Psychology of Narcissism”. Berman also met, in Paris, with the French psychoanalyst Angelo Hesnard. In 1930, the Argentine psychiatrist Jorge Thenon published Psychotherapy and Psychogenesis (Thenon, 1930b) and sent the book to Freud, whose response was published in Revista el Hospital Argentino (1930):


My dear colleague: I receive with joy every new proof that the geographical barriers have been overcome, and for that reason I celebrate with satisfaction the fact that in far-off Argentina, our psychoanalytic problems and our points of view are carefully analysed, and this is promoting the production of scientific and precise works. I propose to you that you write a lengthy summation of your thesis, to be published in our International Journal of Psycho-Analysis. Wishing you the best wishes for the furtherance of your labors, cordially, yours truly, Freud. [Thenon, 1933a]


In 1930, the López Ballesteros edition of Freud’s works was published in Buenos Aires. The 1930s were characterized by the spread of psychoanalysis in intellectual and scientific circles. For example, in 1936, the magazine Psychotherapy, published by Gregorio Berman, dedicated its September issue to Freud, and a group of Argentine writers, headed by Javier Bóveda, went so far as to invite Freud to come and live in Argentina. In declining the invitation Freud replied: “I can read the beautiful language in which you write, and I understand it, but I have no confidence in my ability to write in it.” This was a reference to the fact that in his adolescence Freud and a fellow student had founded the Spanish Academy, with Castilian as its secret language. Thus in this vibrant cultural climate the psychoanalytic movement was rapidly developing in Buenos Aires. But political developments in Europe—the Spanish Civil War and the looming threat of war—were also being closely followed in Argentina. People arriving in Buenos Aires from the Old World, wishing to work in tranquillity and peace, found a cultural and intellectual milieu that was very receptive to psychoanalysis. In fact, though, until 1940 there were no trained psychoanalysts in Buenos Aires, only essentially self-taught psychiatrists like Pizarro Crespo and Jorge Thenon, who were engaged with psychoanalysis. In 1942, however, the Asociación Psicoanalítica Argentina (APA) [Argentine Psychoanalytic Association] was founded by six individuals who had had a more formal exposure to and training in psychoanalysis: Celes Ernesto Cárcamo, Angel Garma, Guillermo Ferrari Hardoy, Enrique Pichón-Riviere, Arnaldo Rascovksy, and Marie Langer. As a group, their personal histories reflect the diversity of Argentine Society as well as the nightmarish political developments in Europe. But their distinctive ideological, social, and religious backgrounds are overshadowed by their shared commitment to the new and revolutionary ideas of psychoanalysis. Cárcamo [1903–90], a descendant of an aristocratic and Catholic family, had perhaps first learned of Freud’s work when he attended talks given by James Mapelli, an Italian, at the Castex Clinic, at the Hospital de Clinicas. Mapelli’s therapeutic ideas were influenced by Freud’s early use of hypnotism and suggestion, and results obtained using his psychotherapeutic principles aroused much interest. Cárcamo was also impressed by Fernando Gorriti’s (1930) book, which described curing a psychotic possession through dream analysis. In 1936, aware of the limitations of Mapelli’s approach, Cárcamo moved to France and underwent psychoanalytic training at the Psychoanalytic Institute in Paris. His analyst was Paul Schiff, his clinical work was supervised by Rudolf Loewenstein and Charles Odier, and he attended seminars given by Odier, De Saussure, and Heinz Hartmann. In 1943 he published a work of applied psychoanalysis, “The Plumed Serpent”. In 1938 Angel Garma [1904–93] arrived in Argentina as an exile from Spain and the Spanish Civil War. Garma had been trained at the Berlin Psychoanalytical Institute, where his analyst was Theodor Reik. Garma, deeply influenced by his training in Berlin, brought this influence with him to Buenos Aires. His doctoral dissertation, Psychoanalysis of Dreams (1940), was the first doctoral dissertation on psychoanalysis in Latin America. “Garma’s research primarily involved the theory of the traumatic genesis of dreams, the characteristics of dream-like thought, the origin of dream hallucinations, and the relation to reality” (Etchegoyen, 2005). Beyond his leading role in the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association, Garma also actively promoted psychoanalysis throughout Latin America. In the 1930s Arnaldo Rascovsky [1907–95] and Enrique Pichón-Rivière [1907–77] discovered Freud’s work and began to lecture about his ideas and apply psychoanalytic techniques in their clinical practice. Rascovsky was from a Jewish family and had leftist tendencies and an interest in revolutionary ideas. As a paediatrician at the Children’s Hospital in Buenos Aires, his research was focused on foetal psychology. In 1937 he formed a Study Group whose participants included his wife Matilde, Luisa Alvarez de Toledo, Simón Wencelblat, Flora Scolnik, Alberto Tagliaferro, and Guillermo Ferrari Hardoy. Ferrari Hardoy subsequently retired and no longer participated in the pioneer group. Pichón-Rivière held a position at the Las Mercedes Hospice, and in 1934 initiated a psychoanalysis section in the journal Nervio. His first wife, Arminda Aberastury de Pichón-Rivière [1910–72], became a leading child analyst and introduced the ideas of Melanie Klein in Argentina, translating Klein’s The Psychoanalysis of Children into Spanish (Klein & Aberastury de Pichón-Rivière, 1948). In the late 1930s the lives of Cárcamo, Garma, Rascovsky, and Pichón-Rivière intersected when Cárcamo and Garma arrived in Argentina and came into contact with Rascovsky and Pichón-Rivière and their study groups. In 1942 Maria Langer [1910–87] arrived in Buenos Aires, having first fled Austria and then Spain, where she had gone to support the Republican cause. She had been trained at the Vienna Psychoanalytic Institute, where her analyst was Richard Sterba; she was supervised by Jeanne Lampl-de Groot. As a teacher and training analyst Langer played a leading role in the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association. Her writings focused on women, notably in Maternidad y Sexo (1952) [Motherhood and Sexuality, 1992], and the influence of cultural and social milieu on psychoanalysis. Her commitment to both psychoanalysis and political engagement are recorded in her autobiography, From Vienna to Managua (1989). The Asociación Psicoanalítica Argentina (APA) was quickly given provisional recognition by Ernest Jones, President of the IPA, and at the 1949 Zurich Congress was recognized as a Member Society of the IPA. The Argentine psychoanalytic journal, Revista de psicoanálisis, which first appeared in 1943, played an important role in disseminating psychoanalysis throughout Latin America. During its first decade, 1942–52, Freud’s writings were the dominant influence on Argentine psychoanalysts, followed by the work of Herman Nunberg, Theodor Reik, and Otto Fenichel. Up until 1950, then, the APA was a largely homogeneous group in which no significant theoretical differences were expressed openly. Beginning in the early 1950s, a new group of analysts began to make important contributions to psychoanalysis in Argentina. In 1950, David Liberman [1920–83], renowned for his writings on analyst–patient communication and styles of interpretation, joined the APA. He was soon followed by Mauricio Abadi, Fidias Cesio, León Grinberg, and Enrique Racker [1910–51], a native of Vienna, all of whom made important contributions to the development of psychoanalysis in Argentina. In particular, Racker’s seminal work on transference and countertransference was extremely influential not only in Argentina, but also in the United States and in Europe (Racker, 1968). In the first months of 1952, Garma and his wife travelled to Europe, where they met Herbert Rosenfeld, D. W. Winnicott, and Melanie Klein. They spent a month in daily scientific discussions with Klein and Paula Heimann, and, although Garma had always declared that he was not a Kleinian, his familiarity with Klein’s work was encyclopaedic. Klein’s work was warmly embraced in Buenos Aires. In particular her ideas on very early infantile development were met with enthusiasm, and her writings on child analysis were extremely influential with child analysts. Hanna Segal, one of Melanie Klein’s most important disciples, visited Argentina several times, and this further reinforced sympathy with Kleinian ideas. However, different groups within the APA responded differently to the work of Klein and her followers. Some accepted her views uncritically, others were more critical, while still others used her work as a point of departure for their own work. Finally, others still returned to a more Freudian or classical point of view. The answer to the question of why Klein’s ideas became so influential in Argentina is complex. The concept of transference at the very early pre-oedipal stage accentuated the schizoid and later depressive positions described by Melanie Klein in “Notes on Some Schizoid Mechanisms” (1946). This contribution acquired very great influence in our midst and gave rise to valuable work that focused on the deflection of the death instinct and the paranoid–schizoid condition. But it was taken to the point of exaggeration, and we succumbed to what Hanna Segal described as “symbolic equivalence”. To the extent that psychoanalysts in Argentina became obsessed with the part objects penis–child, breast–mother, faeces–child, we failed to understand the symbolic value of this type of conceptualization. In 1967, one group, the Kleininan Group, partly for theoretical reasons and feeling somewhat isolated in the institution, created an institution outside the Psychoanalytic Association, known as the CIMP (Centro de Medicina Psicosomática [Center for Research in Psychosomatic Medicine]). This centre was focused on understanding somatic language. The Argentine Psychoanalytic Association, unaccustomed to having outside groups form, was not happy with this development. However, we were able to invite their members to participate in our meetings. Eventually the group dissolved into several groups, but all its members remained in the APA. The years 1971/72 were important years, during which a crisis situation developed in the APA between two groups: the “Platform” group and the “Document” group. This crisis developed in the context of national and institutional political developments, with each group presenting manifestos whose goal was to bring about change. This was a difficult and convulsive moment in the institutions and in the country, and partisans from each group left the APA and did not return to form another group. Another group, “Ateneo”, took shape but found itself isolated. In 1974 a moderate Kleinian group formed, led by Madeleine and Willy Baranger [1922–94] and Jorge Mom. This group won the APA elections that year, and this victory was the beginning of a division within the APA that was to surface three years later. In 1977 the APA split, creating a new group, the APdeBA (Asociación Psicoanalítica de Buenos Aires [Buenos Aires Psychoanalytic Association]), whose president was Horacio Etchegoyen. The APA’s president was Mauricio Abadi. Both APA and APdeBA, however, continued to develop. In 1972, the APA extended an invitation to Serge Leclaire, a French psychoanalyst with Lacanian training. He opened up our panorama to new perspectives. The themes that Leclaire dealt with in his visit were: “The Notion of Pulsional Forces” and “The Object of Pulsion”. Leclaire’s three visits, combining as he did a Freudian background with a Lacanian perspective and his own personal characteristics, brought about a return to Freud, without losing the richness that we had received from Melanie Klein. This development was strengthened by the visits of André Green, beginning in 1974, which stimulated much interest in the new non-Kleinian frameworks. Thanks partly to Rascovsky and to Garma, the Argentine school was spread through Latin America. During the decades from the 1950s to the 1970s, many Latin American doctors trained at the Institute of the APA. One consequence of this was that the Kleinian influence was felt in psychoanalytic groups in Venezuela, Colombia, Chile, Peru, Uruguay, and Brazil; this influence was somewhat less in Mexico, where the United States exerted greater influence. Within Argentina, after much effort, and little by little, the psychiatric services of the public hospitals accepted psychoanalytic psychotherapy, both for individuals and for groups. The psychoanalytic movement has also spread inside the country, and there are three subsidiary IPA associations in Mendoza, Cordoba, and Rosario. There are also study groups in Bahía Blanca, Misiones, San Juan, and Tucuman. This brief outline has recounted a history that began 67 years ago. The brevity of this synthesis only touches on the strength, creativity, and international contributions of the APA and APdeBA. A long roster of pioneers, among them Arminda Aberastury, Jose Bleger, Luisa G. Alvarez de Toledo, David Liberman, León Grinberg, Jorge Mom, Joel Zac, Mauricio Abadi, Heinrich Racker, Willy Baranger, and, recently, Carlos Mario Aslan, left a legacy of creativity and commitment to psychoanalysis that is proudly upheld by psychoanalysts in Argentina today. In closing, the contributions of Horacio Etchegoyen deserve special mention. He was the first Latin American President of the IPA, and he was the first President of APdeBA. He also authored a seminal work, The Fundamentals of Psychoanalytic Technique (1999). Bibliography Cárcamo, C. E. (1943). La “serpiente emplumada”: Psicoanálisis de la religión maya-aztecay del sacrificio humano. Revista de Psicoanálisis, 1: 5–38. Doria-Medina, R., Arbiser, S., Kijak, M. (2005). Argentina. In: de Mijolla, A. (Ed.), The International Dictionary of Psychoanalysis. New York: Thomson/Gale. Etchegoyen, H. (1999). The Fundamentals of Psychoanalytic Technique (rev. ed.). London: Karnac. Etchegoyen, H. (2005). “Angel Garma”. In The International Dictionary of Psychoanalysis. Ed. Alain de Mijolla. New York: Thomson/Gale. Garma, A. (1940). Psicoanálisis de los sueños. Buenos Aires: El Ateneo. English edition: The psychoanalysis of dreams. New York: Jason Aronson, 1985. Gorriti, F. (1930). Psicoanálisis de los sueños en un síndrome de desposesión. Buenos Aires: Ed. L. J. Rosso. Klein, M. (1946). Notes on some schizoid mechanisms. International Journal of Psychoanalysis, 27: 99–110. Klein, M., & Aberastury de Pichón-Rivière, A. (1948). Psicoanálisis de los niños. Buenos Aires: Asociación Psicoanalítica Argentina. Langer, M. (1952). Maternidad y sexo [Maternity and Sex]. Buenos Aires: Editorial Nova. Langer, M. (1989). From Vienna to Managua: Journey of a Psychoanalyst. London: Free Association Books. Langer, M. (1992). Motherhood and Sexuality, trans. N. C. Hollander. New York: Guilford Press. Mouchet, E. (1926). Significación del psicoanálisis. La Semana Midica, 33 (24 June): 1415–1417. Racker, H. (1968). Transference and Countertransference. London: The Hogarth Press and the Institute of Psycho-Analysis. Thenon, J. (1930a). Carta del Profesor Freud con motivo de la publicación del libro “Psicoterapia comparada y psicogénesis”. Revista el Hospital Argentino, I: 445. Thenon, J. (1930b). Psicoterapia comparada y psicogénesis [Comparative Psychotherapy and Psychogenesis]. Thesis. Buenos Aires: Aniceto Lopez Editor.







46The IPA administration from 1993 to 1997: the presidency of R. Horacio Etchegoyen

R. Horacio Etchegoyen & Laura Etchegoyen

We will set out how Horacio Etchegoyen conceives of the International Psychoanalytical Association as the parental organization for all psychoanalysts and remember what he put in place during his presidency (from 1993 to 1997). Etchegoyen is a man of strong democratic convictions, who rejects authoritarian attitudes, without abdicating authority, and who believes in the due process of law and the transparency and accountability of individuals and institutions. We will address three main areas he tackled during his presidency: the relationship between the IPA and its members, the relationship between the IPA and its component societies, and the relationship between the IPA and society at large. Regarding the IPA’s relationship with its own members, and in accord with the general principles referred to above, the first measure he put in place during his presidency was to make the IPA’s Board minutes available for inspection by the general membership. There had been a long-standing misunderstanding between clinical confidentiality and public accountability, transparency, and freedom of information. The guise of protecting the analytic setting or the overall needs of the psychoanalytic movement made it impossible for any ordinary member to challenge or know what was actually going on within the IPA. The relationship between the IPA and its component societies also underwent significant change. Our new President, Professor Charles Hanly, in his then role as the Chair of the Committee on Societies, was instrumental, alongside Etchegoyen, in changing the prevailing institutional philosophy, which at times had been perceived as interventionist and, on occasion, insensitive to local issues. A parallel development of the period was a policy-based focus on the promotion and support of small societies and new groups. Similarly, the relationship between the IPA and society at large was of special, particularly ethical, concern. One way in which this preoccupation became manifest was by institutional self-reflection in relation to incidents of IPA members implicated in torture during certain South American military dictatorships. This situation had previously been investigated, to a degree, but it was only under Etchegoyen’s presidency that the IPA accepted corporate responsibility and, without dramatic gestures, resignations, or expulsions, put things in place for the due legal process to proceed. The achievements in these three areas are, we believe, as relevant today as they were then. Freud warned us about the repetition compulsion, which can affect individuals, institutions, and governments. Of course, this is not all Freud said: one of Etchegoyen’s favourite quotes may remind us all that not only the negativity of repetition but the positivity of reason are at play in society. Or, as Freud puts it: “The voice of the intellect is a soft one, but it does not rest till it has gained a hearing” (Freud, 1927c, p. 53). It is in this sense that vigilance against the repetition compulsion must be tempered with the voice of reason. In addition to the three core areas we have discussed, it is also worth mentioning one further institutional achievement. Horacio Etchegoyen, alongside Ana Maria Andrade de Azevedo, Elizabeth Tabak de Bianchedi, Günther Perdigâo, Jorge Olagaray, and Samuel Zysman, undertook to restructure the finances of the IPA, which had been in the red. By the time his presidency was over, there was a healthy surplus—and this was accomplished without raising membership dues. We would like to convey a message of hope for the future of psychoanalysis as the most free, effective, and dignified psychotherapeutic method to help twenty-first-century man. He strongly believes that the future of psychoanalysis depends on psychoanalysts defending our method and perfecting their efficacy in the treatment of patients. It is in our therapeutic efficacy that the future of psychoanalysis resides. Bibliography Freud, S. (1927c). The Future of an Illusion. Standard Edition, 21.







3France Psychoanalysis in France: the “Société Psychanalytique de Paris” and its relation to the IPA

Alain Gibeault & Nicolas Gougoulis

The Paris Psychoanalytical Society [Société Psychanalytique de Paris (SPP)] was founded in 1926 after the turmoil of the First World War by a group of young and ambitious psychiatrists who joined forces with lay analysts already trained in Berlin or Vienna. Previous attempts to introduce psychoanalytic thinking or practice had only local influence and never took form in an institutional way. From 1926 until 1953 the history of psychoanalysis in France practically coincides with the institutional history of the SPP; from then on, as in most countries, splits and secessions gave rise to numerous societies, some linked to the International Psychoanalytical Association while others followed their own path. In this brief presentation we focus on information and try to give the background that introduced the “French model”. The social and cultural atmosphere in the 1920s created a need for psychological thinking and treatment. The more biologically oriented trends in psychiatry were out of focus, even if major international prizes (e.g. the Nobel Prize) still went to Wagner-Jauregg in preference to Freud. Thus a large majority of the human talent filled with the anxiety of understanding psychological suffering and the human being flocked towards psychoanalysis, then considered as the most powerful tool to understanding and the only school that guaranteed a method of investigation, a body of knowledge, a modality of therapy, and, last but not least, a training that was superior when compared to the theoretical university models. It is not difficult to understand that the first gathering of founding members of a psychoanalytic society, just as before the war, would be dynamic and ambitious as well as heterogeneous and conflicting. Thus, in November 1926 a group of psychiatrists joined forces with three already trained lay analysts and founded a French-speaking Society aiming to bring together doctors who wished to become psychoanalysts and to give them the opportunity to undergo training analysis indispensable to practice Freud’s method. They realized some projects such as the launching of a journal (Revue Française de Psychanalyse) and the annual conference of French-speaking psychoanalysts—two institutions that still exist today. They also found a large number of points of conflict during the meetings of the Linguistic Commission for French Psychoanalytic Terminology. When comparing these institutional movements to what was happening at the same moment in other major Western cities, we can find that there are practically the same problems, but with different balances of influence or power, with respect to the positions of leading figures. Thus in Berlin, where, of course, there is no need for a linguistic commission, the accent was on training and a widening scope of therapeutic methods; in London, the dominating figure of Jones guaranteed loyalty to Freud’s idea of an international orientation and a model of training that includes non-physicians. The English linguistic commission was the basis of translations by psychoanalysts trained by Freud. We can consider this as the premise to the Standard Edition. In the United States, however, things were different and probably comparable to the French movements and conflicts. American physicians were hostile to lay analysis and published their work in a new English-speaking review, much to the dismay of Jones (Paskauskas, 1991). Thus from the beginning in institutional psychoanalysis we can find trends and conflict centres that will tend to form groups or fractions of people who have different things in mind. In Paris the founding of the journal introduced psychoanalysis and translations of Freud’s work. However, from the beginning the more nationalistic group (Edouard Pichon and Angelo Hesnard) were reluctant even to mention Freud’s name on the front page. Marie Bonaparte defended the loyalist positions and often prevailed. Very rapidly the small scientific society had a growing influence in psychiatric wards and intellectual circles. Thus on the one hand we find an expanding interest in psychoanalysis in literary milieux such as surrealists, and on the other hand in psychiatric treatment. New members arrived, such as Sacha Nacht, who joined the Society in 1927 and presented a paper on the treatment of a schizophrenic woman via psychoanalysis. The French-speaking conferences were the social event of the Society. Theoretical reports enriched psychoanalytic thought and technique. Training of many candidates was assured by Rudolph Löwenstein, who became a very influential figure before his departure to New York after the Nazi invasion. Another influential figure was René Laforgue, a German-speaking psychiatrist, who had a correspondence with Freud and introduced Marie Bonaparte to him. He was elected as the first president and was a highly gifted practitioner analysing candidates such as Françoise Dolto. His ambition and the complexity of his personality, however, nearly compromised psychoanalysis in collaboration with the Nazis during German occupation, thus disqualifying Laforgue and his pupils. In 1934 the first Psychoanalytical Institute was inaugurated, with Marie Bonaparte as its first director. This was not a training institute and served as a lecture centre enabling the broadening of psychoanalytic culture. During the 1930s psychoanalysis expanded in Paris. Sacha Nacht became prominent in matters of technique. Sophie Morgenstern, a lay analyst, introduced a drawing technique for child analysis. We must mention the presence and influence of a major figure, Otto Rank, who lived, practised, and lectured in Paris. Much of his work is known from the Diaries of Anaïs Nin (Lieberman, 1985). Even if the official society ignored him, this marks the beginning of what our historical survey must capture: institutional history and the history of psychoanalysis as a history of ideas. Paris became a centre of importance after the destruction of freedom in Germany and the menaces over other European States (Steiner, 2000). Marie Bonaparte helped a large number of analysts who fled Nazi persecution. She was supported by her secretary Anne Berman, who became the first associate member of the SPP. In the Society, René Laforgue and Paul Schiff supported Bonaparte’s proposition to integrate the non-French, while the more nationalistic members were hostile to that idea. Thus René Spitz and Heinz Hartmann were accepted as members but merely passed through Paris on their way to New York. Other members with less notoriety stayed in Paris and made their careers before fleeing France once it became an occupied country (Gougoulis, 2003). It is interesting to note that Hartmann was elected on an institutional compromise that included “a sort of deal”: Lacan’s election. Jacques Lacan was considered a French candidate, as opposed to the foreigner Hartmann. The balance was considered as even (Roudinesco, 1990)! The defeat of France and German occupation put a halt to the developing Society. Marie Bonaparte imposed a closure of the Institute, the Revue ceased its publication, and most of the analysts fled Paris. Sophie Morgenstern committed suicide on the Germans’ entrance into Paris. Laforgue was the only analyst who attempted collaboration with the Göring Institute, thus discrediting himself. Most analysts ceased to practise. However, a new generation following their action in the resistance to Nazi occupation entered the psychoanalytic scene. Young psychiatrists aligned active political resistance and analytic experience. This generation would go on to form the core of the Nacht group, which was highly influential at the rebirth of the Society in 1946, under the presidency of John Leuba. Serge Lebovici, René Diatkine, and Jean and Evelyne Kestemberg were some of the members of this movement. Society was thriving after the disaster of the Second World War. Reforms were needed and specifically to modify the shame of the carceral psychiatric asylums. There was a new need for psychoanalysis as an ideology and of psychoanalysts as leaders of this social movement. Thus psychoanalytic training would become the field of ideological and power conflicts. These conflicts seemed to be concentrated in two groups. Nacht presented a medically oriented model of training and hoped that psychoanalysis would acquire university medical status. His influence was important, but his authoritarian personality presented a drawback. Daniel Lagache, on the other hand, proposed another model open to lay practitioners through university teaching, but from the psychology chair, following the example of Pierre Janet’s teaching. The conflict was exacerbated by legal proceedings, an accusation of “illegal practice of medicine” against a lay analyst (the Margaret Clark-Williams affair). During this period very serious discussions concerned the number, frequency, and duration of sessions for training analysis. Supervision was also a theme of the future institute. Nacht introduced a new setting of analysis of three times a week with 45-minute sessions. Furthermore, candidates had two supervised analyses, one of which was on an individual basis while the other was collective (in a group of candidates with one supervisor). While the original reasons where practical (scarcity of full members able to teach and supervise), these modifications became the basis of a highly original model. Conflicts were not resolved in Paris and in 1953 lead to a split in the Society. The Paris split is major event in French psychoanalysis, but it has a paradigmatic value on the international level (Mijolla, 2001). The British model of compromise was then unknown, and the Freud–Klein controversies that allowed the “ladies agreement” were unpublished (King & Steiner, 1991). The French split, although it did not directly influence any other Society, is comparable to other similar movements in Boston (in 1974) or Los Angeles (in 1950) (Kirsner, 2000). It is very usual to find authoritarian personalities at the leader level who try to impose their training or scientific views being unable to find other solutions than splitting. This questions the fundamental organization of the IPA, a problem that had been raised as early as 1912 by Eugen Bleuler in his correspondence with Freud (Alexander & Selesnick, 1965). The Paris Society was divided into two models of training, under the respective influence of Nacht and Lagache. Lagache seized the moment of a presidency minority in the person of Lacan and resigned from membership of the SPP. He was quickly followed by Juliette Favez-Boutonnier and Françoise Dolto. Lacan, feeling alone in the Nacht group, finally followed them in their resignation. These analysts then created a new group—the “Société française de Psychanalyse (SFP)”—and founded a new journal, La psychanalyse. However, they did not take into consideration the fact that their resignation from the national Society automatically meant that they had resigned from their IPA membership. So they found themselves excluded on the international level. Nacht and Bonaparte can be considered as being responsible for all the problems the new group found in their effort to become an IPA Study Group. The rivalry of the two groups was sad on a personal and institutional level, but, on the other hand, it was highly stimulating for theoretical and clinical creativity. The SPP showed much activity, and the “résistance generation” slowly came to power. Even if a “return to Freud” was a slogan attributed to Lacan, Freud was and still remains the main reference in France. All new proposals had to be referred to the founding ideas. Nacht and Lebovici made their report on indications for psychoanalysis in 1958, Maurice Bouvet created a new theoretical approach, comparable to object relations theory, Pierre Marty introduced psychosomatics, Jean and Evelyne Kestemberg introduced psychoanalytic psychodrama, and René Diatkine was prominent in child treatment. These personalities also grouped themselves around psychiatric reform and were leaders of the pilot service in the 13th arrondissement in Paris that inaugurated psychiatric reform in France. On the other hand, the SFP tried a different approach. Trying to find alliances at the international level, they inaugurated the introduction of Ferenczi, Klein, Balint, and Winnicott. Their journal had a large influence, and they were followed by a great number of the former candidates of the SPP. However, slowly the SFP became the field for a new conflict. While Lagache succeeded in his plan to introduce psychoanalysis in university teaching (his pupils Laplanche and Fedida later became professors of psychology), he lost the ideological battle to Lacan, who became the speaker of the new Society. Lacan’s production slowly but surely became a new theory that, while proclaiming itself loyal to Freud, introduced new ways of thought and practice. The short sessions that would highlight the conflict were only the most obvious part. Although his critique of “ego psychology” is highly interesting, his speculations took him to a new departure (Etchegoyen, 1991). This conflictual atmosphere lead to an integration of some members of the SFP in the IPA, a group that took the name of “Association Psychanalytique de France” (APF). Lacan and Dolto were excluded, so Lacan then founded his own school of psychoanalysis. In a way we see how an institutional movement provokes a new theoretical school. In the 1970s Kohut’s non-election to IPA presidency is considered as the founding moment of self psychology (Rangell, 2004). In the 1960s Lacan’s estrangement and final exclusion from the IPA lead to Lacanian psychoanalysis. During the 1960s we can observe the appearance of a new generation of analysts who would mark the field: Conrad Stein, Serge Viderman, André Green, and Michel de M’Uzan entered the scene. Evelyne Kestemberg was elected in 1964 as the first non-physician training analyst after the death of Marie Bonaparte in 1962 (Perron, 1990). Under her presidency the SPP created a new category of members—“the associate members” [membres affiliés]—a movement that coincided with the foundation of the European Federation and within which the societies’ representation was proportionate to their membership basis. Evelyne Kestemberg’s election was an opening for prominent non-physician analysts such as Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel and Joyce McDougall. The institutional problem and the training issue would be a major preoccupation in the SPP as in France, finally leading through painful birth to a French model. These issues lead the Lacanian movement to introduce an extreme “election”, called “La passe”, in which an analyst would ask a board of training analysts to listen to a third, who would recount the analytic issues. This was thought to eliminate institutional influence in the elections, but under the circumstances it lead to a sort of dictatorship by the only leader figure, who was Lacan himself. In 1969 this finally inaugurated the many splits of the Lacanian galaxy. Trying to resume the French model, we find the rejection of some so-called authoritarian aspects of the Eitingon model (Kern-berg, 2004). The training analysis is supposed to be outside the institution and considered as a personal experience; there was no pre-selection during this period. Admission into the training was advised after a sufficient number of years so as to facilitate the future candidate’s understanding of the unconscious mind. The choice of the analyst was supposed to be free; however, this became a theme of debate, and in practice the SPP limited potential analysts to its members, while the APF was less concerned with membership of the training analyst and more with the candidate’s grasp of the process. In theory the atmosphere was free, but training could go on for very long. Candidates for supervision were rarely under 40 (10% of the actual number of candidates). As concerns the teaching aspect, the candidates enjoyed absolute freedom in their choice of seminars; there was no academic structure. This orientation was supposed to foster a learning process without submissiveness or indoctrination, considered as potential by-products of the Eitingon model. In supervision the French model created a situation parallel to the analytic process; the control case was seen as reflecting and being reflected by unconscious processes generated by the supervisory relationship, thus becoming an intense, private, and emotionally charged experience. The general idea was to favour personal experience as compared to a more academic pathway. These ideas fostered in their turn an interesting evolution and dynamic. However, like every model, the French one has its drawbacks. Training is lengthened and candidates pass a large part of their active and productive years in an atmosphere that idealizes transmission but sterilizes creativity and original contributions. These drawbacks became the source for many attempts to reform the relations between the Society and the Institute, which had become more than a faculty (Mijolla, 2002). The importance given to the criteria for training and selection were such that in public the Society was less known than the Institute. Since 1969 and up to 2006, the Society underwent a number of reforms (Gougoulis, 2006), which corresponded to an evolution of mentalities and organization. This coincided with a great expansion of membership, from 200 members in the 1970s to 800 three decades later. The Society grew successfully in the regions: a new Institute was inaugurated in Lyon, while Toulouse is developing a Training Centre that will aspire to Institute level in the future. This evolution has not always been peaceful. The power conflicts even lead to a temporary investigation in 1982 by an IPA external committee. However, even if the conflicts were important, the SPP did not suffer new splits. We can speculate on a certain homogeneity of theoretical views that maintained the group coherence—something comparable to the evolution in the United Kingdom in the 1920s when Jones dissolved the London Society to form the British Society (Baker, 2001). Bibliography Alexander, F., & Selesnick, S. T. (1965). Freud–Bleuler correspondence. Archives of General Psychiatry, 12 (1): 1–9. Baker, R. (2001).
