47The IPA administration from 1997 to 2001

Otto Kernberg (President) & Robert L. Tyson (Secretary)

We started the 1997–2001 administration of the International Psychoanalytical Association with a set of major tasks that we hoped to accomplish, or at least advance to a point where their objectives had been achieved sufficiently to ensure future continuity of these efforts. We assumed that the major problems facing the psychoanalytic community, and the IPA in particular, included the following:


The challenge to psychoanalysis in contemporary culture, the academic environment, and the university, reflected in negative media coverage and loss of influence of psychoanalysis in the university in those countries where it had established a strong presence, especially in medicine and psychiatry. This diminution was also reflected in a relative decrease in the number of patients in treatment and candidates applying for psychoanalytic training due, in part, to the development of competing methods of treatment in the field of psychopharmacology and by a vast spectrum of psychotherapies. The practical problem to be addressed was the place of psychoanalysis within this total therapeutic spectrum. To begin, we decided to develop a major effort in the direction of improving the relationship of psychoanalysis to society; a new committee, to be chaired by Dr Cláudio Eizirik, was assigned this task. We believed that the lack of scientific research was a major obstacle to the development of psychoanalysis; therefore, the IPA needed to foster interest and investments in a broad spectrum of research, with particular emphasis on empirical research regarding theory, technique, effectiveness of treatment, and application of the psychoanalytic method. This would require strengthening the activities of the Research Committee under the leadership of Dr Peter Fonagy, accompanied by the creation of an Advisory Board on Research, under the leadership of Dr Robert Waller-stein, that would recommend guidelines and approval of concrete projects made possible by increased financial investments on the part of the IPA. We believed that it was important that psychoanalytic societies and institutes become more concerned with the practical application of psychoanalytic technique to the treatment of patients with severe psychopathology for whom modified forms of psychoanalytic psychotherapy rather than standard psychoanalysis might be indicated. In general, we encouraged the development of less intensive forms of treatment that large segments of the population could afford. Efforts to develop psychoanalytic psychotherapy had already evolved, particularly in the United States and in Germany. Fostering this development more widely became more urgent because of the appearance in various countries of the world of societies of psychoanalytic psychotherapy that were independent of the IPA. An IPA effort to explore psychoanalytic psychotherapy would require changes in the general attitude within the psychoanalytic community towards it, a research investment in this area, and expansion of the spectrum of educational concerns of psychoanalytic institutes. In turn, this related to a fourth point—namely, innovation in psychoanalytic education. Over many years we had been concerned by what seemed to us a certain stagnation in psychoanalytic institutes, a failure to develop new methods of education, and the predominance of rigid hierarchical systems resulting in a nonfunctional distribution of authority centred in the training analysis system. This latter issue would probably be the most difficult area to approach, and we asked the Committee on Psychoanalytic Education, now chaired by Dr Jacqueline Amati Mehler, to focus on this challenge. This administration was faced by two major organizational problems: one, the development of a move towards a bicameral system of governance, represented by the recently developed House of Delegates, comprised of representatives respectively elected by the members of the three major regions (Europe, Latin America, North America), in contrast to the globally elected members of the Executive Council. While the constitutional authority of the IPA was centred on the President, the Executive Committee, and the Executive Council, the House of Delegates explicitly challenged this structure by trying to become a legislative body with power equal to that of the Executive Committee and Executive Council. Because representatives from the House of Delegates were already participating as full members of the Executive Council, the House of Delegates—an advisory body—was encouraged to try to become a legislative body equivalent to the Executive Council. Rather than dealing with the urgent present challenges in the social environment of psychoanalysis, an increasing amount of time, energy, and financial resources were absorbed by this developing political struggle and threatened to consume the time and energy required to deal with IPA internal administrative matters. A second group of organizational problems was created by unresolved administrative issues within individual psychoanalytic societies that had been referred to the Executive Council for solution. For example, a large number of candidates of the Swiss Psychoanalytical Society were protesting at the inordinate length, among other difficulties, that prevented them from completing their training. This was due, in part, to the complex internal structure of the educational system of the Swiss society. A major ethical and political conflict between one of the psychoanalytic societies in Rio de Janeiro, Rio 1, and a split-off group, the “Pro Etica” group, illustrated another set of problems confronting the IPA. This conflict was related to the problematic nature of how Rio 1 dealt with the training analyst of a former candidate whose unethical behaviour involved his participation in the repressive military dictatorship of Brazil. Understandably, this situation was particularly stressful for that society, evoked intense passions at an international level, and needed to be dealt with immediately by an ad hoc IPA committee. In fact, the successful resolution of this conflict required a major financial and time commitment of this administration.


Urgent organizational problems such as these made imperative the development of a system of explicit ethical principles and standards that had not been developed heretofore by the IPA. Accordingly, a committee was appointed to recommend to the Executive Council basic ethical standards and the process by which their infringement should be handled. These standards and methods for ensuring their implementation were approved and eventually integrated into the administrative functions of the IPA. They were monitored by a newly established ethics committee chaired by Dr Jerome Winer. In addition, the problem of discrimination on the basis of race, religion, or sexual orientation was addressed by an Executive Council resolution on non-discrimination that was further modified in the following administration. The problem within the Swiss Psychoanalytical Society was resolved in an amicable and functional way, but it highlighted many of the general problems of psychoanalytic education. One of these problems soon surfaced when a request from Brazil to establish “concentrated psychoanalysis” was brought to the Executive Council. (Concentrated analysis, in which two analytic sessions were permitted to occur on the same day, is a modification of the standards for training designed for candidates living at a significant distance from their training analyst.) In turn, this request led to a general discussion of alternative models of psychoanalytic training such as the French and the Uruguayan models, neither of which fit within the predominant Eitingon model of training prevalent throughout most psychoanalytic societies. This proposal intensified and gave a sense of urgency to the concern of this administration with the problem of psychoanalytic standards, alternative models of psychoanalytic education, and general innovation of psychoanalytic education. It was to prove one of the most difficult, long-term concerns for this and the succeeding administration, and a resolution was not achieved until the end of the presidency of Prof. Widlöcher. The struggle to modify and to improve traditional educational procedures exemplifies the intense individual psychological and organizational opposition to change within the IPA. The administrative difficulties and unavoidable politicization of the administrative management of the IPA derived from tensions between the Executive Council and the House of Delegates and necessitated an entirely new look at the organizational structure of the IPA. These tensions represented a major challenge because, as a consequence of efforts to give a greater voice to the regional organizations of psychoanalytic societies, a conflict had been created between a time-consuming, inefficient, and non-functional bicameral system and the administrative needs of the IPA. As we saw it, providing a greater voice to the House of Delegates by means of this bicameral structural change would delay the possibility of bringing about change because the resulting legislative gridlock could be resolved only by creating mediating committees to deal with endless conflicts between equal legislative bodies. The need to work through this dilemma was a major challenge for our administration. As a consequence, over the course of the first year of this administration, recognition of the need for a task force on organizational structure and mission gradually evolved. Dr Robert L. Tyson, secretary of the IPA, made the original proposal, and in 1998 such a task force (SAM) was appointed. It was comprised of members of both the Executive Council and the House of Delegates, with the specific mandate to recommend a lean, functional, effective, and democratic system of governance to resolve the problems created by the proposed development of a bicameral legislature. I return to discuss the SAM Task Force at a later point. Related governance issues that required urgent attention were the functioning of the IPA’s central office in London, the structure of the financial arrangements of the IPA, and the need to reorganize financial investments in terms of a changing tax situation and to streamline the processes of communication and elections under the impact of developing e-mail resources and the potential of the Internet. A corresponding new committee on governance, under the chairmanship of Dr Robert L. Tyson, was able to achieve the beginnings of these objectives within the first year of this administration. We also established a genuine legal basis for the IPA as a registered company structure early in our administration.

1997 The year 1997 was characterized by a number of initiatives representing the early steps in the accomplishment of the major objectives earlier outlined. The formation of the Committee on the United Nations under Dr Afaf Mahfouz expanded the presence of the IPA in relation to international organizations, particularly the system of non-governmental organizations (NGOs) of the United Nations. A Committee on Inter-regional Conferences, under Dr Sara Zac de Filc, began planning conferences, scheduled on a biannual basis, between the major international psychoanalytic congresses. The Committee on New Groups, under the leadership of Dr David Sachs, began a major effort to expand the IPA’s presence in Eastern Europe. We also supported the activation of the IPA Publications Committee chaired by Dr Ethel Person. An additional initiative, responding to the express wishes of the Latin American psychoanalytic societies, involved the question of whether the IPA could set up its own journal. It was not widely known that The International Journal of Psychoanalysis was, in fact, a British journal. Concerns had developed, particularly in Latin America, over the extent to which an English-language journal might favour the publication of authors writing in the English language. Dr Ethel Person chaired a committee to explore the possibility of an independent journal to be launched by the IPA, in consultation with the International Journal of Psychoanalysis. This process led to a decision by the International Journal of Psychoanalysis to open up that journal to contributions submitted in other languages, and to establish a more flexible relationship with the IPA. An underlying consideration was a strong concern on the part of the British Society, the IPA administration, and within the psychoanalytic community at large that it would not be prudent to have two competing English-language journals. The predominant opinion emerged that financial investment by the IPA in such an enterprise would jeopardize the success of both journals. The initiative for a new journal eventually was laid to rest. In preparing the 1998 budget, the budgetary process, undergoing a long-term revision beginning in 1997, began to incorporate the requirements of the major objectives earlier outlined. We were able to ensure Executive Council approval for investing $100,000 in research grants in 1997 and to increase that investment to $200,000 for 1998; we planned to increase that further, to $300,000, in 1999. Significant budgetary support was also given to the Committee on Psychoanalytic Education, the Committee on Psychoanalysis and Society, and the New Groups Committee for the Eastern European project. Other major new initiatives were implemented during 1998. In the area of the expansion of psychoanalytic societies, a major development was the decision of Executive Council to respond to the informal approaches that Dr Kernberg had made to the leadership of the German Psychoanalytical Society [Deutsche Psychoanalytische Gesellschaft (DPG)] to explore joining the IPA. The DPG was a large psychoanalytic society functioning in parallel with the German Psychoanalytical Association [Deutsche Psychoanalytische Vereinigung (DPV)] affiliated with the IPA. The DPG had approximately 630 members and 600 candidates in training; it operated in 12 German cities. A split occurred within the originally unified German Psychoanalytical Society, dismantled under the Nazi dictatorship, after the end of the Nazi regime, when a new German Psychoanalytical Society came into existence. A sharp division developed between those analysts who had continued to be active, in some fashion, during the Nazi dictatorship, and others who attempted to re-establish German psychoanalysis after the end of the Nazi regime. The DPV derived from those who wished for a total repudiation of the links with the past, while the DPG originally tolerated some continuity with respect to the activities of some of its older members during the dictatorship. In addition, there were significant theoretical differences between the two groups. Over the years following the war, however, the remaining links with some early members active in the years of the dictatorship were totally severed by the DPG, and the evolving DPG organization completely repudiated the Nazi past. Moreover, there was a gradual resolution of theoretical differences between DPG and DPV that had emerged immediately following the end of the Second World War, differences that had contributed significantly to the original organizational split. The DPV had joined the IPA shortly after the end of the war. A younger generation of the DPG also became interested in joining the IPA. Their leadership was eager to participate in a process that would facilitate this rapprochement, and the original resistance of the DPG against such a process was gradually resolved. Both organizations also recognized the advantages of increasing the strength of an overall united psychoanalytic community in Germany. The proposal to explore the integration of the DPG within the IPA that we advanced met, at first, with strong opposition within the IPA Executive Council. Gradually, however, over the first half of 1998, this opposition was overcome. At the Executive Council meeting of December of that year the mandate for an exploratory committee for the relationship with the DPG was approved. This gave rise to a process that, eventually, after several years of the work of a committee under the leadership of Dr Anne-Marie Sandler, culminated in the incorporation of the DPG into the IPA. It was a difficult and lengthy process, and the two of us considered this development a major achievement of this IPA administration. Our efforts were also invested in further increasing the number of psychoanalytic study groups and societies in Eastern Europe and Latin America, lending support also to a long-standing psychoanalytically oriented group in South Korea. The Koreans had established an interest group, the Seoul Psychoanalytic Study Group, in 1980, recognized by the IPA as a Guest Study Group in 1991. They progressed towards official IPA Study Group status by having several of their members spend two to four years (one spent as long as ten years) pursuing psychoanalytic training in institutes of the American Psychoanalytic Association (APsaA). On their return to Korea, they participated in ongoing telephone supervision, combined with occasional face-to-face meetings. They also attended special yearly conferences on theory and technique. When each individual completed equivalent training, an IPA committee evaluated his or her clinical work. If found qualified, the individual was awarded Direct Membership in the IPA. Through this process the group eventually attained official IPA Study Group status in 2008. In addition, this administration extended tentative approaches to independent psychoanalytic groups such as the Arbeitskreis in Vienna and the Quatrieme Groupe in Paris. As a consequence of a lawsuit against the APsaA and the IPA, the IPA was obligated to accept the application for affiliation into the IPA of non-APsaA societies. The interest of psychoanalytically oriented groups in the United States not affiliated with the APsaA to directly join the IPA created, at first, some degree of tension between the APsaA and the IPA. But, after a problem-solving and friendly interchange of views and efforts at conciliation of our respective policies, an agreement was reached that permitted this expansion in North America to take place.


1998 The year 1998 witnessed the continuation of the conflict around Rio 1 and the “Pro Etica” group. The conflict was caused by a training analyst who had not ended the training of a candidate linked to a torture group of the military dictatorship, and had not been repudiated by Rio 1. This conflict gradually evolved into a straightforward political conflict between two groups, rather than centring on the original ethical problem that had caused it. It would take another year to finally put this conflict to rest, with the decision of Executive Council to accept a nucleus of the original “Pro Etica” Group as a new psychoanalytic study group in Rio de Janeiro. As mentioned before, a major request by the Brazilian psychoanalytic societies for “concentrated analysis” initiated the opening of a general discussion regarding psychoanalytic standards and alternative models of training. The approval of concentrated analysis under specific guidelines developed by the Executive Council focused the attention of the Council on the broader issue of uniform standards of psychoanalytic training based both on criteria of training and actual standards of competence in psychoanalysis. Over the following years this proved to be a major problem that eventually evolved into extended discussions of Executive Council, resulting in a major new project, which proposed the acceptance of three distinct models of training; this was finally approved under the presidency of Daniel Widlöcher. Several initiatives begun in 1997 came to fruition in 1998: the first Latin American Research Conference took place in Buenos Aires; in Vienna, the first Inter-Regional Conference proved to be a success and led to the planning of a second one in Montevideo; the Executive Council, in an effort to integrate derivatives of the psychoanalytic method within psychoanalytic education, explicitly encouraged psychoanalytic institutes to consider psychoanalytic psychotherapy training, rather than witnessing the development of independent institutes and societies of psychoanalytic psychotherapy that would enter into competition with psychoanalytic societies. A code of ethical principles was approved by Executive Council and led to a focus on its implementation by specific procedures under the control of a new, standing IPA Ethics Committee chaired by Dr Jerome Winer. The above-mentioned negotiations with the British Psychoanalytical Society regarding the establishing of an independent IPA journal or opening the International Journal of Psychoanalysis to IPA influence continued. They eventually led to the agreement that, at this time, the IPA would not launch an independent journal, and that the International Journal of Psychoanalysis would become more receptive to scientific work stemming from non-English-language psychoanalytic societies, particularly from Latin America. Political tensions between the House of Delegates and the Executive Council continued during 1998. In an effort, on the part of Executive Council, to accommodate the interests of the House of Delegates, it invited the participation of representatives of the House of Delegates as full voting members at Executive Council, supported the participation of representatives of the House of Delegates at regional meetings, and financially supported the participation of representatives of the House of Delegates at the meetings of Society presidents. In this connection, the Assembly of Presidents, which had in the past met with the President of the IPA at international congresses, decided that this meeting was no longer necessary, given the increased relationship between presidents and the regional members of the House of Delegates. From a broader perspective, this overall process reflected not only the tension of the bicameral situation that had evolved at the administrative centre of the IPA, but the growing efforts of regional organizations to assert their leadership function, their executive role in organizing regional meetings, and relationships between psychoanalytic societies within each region. The Task Force on Structure and Mission (“SAM”), referred to earlier, mandated to solve the structural problem created by the evolving bicameral organization of the IPA, solicited and reviewed the experience of all these political processes. It then worked intensively, under the chairmanship of Dr Inga Villareal, to incorporate greater democratization as well as regionalization into the IPA organizational structure. At the same time it found a harmonious way to maintain the integrative role and the authority of the Executive Council as both an executive and an advisory body. Other initiatives that were implemented at this time included the activation of the Committee on Child and Adolescent Psychoanalysis under the chairmanship of Dr Anne-Marie Sandler and the establishment of the Committee on Women and Psychoanalysis, chaired by Dr Joan Raphael Leff.


1999 The year 1999 witnessed further development of the projects and trends that had emerged in the previous two years. The Latin American psychoanalytic societies raised the question of psychoanalytic models that accepted three sessions instead of four per week of personal analysis for candidates in training. This expanded the discussion of standards of psychoanalytic education to include the current practice of the French and the Uruguayan models of training, which accepted three sessions per week as part of their overall organization of psychoanalytic education. The discussion of standards raised other educational issues, such as the concern over the rigid hierarchical structure of the training analysis system in psychoanalytic institutes, the relative isolation of psychoanalytic institutes from new developments in related sciences, concerns over psychotherapy training, and the need for keeping psychoanalytic and psychotherapy training separate within psychoanalytic institutes. Major differences regarding psychotherapy training in psychoanalytic institutes emerged and were to continue to occupy the attention of Council over the next two years. A major thrust to develop new psychoanalytic societies in Eastern Europe culminated with the planning for an Eastern European Psychoanalytic Institute. The German DPG Society progressed towards Provisional Society Status, and the development of new centres of training made the question of innovation of educational methods even more urgent, clearly exceeding the realm of responsibilities of the Committee on Psychoanalytic Education and requiring the focused attention of the entire Executive Council. At the same time, the spontaneous move towards strengthening the regional psychoanalytic associations brought increased pressure from the House of Delegates for major representation on the committee structure of the Executive Council. This made it urgent that the SAM Task Force (on structure and mission) crystallize a proposal for the overall reorganization of the IPA governance. The previous agreement, on principle, to provide $300,000.00 for research during this (1999) budget year now conflicted with the increasing expenses connected with the expanding activities of the House of Delegates, and the ever greater amount of time and resources absorbed by the negotiation of the corresponding political issues. Increasing legal expenses were accompanied by a growing sense that the overall financial and organizational coordination of the IPA London central office needed to be modernized to accord with the anticipated needs of the twenty-first century. This led to the decision to hire a senior organizational administrator and establish the position of a Director General. An international selection process was carried out, which culminated in the appointment of Piers Pendred as Director General, to start his functions in January 2000. At the same time, in a succession of three reports, the SAM Task Force delineated the structure of a Board of Representatives, with a combined representation from the global psychoanalytic community, its regions, and the presidents of all Component Societies, with extended discussions of the desirable relative strength of these constituencies of the Board. Simultaneously, in response to increased concern over the IPA’s internal structural problems, we urged the Executive Council to keep in mind that its highest priority was to deal with the external realities facing psychoanalysis. Looking inside the organization would not solve these problems. We stressed, therefore, our concern with the need to strengthen our outreach into the community and to support research and with the renovation of psychoanalytic education. In December 1998, a major joint conference of the Executive Council and the House of Delegates around the subject of psychoanalysis and society took place in New York. It was followed by active efforts on the part of the Committee on Psychoanalysis and Society to deal with the problems of psychoanalytic presence in the universities and how to stimulate psychoanalytic societies to actively engage in this effort. The major focus of this administration in 1999, however, was on the IPA Congress in Chile. In spite of initial concerns about the aftereffects on the cultural atmosphere of the country of the recent termination of the dictatorial regime of Pinochet, the Congress resulted in a very successful exploration of various scientific issues, including the theoretical and clinical role of contemporary affect theory within psychoanalysis. A conference and a symposium organized by the IPA Conceptual and Empirical Research Committee provided a major stimulus for the development of further research, particularly empirical research within psychoanalytic societies. Committee work at the Santiago Congress achieved the setting up of standards for child and adolescent psychoanalytic training, the establishment of the Ethics Committee, and the beginning of the resolution of the “Rio 1” problem.


2000 In the year 2000, the recommendations of the SAM project (Task Force on Structure and Mission) acquired their final form. Responding to political pressures by the regional groups and the House of Delegates, an agreement was reached that the future Board of Representatives of the IPA, its new dominant executive body, be comprised of two representatives per region elected by the global membership and five representatives per region to be nominated by the regional committees. The executive structure of the IPA would be comprised of the three members of the Executive Committee (president, secretary, and treasurer) and 24 representatives. The Board of Representatives would select one representative from each region to become part of the Executive Committee for three years, possibly re-elected for two more years, and the president-elect and the secretary designate would be non-voting members of the Board. The Regional Nomination Committees would propose the regional slates for representatives and select the Central Nominating Committee. The term of president, secretary, and treasurer would be four years, and that of representatives two years, plus two more years if re-elected. A clear change in the overall structural arrangements of the administration of the IPA was taking place, with the formalization of the increased role of the regions and an implicit limitation of the authority of the Executive Committee. This represented a major move towards overall regionalization and democratization of the IPA and, at the same time, a potential reduction of the authority of the President and the Executive Committee to quickly foster and implement new initiatives. In so far as at its boundaries psychoanalysis had entered a period of turbulence with the surrounding social and cultural environment, an evolving situation that might require strong centralized leadership, this reorganization might represent a risk in that potentially it could slow down rapid institutional response and innovation. If, on the other hand, the regions were to take over these initiatives, a more federalized—that is, representative—IPA might still effectively respond to external challenges confronting the psychoanalytic community. In fact, positive aspects of regionalization developed in the following years, with a major initiative of the APsaA to increase its outreach into the community, a major emphasis by the European psychoanalytic community to increase standing interchanges about psychoanalytic practice and their theoretical implications, and a significant increase in formalized interchanges among Latin American psychoanalytic societies. The long-term effects of increasing regionalization of the IPA remain, however, to be seen. In the educational realm, a joint effort of Executive Council and the European Psychoanalytical Federation in 2000 led to improvements in the IPA outreach to develop psychoanalysis in Eastern Europe, begun in the 1990s after the fall of Communism. During that time a special committee of the International New Groups Committee was responsible for the development of training. The success of the programme made it evident that a special committee, the Psychoanalytic Institute for Eastern Europe (PIEE), should be established to monitor the many groups that had already come into existence and the new ones that soon would be formed. This step greatly increased the efficiency of the programme, because the PIEE could establish procedures that fit the special circumstances of developing training where none had existed. Under the leadership of Paolo Fonda, the hopes for the PIEE were realized. Because of financial concerns and the great distances involved, this process employed phone supervision in addition to in-person meetings, conferences on theory and technique, and occasional concentrated analysis, as described earlier. Following the recommendation of the Committee on Child and Adolescent Psychoanalysis, the Executive Council formally recognized the designation of child and adolescent psychoanalysts by the IPA. The interregional conferences organized by the Committee directed by Dr Sara Zac de Filc carried out a successful conference on mental health at Geneva and one on confidentiality in Montreal. The Joseph Sandler Research Programmes successfully implemented training of psychoanalytic researchers. Preparations for the 2001 IPA Congress in Nice proceeded intensively. The effort to stimulate innovation within the administrative structure of psychoanalytic institutes and their educational methodologies encountered strong resistance throughout virtually all levels of central and regional organizations, with the exception of Latin American psychoanalytic societies, which were pressing strongly for more flexible procedures in maintaining standards of training. Innovation would, for example, require the formal acceptance of the French and Uruguayan models as bona fide methodologies of psychoanalytic education. The Committee on Psychoanalytic Education, under the leadership of Dr Jacqueline Amati Mehler, had proposed the development of a research project to study current differences in psychoanalytic education at different institutes, and the comparative effectiveness of these alternative methods. Difficulties in reaching an agreement regarding research methodology between the Committee on Research and the Committee on Psychoanalytic Education slowed this process and, finally, led to the transfer of the exploration of innovation of psychoanalytic education back to Executive Council. We considered it as part of our essential responsibilities to present a series of statements proposing various aspects of changes in the training analysis system, the supervisory system, and the seminar system of psychoanalytic education, as well as promoting discussions regarding the advisability of efforts to expand the training in psychoanalytic psychotherapy within psychoanalytic institutes. This became an unfinished battle to the end of this administration.


2001 Some major projects of this administration were completed during its final year, 2001, and other major initiatives advanced to a state that permitted their completion under the administration of President Daniel Widlöcher, which followed it. The long, delicate discussions around the integration of the German Psychoanalytical Society (DPG) into the IPA culminated in the Executive Council’s approval, at the Nice Congress in July 2001, of the DPG as an Executive Council Provisional Society. This recognized the extraordinary circumstances within the development of this major psychoanalytic society and thus initiated the definite steps towards its integration into the IPA. It signified an immediate growth and expansion of the potential realm of influence of psychoanalysis within German universities, academia, and culture and a full restoration of the important role that German psychoanalysis had played in the past in the history of psychoanalysis. The IPA Eastern European Institute was formally approved as an educational institution, with full authority for developing psychoanalysis in the countries of the former Communist Block, and with the acceptance, at the same time, of flexibility regarding the organization of training analysis, supervision, and seminars. At the time, such acceptance took into account the geographic constraints and financial limitations of Eastern Europe. Overall IPA membership increased by over 1,000 over these four years (from 9,524 members in 1997 to 10,570 members in 2001). The long-smouldering conflict of the “Rio 1” Society in its relation with the “Pro Etica” Group was finally resolved, with the Executive Council’s approval of Study Group status for the former “Pro Etica” Group, while the ideological and political overtones of this conflict also disappeared as an issue. The SAM proposal, with minor modifications, was approved by the Executive Council, was submitted to the membership as a proposal for the required changes in the IPA Constitution and Bylaws, and was formally accepted by the membership, thus streamlining the organizational structure and increasing its ability to function efficiently and responsively. The transition of the IPA into a unicameral Board of Representatives that reflected the new ascendance of the regions as major players in the leadership of international psychoanalysis justified the recommendations of the SAM Task Force. In the area of psychoanalytic education, an important development was the approval of the mandate for a committee on Transitional Models of Psychoanalytic Education (TRAMPE), in charge of further work to accept a broadening of the models of psychoanalytic education, with the inclusion of the French and the Uruguayan in addition to the Eitingon model. One underlying political issue, formerly considered a scientific issue but now openly recognized as such, was the question of whether personal psychoanalysis with three sessions a week could be formally accepted by the IPA. A gradual consensus evolved that the importance of the French and Uruguayan models resided in the total nature of the corresponding educational enterprise, of which the frequency of sessions was only one aspect. The French model very clearly rejected the category of training analyst. An agreement was reached that changes in psychoanalytic education could not be reduced to a simple reduction in the number of sessions required for psychoanalytic training but had to involve the total related educational structure linked, respectively, to the French and Uruguayan models. This agreement on principle and the work of the TRAMPE Committee prepared the groundwork, on the basis of which the next administration under Prof. Widlöcher proceeded to approve and implement the acceptance of these alternative models of education, together with the dominant traditional Eitingon model. The Executive Council also moved ahead in other areas included in the objectives of this administration. A more active relationship of the IPA Council with the International Psychoanalytic Organization of Candidates was achieved, expressed practically in financial support for the candidates’ pre-congress at the IPA Congress in Nice in 2001. An agreement, in principle, on the importance of increasing the relationship between the IPA and allied psychoanalytic psychotherapy societies throughout the three regions was combined with a growing awareness of the importance of the integration of psychoanalytic psychotherapy within the competence of psychoanalytic education. This reflected the fact that our graduates are spending an increased amount of time in psychoanalytic psychotherapy: psychoanalytic education should prepare them optimally for the practice they will be involved in. The outreach activities of the IPA now included a growing interest in the relationship with universities, concretely explored in an inter-regional conference on psychoanalysis held in Uruguay. The 2001 IPA Congress in Nice marked the harmonious transition from this administration to the next. Our interests in increasing outreach to the community, strengthening psychoanalytic research, innovations in psychoanalytic education, expanding new psychoanalytic societies, and opening the field to the exploration of psychoanalytic psychotherapy and our relationship with psychoanalytically oriented groups involved in psychoanalytic psychotherapy were common goals of both the outgoing and the incoming administration that continued to inspire the leadership of the IPA. At the same time, some newly developing challenges that required attention raised the general issue of the relationship between the central leadership and the membership at large, the difficulty of maintaining an ongoing identification of the international membership with the central organization, and the need to further strengthen the two-way communication between “periphery” and “centre”. Within the expanding field of psychoanalytic research, the relationship between empirical and conceptual research, and the relative investment of resources in these two major areas emerged as an issue that the new administration would have to explore further. An intriguing minor conflict around the development of psychoanalysis in Korea raised, for the first time, the question of to what extent telephone analysis and supervision might be a way of expanding psychoanalysis in geographically distant and financially prohibitive areas. The evolution of psychoanalytic theory and technique, with the development of both converging trends and diverging trends within and among ego psychology, post Kleinian psychoanalysis, the relational school, and the French approach to psychoanalysis, signalled the dynamic development of psychoanalysis and the important role of research in clarifying these issues and in this context increasing our relationship to universities and academia.








24Colombia History of psychoanalysis in Colombia

Guillermo Sánchez Medina

Psychoanalysis in Colombia can be divided into four distinct stages. Several internal factors related to the development of social science in general, and sociopolitical events in the world, especially the First and Second World Wars in Europe, have influenced these stages. First Stage: This period starts at the end of the nineteenth century and finishes at the beginning of the post-war years, with the publication in Spanish of Freud’s works and then, later, with the arrival of two pioneers trained in analysis. Arturo Lizarazo, trained in Chile, arrived in 1948, and José Francis Socarrás, trained in Paris, arrived in 1950. This first stage can be called the stage of initiation (1948). Second Stage: The next period saw a process of psychoanalytic development, beginning with the formation of the psychoanalytic group. This period lasted from 1948 to 1958, during which people who were interested in psychoanalysis came together, and the study of individual analysis was initiated. This psychoanalytic group, made up of the pioneers Drs. J. F. Socarrás, A. Lizarazo, G. Angel, A. Martínez, G. Saavedra, and T. Meluk, was recognized internationally in 1956. Third Stage: This period began in 1959, when Dr Carlos Plata came from Argentina and formed the Psychoanalytic Institute. In the ensuing years the teaching process developed, and the group was consolidated. It was accepted by the IPA Congress in Edinburgh, which approved its status as a Society. This period also saw other important events, such as the conflicts between societies, the division and eventual separation of a very esteemed group of analysts, and the consolidation of the society. Recognition at the international level came only after several visits from foreign analysts, who gave a series of theoretical, technical, and clinical seminars; at the same time, Colombian analysts began to attend international congresses This third period ended in 1962. Fourth Stage: This period, from 1962 to today, has been characterized by organizational reforms in the Society, and by scientific productions. We carried out two reorganizations of the Institute’s programmes and made changes in the statutes and bylaws, with the aim of giving more mobility, better training, and a more well-rounded formation to the candidates for adjunct and titular analysts, as well as the didacts. At this time the Revista de la Sociedad Colombiana de Psicoanálisis (Review of the Colombian Psychoanalytic Society) was founded. Also the library was strengthened and enriched; a “Center for Promotion” of psychoanalysis was established with the aim of training psychological analysts, who would later become members of the Institute; and courses were organized to provide information to psychologists, teachers, paediatric specialists, psychiatrists, and general medical practitioners. Also, psychoanalytic seminars, encounters, symposia, and congresses were organized. At the end of 1998 another psychoanalytic group, the “Sigmund Freud Psychoanalytic Studies Group”, was formed; this later became the Colombian Psychoanalytic Society. The Colombian Psychoanalytic Society was approved by the IPA in 1961. At the present time (2011) it has 2 Honorary Members, 1 Distinguished Member, 45 Members, and 37 Associate Members. The ages range from 30 to 88; 32 are women and 53 are men. (Since 1959, 16 members have died.) There are 9 candidates, of whom 5 are medical doctors and 4 psychologists. There were 25 classes between 1959 and 2011, from which 151 students have graduated, 32 of whom are psychologists and 67 medical doctors. Forty-two members left the Society, some of them to join other societies (the Freudian Psychoanalytical Society of Colombia, and the Colombian Psychoanalytic Association). Various members of the Society have participated on the governing boards of the IPA, the Latin American Psychoanalytic Federation (FEPAL), and the International Association for the History of Psychoanalysis (IAHP). The journal (the Review) of the Society has been published since 1976. It has come out four times a year, and now totals 30 volumes. The Colombian Psychoanalytic Association, certified in 1999, as of 2006 had 22 Members, 5 Associates, 9 Candidates, and four classes, made up of 14 women and 29 men, ranging in age from 38 to 77. Its journal, Psicoanálisis. Revista de la Asociación Psicoanalítica Colombiana, has published 16 editions. The group originally known as the Group for Freudian Studies, IPA, formed in 1993, is now known as the Freudian Psychoanalytical Society of Colombia. It has 6 Teaching Members, 6 Members, and 8 Candidates, of whom 11 are women and 9 are men, ranging in ages from 41 to 74. All told, the various groups have published 60 books on psychoanalysis, written by 17 of the members. Colombian analysts study the Freudian models, the psychology of the self, Klein, the Post- and Neo-Kleinians, Bion, Winnicott, Kohut, Kernberg, Meltzer, Balint, Fairbairn, Guntrip, Mahler, Lacan, and Green; also the intersubjectivists, cognitive psychology, and the theory of complexity of the neurosciences. Teaching is open-ended and pluralistic, with specialities according to the preference of the instructor but always stressing individual analysis through which the transference–countertransference process is played out. Our research opens doors to the mental complexity that includes psychopathological organizations in its structures, which, while they present as disorders, have a psychic organization. History is continuous, made up both of events and of those who produce them—people who make their mark on our understanding. In fact, everything happens in life; even the very sensation that everything happens is a happening. It is up to those who follow to gather up and to give order to new knowledge, applying the psychoanalytic scientific perspective, so that the wisdom of yesterday, today, and tomorrow will continue to be created. Bibliography Sánchez Medina, G. (1991). Psicoanálisis, ayer, hoy y mañana. Historia del psicoanálisis en Colombia. Bogotá: Editorial Industria Gráfica Gaviota. Sánchez Medina, G. (2006). Un pensamiento histórico. Breve historia del psicoanálisis en Colombia. Revista de la Sociedad Colombiana de Psicoanálisis, 31 (4): 513–599.







9Italy The Italian Psychoanalytical Association

Jacqueline Amati Mehler & Giovanna Ambrosio


Our origins Until the year 1992, the Italian Psychoanalytical Association [Associazione Italiana di Psicoanalisi (AIPsi)] shared its history and tradition with the Italian Psychoanalytical Society [Società Psicoanalitica Italiana (SPI)], and many of the AIPsi founding members had been active participants of the SPI’s institutional life, in office in a variety of positions. On 26 July 1992 some analysts of the Italian Psychoanalytical Society, including Emilio Servadio—a pioneer of psychoanalysis in Italy, founder in 1932, together with Edoardo Weiss, of the SPI—addressed a request to the Executive Committee of the International Psychoanalytical Association to set up a Study Group. The reasons for the dissent with the SPI were essentially of an ethical and only subsequently of an institutional nature. They regarded in particular serious dysfunctions and ethical flaws in the Training Institute of the Rome Psychoanalytic Centre that had not only compromised the training programme of certain individual candidates, but had gradually affected the whole group of students, the relationships between training analysts, and, consequently, all the activities of the Society. In spite of many years of painful and at times dramatic internal discussions and a generalized climate of intimidation, it was not possible to resolve these difficulties. Problems that should have been confronted and resolved at the individual and ethical level ended up, therefore, being confused with and overcome by political conflicts and the taking of sides, which disguised and took precedence over the original problems. The very people who had initially tried to throw light on the serious institutional pathologies that would have been transmitted to future generations of analysts were themselves subjected to disciplinary action and retaliation by a special “ad hoc” committee that denounced their action as “slander”. However, it should be pointed out that there was no evidence of any significant technical or scientific divergences among the various factions. Following a visit by IPA Site Visit Committee appointed for the purpose of investigating the complex dysfunctions of the SPI, the request to form a new Study Group was accepted on 12 August 1992 by IPA President Joseph Sandler and the whole Executive Board. The activities of the new Association began under the auspices of a Sponsoring Committee composed of Adam Limentani (Chairman), Yolanda Gampel, and Gemma Jappe, joined later by Liza Gutierrez Green. The first task of the AIPsi was to draft bylaws that reflected the common project of institutional and scientific life, and especially the organization of an Institute that would best guarantee the training of candidates. On 28 July 1993, during the Amsterdam Congress, the change from Study Group—chaired by Adriano Giannotti, its first president—to Provisional Society was approved. On 1997, at the Barcelona Congress, the AIPsi was accepted as a Component Society. From 1993 onward, the presidency was held by Adriano Giannotti (1992–94), Jacqueline Amati Mehler (1994–99), Andreas Giannakoulas (1999–2003), Carlo Vittorio Todesco (2003–07), and Jorge Canestri (2007–). From 1994 on, the honorary president was Emilio Servadio. From the first presidency of Adriano Giannotti, who passed away prematurely in 1994, the Board of the Association was engaged in the complex work of constituting and organizing the new association, ranging from the search for a venue suited to the needs of the members to finalizing the organization of the Training Institute and setting up its valuable library, thanks to the donations of Emilio Servadio, Stefano Fajrajzen, and Bernie Pacella. More recently, out-reach activities that support the main body of institutional activities represented by theoretical-clinical scientific initiatives have been strengthened.


The AIPsi members Somehow, the experience of constructing a new psychoanalytic society in Italy and the gradual consolidation of a societal identity have allowed the painful process of separation from the SPI—a laceration resulting from the troubled situation that had been going on for years (but, then, as an ancient playwright said, “The reward for pain is experience”!)—to be overcome. At the same time it represented, for those who carried it forward, the only way of preserving the sense of continuity with themselves and with values that had been shared with their own teachers and, in the past, with many colleagues. Right from the start, the Association was characterized by psychoanalytic rigor, combined with the theoretical pluralism of the original training of the founding colleagues, firmly welded to Freudian tradition, and, last but not least, the diversity of geographical origins: Rome, Milan, Florence, Bologna, but also Buenos Aires, Boston, and London. In addition to the outstanding personality of Emilio Servadio, among its founder members there was a “Roman” nucleus, including Adriano Giannotti, Jacqueline Amati Mehler, Simona Argentieri, Salvatore Grimaldi, and Anna Schober. Other members, such as Mario Bertolini, Silvio Zucconi, and Sebastiana Sciré came from different geographic areas but had been trained at the Milan Institute. Still others had done their training abroad—for example, Jorge Canestri was a full member of the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association and then a full member of the SPI, and Andreas Giannakoulas was a full member of the British Society and then also a full member of the SPI. Among the present AIPsi members there are classic Freudians, Kleinians, Winnicottians, and Bionians, which makes for lively scientific meetings. This “imprinting” has led to a breadth of views and an attention to the clinical–theoretical comparison between different conceptual ideas and experiences that has protected the new Association from a dull and dreary psychoanalytic provincialism. During the AIPsi scientific meetings, often attended by external colleagues, an attentive reciprocal listening (without sacrificing psychoanalytic strictness or constructive criticism) usually results in fruitful and animated discussion. The Association is particularly open to international relationships and the political–institutional debates of the IPA through a continuous involvement of members in these issues. Some members particularly are representatives at an international level in terms of both institutional life and the scientific debate within the IPA and the European Psychoanalytical Federation (EPF). It is difficult to list all the many post-Freudian masters and teachers who have inspired us. As far as Italy is concerned, we must remember Edoardo Weiss, Emilio Servadio, Eugenio Gaddini, Piero Bellanova, Stefano Fajrajzen, and Cesare Musatti; and, from other countries, D. W. Winnicott, P. Heimann, W. R. Bion, J. Sandler, H. Rosenfeld, W. and M. Baranger, J. Bleger, P. Greenacre, and F. Tustin.


The basic criteria of our Association The combination of a strict adherence to psychoanalytic tenets and open-mindedness is reflected above all in our training programme, policies, and procedures. A considerable investment in resources for the training of candidates is accompanied by special attention to the difficulties of those who have to travel from other cities to attend seminars (for example, they are offered accommodation in the AIPsi venue) as well as to an extremely careful evaluation of their progress. The AIPsi follows the Eitingon model. Candidates are first selected after the evaluation of their motivation as outlined in their written application and following three interviews with three training analysts. If accepted, potential candidates start their analysis with a training analyst, whom they choose from among those available. Their analysis remains totally private; the candidates have no contact at all with the Institute until they are admitted to a second selection after a minimum of two years of analysis. If they are admitted, they attend seminars organized in classes lasting four years, in which an intense study of Freudian texts is integrated with post-Freudian authors and clinical seminars. All candidates are invited from the start to take part in the activities of the International Psychoanalytical Studies Organization (IPSO). Our main purpose has been to construct a Training Institute that would offer candidates broad theoretically and clinically integrated training and learning programmes, to which all the training analysts as well as full members are committed. Occasionally associate members also participate in teaching seminars, according to their specific competencies. An essential point has been that of limiting the training function by statute, so that it is regulated according to criteria that will prevent risks of distortion in the direction of personal power (i.e., age limits, limited number of candidates per training analyst, continuous monitoring of students by the whole Training Committee, which meets every month). The Director of the Training Institute meets regularly with the candidates to discuss issues and/or problems connected with training matters. An important point of AIPsi policies has been to establish the principle of a permanent training programme, aimed not only at offering continuing opportunities for theoretical–clinical learning to younger members, but also to constitute for the older members an area of reciprocal guarantee, of open discussion, and of integration of scientific and institutional themes. The venue of the Institute and the Association is in Rome, which, due to the geography of the country, places a considerable strain on those members and candidates who live in other cities. This means that seminars and scientific activities usually have to be conducted at the weekends. Members, trainers, and trainees are distributed throughout various cities; in addition to Rome, where the majority live, there are members and candidates in Milan, Turin, Padua, Bologna, Florence, Bari, Sardinia, and Sicily. Our Association attributes great value to scientific activities as a permanent opportunity for meetings, hosting guests from other IPA societies when possible, and organizing interdisciplinary meetings (neuroscience, psychiatry, literature, art, cinema, philosophy) as an exercise ground for communication and discussion both in content and in methodology, and as the best premise for linking everyday clinical practice to a common theoretic field and conceptual controversy in all its articulations and diversifications. This is an attempt to encourage a rich and intense societal life as an essential premise for pursuing another of our basic objectives: that of a constant open and international exchange with other IPA societies. Over the years there have been many prestigious visiting guests at our scientific AIPsi meetings, such as O. Kernberg, J. Sandler, A. Green, H. Blum, and R. Hinshelwood, to mention just a few. A public free consultation service is carried out by the candidates and recent graduates, who meet in a monthly workshop to investigate, under the supervision of a training analyst, issues connected with the first intake interviews from both a technical and a theoretical perspective. It also serves the community as a place for seeking qualified help. When we first formed the Association, there were almost no books at all. However, as mentioned above, our library has benefited from very important donations by Stefano Fajrajzen and Bernie Pacella, and from Prof. Servadio’s remarkable collection of books. Among these there are some very valuable historic volumes, with personal dedications by some of the first analytic pioneers. We have now computerized the library, containing over 10,000 books, journals, and reviews. The library is open to the public. The AIPsi Journal called Psicoanalisi was founded in 1997—a six-monthly publication of specifically psychoanalytic works open to its members and, by invitation, to foreign colleagues whose work has either not been translated into Italian or is significant for its clinical and conceptual qualities. To guarantee good scientific quality, AIPsi members’ papers are anonymously peer-reviewed by a committee of foreign IPA colleagues. Our task is to preserve the transmission of psychoanalysis with its methodological specificity, while at the same time remaining sensitive to cultural development and to the use of psychoanalysis as a theory of psychic organization and functioning in the field of mental health professions.

27Mexico History of the Mexican Association for Psychoanalytic Practice, Training and Research

Raquel Berman

Our history encompasses the 35 years since the establishment in 1965 of the Mexican Association of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy (MAPP) and the period from 2000 to the present, which covers MAPP’s affiliation process to the International Psychoanalytical Association under a new name: Asociación Mexicana para La Práctica, Investigación y Enseñanza del Psicoanálisis, A.C. (AMPIEP) [Mexican Association for Psychoanalytic Practice, Training and Research (MAPPTR)]. During the Chicago IPA Congress (2009) we reached Component Society status (interim), and in January 2010 MAPPTR was ratified as a Component Society.

Prehistory of MAPP and its foundation In 1957, under the initiative of Santiago Ramírez, one of the founding members of the recently established Mexican Psychoanalytic Association (Asociacion Psicoanalítica Mexicana), four non-medical professionals and one medical doctor formed the first group to be trained by the Mexican Psychoanalytic Association (MPA) following the Eitingon’s model of psychoanalytic training. Their teachers, analysts, and supervisors were the founding members of MPA: Drs Ramírez, Remus, Parres, A. Gonzalez, Ch. Gonzalez, as well as Feder (psychologist) and Cesarman (MD, who at that time had not yet finished their training). Three and a half years later (1960) the four non-medical candidates—Raquel Berman and Dolores Sandoval among them—were abruptly dropped from training because they were not medical doctors. Only the medical candidate, Diaz Infante, was allowed to continue training. In 1964 Diaz Infante responded to a request for psychoanalytic training from Frida Walerstein de Rosenberg, a psychologist, and they contacted Ramírez about the possibility of founding a psychoanalytic training institution for psychologists. Infante and Ramírez approached Berman and Sandoval and invited them to join the first group of trainees. They both initially declined because of the traumatic experience they had suffered in 1960, and they feared that it would be repeated. Ramírez gave assurances that as they were to be the first generation of graduates of this new training organization, its bylaws would establish their status as founders, thus securing the group’s autonomy against outside interference. The founding group of candidates was composed of six women professionals, all of them non-medical—Berman, Ramos, Sandoval, Walerstein de Rosenberg, Poveda, and Rosas y de Mendizabal—and one male candidate, Jaime de Leon, who left after one year. Ramírez was able to attract a group of analysts from the MPA to become the group’s first teachers, analysts, and supervisors. They were Ramírez, Diaz Infante, Valner, Arizmendi, Carrera, and, later on, Bellon. On 27 April 1965 the Mexican Association of Psychotherapy (MAP) was legally constituted. The aforementioned analysts became the first members of the Board of MAP, as distinct from being members of MAP. Ramírez later said of his approach to training that he “taught what he knew, which was psychoanalysis, and that he supervised as a psychoanalyst”. But there was a contradiction between the name the MAP, the psychoanalytic training it offered, and the confusing messages given by some analysts to the first candidates—for example, not to use the couch and not to interpret within the transference. Later Diaz Infante explained his motives for encouraging the foundation of this new institution as related to the fact that he was “the only male member in a family made up by women, and he felt his sisters did not have the opportunity obtain a career as he did and that in this case he desired that his professional sisters, Raquel Berman and Dolores Sandoval, with whom he had developed friendly ties during their shared analytic training, should have the same opportunities as he did”. Ramírez later gave another reason for his support of MAP: he said that he was “mainly motivated to found the institution because he felt that psychologists were being unfairly treated, but that his decisive motive was his desire to repair what was done to the two persons Berman and Sandoval whom he encouraged to train in MPA”. In 1970 all the MPA analysts on the Board of MAP attempted to close the Institute when, without explanation, they abruptly left the board and halted the training. It was later discovered that their actions were tied to internal tensions in the MPA, which culminated in a split (1971–1972–1973) in the course of which 14 members left the MPA. Upon their abrupt departure from the Board, the six graduates/founders immediately constituted themselves as the new Board, taking charge of the organization and its different functions. In 1973 they legally changed the name of the organization to Mexican Association of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy (MAPP). The new name was a compromise, as it both affirmed MAPP’S psychoanalytic training and practice, and, by including the word “psychotherapy”, expressed anxiety that we were “only psychoanalytic therapists but not psychoanalysts”. The ambivalence about the group’s psychoanalytic identity was introjected by some graduates, while others never doubted their identity as psychoanalysts. From 1970 to 1976 the following MPA analysts occasionally taught seminars at MAPP: Arizmendi, Aiza, Barriguete, Carrera, Cueli, Diaz Portillo, Dupont, Gaitan, Gonzalez Pineda, Guarner, Gustavo Lutteroth, Díaz de Mathman, Meza, Moreno Corzo, Ortega, Remus Araico, Rojas, Salles, Toscano, Valner, and Vega Coronado. Some of them later left the MPA. Those who remained as teachers and supervisors were Ramírez, Diaz Infante, Bellon, and Valner. In spite of the repetitive traumas of abandonment by MPA analysts (in 1960 as well as in 1970), there remains gratitude in MAPP for the psychoanalytic knowledge transmitted by these analysts to the first generations of our members. MAPP was indisputably the first organization in Mexico (since 1965) to offer analytic training to non-medical professionals, primarily psychologists. The association led the way on this issue and was followed by many other parallel psychoanalytic organizations that sprang up in the wake of MAPP: developments that transformed the Mexican psychoanalytic landscape. To compensate for the loss of local analysts as psychoanalytic teachers and trainers, MAPP’s International Committee (headed by Berman) established a programme in 1970 that brought foreign analysts (from the IPA and from non-IPA institutions) to lecture, supervise, give seminars, lead workshops, and participate in symposia. Among these visitors were Rudolph Ekstein, Anni Bergmann, Anne-Marie Sandler, Ernest Freud, Joseph Sandler, Paulina Kernberg, Judith Wallerstein, Calvin Settlage, and Ramon Ganzarain. In 1981, under the initiative of Sandoval, MAPP’s members acquired a floor in a condominium building for the organization’s premises on Arquímedes 3, which was situated almost on the corner of the Paseo de la Reforma, the most important thoroughfare in Mexico City. It houses the Institute and the Association as well as the library and offices for the administrative staff. A two-year course in psychoanalytic research was instituted in 1982, and the course was later approved by the Ministry of Education as being equivalent to an MA degree. Up to 2009, 13 classes numbering 135 individuals have graduated from this course. Torres Torija de Bolio, C. Lasky, and A. Valencia have been involved in administering this course. In 1986, J. de J. Gonzalez, a former president of MAPP, left the organization, taking with him four candidates from the 8th class and one from the 7th class, all of whom he had analysed, forming with them a new training organization. After this negative experience, analysts were restricted to analysing or supervising a maximum of two candidates; later this was further reduced to only one candidate per analyst. During the 1985 Mexico City earthquakes AMPIEP was involved in therapeutic rescue activities. The reactions of the therapists and patients during and after the crisis intervention in 8 of the 14 therapeutic groups have been described in publications in English, Spanish and Japanese (Berman & Roel, l993, l995). Encouraged by Jean Sanville of Los Angeles, in 1987 MAPP joined the North American Council for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapists (New York), whose struggles to become psychoanalysts paralleled our own in many ways. For 35 years MAPP had functioned as an association of members with a Board composed of a president, secretary, and working committees, each covering different areas such as clinic activities, research, outreach activities to the community, publications, library, international relations, and the ethics committee. The highest authority in the Society is the General Assembly of members, which meets four times a year for decisions requiring members’ votes, notably the election of its officials every two years. MAPP pioneered Mahler’s separation–individuation theory in Mexico by organizing four international Mahler symposia (in 1979, 1981, 1984, and 1989). In 1991 R. Radosh founded an innovative course in group supervision for supervisors, tied to transference–countertransference and parallel group phenomena. Our journal Imagen Psicoanalítica, founded in 1992 (under the editorship of Sanchez Escárcega, and presently of Alveano Hernandez), has appeared continuously to the present day. The Clinic, founded in 1965, continues to provide low-fee patients for candidates for training as well as for members. The Institute Sigmund Freud is also a committee of the Board of the Association, but because of its extraordinary central importance related to the multiple functions of psychoanalytic training, it has its own board, composed of a director of the Institute, a coordinator in charge of training, and seven committee heads who deal with various aspects of the process of training. At present (2010), the 15th class of candidates is being trained (now under the IPA). Important contributions to the Society’s scientific activities are the ongoing Study Groups. A research project on “Couples Disruption and Its Effects on Children” (1980–90), founded by Berman and Roel and lead by Roel for ten years, obtained official support from the National Council of Science and Technology and benefited from the supervision of Judith Wallerstein. At present (2010), there are, within MAPPTR, the following Study Groups: Father–Daughter relationship, Gender studies, Psychodynamics of Psychoanalytic Institutions, and Enactment. MAPP has organized 14 psychoanalytic film festivals and has published its psychoanalytic commentaries in 14 printed editions as well as a film festival in 2003 about “Father’s Imprint on Daughter’s Development”.


Summary of the ten-year process of affiliation with the IPA Since 1989, Berman and Roel (then President of MAPP) explored with Robert Wallerstein (then IPA President) the possibility of MAPP’s integration into the IPA. Wallerstein explained that this move would be opposed by the Latin American societies. When later the suit against the American Psychoanalytic Association (APsaA) resulted in an integration of the North American non-medical and parallel societies into the IPA, under the presidency of Wallerstein, Berman became a member of IPTAR (the Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research) under the “limited window of opportunity clause” by fulfilling requirements demonstrating her psychoanalytic competence through public presentations of clinical cases, written records of cases as well as undergoing supervisions with Grand, Franco, and Lauro, IPTAR senior training analysts. During the 1997 Barcelona IPA Congress, Berman contacted Joseph Sandler, then President of the IPA. He encouraged her to continue her efforts to integrate MAPP into the IPA and referred her to David Sachs, head of the International New Groups Committee, who, in turn, recommended that she contact Garza Guerrero, then Latin American head of ING. In 1998 Steven Ellman and Jean Sanville, co-chairs of Independent Psychoanalytic Societies (IPS), suggested that MAPP and SPM–Parque Mexico (a parallel Mexican Psychoanalytic Society founded by A. Gonzalez, an ex MPA member as well as a former vice-president of IPA, and by A. Gaytan, formerly of the MPA, who had both left the Society in its 1971 split) should merge into one society and become members of IPS. Several encounters in 1998 were attended by Bolio, Hoffs de Sod, Berman, Baram from MAPP, and by Bueno, Gaytan, Radchik from SPM–Parque Mexico, but a merger of the two societies was opposed by a majority of MAPP’s members and so did not take place. However, a shared decision was taken in 1999 to invite Steven Ellman to visit Mexico, so that he could share with both groups the experience he had gained as IPTAR’s negotiator with the IPA and with the IPTAR–IPA Site Committee. In May 1998 Berman met Cesarman from the MPA about MAPP’s intended application to the IPA. He responded that “there is nothing against MAPP, and if we fulfil all IPA requirements there was no reason that we could not enter the IPA”. Steven Ellman visited Mexico on 15 January 1999 and met with both groups at the University Club; he visited MAPP’s premises on 16 January, and on 17 January he met in the Hotel Camino Real with Berman, Fontanot de Rhoads, Hoffs de Sod, and Hazan, with whom he discussed our future application to the IPA. Ellman promised to inform Kernberg, Hanly, and Sachs about our intentions. On 13 February 1999, Arlene Richards (IPTAR and Freudian Society) and Jean Sanville (LAISPS: the Los Angeles Institute and Society for Psychoanalytic Studies), as unofficial representatives of IPS, visited Mexico City and participated in a joint meeting of AMPP (Asociacion Mexicana de Psicoterapia Psicoanalitica) and SPM–Parque Mexico, in which they talked about their own personal experiences during the legal suit against the American Psychoanalytic Association, their application to the IPA, and the accomplishments as well as losses they suffered due to this process. In March 1998, Berman asked the General Assembly of MAPP to officially recognize her role as liaison and negotiator with IPA, and she was approved by unanimous vote. On 24 February 1999 Torres Torija de Bolio, as President of MAPP, and Berman, as liaison MAPP–IPA, wrote to Garza Guerrero expressing MAPP’s interest in applying to the IPA, to which he responded that according to IPA bylaws we must apply as a Study Group. However, Sachs’s report in the International Newsletter of the IPA, in March 1999, describing ING’s new open policy towards new groups in Europe and North America, showed a different attitude towards parallel groups. Fontanot de Rhoads summarized Sachs’s relevant points, which were discussed in reflective encounters among some MAPP members. Especially important was Sachs’s focus on the demonstration of analytic clinical competence, innovative approaches to supervision, the concept of “historical anomalies” referring to psychoanalytic organizations established prior to 1975 and not affiliated with the IPA (due to political conflicts), but who offered training under the Eitingon model and were willing to increase analysis for candidates from three to four times a week. Thus he expressed the IPA’s wish to integrate new groups into the IPA, but at the same time insisted on maintaining psychoanalytic training standards. Berman decided that in spite of Garza Guerrero’s indication that we needed to become a Study Group, it was important to renew contact with Sachs during the Santiago 1999 IPA Congress and present our views about becoming integrated as a Provisional Society. Baram, Hazan, Fontanot de Rhoads, Alvarez, and Berman participated in the 26 July 1999 meeting with Sachs and Compton. Berman discussed the following points with Sachs: (1) our fulfilled expectation that Sachs was open to meeting with and listening to us; (2) the reasons why MAPP was eligible to become a Provisional Society, as well as expected obstacles to reach this aim; (3) why the IPA should encourage MAPP’s affiliation with the IPA; and (4) providing Sachs with more information about MAPP. A verbatim version of this long meeting was given by Fontanot de Rhoads, and therefore only highlights of this memorable encounter will be mentioned here. Sachs put the question why was the MPA not receptive to our aspirations, and why did they not support us, accompanied by concrete examples of derogatory pronouncements towards us by the MPA. Sachs inquired about our name, and it was explained that it related to the vicissitudes of our origins and history. Sachs then informed us “that the approval of the local psychoanalytic society was not needed any more to initiate a new group; there existed a new direct route to apply to the IPA through ING, which would decide after examining all the documents about our past functioning as a psychoanalytic training group if we were eligible to start the process of application”. Berman thought that Sachs was considering MAPP as a possible Study Group; she explained that with a history of 35 uninterrupted years of training 13 classes of candidates, we aspired to become integrated as a Provisional Society, following the example of the four North American non-medical psychoanalytic societies. Compton inquired why MAPP wanted to belong to the IPA in view of its autonomous history. Berman responded that due to its origins and history it was a logical step for MAPP to become integrated into the IPA mainstream. Also, it was a step that would strengthen MAPP and counteract the increased eclecticism of some members due to the intense impact of competitive therapies, the medicalization of mental disturbances, and the perils of a diluted psychoanalytic identity. Berman explained that the IPA’s mission should be to encourage and support parallel psychoanalytic associations that grounded their therapeutic work in the Unconscious, and with long achievement records such as MAPP, offering us innovative transitions to reach the goal of integration into the IPA, as had already happened with some societies in North America and in Europe. From 1998 to 1999, the Affiliation Committee (Baram, Fontanot de Rhoads, Alvarez, and Hoffs de Sod) designed, applied, and evaluated a survey in order to explore the membership’s attitudes towards joining the IPA, with the following results: 74.19% agreed, 21.70% were opposed, and 4.09% were neither for nor against. However, when in 2000 the formal petition to apply was sent to Otto Kern-berg, then President of the IPA, the entire membership unanimously signed, indicating that they were in agreement with the stipulations of the Equivalency Procedures document. On 5 October 2000, a formal application, signed by Torres Torija de Bolio, then President of MAPP, Beatriz Rosas y de Mendizabal, then Institute director, and Berman as liaison MAPP–IPA, as well as the Affiliation Process Committee (Alvarez, Baram, Fontanot de Rhoads, and Hoffs de Sod), was addressed to Garza Guerrero, with copies to Kernberg, Tyson, and Sachs, accompanied by requested documents about our institutional functioning reporting features of our 35-year history, which justified our aspiration to become involved in a process leading in the future towards an IPA Provisional Society status.
