On 16 October 2000 we received a most significant reply from Garza Guerrero, informing us that we could apply as a Provisional Society according to the Functional and Evaluated Equivalency Procedures, which had been approved during the 1999 Santiago, Chile Congress. This new route implied that each member formerly trained in and graduated from MAPP could gain individual membership in the IPA by fulfilling the requirements established in this document. This was a complex process, but at present (2010) the number of IPA members is 29, plus 4 associate members, and 14 are at various stages of preparation to take this step. From 2003 to 2009, the IPA assigned Drs Jorge de la Torre and Romualdo Romanowski to MAPP, first as an exploratory committee, later as a site committee, and finally as a liaison committee. We were most fortunate with the appointment of these experienced analysts. They offered steadfast leadership and uninterrupted oversight of our process of affiliation, as well as models of psychoanalytic leadership. They recommended to the IPA Board, then under the presidency of Cláudio Eizirik, that we be approved as Provisional Society during the Rio Congress in 2005. Berman, Fontanot de Rhoads, Bolio, and Hoffs de Sod attended the Rio Congress. Our admission was postponed in Rio by the Board and House of Representatives due to their rejection of the word “psychotherapy” in our name. We were painfully surprised by this negative outcome, as during the past years we had never been told explicitly by any IPA authority that a change in our name was required. In Rio our Liaison and Affiliation Committees protested to Eizirik and to ING chair David Iseman. Torres Torija de Bolio, then President of MAPP, obtained a written statement that if we changed our name and our process continued successfully, we would become a Component Society in Chicago in 2009. These events produced a process of mourning and elaboration by the membership because of the loss of the old name, and the rejection suffered in Rio, as well as the implementation of a series of complex legal steps and economic expenditure. In 2005, 14 of our members (Cimet, Díaz de Mathman, Fontanot de Rhoads, Guzik, Hazan, Hoffs de Sod, Lasky, Oñate, Radosh, Rosas y de M., Salomón, Torres Torija de Bolio, Walerstein de Rosenberg, Wollmershausser) became Direct Members of the IPA after fulfilling the Equivalency Procedures. In January 2006 in New York we were recognized as an Interim Provisional Society under the name Mexican Association for Psychoanalytic Practice, Training and Research (MAPPTR). In Berlin, in July 2007, we were ratified as Provisional Society, and then MAPPTR’s President Fontanot de Rhoads addressed the Business Meeting and received the diploma from President Eizirik. During the Berlin Congress MAPPTR co-signed, as one of the five Mexican societies, a letter to Eizirik proposing that the 2011 47th IPA Congress take place in Mexico. In October 2008, the project for a doctoral programme in Psychoanalytic Research coordinated by C. Oñate V. was approved by the DPPT (the Developing Psychoanalytic Practice and Training Programme). We became a Component Society (interim) by unanimous vote in Chicago 2009, to be ratified in New York in January 2010. Berman addressed the Business Meeting and received the diploma from outgoing President Eizirik. The Liaison (Berman) and Affiliation (Fontanot de Rhoads, Hoffs de Sod, T. T. de Bolio, and later Radosh, replacing T. T. de Bolio) Committees of MAPPTR have worked very hard during these last ten years, always attentive to any obstacles that would interfere with the goal of integration into the IPA. These were exhilarating but also extremely stressful times, as these colleagues worked simultaneously at four levels: (1) constant communication with IPA (Broom-hills); (2) continuous contacts with J. de la Torre and Romanowski and planning their visits; (3) ongoing negotiations with successive ING head (Sachs, Iseman, and Aisenstein) as well as IPA presidents (Kernberg, Widlöcher, Eizirik, and Hanly); (4) at the same time, always keeping the MAPPTR Board and Institute, as well as the membership, informed about the development of the affiliation process as well as what the IPA was expecting from them. A fifth task was added when de la Torre and Romanowski officially passed on to these committees, with IPA authorization, the evaluation process and criteria of psychoanalytic competence of those members who wished to become IPA members in the future. Some of our members (Berman as co-chair, Fontanot de Rhoads, Hoffs de Sod, and Guzik) are participating in the Local Arrangement Committee as part of the Cultural Program Committee for the Mexico City IPA Congress in 2011.

Bibliography Archives of the Liaison and Affiliation Committees, MAPPTR, 1989–2009. Berman, R., & Roel, G. (1989). Las luchas de poder en instituciones sicoanalíticas en las que predominan las mujeres en el liderazgo [Power Struggles in psychoanalytic Institutions in which Women predominate as Leaders]. Presented in the 10th Congress of the International Group Psychotherapy Association, Amsterdam.] Berman, R., & Roel, G. (l993). Encounter with death and destruction: The l985 Mexico City earthquake. Group Analysis, 26: 81–89. [Confrontacion con la muerte durante el desastre provocado por el Temblor de l985. Reflexiones a 10 años de distancia. Imagen Psicoanalitica (l995), 4 (6): 11–20]. Folletos Informativos, Asociación Mexicana de Psicoterapia Psicoanalítica, 1973, 1976, 1978. Ramírez, S. (1971). Historia del movimiento psicoanalítico en México. Revista de Neurología y Psiquiatría, 12: 4. Ramírez, S. (1979). Ajuste de Cuentas. Mexico: Nueva Visión. Rosenberg, F. (1990). El psicoanálisis de ayer, hoy y mañana en la Asociación Mexicana de Psicoterapia Psicoanalítica en Memorias del Simposio “El psicoanálisis en un Mundo en Transición. Mexico: AMPP. Sandoval, D. (1992). Historia de la Asociación Mexicana de Psicoterapia Psicoanalítica. Imagen Psicoanalítica 1.







Part VIIPA leadership







8Italy A brief history of the Italian Psychoanalytical Society

Domenico Chianese

Idealism, Catholic culture, fascism, and then, successively, Marxism, all represented rigid barriers against the entry of psychoanalysis into Italy: not only a science—even though it was long denied such status—but also a way of conceiving the world at large. Despite these enormous obstacles, psychoanalysis was born somewhat precociously in Italy, and this is due to the temerity and courage of a few “pioneer spirits” who were able to sow their seeds in what was apparently barren terrain. On the cusp between the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, Italian culture was dominated by the idealism of the philosophers Benedetto Croce and Giovanni Gentile, a spiritualistic current that was hostile not only to psychology, but also to the emergent phenomenon of psychoanalysis. Croce had read little of and understood even less of Freud’s writings, whose doctrine was, in his opinion, both “deterministic” and “irrational”, and psychoanalysis—likewise psychology—were considered “pseudo-sciences”. With his scholastic reform of 1923, Gentile, who was Minister of Education during the fascist regime, eliminated the teaching of psychology in schools in a decision unequalled in any Western nation of the day. The other culture opposed to the entry of psychoanalysis into Italy was the Catholic culture. It is well noted that the Catholic religion has always weighed heavily and negatively upon Italian culture. The Catholic Church criticized the “pan-sexuality” and “materialism” of Freud. It is therefore singular that Communist Marxism, which was to develop widely in Italy in the years following fascism, criticized Freud’s doctrines for precisely the opposite reasons: a lack of materialism and an excessive spiritualism. As can be seen, radical and contradictory criticisms abounded. As regards the Marxist environment, in 1930 the Soviet Union issued its official condemnation of psychoanalysis. The Italian Marxist philosopher, Antonio Gramsci, was imprisoned by the fascist regime, and it was during his incarceration that he wrote his famous Lettere dal carcere [Letters from prison] (Gramsci, 1965). Hegelian like Benedetto Croce, Gramsci criticized Freud’s Kantism, but, exceptional and acute intellectual that he was—and despite having difficulty in accepting Freud’s “philosophy”—he did not negatively evaluate psychotherapy as a therapy. In his letter of 20 March 1931 (Gramsci, 1965, p. 428), he wrote:


… it is possible that Giulia [Gramsci’s wife, who suffered from mental disturbances] might avail herself of a psychoanalytic cure, if her illness is of a purely nervous origin. Furthermore, I believe that more than psychoanalysis, what really counts is the doctor himself…. It is possible that psychoanalysis is more concrete than old psychiatry, or at least that it obliges doctors to study single patients in a more concrete manner; that is, see the patient and not the illness: furthermore, Freud … wished to create a general philosophy out of a few empirical criteria of observation, but this is of little importance.


The complex and controversial relationship between fascism and psychoanalysis would merit greater discussion; however, for reasons of space, here we can only speak of some the highlights. Initially, fascism had no radical criticism towards psychoanalysis. The negative judgement that was, successively, to be affirmed with increasing frequency was principally determined by the anti-Semitism of fascist culture. The developing hostility was not so much towards psychoanalysis, but towards the psychoanalysts themselves, almost all of whom (beginning with Freud) were Jewish. In Italy, all the pioneers of psychoanalysis—Levi Bianchini, Weiss, Servadio, Musatti, etc….—were Jewish. Piero and Anna Bellanova, in their precious short-volume of 1982 entitled Le due Gradive, bring to light a series of previously unpublished documents from a confidential file from the State Archives, which is labelled “The International Psychoanalytical Society”. This file was opened following an application (dated 27 November 1934—XIII fascist era) by Emilio Servadio to join the International Psychoanalytical Association. This application was, in accordance with the practices of the day, made to the Ministry of Internal Affairs and gave rise to a full-blown secret investigation whose practices are documented in the research material of the Bellanovas. These documents bring back to life the dramatic and, at the same time, grotesque environment in which Italian psychoanalysis made its first steps. To cite but one example of grotesqueness, we can read the Italianization of Freud’s name as “Sigismondo”—Sigismondo, born in “Freibor”—and a certain Dr “Feder” (presumably Dr Federn) is also mentioned. The IPA is described as a coven of Jewish socialists, and regarding Servadio we read, among other things: “… The mother [of Servadio], and with this, we in no way wish to taint her honour, seems to be a Jewess”. These documents date back to the end of 1934 and the beginning of 1935. From that moment on, fascism was to manifest a more explicit form of hostility towards the Jewish psychoanalysts. As Michel David notes in La psicoanalisi nella cultura italiana [Psychoanalysis in Italian culture] (David, 1966), the years between 1930 and 1934 had been a particularly positive moment towards Freudianism, and Mussolini himself had not disdained having contact with Freud. The following anecdote is reported in Vita ed opera di Freud [Life and works of Freud] by E. Jones (Milan, 1962, pp. 213–255), and in S. Freud “Letters 1873–1938” (1960, pp. 351, 387–388). In 1933, Dr Weiss, a psychoanalyst in Rome, had sent Freud a patient accompanied by his father, who was a close personal friend of Mussolini. At the end of treatment, the father asked Freud for one of his volumes with a dedication as a gift for the dictator. Freud chose Why War? and hand-wrote a flattering dedication: “From an old man who greets, in the [figure of] legislator, the hero of culture.” Freud was here alluding to Mussolini’s role in the recent archaeological excavations in Rome. In 1934 Weiss wrote to Freud that the Rivista di Psicoanalisi [Review of Italian Psychoanalysis] had been banned and that, despite having “privileged access to Mussolini” and having received a favourable reply from him, the ban could not be lifted. It seems that the order had come from the Vatican, following suggestions from Freud’s Viennese adversary, Padre Schmidt.1 Mussolini, having promised to have the ban lifted, later confessed that he lacked the necessary authority to do so. In 1938 Mussolini was to again demonstrate his tolerance, but also the limits of his “protection” of Freud. M. David (1966, p. 49) says that Weiss had interceded on the aging scientist’s behalf, via the Italian Ambassador in Vienna, to avoid his being exiled. However, Hitler was having none of it, and Freud was duly obliged to leave for England. The Freuds travelled through Germany by train, entering France at Strasbourg. From these anecdotes, it can perhaps be deduced that Mussolini nursed no grave preconceptions against Freud, or indeed against psychoanalysis itself. However, as David rightly concludes: “… as in many other fields, the often contradictory empiricism of the fascist leader revealed itself to be merely an improvisation and passing affectation”. Still following M. David,2 we can divide the entry of psychoanalysis into Italy into three periods: the first period: high-level advance from 1908 to 1915; the second period: from 1915–18 (the First World War) to 1940–45 (the Second World War); the third period: from the end of 1945 until the end of the 1960s, the period in which David’s investigation concludes. In the first period (1908–15), the figure of Sante de Sanctis is of note. A doctor and experimental psychologist, he was (in Rome) the dean of one of the first university faculties of psychology in Italy. De Sanctis was also one of the first promulgators of Freud’s thinking in Italy. In the second period (1915–45)—in part already described in relation to the relationship between fascism and psychoanalysis—we witness the birth of the Italian Psychoanalytical Society [Società Psicoanalitica Italiana (SPI)]. However, before speaking of this “double” birth, it is well worth remembering Vittorio Benussi. Born in Trieste in 1878 and analysed by Otto Gross in Austria, Benussi was the analyst of Cesare Musatti. He was an experimental psychologist who investigated the problems of hypnotic suggestion. A charismatic and enigmatic figure, his lessons at the University of Padova were passionately followed by colleagues and students alike (C. Musatti among them). He had just begun writing a comprehensive work on psychoanalysis when, for reasons unknown, he committed suicide on 24 November 1927 in his own institute at the University of Padova. However, let us now return to the “double” birth of the SPI. The first Society came into being thanks to the work of Marco Levi Bianchini [1875–1961], a doctor who first directed the Psychiatric Hospital of Teramo and later that of Nocera Inferiore (Naples). An enthusiastic and passionate extrovert, he was a fervent propagandist of Freud’s doctrine, despite knowing little about it and never fully assimilating it. In 1925 Levi Bianchini, personally encouraged by Freud, founded the Italian Psychoanalytical Society in Teramo; among its founder members, this included E. Weiss. Thus the Italian Jew, Levi Bianchini, discovered in the Viennese Jew, Freud, a hero of his faith: the Jewish faith and the scientific faith. However, for the sake of correctness, it has to be said that Levi Bianchini, for all his extroversion, never forgot that he was a dilettante in psychoanalysis; in fact, he defined himself “a student and underage … For systematic research I never feel sufficiently prepared”—and therein lie the limits of his Freudianism. The Trieste-born Edoardo Weiss was the first real Italian psychoanalyst. He began his career in Trieste before moving to Rome. “Perhaps you know that Trieste is the only city in Italy which can boast a doctor practicing psychoanalytical cures; one of Freud’s best students, Dr Weiss is a wonderful person …”—thus writes the poet Umberto Saba, one of the first patients of Weiss; he was soon followed by Italo Svevo, the author of Zeno’s Conscience.3 Trieste was truly the cradle of Italian psychoanalysis. It is impossible, even synthetically, to describe here that unrepeatable moment of Italian culture that was Trieste at the beginning of the twentieth century. A frontier city, the bridge between Austria and the Mediterranean, a “melting-pot” of diverse races (Italian, Austrian, and Slavic), Trieste was criss-crossed by intense and profound cultural changes. It was in Trieste that, following his own analysis by Freud’s student Federn, E. Weiss began working as a psychoanalyst. In 1931 he moved from Trieste to Rome, was given authorization by the IPA to teach psychoanalysis in Italy, and analysed his first students. In 1932 Weiss found himself faced with the need to “re-found” the Society with members who had all been analysed. Among the founders were Perrotti and Servadio (analysed by Weiss), Musatti (analysed by Benussi), and Levi Bianchini, who was nominated honorary president for life. In 1932, Weiss founded the Rivista di Psicoanalisi [Review of Italian Psychoanalysis], the official organ of the SPI. However, as we have seen, just two years later the fascist regime—perhaps pushed by the Vatican—was to revoke its publication permit. The year 1938 was defined by Servadio as a year of international crisis in the psychoanalytic movement—a crisis determined by the Nazi persecutions. Freud was obliged to emigrate to London; in Italy, Weiss emigrated to America—never to return—and Servadio emigrated to India. Musatti and Perrotti were obliged to cease all psychoanalytic activity. From 1938 to 1945 there is no further mention of psychoanalysis in Italy. Before returning to our journey from 1945 onwards, I would like to paint a brief portrait of the “pioneers”. Nicola Perrotti [1897–1970] was a classical psychoanalyst, not only gifted with a special intuition to understand the world around the suffering individual, but also untiring in his investigations into the meanings and transformations of the surrounding external world, beginning with social and political phenomena. In 1921, having attained his doctorate in medicine, Perrotti actively practised his profession, initially working as a doctor and then as a psychoanalyst—politically active all the while in the ranks of the Socialist Party, both during the fascist period and also during the war years, in the Resistance. From 1946 to 1951 he was president of the SPI; in 1948 he was elected Member of Parliament, and in 1950 he was appointed high-commissioner for health and hygiene. In 1952 he founded the Psychoanalytical Institute of Rome and the cultural review, Psiche: a periodical of psychoanalytic “inspiration”, which since its inception has dedicated particular attention to social, cultural, and artistic events. Among Psiche’s prestigious former directors is Roberto Tagliacozzo; the review is currently directed by Lorena Preta. Fundamental among Perrotti’s clinical contributions remain his works on aggressiveness; psychoanalysis of the self; collective psychology; depersonalization and various psycho-somatic questions, all collected in the posthumous volume, L’io legato e la libertà [The tied self and liberty] (Perrotti, 1989), together with the complete bibliography of his writings. In the many themes he dealt with, we can find many harbingers of those that would go on to become key problems of psychoanalysis today: the death instinct and the anxieties of modern man, his psychic and social alienation, and the threats deriving from the fascination of phenomena of collective and mass psychology. Cesare Musatti [1908–89], who had studied philosophy, immediately turned towards psychology and was initiated into psychoanalysis by his then professor, the Trieste-born psychologist Vittorio Benussi. For almost fifty years, Musatti was a professor of psychology—first at the University of Padova and then at the University of Milan. From 1948 on, he set about forming a group of students who were to go on to become the third generation of psychoanalysts in Milan and providing, moreover, a strong guiding force for the local Institute, which became independent in 1961. Musatti was president of the SPI from 1951 to 1955 and again from 1959 to 1963. In 1949 he published the first Italian “Treatise of Psychoanalysis”; however, his name will remain indelibly linked to the Italian edition of the complete works of Freud, which was completed over a 13-year period (1967–0) and was published by Boringhieri. This work was an outstanding success and was judged the “The most up-dated in the world from a philological point of view” (David, 1966). Musatti was a prolific author and dedicated himself to the application of psychoanalysis in the most varied fields of culture, from criticism to journalism, from narrative work to biographies, from theatre to methods of mass-communication, remaining all the while politically active in the Socialist Party. Emilio Servadio [1904–98] created the Psychoanalytic Centre of Rome and its Institute. He was president of the SPI from 1963 to 1969, and it was under his presidency that, for the first time ever in Italy, the 26th IPA Congress was held. Of particular note within his extremely prolific scientific production is his ground-breaking relation on “The Role of Pre-Oedipal Conflicts” presented in 1953 at the 26th Congress of Psychoanalysis in Romance Languages. The bibliography of his principal writings was published in the Review of Psychoanalysis in 1974, on the occasion of his 70th birthday (20, p. 68), and his work continued to expand after this date. Servadio is known throughout the world for his psychoanalytic research on so-called “parapsychological” phenomena (telepathy, extra-sensory perception etc.); however, exactly as happened to Musatti and Perrotti, his unlimited culture, coupled with his desire to extend psychoanalysis and its applications to the external world, led him to write on innumerable subjects: his most constant interests were sexology, the magic of thought, the effects of hallucinogenic drugs, and literary theory. In 1992, after a crisis that cut a swathe through the Italian Psychoanalytical Society, Servadio left the SPI and went on to form, together with other colleagues, the Italian Psychoanalytical Association [Associazione Italiana di Psicoanalisi (AIPsi)]. Another figure of note in the panorama of early Italian psychoanalysts was Alessandra Tomasi di Palma [1894–1972]. One of the most fascinating female figures of the twentieth century and founder of the Sicilian school of psychoanalysis, Alessandra Tomasi, Baroness Wolff of Stomersee, Duchess of Palma, Princess of Lampedusa, was born in Nice 1894 to an ancient bourgeois family ennobled by Peter the Great. “Of Latvian and German family origin, French by birth, Russian by Imperial Decree, of English and Italian culture and Sicilian by adoption, Alessandra Tomasi was an extremely cultured polyglot. She lived out her life over two centuries; crossing over, and making her own, the perilous and captivating existence of multiple sagas”—thus writes the Sicilian psychoanalyst, Malde Vigneri, in a fascinating essay that she published in the Rivista di Psicoanalisi (2008). Tomasi di Palma studied at the Psychoanalytical Institute of Berlin directed by Karl Abraham. She did her personal analysis with F. Boehm and her training analysis with Max Eitingon. In London, in 1925, she met Giuseppe Tomasi di Lampedusa, the future author of The Leopard, whom she married at the beginning of the 1930s when she moved to Palermo. In the years to come, she was to pass long periods in Rome and join the group of pioneers (Musatti, Servadio, and Perrotti). In the 1940s and 1950s she participated actively in the life of the SPI and was the first and only woman to hold the Society’s presidency, which she did from 1955 to 1959. Among her works are “Depersonalisation” and the intriguing “Case of the Werewolf”. Before her death she wrote “Pact with the Devil”, a work which she herself defined as the “compendium of a life dedicated to psychoanalysis”. Following the death of her husband, she contributed actively and passionately to publishing her husband’s book, The Leopard, universally regarded as one of the masterpieces of twentieth-century literature. * * * Returning once more to our interrupted journey, in 1945 Servadio returned to Italy and re-established contact with Musatti and Perrotti. In 1946 the first national congress was held in Rome under the presidency of Perrotti (from 1946 to 1951), and the SPI was officially recognized by the IPA. The second congress was held in 1950. However, this was followed by a long period with no national congress (the third only being held in Venice in 1976) due to the growing tensions regarding the organization of training among the eight analysts scattered between Rome and Milan. This state of affairs continued until 1961, when the Executive Committee of the IPA, under the presidency of W. Gillespie, convened a commission composed of three Swiss analysts (de Saussure, Morgenthaler, and Parin) to definitively resolve the question. The commission proposed a series of standards designed to bring about the new structure of training that is still used to this day. Three training institutes, in particular, were recognized: the Institute of Psychoanalysis of Rome founded by Perrotti, the Roman Institute of Psychoanalysis founded by Servadio, and the Milan Institute of Psychoanalysis founded by Musatti. To these three institutes, all referring to the National Institute of Training, has seen the addition in recent years of the Veneto-Emiliano Institute. Between 1946 and 1969, the presidency was held by: Perrotti, Musatti, Tomasi di Lampedusa, Musatti, Servadio. The 1960s witnessed a great expansion in psychoanalysis and an increase in the number of analysts; thus psychoanalysis became an integral part of the Italian cultural panorama. With the presidency of Servadio (from 1963 to 1969) the epoch of the pioneers was concluded; the investigations of M. David likewise also reached their end.4 Servadio’s presidency was concluded with the nomination of new training analysts and the handover of the baton from the pioneers to the succeeding generation. Corrao, Fornari, and Gaddini were key figures in the transition, and they inaugurated a new generational race, beginning with Corrao, who was president from 1969 to 1974. Under his presidency, an important statutory reform was enacted: the institution of the National Training Committee and the institution of psychoanalytic centres, where members meet to hold scientific evenings and study and research groups. This organizational structure has remained unvaried and there are currently 10 psychoanalytic centres active in Italy: Rome I, Rome II, Milan, Naples, Bologna, Florence, Genoa, Palermo, Turin, and Venice. Francesco Corrao, a doctor, lived in Palermo, the city of his birth, but for his entire professional life he travelled between Palermo and Rome, where he was one of the leaders of the Roman Institute of Psychoanalysis. Analysed by Tomasi di Palma, he became a training analyst in 1962; his activity for the promulgation of psychoanalysis in Sicily lasted for over 30 years, as he himself noted in the catalogue of the exhibition “Italy in Psychoanalysis”. The centre that he founded in Palermo has organized numerous conventions, some of them open to contributors from other disciplines. A refined and knowledgeable expert on the thinking of Wilfred Bion, Corrao introduced the thinking of the English analyst into Italy. Lastly, he introduced group analysis into Italy. Franco Fornari [1925–85], a doctor and a student of Musatti, taught at the University of Trento as a professor of sociology; he went on to succeed Musatti as Dean of Psychology at the University of Milan. He was president of the SPI from 1974 to 1978. His complete bibliography was published in the Review of Psychoanalysis (1986, vol. 32: 59). During one of the first phases of his development, which we can define as social/analytic, he began with Kleinian hypotheses and applied them to war and other social phenomena. With his treatises on the subject, Psicoanalisi della guerra atomica [Psychoanalysis of the atomic war] (1964) and Psicoanalisi della guerra [Psychoanalysis of war] (1966), he became known to the public at large. Eventually, his fascination with semiotics, linguistics, and epistemology led him to study the relationship between individuals and institutions and the unconscious codes that regulate and express emotions. It was thus that he came to formulate the so-called “theory of the Koine”: a psychoanalytic theory of language based upon the “koin”—the minimum unit of emotional meaning and communication present in every communicative exchange. The “koin” is used to explain the passage of the drive in object relations, in that it gives each drive the possibility to transmit to the object its emotional meaning. The developments of this theory are contained in Fornari’s last works: Simbolo e codice [Symbol and code] (1976), Fondamenti di una teoria psicoanalitica del linguaggio [Fundamentals of a psychoanalytic theory of language], Cinema e icona [Cinema and icon] (1979), Il codice vivente [The living code] (1981), La riscoperta dell’anima [The rediscovery of the soul] (1984), and Affetti e cancro [Affections and cancer] (1985). Eugenio Gaddini [1916–85], a doctor of medicine and a student of Servadio, was the first psychoanalyst in Italy who actively worked upon bringing every aspect of Italian Psychoanalytic institutions (regulations and organs of the Society, congresses, reviews, didactic activity) in line with IPA standards. His biography appeared in the Review of Psychoanalysis (1986, Vol. 32, p. 187); his collected writings were published posthumously, in 1989. Beginning with the theoretical positions of Winnicott, for over 20 years Gaddini carried out research on preconscious mental processes, arriving at the formulation of a theory of imitative processes that was to find recognition throughout the world of psychoanalysis. He described a “basic mental organization”, which precedes the mental structure described by Freud. He studied the formation of the paternal image in the mind of the child and carried forward research on aggressiveness, on the relationship between the body and the mind, on the self of the child, and on creativity—above all, artistic creativity. The Chilean professor of psychiatry Ignacio Matte Blanco studied as a psychoanalyst in London before moving to Rome in 1966 and becoming an eminent figure at the Psychoanalytic Centre of Rome. His research, begun at the end of the 1950s, on the logic of the unconscious, led him to publish in 1975, first in English and then in Italian, his world-famous book, The Unconscious as Infinite Sets. Finally, a word about the presidencies of G. Carloni (from 1982 to 1986) and G. Hautmann (from 1986 to 1990), under whose guidance Italian psychoanalysis was to acquire ever more the physiognomy and identity that was to become increasingly recognized in the European and international fields. Carloni was one of the founders of the Bologna Psychoanalytic Centre, and he contributed towards the introduction of Ferenczi’s thinking in Italy. The period between the Italian Congress of 1980 (“The analytic relation”) and that of 1994 (“The contribution of the analyst in the constitution and maintenance of the analytic field”) is an incredibly rich one. The concepts of “relation” and “analytic field”, which appeared in the titles of the two congresses, contributed to delineate the landscape of Italian psychoanalysis of those years. Let us begin with the concept of “Relation”. Towards the end of the 1980s, long period of crises hit the SPI: within these crises were intertwined moral questions, the personal vicissitudes of some analysts, and political vicissitudes. It was during this period that voices began to circulate about the morally grave behaviour of a noted, elderly training analyst of the SPI. The question could not be resolved within the SPI itself; therefore, in 1992, Joseph Sandler, president of the IPA, sent a committee led by Serge Lebovici to visit Italy. After a long inquiry, the training analyst accused of ethically incorrect behaviour was expelled by the SPI. Servadio, Giannotti, Amati, Argentieri, Canestri, and others split away from the SPI and, as mentioned earlier, went on to form another Society, AIPsi, which was officially recognized by the IPA. These were confused and dramatic years: a painful and bloody generational change, which was followed by a wide-scale movement of renewal, led by Giuseppe Di Chiara, who was president from 1993 to 1997. Statutory reform was effected and was followed and approved by the entire membership of the Society. Most importantly, a modification was effected to the criteria in the attribution of the functions of training, which had been one of the fulcrums of the crisis. From those crucial years emerged a more open, democratic society. The power of the training analysts was reduced. On a cultural level, there was a development and widening of horizons and an opening of exchanges with European and world-wide psychoanalysis through periodical conventions and other activities. The work of Di Chiara was continued by the other presidents succeeding him: Fausto Petrella (from 1997 to 2001), Domenico Chianese (from 2001 to 2005), and Fernando Riolo, the current president of the SPI. The SPI membership—in numeric terms—is, after that of Germany, the second largest in Europe. Currently the SPI has 767 members, of whom 225 are full members; 110 are full members with training functions; and there are 270 candidates. The SPI has two official organs: the Review of Psychoanalysis and Psiche (the latter journal is open to other disciplines). Psychoanalysis in Italy is a Society in expansion, which looks to the future with confidence. Notes 1. In a letter of 30 September 1934, Freud wrote thus to A. Zweig: “… here we live in an atmosphere of rigid Catholic faith. It is said that politics … are in the hands of a certain Padre Schmidt … who is a confidant to the Pope and unfortunately, also an ethnologist and religious scholar, who in his books does little to hide his horror for analysis and my theory on the totem in particular….” 2. Michael David, the author of Psychoanalysis in Italian Culture (1966), is not a psychoanalyst. Born in Savoia at Saint Michel de Maurienne in 1924, he studied at the Sorbonne and specialized in the study of Italian literature and culture. The SPI awarded M. David the “Premio Musatti” [Musatti Prize], a prize that is presented to non-analyst exponents of culture who have contributed to the diffusion of psychoanalysis in Italy. 3. Since its beginnings, the relationship between Italian psychoanalysis and Italian literature has been intense and productive: after Saba and Svevo, Gadda, Berto, Voghera, Camon through to Bassani, Moravia, Citati, Eco, Manganelli, and Ottieri. Regarding literary criticism, to name but two, Francesco Orlando and Mario Lavagetto (both Musatti Prize winners) come to mind. 4. For the subsequent years I referred to the following sources: Novel-letto (1992), Accerboni (1990), Di Chiara & Pirillo (1997), and Chianese (1997). Bibliography Accerboni, A. M. (1990). Edoardo Weiss e le origini della psicoanalisi in Italia [Edoardo Weiss and the origins of psychoanalysis in Italy]. Turin: Boringhieri. Bellanova, P., & Bellanova, A. (1982). Le due Gradive. Notizie sull’attività della SPI (1932–1983) [The two Gradive: News on the activities of the SPI 1932–1983]. Rome: CEPI. Chianese, D. (1997) Paesaggio Italiano [Italian landscape]. In: Costruzioni e campo analitico [Constructions and the analytic field: History, scenes and destiny]. Rome: Borla. David, M. (1966). La psicoanalisi nella cultura italiana [Psychoanalysis in Italian culture]. Turin: Boringhieri. Di Chiara, G., & Pirillo, N. (1997). Conversazioni sulla psicoanalisi [Conversations on psychoanalysis]. Naples: Liguori. Gramsci, A. (1965). Lettere dal carcere [Letters from prison]. Turin: Boringhieri. Freud, S. (1960). “Letters 1873–1938” [Italian edition]. Turin: Boringhieri-Einaudi. Matte Blanco, I. (1975). The Unconscious as Infinite Sets. London: Duck-worth. Musatti, C. (1949). Trattato di psicoanalisi. Turin: Boringhieri-Einaudi. Novelletto, A. (1992). Italy. In: P. Kutter (Ed.), Psychoanalysis International: A Guide to Psychoanalysis Throughout the World, Vol. 1: Europe. Stuttgart-Bad Cannstadt: Frommann-Holzboog. Perrotti, N. (1989). L’io legato e la libertà. Rome: Astrolabio. Petacchi, G. (1987). Vita da Pionieri [Lives as pioneers]. In: La cultura psicoanalitica [Psychoanalytical culture]. Trieste: Studio tesi. Vigneri, M. (2008). Tomasi di Palma. Rivista di Psicoanalisi, 2 (February).







14Spain A brief history of the Madrid Psychoanalytical Association

Luisa Muñoz de la Cruz

In February and March of 1893, a month after the publication by Freud and Breuer of “On the Psychical Mechanisms of Hysterical Phenomena” in the Neurologisches Zentralblatt of Vienna, this paper was published in two Spanish scientific journals: the Journal of Medical Sciences of Barcelona (Vol. 14, No, 3) and the Medical Newspaper of Granada (Vol. 11, pp. 105–111, 129–135). In the words of Strachey, “these were the first translations of a psychological paper of Freud in the world” (Standard Edition, 2, p. 9). In 1911, Ortega y Gasset, an intellectual with great prestige, educated at the universities of Leipzig, Berlin, and Freiburg, published a long essay, “Psychoanalysis: A Problematic Science”, recognizing the importance of many of Freudian contributions, to the point of recommending the publication of the texts of Freud in Spanish. In 1917, the editor Ruiz Castillo, following the recommendations of Ortega y Gasset, acquired the rights for the Spanish publication of all the texts written or to be written in the future by Freud, commissioning López Ballesteros for the translation. Again, a Spaniard was to be the first editor of the complete texts of Freud. The compilation of 17 volumes made between 1922 and 1932 was almost simultaneous with the first compilation of the original writings, the Gesammelte Schriften (Vienna: International Psychoanalytischer Verlag, 12 vols. 1924–1934). In May of 1923, Freud wrote a letter to Luis López Ballesteros, admiring the correct interpretation of his thinking as well his mastery of a sometimes obscure and intricate discipline. In 1924, in a note in “On the History of the Psychoanalytic Movement”, Freud expresses the satisfaction that this translation will generate in Spanish-speaking countries. The most eminent psychiatrists of that time published many articles analysing psychoanalytic contributions. These contributions had an important influence on jurists, educators, and scholars. Writers and artists also felt attracted by the Freudian discussions: Dali, Machado, Sanchez Mejías, Buñuel, and Azorín, among others, expressed in their writings the influence of psychoanalytic ideas. A group of writers and artists, young men belonging to the students’ residency, invited Freud to come to Spain to give a series of conferences, but Freud’s illness did not allow him to come. However, in October of 1928, Sándor Ferenczi came to Spain and gave a conference entitled “The Learning of Psychoanalysis and the Psychoanalytic Formation of the Character”. Dali and Picasso, together with a group of writers and artists lead by Thomas Mann, organized a homage to Freud on the latter’s eightieth birthday; later, Dali visited Freud in his exile in London and sketched his portrait. The interest around psychoanalysis within Spanish psychiatry motivated two Spanish psychiatrists, R. Sarró and A. Garma, to acquire a psychoanalytic education. Ángel Garma studied at the Psychoanalytic Institute of Berlin. He returned to Madrid in November of 1931 and left definitively in 1936. During these years he made an intense effort to acquaint people with psychoanalysis, due to his interest in creating a psychoanalytic movement in Spain. The Spanish Civil War, which started in July 1936, put an end to this project. Garma went to Argentina, where he participated in the creation of the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association. Another Spanish exile, Prado Such, was cofounder of the Psychoanalytic Society of Canada. By the end of the 1940s, two small groups of psychiatrists and intellectuals, one in Madrid and the other in Barcelona, started negotiations to acquire psychoanalytic training and to introduce psychoanalysis in Spain. The psychiatrist from Madrid, Ramón del Portillo, following the recommendations of Ángel Garma, went to the German Psychoanalytical Society (GPS). There he was analysed by M. Steimback. Portillo proposed to his analyst that he should come to Madrid as a didactic analyst. Steimback agreed and until 1954, when he died, participated in the formation of a group—Drs R. del Portillo, Teresa Ruiz, C. Zamora, J. Pertejo, and J. Corominas among them. During this same period, Drs Bofill, Folch, and Abelló, from Barcelona, went to Switzerland in order to be trained as psychoanalysts. There they contacted Drs Rallo from Madrid and F. Alvim from Lisbon. In 1957, at the 20th Congress of the IPA, the Portuguese–Spanish Society of Psychoanalysis—formed by Drs Bofill, Folch, Alvim, Corominas, and sponsored by the societies of Paris and Switzerland—was recognized. In 1958, Drs Portillo and Teresa Ruiz incorporated to the group. The Portuguese–Spanish Society was recognized as a Component Society of the IPA at the 21st IPA Congress in 1959. In 1966, after the separation of the Portuguese, the Portuguese–Spanish Society of Psychoanalysis was reconstituted as the Spanish Society of Psychoanalysis. In 1973, in the course of the 28th IPA Congress held in Paris, the psychoanalysts living in Madrid—Drs Zamora, Ruiz, Pertejo, Rallo, J. Tomas, P. Tomas, González Morado, and Rodríguez—applied for recognition as a Study Group, with the sponsorship of Drs M. Roch (Chairman), R. Henry (Secretary), R. Diatkine, P. Marty, and G. David. In 1975, Dr Carlos Sopena from Uruguay joined the group, followed, in 1976, by Drs León and Rebeca Grinberg from Buenos Aires. The Madrid Psychoanalytical Association (Asociación Psicoanalítica de Madrid—APM) was recognized as a Component Society at the 31st IPA Congress (New York) in 1979. In Helsinki, in 1981, at the 32nd Congress, Madrid was elected to host the 33rd IPA Congress. The first scientific objective of the APM has been the training of new psychoanalysts, following the tripartite model of personal analysis, supervision, and theoretic–clinical training, with a special emphasis on the study of the work of Freud. The intense work of the different commissions has resulted in an increase in the number of members and candidates. At present, the APM has 49 analysts with didactic function, 64 full members, 58 associate members, and 206 candidates. In its 30 years of existence, the APM has also incorporated an important number of members from other societies, from the American Psychoanalytic Association (APsaA), APdeBA, Uruguay, German, as well as some Spanish psychoanalysts who were trained in other societies—APsaA, MAPP, SPS. The Madrid Psychoanalytical Association has since its beginning been a receptive Society, where its members, trained in different societies, have distinct referential schemes. The institute for the training of candidates had to establish and maintain a cohesion between the different schemes. The arrival of new professors turned the training into an adventure, intellectually speaking, which promoted liberty in thinking—something that rarely happens in more traditional societies and institutes, where a single theoretical approach predominates and the task of being receptive to other, different points of view is always difficult to process and requires a great deal of time. Within the process of training of new analysts, in 1994 the department for children and adolescents was created, and, at the same time, an intense scientific programme was begun, including symposia, workshops, conferences, and colloquies in which analysts both from the APM and from other societies participated. Apart from the scientific activities in which psychoanalysts of our association as well as many psychoanalysts from other societies participate, every year symposia are organized to study and discuss specific themes. We have study groups on the psychoanalysis of the psychosis and on the psychoanalysis of groups and institutions, for psychoanalysts especially interested in investigating and deepening their knowledge of the development of psychoanalysis in these areas. The number of psychoanalysts as well as the evolution of psychoanalysis in Valencia, as well as in the north of the country, favoured the creation of two new centres within the APM: the Psychoanalytic Centre of Valencia and the Psychoanalytical Centre of the North. Both centres, established in 1997, are involved in intense scientific activity, institutional as well as in the diffusion of psychoanalysis. The centre of Valencia has an institute of psychoanalysis. Through the years the APM has maintained a presence in the clinical institutions. The pioneer work here was the one established by Dr Rallo at the department of psychiatry of the “Clinica de la Concepcion” in Madrid. Drs Cruz Roche and Luis Fernando Crespo have carried out heavy clinical activity at the Leganes Hospital. Nowadays, many psychoanalysts work and consult in clinical and psychiatric centres for adults, children, and adolescents. Concerning the presence of psychoanalysis in the university, we can say that although the Spanish university has been traditionally critical of psychoanalysts, the presence of psychoanalysts as professors of psychiatry, psychology, and philosophy has permitted the diffusion of psychoanalysis as well as of psychoanalytic theories concerning psychic development and mental dysfunction. Apart from the presence of psychoanalysts as professors at the universities of Madrid, Salamanca, Bilbao, San Sebastian, and Valencia, the three universities of Madrid jointly organize a doctoral programme on psychoanalytic theory. A master’s degree course in psychoanalytic theory is also currently (2010) being taught at the Complutense University. The APM created in 1994 a “Centre of Clinical Assistance and Investigation” (CACI) with three main objectives: to fill the need for analysis of patients with low economic resources, who are treated by candidates; to organize teaching courses and clinical supervisions in public psychiatry centres that have clinical assistance; and to give psychoanalytic training and orientation to the professionals who work in different areas of health care and public education. The APM participates actively in different psychoanalytic forums within the peninsular area, in Europe and around the world. Con-jointly with the Portuguese Society and the Spanish Society, it organizes “the Iberian Congress” with the participation of three societies of the peninsula. These three societies together also publish The Iberian Journal of Psychoanalysis. The Madrid Psychoanalytical Association is a Component Society of the Congress of French-Language Psychoanalysts, organized by the Paris Psychoanalytical Society, and, as members of the group of psychoanalysts of Romance languages, they participate actively in the biannual congress. Three of these—in 1974, 1990, and 1996—have been held in Madrid. As a member of the European Psychoanalytical Federation (EPF) and a participant in the different activities of this organization (congresses, symposia, conferences on education of psychoanalysis), the APM held the EPF Congress in Madrid in 2001. In 1998, the second Ferenczi Congress was also held in Madrid. The APM, in addition to being regularly present at the various international congresses, with scientific presentations by its members, participated intensively in the organization of the two IPA Congresses that took place in Madrid in 1983 and Barcelona in 1997. The APM carries out an intense programme of discussions. In 1987 the professoriate of psychoanalysis was created at the athenaeum of Madrid, directed by Dr L. Grinberg. Nowadays, it also organizes annual conferences of psychoanalytic thought at the Ateneo. These two institutions are important cultural centres that have high prestige among intellectuals and professionals. Also, periodically the association organizes courses in psychoanalytic orientation for health and education professionals. These courses are given at the headquarters of the APM. It is important to mention the diffusion of psychoanalysis through publications. One of these is the psychoanalysis journal of the APM, Biblioteca Nueva. The publisher of the complete texts of Freud maintains a section dedicated to psychoanalysis directed by the APM, where texts of psychoanalytic thinking written by psychoanalysts of our Society and of other societies are published. The Association has a contract with the Dossat medical and scientific publishing house for the publishing of books on psychoanalysis. Also, the APM has an agreement with the Complutense University for the publication in electronic form of those books about psychoanalysis presented by the APM. The psychoanalytic centre of Valencia has an “editorial Promolibro” dedicated to the diffusion of psychoanalytic thought. We think that the activity of the APM through its 30 years of existence—the work of the psychoanalysts, the presence of psychoanalysis in the scientific, academic, and professional circles—has promoted psychoanalysis as a therapy and as a means of helping a growing number of adults, children, and adolescents and has also fostered the presence of psychoanalysis in scientific and professional areas. Note Translated by Juan Hernández Rodríguez. Bibliography Jones, E. (1957). The Life and Work of Sigmund Freud, Vol. III, 1919–1939. New York: Basic Books.







45The IPA administration from 1985 to 1989: organizational unity maintained

Robert S. Wallerstein

Though a number of developments that were very significant for the unfolding history of the International Psychoanalytical Association took place during the time of my presidency, 1985 to 1989, they were all overshadowed by the major threat to its organizational integrity—the lawsuit that threatened to split it asunder—which I will describe first. The suit was over the place of non-physicians as psychoanalytic clinicians—the issue of so-called “lay analysis” that had bedevilled the organization and its practitioners over almost the entire twentieth century until the agreed final settlement in October 1988. I have recounted this story in detail over its entire duration, which I date from 1910 to 1988, in my book Lay Analysis: Life Inside the Controversy (Wallerstein, 1998). Two events in 1910, on opposite sides of the ocean, foretold the controversy. In Europe, Freud published his paper on “Wild” analysis (Freud, 1010k), in which he distinguished between proper and “wild” analysis and outlined what he felt were appropriate qualifications for a psychoanalytic career, indicating no distinction on the basis of antecedent experience or academic degree.1 In that same year in America, the famed Flexner Report was published, detailing the sorry state of American medical training at that time, beset by diploma-mill medical degree factories and by widespread quackery among the self-styled and mostly inadequately trained practitioners. From these events, the Europeans, (mostly) following Freud, kept the analytic doors open to physicians and non-physicians alike, as long as the aspirant had the necessary personal qualities and motivation. The Americans, on the other hand, dedicated to upgrading their medical schools and eliminating the quacks and the ill-trained, declared the medical degree from the newly upgraded medical schools to be a necessary prerequisite for psychoanalytic training and practice. This difference between the Europeans (the IPA majority) and the Americans (a growing minority) over the issue of lay analysis intensified over the three decades prior to the Second World War. after Hitler’s rise power in Germany in 1933, European analysts—many of them non-medical—began to emigrate in large numbers to America and then tried to claim their former status within the then newly emerging American institutes and societies. These differences came to the breaking point at the Paris IPA Congress in 1938, where the Americans demanded to be relieved of IPA oversight—via its International Training Committee, established by Eitingon in Berlin in 1925—and to have complete control over issues of training and certification in the United States, with the threat of otherwise seceding and fracturing the IPA—on the very threshold of the forthcoming World War. The IPA’s decision in the face of this threat was deferred until the next congress, which, however, because of the war (1939–45), was not held until 11 years later, in 1949, in Zurich. In the meantime, with Hitler’s purge of psychoanalysis in its original European heart-land and the flight of most of the refugees to America, the American Psychoanalytic Association (APsaA) had become the dominant IPA majority, and it had de facto enacted the demands it had made just prior to the war. The IPA was therefore reunited at the Zurich Congress in 1949 with many new organizational arrangements, including, centrally, beginning the process of certifying the established American position de jure with the establishment of APsaA as a special Regional Association, with two special features: complete control over training in the United States and a geographic monopoly, covering all IPA training within the United States. But there were always minorities within the APsaA, and, to a far lesser extent, within the European societies, that did not agree with the established position of their region and that over the post-war years—from the early 1950s on—sought to bridge these training and certification differences and to re-establish world-wide agreement on these central issues. Within the APsaA this minority consisted of medical refugee analysts (and likeminded American members) who, on principle, disagreed with the American position—and often participated surreptitiously in training in non-IPA institutes created outside the APsaA, centrally in New York, but also in Los Angeles and other cities—together with research-interested analysts eager for the research contributions to analysis that could be made by colleagues from academic disciplines—mostly, but far from exclusively, clinical psychology. Over the years between the early 1950s and the early 1980s, the APsaA, under these pressures, accorded membership to a cadre of prestigious non-medical European refugee analysts who were granted training analyst status within American member institutes, established a small carefully monitored psychoanalytic training programme for eminent psychoanalytically oriented researchers who would turn their research activities into directly psychoanalytic research, and established four sequential committees (from 1975 to 1987), to report, first, on the appropriate prerequisites for psychoanalytic training, then on the feasibility of training non-medical candidates, then on its desirability, and, finally, with the charge to create a workable plan for non-medical training. At its May 1984 meeting in San Diego, however, the APsaA could not agree on which of the possible plans submitted for its adoption to endorse and unhappily sent the entire controversy back to the drawing board. The organized psychology world in the United States, spearheaded by the Division on Psychoanalysis (39) of the American Psychological Association, which had been hopefully but impatiently watching this gradually increasing pressure within the APsaA towards an accommodation on this issue, found this setback impossible to accept and immediately made plans to enter a lawsuit against the APsaA, for which contingency they had already amassed a war chest of over a million dollars. Despite an awareness within the APsaA that the lawsuit—and possibly more than one—was coming and the December 1984 appointment of a new APsaA committee (the Gaskill Committee) charged with bringing forward a single plan for non-medical training for the APsaA’s consideration, the lawsuit was officially filed on 1 March 1985 by four psychologist plaintiffs on behalf of a declared class of several thousand psychologists. The suit was primarily against the APsaA on Sherman Antitrust grounds, alleging that the APsaA had illegally conspired to monopolize the training of psychoanalysts and had thus deprived qualified psychologists of proper access to this way of earning a livelihood. The IPA was secondarily sued for allowing its American component to engage in these improper and illegal practices; two of the APsaA’s institutes in New York (where the lawsuit was filed) were also named as defendants. At the time it was clear that I would be standing for the IPA presidency at the coming IPA congress, just five months later, in Hamburg, and no one else had been nominated to that point to run against me; and it was also clear that this complicated lawsuit (against the IPA, as well as the APsaA) would take place over most, if not all, of the coming presidency period (1985–89). Actually, the lawsuit was not finally settled until October 1988, three and a half years after it had started, and with ongoing continuing related business through the balance of my administration. When the lawsuit was filed, some concerns were raised within the IPA that I, the prospective president, as an active member of the APsaA, of which I had at one time been President (1971–72), might have a serious conflict of interest, since it was clear that the stance of the APsaA (then representing about 35% of the IPA membership), to that point committed to fighting a defence against the lawsuit, could be radically different from that of the IPA, which had 65% of its membership essentially in agreement with the plaintiffs and largely in favour of settling the lawsuit on terms acceptable to the plaintiffs. There were some calls that I should depart from IPA custom and at least select the IPA secretary from a region different from my own. I chose not to do this but selected a colleague—Edward Weinshel—on the basis of geographic proximity and an established positive working relationship, which had until then always been the IPA norm. I did make it clear that I would represent only the IPA during the lawsuit proceedings and that I was resigning all my committee memberships and other official positions within the APsaA for the duration of the coming IPA administration. Over the several years of negotiating sessions, depositions, and legal motions and manoeuvres, the positions of the contending parties can be summarized as follows: The plaintiffs, spurred on by their attorneys, who expected victory on antitrust (anti-monopoly) grounds, fought to prevail and to demolish the APsaA’s exclusionary practices. The APsaA, spurred on by their attorneys, who sought victory on the grounds that legal authorities should not—and could not—dictate proper practices for professional and scientific organizations, fought for a successful defence that would allow it to maintain its prevailing practices intact. The IPA, aided by very able legal counsel—principally Trip Dorkey from the New York office of the IPA’s legal firm in London—sought to play the mediating role of “honest broker” looking for a settlement that would afford non-medical applicants access to psychoanalytic training within the United States while allowing the APsaA to maintain its organizational integrity and commitments, for example by allowing other psychoanalytic groups, conforming to IPA standards, to grow within the United States outside the APsaA. Such a plan was proposed by the IPA within the first year of the three-and-a-half-year struggle, but it was rejected by both the plaintiffs and the APsaA, each intent on the sought-for total victory. This to-and-fro deadlock intensified as time went on and was not altered by the APsaA’s Gaskill Committee itself proposing a plan, finally accepted by the APsaA’s membership, according monitored access to non-medical applicants. This gridlock became even more difficult as the hearing judge had—for reasons extraneous to the lawsuit—to be shifted twice, with one of the three judges declaring that he did not expect a resolution to the complicated issues in this suit within his own prospective lifetime on the bench. Two major events finally transpired to bring matters to an agreed conclusion. In the autumn of 1986, ten senior—most of them internationally renowned—members of the British Society proposed a resolution, to be scheduled for vote at the coming 1987 IPA Congress in Montreal, which would declare that any Component Society, in legal difficulties in its own nation, would have to hold the IPA harmless and reimburse the IPA for any legal expenses it entailed in the matter. The APsaA, of course, strongly opposed this planned action; in voting against it and insisting that the IPA continue with it as a defendant and share the expenses of the defence, it might also choose to secede and thus split the IPA asunder. During the intense negotiations between the APsaA and the IPA that then took place, the IPA asked the APsaA to renounce at least one or the other of the two exceptional features of the APsaA’s special Regional Association status: it should give up either its barrier to the admission of non-medical applicants for clinical psychoanalytic training or its “exclusive franchise” in the United States, and allow groups that could be eligible for IPA membership to form outside the APsaA. These negotiations were made more productive and easier to conduct by the second concurrent event. The APsaA had had a change of administration, and the new president, Richard Simons, strongly dissatisfied with their fiercely “litigating attorneys” and their very high litigating expenses, had engineered a change, at first secretly, and then openly, to have a new Washington firm of “negotiating attorneys”, with Joel Klein as their lead man. Klein worked very comfortably with the IPA’s Dorkey (in contrast to the previously prevailing tensions between the APsaA’s previous attorneys and Dorkey), and the APsaA and the IPA quickly came to an agreement: that the APsaA would offer at the forthcoming Montreal Congress to renounce its exclusive franchise in the United States and allow other psychoanalytic groups to grow and seek IPA membership. This, together with the APsaA’s already having accepted the Gaskill Committee report providing monitored acceptance of non-medical applicants for clinical psychoanalytic training, would be the new shared position of the APsaA and the IPA in the continuing negotiations with the plaintiffs. When this was announced, the British Society members immediately withdrew their planned proposal for the Montreal Congress, and the potential split of the IPA was averted. It still took another two years of painstaking negotiations before both the plaintiffs and the APsaA gave up their dreams of total victory and fashioned an enduring settlement agreement—one that was essentially the same as the one that the IPA had proposed some three years earlier—and the new, now current, operating agreement came into being, with the IPA organizationally united, the APsaA still internally in full control of its training procedures (but now admitting non-medical and medical applicants on an equal basis), and with independent analytic societies, within the United States but outside the APsaA, admitted as IPA Component Societies. Four of these new societies actually secured Provisional Society status at the 1989 IPA Congress in Rome (still within the final days of my term of presidency). This long three-and-a-half-year struggle over the lawsuit, a struggle both to maintain the integrity and stability of the IPA and to advance it in a way beneficial to psychoanalysis, indeed preoccupied and overshadowed all else across my entire administration. Nonetheless, despite the endless hours and the recurrent frustrations involved in these years of negotiations with both adversaries, of artful compromises fashioned to mark a joint defence with the APsaA while maintaining the differing agenda of the IPA, and all the depositions and court appearances, the other important work of the IPA did go on without letup or delay. That work can be listed under three main headings:


the equalization of IPA regions; the fostering of psychoanalytic research; the official acknowledgement and acceptance of the growing theoretical (metapsychological) pluralism within analytic theoretical and clinical discourse.


On the subject of equalization: prior to the Second World War, the IPA was totally Europe-centred in its functioning, with all of its administration, the venues of its congresses, and the majority of its membership all in Europe. Its main operating language was then German. This shifted fundamentally when the IPA was reconstituted after the 11-year wartime hiatus between international congresses (1938 in Paris and 1949 in Zurich). Along with the acceptance of the now American IPA majority—created by the mass migration of European Hitler refugee analysts, 90% of them to the United States—and the position on the lay analysis issue, the organizational structure was also altered in the APsaA’s favour, with the presidency and the administration of the IPA now to alternate between Europe and North America, and after some years of delay, with the venues of the bi-annual congresses to alternate across the Atlantic ocean as well. The administrative language became English, also dominant now in the scientific sessions at the congresses. Gradually, the vice-presidencies (constituting, along with the president, the secretary, and the treasurer, the governing Executive Council), came to number eight: three each from Europe and North America, but only two from Latin America. In the summer of 1986, Weinshel and I made a month-long tour of Latin America, visiting each Society and absorbing centrally the vitality of this smallest (then 20%) but fastest growing of the three IPA regions, with Argentina as its originating Kleinian hub but now spread around the continent (plus Mexico) and propounding every major theoretical perspective within world-wide psychoanalysis. Out of this came a major administrative reorganization of the IPA, with now the inclusion of Latin America in the rotation of presidencies, with Horacio Etchegoyen of Buenos Aires to become the first Latin American president (1993–97), inclusion also in the rotation of congress venues (first in Buenos Aires, in 1991), and in having an equal number of vice-presidencies, three for each region. It was taken for granted that, as the originating institute and training centre in Latin America, the first Latin American presidency and congress venue—since then, of course, spread elsewhere across the continent—should be in Argentina. In terms of its relationship to psychoanalytic research, especially formal empirical research, the IPA stance had always been chequered. Although Freud was adamant in his conception of psychoanalysis as a biologically and evolutionarily grounded science, he was always sceptical of the need for, or the usefulness of, any formal effort to systematically explore and test psychoanalytic propositions, and only once indicated approval of a psychoanalytic research work, when, in a footnote added in a 1919 (fn, p. 181) revised edition of The Interpretation of Dreams (1900a), he praised Otto Pötzl’s 1917 publication of psychoanalytically guided experiments in subliminal perception (in Pötzl, Allers, & Teler, 1960). And in a 1934 letter to an American psychologist, Saul Rosenzweig, Freud rejected any need for Rosenzweig’s psychopathological experiments confirming some central psychoanalytic postulates, declaring that psychoanalytic clinical substantiation was all the support that was needed (Shakow & Rapaport, 1964, pp. 129–130). It was this commanding attitude that even today has very substantial advocacy, though no longer dominance, in psychoanalytic discourse. In addition, all the issues of the undoubted complexity of a research which would need be true both to the uniquely idiosyncratic and subjectivistic psychoanalytic database and simultaneously to the canons of proper scientific objectivity, as well as the lack of a sufficiently established cadre of psychoanalytic researchers and research mentors, and of adequate institutional and academic support with the necessary built-in training base, have together made the growth of formal psychoanalytic research initially very slow and halting. But, nonetheless it did begin in various forms—clinical, developmental, psychosomatic, psychopathological, historical, and archival—even early in the twentieth century, and by mid-century was somewhat established, first in the United States and Great Britain, and then, after the Second World War, firmly implanted in Germany and elsewhere in western and northern Europe, and then finally across the romance-language countries over all three major psychoanalytic regions. My administration gave the first formal recognition to the growing psychoanalytic research enterprise by instituting panels dedicated to the presentation of empirical research within the scientific programmes of the IPA congresses: at first primarily clinical (therapy process and outcome) and developmental research. These presentations drew enough interest that they have been continued, with the addition of poster sessions, where researchers are present, at designated times and places, to discuss graphic presentations of their research activities and to respond to questions about them—both at IPA congresses, and also at regional and national IPA meetings. These research presentations at psychoanalytic scientific meetings have been built upon and extended by subsequent administrations. Joseph Sandler’s administration (1989–93) added an annual IPA Research Conference in London (re-named after his death the Joseph Sandler Research Conference), and this has subsequently been extended to other venues in all three regions—Europe, North America, and Latin America. Sandler also started a two-week-long research “summer school” in London for presentations by aspirant psychoanalytic researchers, with a mentoring faculty of established psychoanalytic researchers, and this too has now been extended to other venues (Yale, in New Haven). And Otto Kernberg’s administration (1997–2001) established a fund for psychoanalytic research, administered by a world-wide Research Advisory Board (RAB) of research-knowledgeable psychoanalysts. This allocates up to $200,000 per year of IPA-designated funds in very modest starter grants to applicants (who need not be IPA members) worldwide, covering the entire realm of IPA research activities, process and outcome therapy research, developmental studies over the entire age span from infancy to gerontological issues, psychoanalytically linked cognitive science and neuroscience studies, psychosomatic research, psychoanalytic personality and psychopathological studies, dream and subliminal perception studies, and historical and archival research. The RAB has now been functioning successfully for over ten years, and some of its grantees have succeeded in subsequently garnering much larger grants from governmental, industrial, and private foundation sources in their own nations. Intellectually, the dominant theme during my IPA presidency was the widespread consideration of the burgeoning theoretical diversity or pluralism in psychoanalytic thinking and practice. The theme selected for the 1987 IPA Congress in Montreal was the exploration, after 50 years, of Freud’s “Analysis Terminable and Interminable” (1937c). I chose to open the congress week with an address, One Psychoanalysis or Many? My theme (Wallerstein, 1988) was the breakdown of Freud’s strong pressure to maintain psychoanalysis as a unitary and unifying system of thought, and the rise of rival meta-psychologies and their alterations of clinical technique, starting (even within Freud’s lifetime) with the Kleinian, followed by the British object-relational, Bionian, Lacanian, self-psychological (Kohutian), and other schools. The issue posed by this theoretical proliferation was what, in view of this increasing conceptual diversity, still held us all together as common adherents of a shared psychoanalytic science and profession? The answer that I proposed rested on the distinction that George Klein had made between the clinical phenomena and the clinical theory that described them on the one hand, and the superimposed general theory (metapsychology) purporting to explain the discerned phenomena on the other. My proposal took the clinical theory—of conflict and compromise, of impulse and defence, of anxiety and resistance, of transference and countertransference—as the shared givens, the common ground, of our clinical concerns, to which we all relate in more or less comparable ways in our consulting rooms, while our general theories (our variant metapsychologies) represent the diverse explanatory frameworks to which we differentially adhere—out of our differing trainings and personality dispositions—in our efforts to comprehensively understand how our minds work and can be influenced. This opening plenary address at the 1987 Montreal Congress (Wallerstein, 1988) was much discussed and commented upon at any number of scientific sessions during the week. Some wondered, not quite openly, whether my overall intent was more political than scientific, striving to maintain the organizational integrity of the IPA against the fragmenting pressures of our differing commitments to our chosen guiding metapsychologies with their defensive in-group pressures. The overall impact was such that this theme of one psychoanalysis or many was selected decisively by the programme committee for the next 1989 IPA Congress, in Rome. For that congress, three main plenary speakers would be selected, one from each of the three IPA regions, each trained within a different metapsychological framework, and each charged with presenting a psychoanalytic therapy process, carried out within the guiding metapsychology that they represented. These main plenary sessions would be on Monday, Tuesday, and Thursday mornings; I was asked to make my presidential address on Friday morning, and to discuss the three plenary presentations from the point of view of evidence for—or not—a shared clinical common ground. The three selected plenary speakers were Michael Feldman from London (the European, a Kleinian), Anton Kris from Boston (the North American, a Freudian ego psychologist), and Max Hernandez from Lima (the Latin American, trained in the British Middle Group, an object-relationist). The three, quite separately, chose very similar patients: women in child-bearing years, with mixed depressive and anxiety symptoms and phobic to varying degrees, all preoccupied in one way or another with issues of motherhood, of being or becoming a mother. The three papers were each unmistakably couched within the language and thinking of the author’s espoused meta-psychology, and yet I felt able to trace out various clinical issues dealt with in comparable clinical fashion—the common clinical ground that I postulated—within each author’s preferred metapsychological system. To me it was the shared universe of the clinical common ground that I felt I discerned within the three seemingly disparate papers that marked our shared identity as psychoanalysts in our overarching psychoanalytic home (Wallerstein, 1990). Not everyone agreed with my assessment. Among the discussants, the sharpest dissent came from Roy Schafer, who felt the search for common ground to be a fruitless, politically inspired quest, and that the burgeoning theoretical pluralism represented, rather, the increasing vitality and complexity of psychoanalytic discourse. He presented as his own opposed battle-cry the French proclamation, “vive la difference”. Subsequently, I edited a book, The Common Ground of Psychoanalysis (Wallerstein, 1992), bringing together my two presidential addresses on this issue, the invited clinical presentations on this theme, plus an entire panel of six diverse presentations on this theme, also from the 1989 IPA Congress in Rome, plus other commentary and chapters written explicitly for the book. The whole issue of common ground (or not) proved a major intellectual current throughout my IPA administration (1985–89) and has continued as an important conceptual preoccupation ever since. There have been varying ongoing responses: Fred Pine’s proposal, on the one hand, that the body psychoanalytic comprises four distinct psychologies—designated as of the id, the ego, the self, and the other—and that these should be varyingly called upon, as needed, to understand the varying clinical phenomena observed in the analytic situation, and, at the other extreme, the persistent call by Leo Rangell for adherence to what he dubs “total composite psychoanalytic theory”, created first by Freud and added to by the followers of the ego psychological tradition pioneered by Anna Freud and Heinz Hartmann. To Rangell, all other metapsychological systems are partial offshoots from the original corpus, elevated to be all-encompassing wholes; he has faulted me for according them equal psychoanalytic legitimacy in my presidential addresses. To date this theoretical—and clinical—contention remains a major conceptual debate within psychoanalysis and is probably the enduring intellectual legacy of my presidency. The lay analysis issue that eventuated ultimately in the lawsuit has by now long been laid to rest, with full clinical psychoanalytic training now being available worldwide (in America as well as everywhere else) on the same basis to medical and non-medical aspirants alike. For those who have come for training within the past two decades, the circumstances and the events of the lawsuit have been but a distant awareness, if that. Additionally, the IPA has been sequentially transformed administratively more than once in the intervening years, with little if any active memory of a time when the three regions did not have equal status and equal determining power. Support for empirical research, along with ever-continuing conceptual research, is now thoroughly established in IPA ranks, even if not yet completely securely: there is still a considerable call in organized psychoanalysis—propounded in a major way, for example, by André Green—for psychoanalysis to be the conceived of as a discipline sui generis, hermeneutic rather than a science, with advance to come conceptually solely from the observational data of our consulting rooms, without the need or a place for the kind of empirical research on which scientific disciplines are built. This has been presented in numerous still ongoing debates (see, for example, Sandler, Sandler & Davies, 2000).
