Nonetheless, psychoanalytic research goes on, is growing now seemingly exponentially, and has garnered sufficient (majority) support to receive official funding from both the IPA (the Research Advisory Board) and, actually earlier, from the APsaA (the Fund for Psychoanalytic Research). That, too, seems an enduring legacy from my administration. Overall, I had the fortune to lead the IPA through a period of major problems, major initiatives, and with major consequences. I owe an enormous debt to those who were with me on the IPA Executive Council and its many operating committees, with whom collectively these many changes could be engineered and managed. I can only single out, most briefly, Adam Limentani and Moses Laufer, both of London, president and secretary in the immediately preceding administration, Edward Weinshel and Howard Schlossman, the secretary and treasurer during my administration, Charles Hanly, who chaired the committee that so superbly devised and monitored the process by which non-medical psychoanalytic groups that had grown in the United States when access to the APsaA had been denied could enter the IPA in a way that met IPA requirements and standards, Trip Dorkey, the IPA’s lead attorney through the lawsuit, Richard Simons, president, and Joel Klein, successor lead attorney for the APsaA, and Irene Auletta and Dorothy Unwin, who together managed the IPA office operations in London in marvellously helpful ways. The four years were a fully joint endeavour with these and many, many others too numerous to mention. Note 1. Though it was not until the 1926 publication of The Question of Lay Analysis—Freud’s spirited defence of the psychoanalytic bona fide of Theodor Reik, a non-physician—that Freud made explicit that he felt that the medical degree was not a necessary prerequisite for clinical psychoanalytic training and practice (and might even pose some handicaps that would need to be overcome), the earlier 1910 “Wild Psycho-Analysis” paper was taken as a clear statement of this same conviction and played its signal role in buttressing this position in Europe, in the face of the already (well before 1926) growing American insistence on the necessity of medical training for a psychoanalytic career. Bibliography Freud, S. (1900a). The Interpretation of Dreams. Standard Edition, 4: xi–338. Freud, S. (1910k). “Wild” psycho-analysis. Standard Edition, 11: 219–227. Freud, S. (1926e) The Question of Lay Analysis. Standard Edition, 20: 177–258. Freud, S. (1937c). Analysis terminable and interminable. Standard Edition, 23: 209–253. Pötzl, O., Allers, R., & Teler, J. (1960). Preconscious stimulation in dreams, associations, and images: Classical studies. Psychological Issues, 2 (3, Monograph 7): 41–120 [originally published 1917, in German]. Sandler, J., Sandler, A.-M., & Davies, R. (2000). Clinical and Observational Psychoanalytic Research—Roots of a Controversy: André Green and Daniel Stern. London: Karnac. Shakow, D., & Rapaport, D. (1964). The influence of Freud on American psychology. Psychological Issues, 4 (1, Monograph 13): 1–243. Wallerstein, R. S. (1988). One psychoanalysis or many? International Journal of Psychoanalysis, 69: 5–21. Wallerstein, R. S. (1990) Psychoanalysis: The common ground. International Journal of Psychoanalysis, 71: 3–20. Wallerstein, R. S. (Ed.) (1992). The Common Ground of Psychoanalysis. North-vale, NJ: Jason Aronson. Wallerstein, R. S. (1998). Lay Analysis: Life Inside the Controversy. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press.







40Taiwan The Formosa Model: an emerging tradition of developing psychoanalysis in Taiwan

Chia-Chang Liu

The Taiwan Centre for the Development of Psychoanalysis (TCDP), also named the Taiwan Psychoanalytical Society, was founded in November 2004. By the end of 2005, it was granted IPA Allied Centre status by the Board of the IPA from 2006 on. The following is a brief review of the development of psychoanalysis in Taiwan against the background of the history of the TCDP.

TCDP: the historical background Beginning four decades ago, psychoanalysis began to draw the attention of Taiwan intellectuals. This was a period when there was a surge in the translation of various Western classical and contemporary works, including many famous works by Freud. Many of the translators were medical students or young psychiatrists. Psychiatry in Taiwan after the Second World War was influenced first by Japan and then by the United States, which instilled the seeds of psychodynamic thinking in Taiwan’s psychiatry. However, a formal system for training psychoanalysts had not yet been developed. In the past two decades, biological psychiatry has gradually dominated mainstream Taiwan psychiatry. Nonetheless, there are still quite a few psychiatrists, especially those linked to the Taiwan Centre, who have continued to take a psychoanalytic point of view into account in their clinical work. As for psychotherapy, there has been an increasing interest in, and need for, psychotherapy in general in Taiwan in recent decades. After the withdrawal of martial law in July 1987, workshops and training courses in various schools became quite popular. Psychotherapy organizations were founded, including the Taiwan Association of Psychotherapy (TAP) in August 2001 and the Taiwan Institute of Psychotherapy (TIP) in November 2001. However, they were not exclusively meant for psychoanalysis. Taipei City Psychiatric Centre (TCPC), one of the leading psychiatric hospitals in Taiwan, played an important role in Taiwan psychoanalysis before the founding of the Taiwan Centre. Dr Jung-Yu Tsai, the founding President of the Taiwan Centre, and Dr Chia-Chang Liu, the current President, were both consultant psychiatrists there. They inspired and encouraged many psychiatric residents in the field of psychoanalysis. Dr Tsai had studied at the Tavistock Centre, and he was especially creative in promoting psychoanalytic thinking. He initiated the “To Think” group in the hospital, which became the precursor of the Taiwan Centre. Dr Liu has studied at University College London and has long worked with Dr Tsai. Other individuals also made a great investment in going abroad to train in psychoanalysis. For example, Dr Ming-Min Yang lived in Paris for many years and was a candidate of l’Association Psychoanalytique de France (APF). In addition, Dr Teresa Pai, Prof. Yu-hua Clare Lin, and Dr Jen-Yu Chou were all pioneers who went abroad to study psychoanalysis. In summary the founding of the Taiwan Centre was preceded by a deepening interest in psychoanalysis among different generations of psychiatrists, psychologists, and other clinicians with diverse training backgrounds, all drawn to the field of psychoanalysis.


TCDP: the institutional work The Taiwan Centre is characterized by its diversity, which can be understood on several levels. First, its senior leading members have different training backgrounds, including many with ties to British psychoanalysis, whereas others trained in France, and still others in North America. Second, the members represent different professional disciplines. The early members of the TCDP were mostly psychiatrists, but many individuals from other disciplines soon joined the Taiwan Centre and became active members. At present the Taiwan Centre has approximately 100 members. In addition to psychiatrists, there are many counselling psychologists, clinical psychologists, nurses, and others. Third, the members vary in their relation to psychoanalysis. Some are experienced clinicians who wish to enrich their clinical work through psychoanalysis, while others are newcomers in the clinical field, and still others have growing international connections and are determined to train as psychoanalysts. It is challenging for the Administrative Team to lead such a diverse group. From our beginnings we explicitly agreed with Freud’s opinions on lay analysis. We believe it is the training rather than the trainee’s professional background that makes a good psychoanalyst. So, despite the lack of a formal training system, we set high standards and encourage our members to try their best to meet them. We adhere to the triad of personal analysis, case supervision, and course-work as the necessary ingredients of good training for a psychoanalyst. But we also acknowledge that adaptations may be necessary in order for each member to progress satisfactorily. In order to meet the needs of the diverse members and to promote psychoanalysis to the general public, we have tried very hard to build up a multi-layer network of teaching and learning. First, there are eight regular internal seminars offered by the Academic Committee, composed of the Centre’s most senior and experienced members. These seminars are small discussion groups held for members only. Second, there are the on-line international discussion groups with French, British, and US analysts, respectively, which are also restricted to members. Third, there is an “Introduction to Psychoanalysis” lecture series for the general public, which is intended to promote psychoanalysis in society and to recruit potential future members. This series takes place in a university on a weekly basis. Fourth, there are one-day psychoanalytic workshops, which take place three times a year. Each workshop consists of a clinical case discussion and two lectures given by senior members. This activity is open to individuals in the mental health field and is a way to have deeper discussions of our clinical work with local mental health colleagues. Fifth, every year there are conferences with international guest analysts. The Taiwan Centre regularly organizes two or three international conferences. On the occasion of each conference, we invite IPA psychoanalysts from other countries to spend a week in Taiwan. Members benefit from these events by being exposed to the most recent psychoanalytic developments throughout the world. In order to maintain the Centre’s momentum, we have paid special attention to the following. First, we try to recruit new members via all possible routes. Second, we emphasize both the ideal and the practical aspects of psychoanalysis in Taiwan to our members. As an IPA Allied Centre, our ultimate goal is to become an IPA Component Society entitled to independently train local analysts. On the other hand, we are realistic, and we emphasize that there is a wide range of work to be done and each individual can proceed at his or her own pace. This leads to the third point: although we try to construct the subjectivity of psychoanalysis, especially the professional identity of our Centre, in Taiwanese society, we also respect the individual differences within the Centre. Regarding such differences, we believe that the best policy is to understand and tolerate the conflicts that arise from the different values of the disciplines that constitute the Taiwan Centre. We consider and discuss them in our group, thus providing a space for integration to take place.


TCDP: the scientific work Given the great variation, in relation to psychoanalytic theories, among our members, we have deliberately emphasized the position of “Starting from Freud” in our scientific work. Thus, the reading of Freud’s works is a significant part of many different educational activities, from internal seminars to sessions of reading Freud in international conferences. Against this background of an emphasis on Freud, the Taiwan Centre has organized a wide range of programmes. Conceptually, these programmes have explored classical works and the latest developments in theory and research. Viewing diversity more as a strength than as a flaw, we have encouraged our members to take an open-minded and respectful attitude to the different schools of psychoanalytic thought and practice addressed in these programmes. The main themes of the international events over the past five years have included: “The Front of Psychoanalysis” (2004), “Freud in Latin America” (2005), “Witch Metapsychology: Psychoanalysis in France and Germany” (2005), “Intersection of Theory and Practice” (2005), “British Perspectives on Narcissism: Contemporary Freudian and Kleinian” (2006), “From the Couch to the Way Home” (2006), “Infancy Terminable and Interminable” (2006), “Fantasy, Conflict, and Intervention: Perspectives of Contemporary Ego Psychology” (2007), “The Reminiscence of Mourning—Childhood Depression and Adolescent Identification” (2007), “A Story for Our Time: Trauma and Depression” (2007), “Yesterday, Today and Tomorrow in Psychoanalysis: Remembering, Repeating and Working Through” (2008), “A Hospital-Based Treatment for Personality Disorders” (2008), “Spontaneity and Impingement: Winnicottian and Bionian Ideas and Techniques” (2008), “When a Patient Is Leaving” (2008), “Phantasies of Our ‘Beginnings’ (and Their Impact on Daily Life)” (2009), “Sex and Language in Psychoanalysis” (2009), “Sexuality: A British Point of View” (2010), and “Woman: The Dark Continent” (2010). Geographically, we have invited Latin American (Argentinian), Continental European (French, German, and Belgian), British, and North American analysts to participate in these programmes. Due to the variety of viewpoints within the Taiwan Centre, many controversial issues have been touched upon in the educational curriculum, including a comparison of psychoanalysis and psychotherapy, the relevance of psychoanalysis to contemporary psychiatry, especially for the deeper understanding of psychotic patients, and the different points of view on transference interpretation. Other vital issues include infantile sexuality, the death instinct, the controversial notion of the transformation in “O” of the later Bion, and the powerfully formulated theory of mentalization. It is interesting to observe that some of the most controversial concepts in psychoanalysis, such as the death instinct and the mysterious “O”, do not seem to have aroused reactions of misgiving in Taiwan. This response has inspired our members to think more deeply about the role of cultural influences in assimilating controversial ideas.


The Formosa Model: an emerging tradition Is there an intrinsic logic underlying the kaleidoscopic nature of the activities of the Taiwan Centre over the past several years? These activities have been undertaken in order to realize our wish to establish a sound and lasting scientific community of psychoanalysts in Taiwan, to train our own analysts, to offer analyses to those who need them, to work with and support clinical colleagues in the mental health field, and to bring about mutual enrichment between psychoanalysis and other disciplines, especially art, literature, and the humanities. In order to fulfil this wish, we have made great efforts to bring together a diverse group of people. When the IPA Allied Centre Review Committee made its first visit to Taiwan in 2007, Dr Jung-Yu Tsai, the founding President of the Taiwan Centre, through intensive discussions with its core members, proposed the Formosa Model. It may be summarized by the saying, “Let every tree in the forest grow”—just as different plants flourish in the highly varied environment of Taiwan. Thus, it has two meanings. One is to encourage every form of psychoanalysis, until the most suitable form takes shape in Taiwan. On a more personal level, this model means supporting individual members to pursue their own study of psychoanalysis in terms of what is meaningful for them. For a more creative understanding of our diversity, we have tried to learn from the controversy over theoretical pluralism in the contemporary psychoanalytic world. The fact that analysts tend to differ in their theoretical orientation but converge in practice may teach us a lesson in dealing with the differences among ourselves. Whatever metapsychology (or theory of mind) is consciously held, one should always be open to its revision when confronted with the obscurity of clinical material. By the same token, how we understand diversity among ourselves is, like our working theory of the mind, always tentative and biased. What is more important is the actual experience of getting along with a diverse group of people, a process that is more akin to clinical practice. It is crucial not only to tolerate differences but also to acknowledge the existence of what is unknown and to wait for something to emerge from the unconscious—just as a good analyst would do in analysis. Furthermore, our experiences in the Taiwan Centre have taught us that people work better if they are friends before they are colleagues. We believe that our friendship and common belief in psychoanalysis is of greater importance than our differences or conflicts that may accompany these differences. This conviction may help to explain our passion and the pleasure we take in the Formosa Model.


Acknowledgements The Taiwan Centre for the Development of Psychoanalysis would like to express our thanks to the following international friends who have been invited to Taiwan to teach and support us: Dr Teresa Yuan (Argentine Psychoanalytic Association), Dr Alf Gerlach (German Psychoanalytical Association), Mme Gilberte Gensel (APF), Prof. Daniel Widlöcher (APF), Dr Helene Trivouss Widlöcher (APF), Dr Monique Serebriany (Buenos Aires Psychoanalytic Association), Dr Mary Target (BPAS), Ms Nicola Abel-Hirsch (BPAS), Mme Corinne Ehrenberg (APF), Monsieur Alain Ehrenberg, Mme Laurence Kahn (APF), Dr Ann De Lancey (APsaA), Dr Lorna Rhodes, Dr Nina Schorr (APsaA), Dr Michael Gundle (APsaA), Dr Erik Gann (APsaA), Dr Jean-Claude Arfouilloux (APF), Mme Françoise Neau (APF), Ms Caroline Garland (BPAS), Dr David Taylor (BPAS), Dr David Sachs (APsaA), Ronald W. Levin (APsaA), Prof. Maggie Mills (BPAS), Prof. Rudi Vermote (Belgian Psychoanalytical Society), Dr Dominique Vanwalleghem, Mme Jocelyne Malosto (APF), Prof. Jaques André (APF), and Dr Athanassios Alexandridis (APF).








33Australia The Australian Psychoanalytical Society: the evolving relationship with the IPA

Frances Thomson Salo


I hope to become in eighteen months or two years a doctor in whom people can have confidence and will first try my luck in my native town. If I don’t get on here quickly enough, which is likely seeing that a young doctor needs capital and I have none, I will emigrate to England, or perhaps to America or Australia. Freud to Martha Bernays, 1882 (cited in Jones, 1972: 196)

In the light of what might have happened, let us see what did happen. Interest in psychoanalysis grew in Australia in the period before the Second World War when there was a “fertile intellectual climate of discussion and debate” about Freud and psychoanalytic theories which, in becoming part of the cultural discourse, helped shape it (Damousi, 2005, p. 180). In the early days psychoanalysts whose training in Europe had been recognized by the International Psychoanalytical Association moved to Australia, but it was not until the 1960s that relations with the IPA became more formalized.

Arriving in Australia Psychoanalytic practice in Australia did not begin until the 1930s, but as early as 1909 Freud reported learning that a group established in Sydney by Dr Donald Fraser was eagerly studying his work. Freud was subsequently invited by Dr Andrew Davidson, the secretary of the Neurological Section of the Australian Branch of the British Medical Association, to read a paper at the Australian Medical Congress in Sydney in 1911. He was unable to attend, but his paper “On Psychoanalysis” was read there. Dr Roy Winn, returning to Australia after he had become interested in using psychoanalysis with soldiers suffering from shell shock in the First World War, was the first psychoanalyst to practise full-time. He had trained in the British Society, and from 1931 on he worked in Sydney as a psychoanalyst for about 30 years. For most of the interwar years he was the only trained psychoanalyst in Australia. He published a number of papers in the Medical Journal of Australia, including a 1936 paper, “Psychoanalysis and General Practice”, which emphasized psychoanalysis as the therapy most likely to prove effective in cases of psychogenic illness. While there were some scathing criticisms, other psychiatrists published in support of psychoanalysis. In 1938, with the Second World War imminent, Ernest Jones raised the possibility of six psychoanalysts emigrating to Australia. Winn, together with Dr Paul Dane in Melbourne, who had been analysed by Joan Riviere, backed Jones’s efforts. There was, however, considerable difficulty in getting government support for displaced European psychoanalysts to emigrate to Australia. Only Dr Clara Lazar-Geroe, who had been trained in Budapest with Balint and Ferenczi, was approved. To support her migration, Jones, without formally involving the IPA, and although she had only completed training two years earlier, appointed her a training analyst of the British Society, and Melbourne was authorized to function as a branch of the Society. Candidates trained and recommended by Geroe would, after writing a clinical paper, be elected associate members of the British Society. In 1940 Geroe, who quickly had to learn English, was appointed Director of the Melbourne Institute of Psychoanalysis, which had been established that year with the support of Dane and other psychiatrists. In Deirdre Moore’s memoir of her analysis Geroe was described as “little and plump like Queen Victoria”, her office had brightly coloured, heavily embossed embroideries, a Persian carpet, and a brown leather-buttoned couch (Moore, 1999, p. 76). A number of émigrés found that the promised beautiful land was harsher in reality than they had dreamt: they never overcame their homesickness for the faraway shores of Europe. Geroe, however, worked tirelessly for the Institute, promoting psychoanalysis to a wide audience interested in mental health. For many years she taught seminars for psychiatrists, teachers, and parents and established a child guidance clinic to provide psychoanalytic services for those who could not afford them. In recognition of her contribution she was appointed the first Honorary Psychoanalyst in Australia at the Royal Melbourne Hospital. To get training started, in conditions of relative isolation, Geroe had to function as a training analyst solely responsible for selection, analysis, teaching, supervision, and recommending for qualification, similarly to other psychoanalysts around the world. She was, however, familiar with the Hungarian model, where the psychoanalyst was the first supervisor, because they would be more aware of the candidate’s transferences. Two other Hungarian psychoanalysts followed Geroe as part of an influx of European-trained psychoanalysts. In 1949 Dr Andrew Peto, a respected training analyst of the British Society, moved to Sydney, and two years later, when the Sydney Institute of Psychoanalysis was established, he became a Director. One of Peto’s analysands was trained in Sydney before he moved to the United States six years later, delaying psychoanalytic training in Sydney until the 1960s. Mrs Vera Roboz, however, who joined Geroe in Melbourne in 1957, was influential in the practice and teaching of analysis in Australia for 30 years.


Towards IPA recognition The arrangement in the 1940s that had enabled psychoanalytic training to begin in Australia had been the only feasible solution, since there was only one training analyst. In the 20 years from 1941 on, when Geroe first began training, only three psychoanalysts qualified as associate members: Frank Graham in 1952, Harry South-wood in 1953, and Rose Rothfield in 1956. While they were all medical psychoanalysts, non-medicals had been equally accepted for training. But despite the earlier acceptance of psychoanalysis in the culture and a lively scientific life, training grew only slowly. One can wonder about processes that were not fully resolved for Geroe and the Society, among them mourning in the face of trauma and considerable losses. Perhaps for Geroe to yield control was felt as too dangerous, after the situation in Europe that she had had to leave? One can wonder what had been deposited in the unconscious group life as well as the later “bitter wrangling, the suspicions and accusations” in London that Steiner (1985, p. 39) describes (Frisch, 2009). Was an experience to do with training that was profoundly difficult for the early psychoanalysts transmitted within the Society, so that in subsequent years training was experienced as “traumatic” for some psychoanalysts? That there are no centrally held archives and documents are scattered among members and institutes suggests that there may still be some memories to be integrated.1 And on the wider scene, of note is the parallel existence throughout this time of the White Australia policy, which intentionally restricted “non-white” immigration, from about the start of the growth of interest in psychoanalysis in Australia. In 1958, after gaining full membership in the British Society, Southwood initiated psychoanalytic training in Adelaide in conjunction with the Melbourne Institute. From the early 1960s the progressive increase in the number of analysts was accompanied by growing dissent over the training model. Geroe had trained in a Society that, similarly to the British Society, emphasized countertransference and early mother–infant relations. However, the Hungarian approach, where the function of psychoanalyst and supervisor to the candidate were combined, in comparison to the greater separation of functions in the British approach, evoked strongly felt differences among psychoanalysts (although over time Geroe was noted to observe stricter boundaries in this regard). There were tensions between those who trained in Australia and those who trained in London: Graham, who went to London for first-hand experience of the Kleinian approach, felt that there was a “simmering jealousy” in those who had not gone overseas (Graham & Brumley, 1995). The model of analysis, supervision, and clinical seminars by the same psychoanalyst had become increasingly at odds with IPA standards, and the British Society, in consultation with a number of Australian psychoanalysts, approached the IPA to take responsibility for training in Australia. In 1967, at the Copenhagen Congress, the IPA established the Australian Study Group and assumed responsibility for training, appointing a Sponsoring Committee of seven psychoanalysts, chaired by Dr Fanny Wride. In 1968 Dr Adam Limentani and Dr Leo Rangell, members of the Sponsoring Committee, visited Australia and appointed four training analysts. The committee and the Australian group worked towards Provisional Society status, which involved broadening the training arrangements and developing a Constitution. The Sponsoring Committee appointed a Training Committee in Australia (TCIA), responsible for training and answerable through them to the IPA. The initial TCIA members were Geroe, Graham, Rothfield, Roboz, Southwood, and R. Martin. In 1971, at the 27th IPA Congress in Vienna, the Australian Provisional Society was formally approved, with Prof. Martin as the inaugural president. At the 1973 Paris IPA Congress—the same year as the Sydney Opera House was opened by Queen Elizabeth II and the “White Australia” policy ceased—the Australian Psychoanalytical Society (APAS) was approved as a Component Society of the IPA.


Australian Psychoanalytic Society and the IPA: containing splits during transformation The difficult task begun by the Study Group in 1968 was changing the Hungarian-based training system that had been in existence for 27 years. Much of the difficulty was linked with differences in theoretical orientation. Increasingly, the group’s backgrounds diverged: some had completed training in Australia; others had, after training in Australia, travelled to London for further analysis; yet others were overseas-trained in Europe or from the British Society. Kleinian theory had a widening influence on training in Australia, although this was rarely acknowledged openly. The Adelaide members who had trained under the Hungarian-based system had been relatively isolated from the influence of different theoretical views. Theoretical differences were heightened by problems of communication due to the long distances between the three centres. The transition from one training system to another was at times acrimonious, with the Adelaide group often the focus of much of the conflict. Their contribution to the Society’s scientific life had lessened as they felt that the group that was changing the previously accepted style of analytic work would be unsympathetic. Decisions unfavourable to some members contributed to the belief that training was prevented from taking place in Adelaide. Training analysts were appointed only if they were “known and respected”, and the Education Committee had not appointed any in Adelaide. The Adelaide group felt, “rightly or wrongly, that they were locked into a colonial position in relation to Sydney and Melbourne” (Blomfield, 1980, p. 4). In 1979, as the result of work by Southwood, the Adelaide Institute had been established. New developments followed the passing of the old order: in 1980 Geroe died, and that year, in a threatened split from the APAS, the Adelaide psychoanalysts requested Study Group status because of the difficulties about training. The situation had come to a head at a meeting of the Australian Society at Melbourne airport.


The tension was so high that there was difficulty in getting the Sydney, Melbourne and Adelaide groups to meet in the city itself. This was an extremely difficult meeting with feelings running very high …. The whole Society/Institute structure … seemed to be in flames. [Blomfield, 1990]


The IPA appointed a Site Visiting Committee (SVC), with Dr Edward Joseph, the IPA President, as chair, and Prof. R. Moses and Dr McLaughlan. Adelaide’s request was resented, with the SVC felt to be an “unwarranted intrusion” and strongly held convictions that a valid assessment of the situation could not be made by a group that included ego psychologists (Blomfield, 1990). The SVC made considerable efforts over three years to reconcile the differences. They commented on the relatively slow growth of the Society, as only two full members had been trained by the Society in the previous 12 years, and they noted the barriers to becoming a candidate. They suggested that the scientific function of the Society had been relatively neglected, in part because some members felt that presentations other than clinical ones would be disparaged and were unwilling to express differing opinions. Some members, perhaps partly in response to the extent of the SVC’s negative findings, felt that it had not recognized their efforts to resolve the problems and had concluded too quickly that the Adelaide group had not been treated fairly. Problems around training and the claim for autonomy continued, and the Australian Society requested a second site visit. It argued that the committee should include a member of the British Society, as there had been a long association between the two societies. The IPA agreed, appointing a two-man SVC in 1986: Dr Arnold Cooper (United States) as chair, with Professor Joseph Sandler (Britain). The SVC recommended a federated structure for the Adelaide, Melbourne, and Sydney groups as more transparently equitable. Incorporating many of the previous SVC’s suggestions, it likewise recommended that the relatively minor role of the Society’s scientific life be developed, and its Visiting Psychoanalyst scheme broadened to include British and American psychoanalysts who brought a perspective other than a Kleinian one. The SVC offered to hear presentations by Adelaide members in order to appoint new training analysts, to unblock the training situation. In discussion with Society members, Cooper said that he would pass on the Society’s valuable suggestion that the IPA insist that every Component Society set up an ethics committee. While Sandler commented that “There’s been nothing but trouble with the Australian Society” (1993, personal communication), the reorganization suggested by the SVC has held without a split. Under the new Constitution, the Australian Society is a federation of three branches; the Society assumes overall responsibility for training, while the branches work within guidelines drawn up by the elected Society Executive Committee. This committee alone has the authority to qualify IPA psychoanalysts and is responsible for all national decisions about psychoanalysis, for Society scientific conferences, and for the online journal, Psychoanalysis Downunder.2 The Society is non-reporting, and training follows the Eitingon system: training analysis concurrent with two training cases seen 4–5 times a week, each supervised weekly. There is an integrated four-year curriculum, including an infant observation parallel with theoretical seminars in the first year of training. Several psychoanalysts have trained as child psychoanalysts. Currently the orientation of the majority of psychoanalysts would be more broadly representative of the British object relations group.


Growing the Society and outreach Today, in the psychiatric and academic fields and among the general public, there is a wide range of views about psychoanalysis. Previously there had been a relative withdrawal from public sector psychiatry positions. This was viewed, from one perspective, as the Australian Society isolating itself from “academic and mental health sources from which support might be found, mainly because of an adherence to an unrealistic ideal of the ‘purity’ of psychoanalysis” (Blomfield, 1980, p. 5). The withdrawal meant that opportunities for younger professionals to see psychoanalysts at work in the public sector were lost, but some psychoanalysts are again active today in mental health institutions. The APAS played a leading role among the mental health professions in the formation of the Section of Psychoanalysis and Psychoanalytic Psychotherapies, which joined the Psychotherapy and Counselling Federation of Australia for self-regulation of the profession and protection of the public. In the academic world, psychoanalytic theory has an acknowledged place in many Arts and Social Science university departments, but one that is not matched by positions in academic and clinical departments, although some Society members do contribute to university teaching. At the beginning of the new millennium, and a time of expansion of psychoanalysis in Australia, the Society, following Maria Teresa Hooke’s lead as chair of the Society Scientific Committee and then as president, wished to reach out and reconnect with the roots of their culture and identity. With the indigenous Aboriginal culture as the world’s oldest surviving culture, topics such as Aboriginal reconciliation and indigenous Australians’ connections with the land in the Dreamtime were presented in an exploration of the “geography of meanings”, at the annual Society Conference Open Days for mental health professionals and the public (Hooke, 2007, p. 5). The three institutes carry out educational functions, with public relations programmes providing a range of activities such as lectures and seminars and opportunities to study psychoanalytic concepts as applied to literature and film. There are informal links with groups such as the Lacanians and the Jungians, who were invited to the Open Days and the Freud anniversary celebrations, as part of starting a scientific dialogue with other psychoanalytic groups. The recent outreach activities have succeeded in increasing the visibility of the Society and of psychoanalysis, and in counteracting some of the tendency towards negative attitudes in the wider community and the media. In recent years, projected membership figures had shown—in line with those of many societies—that an increased number of psychoanalysts needed to be trained, as numbers had declined due to retirements and death. In a country the size of Europe, about 80 psychoanalysts and 20 candidates are mainly concentrated in three major cities on the Eastern seaboard. In 2006, on the anniversary of Freud’s birth, the Society made a concerted effort to organize vigorous and imaginative outreach efforts (Thomson Salo, 2008). This brought immediate results, and, as has been demonstrated elsewhere, the number of applicants increases in proportion to outreach activities. In this respect the future of the Society looks somewhat more assured. Simultaneously, the APAS began a drive to extend the availability of psychoanalytic training in Australia and New Zealand, as it had neither spread outside the original three branches nor grown in comparison with other societies. Initiatives in 2008 included establishing a Psychoanalytic Study Centre in Canberra and drawing up guidelines, based on the IPA guidelines for training using concentrated analysis. This training possibility is now available for applicants who are not able to relocate from their home town for training.


The changing relationship of the APAS to the IPA The IPA is seen as playing an increasingly larger part in the life of the Society, both scientifically and educationally. Over the years the number of members participating in the scientific programme of IPA Congresses has grown. APAS psychoanalysts have also become members of IPA committees, such as, for example, the Committee on China, the Psychoanalytic Perspectives on the Ageing of Patients and Psychoanalysts Workgroup, the Committee on Public Information, and the Committee on Women and Psychoanalysis. Lastly, an important development is that the APAS is working with the IPA to explore the contribution that the Society could make in the area—Asia—in which it is situated. The APAS guidelines for training based on concentrated analysis offer an additional possibility for training for applicants from Asia. In this way Australia is linking to its geographical region. How the APAS has moved from isolation both nationally and internationally can be seen by retracing its path: from the APAS origins in the Hungarian tradition, through an identification with the British Society, then taking its place in the scientific endeavours of the European Psychoanalytical Federation, and, finally, to a forward-looking exploration, in partnership with the IPA, of the contribution that the APAS could make in its geographical region. The relationship of the APAS with the British Society has evolved from one of dependence to a fruitful exchange. Australia is a country of migrants. The Society has its migrants too, from other Societies as well as the British Society, all of whom have greatly contributed, so that the contribution from overseas, combined with the energy and talent of the local group, have produced an innovative mixture.

Foreword Charles Hanly President, International Psychoanalytical Association 2010 is a year to remember for psychoanalysis, for it marks the hundredth year of our International Psychoanalytical Association and invites us to celebrate our psychoanalytic community across the world. The beginnings of our Association were modest: at the founding Congress in Nuremberg in March 1910, the IPA counted fewer than one hundred members, mostly friends and colleagues. But its charter members were prestigious: Freud, Ferenczi, Abraham, and Eitingon. Upon the shoulders of these great men, psychoanalysis and the International Psychoanalytical Association have been built. In one hundred years, the IPA has grown to its current membership of over 12,000 members. Men founded the IPA, yet psychoanalysis is a profession in which women gained early recognition, institutional equality, and governance roles well before achieving comparable status in other sciences. Many great women, such as Anna Freud, Melanie Klein, Lou Andreas Salome, Joan Riviere, Ruth Mack Brunswick, Helene Deutsch, Karen Horney, Marie Bonaparte, Phyllis Greenacre, Jeanne Lampl-de Groot—and the list could go on—have made major clinical and theoretical contributions and have given leadership to psychoanalysis. Our profession has truly been open and historically ahead of its time. The International Psychoanalytical Association is unique among international scientific organizations. Like other scientific and professional associations, it advances its field of knowledge and practice by means of congresses. In addition, the IPA also maintains minimal ethical, training, and professional standards, and it creates new study groups, provisional societies, and component societies throughout the world. The IPA certifies psychoanalysts worldwide according to the highest standards of training and practice in the field. These first hundred years offer much in which we can take pride, many achievements we can remember with joy. In this centennial year, we unite locally, as many component societies across the world organize anniversary festivities. We unite regionally in New York, London, and Bogotá and internationally at the 2011 Mexico Congress to celebrate one hundred years of achievements in psychoanalysis. We also commemorate the history of our Association in a more permanent way with the publication of the present volume. But let us not rest content with nostalgic memories or with celebration for its own sake. Rather, let us use our legitimate pride in what has been accomplished to meet the challenges facing psychoanalysis in the future. Let us preserve and advance the science of individual human nature that the founders of the IPA developed. Let us use the knowledge that “disturbed the peace of the world” to ameliorate the condition of our fellow human beings. Let us dedicate the celebrations of our 100th year to outreach and to the exploration of new ways to serve the communities in which we work and live. We shall thus improve and strengthen the image of psychoanalysis as science and practice. The year 2010 is an opportunity for each of us to celebrate what has been achieved in the last one hundred years and an invitation to take up the challenges of the next one hundred years. Proud of what the IPA and psychoanalysts have accomplished, let us unite and meet the tasks before us: the strengthening of psychoanalytic knowledge and practice through outreach, the progress towards a more integrated psychoanalytic theory securely based on clinical observation, and increased therapeutic gains for our patients.








Part IINorth America







34China Development of psychodynamic psychotherapy and psychoanalysis in China

Sverre Varvin & Alf Gerlach


History Interest in psychoanalysis and in practising psychodynamic and psychoanalytic therapy has a long and a short history in China. The long history dates from 1912, when Qian Zhixiu wrote an article on Freud’s psychoanalysis in the journal Dongfang zazhi. In the years following there was considerable interest, both in psychoanalysis as a clinical discipline and in the relevance of psychoanalytic theory to political, literary, and philosophical thought. Beginning in the 1920s, some of Freud’s writings—Group Psychology and the Analysis of the Ego (1921c) and An Autobiographical Study (1925d [1924])—were translated into Chinese. From 1935 to 1939 Dr Bingham Dai, a psychiatrist of Chinese origin who had received training in psychoanalytically oriented psychotherapy, worked at Peking Union Medical College. He had trained with H. S. Sullivan in New York and with L. Saul in Chicago. Through his teaching, supervision, and training therapies he transmitted his experiences to his colleagues (Gerlach, 1995). Adolf Joseph Storfer, a psychoanalyst and a Jewish refugee from Vienna, came to Shanghai, where he published articles on psychoanalysis that influenced both Chinese psychiatrists and intellectuals (Plänkers, 2010). Between 1920 and 1949 there was also a “wide reception of psychoanalytic ideas in philosophy, literature and criticism. Many philosophers, psychologists and literary critics translated and interpreted Freud’s and other psychoanalyst’s works, and Freudian issues were drawn upon by a number of famous Chinese writers. The Chinese psychologist Gao Juefu, for example, translated Freud’s Introduction to Psychoanalysis, and wrote critical reports about the new science” (Gerlach, 2003). In the 1930s there was interest in the relationship between psychoanalysis and politics in China similar to that in many European countries. In 1936 Wilhelm Reich’s Dialectical Materialism and Psychoanalysis was published under the Chinese title Jingshen fen xi xue pipan, and in 1940 Freud and Marx, by R. Osborn, was published under the Chinese title Jingshen fen xixue yu ma kesi zhuyi (Gerlach, 2003). After the founding of the People’s Republic of China in 1949, central political directives determined the future development of the sciences and of intellectual life. Not until after the end of the Cultural Revolution in 1976 and the initiation of a more open policy towards the West could public interest in psychoanalysis be observed again. Psychoanalysis was then given considerably more attention in the cultural life of China, and the works of Freud and of other psychoanalysts were again printed and even new translations published.


Training programmes in China The shorter history started in 1983, when German analysts were invited by Professor Mo Gan-Ming, the head of the psychiatric hospital in Guangzhou (Canton), to give lectures and seminars and to participate in psychiatric research on an epidemic of mass hysteria on Hainan Island. Between 1997 and 1999, a group of German analysts belonging to the German–Chinese Academy for Psychotherapy offered continuous training in psychoanalytically oriented psychotherapy to Chinese psychiatrists and psychologists. They worked in Kunming, Beijing, Shanghai, Wuhan, and Chengdu. Since 2000, under the leadership of Dr Alf Gerlach, they have established training in Shanghai in cooperation with the Shanghai Mental Health Centre. This group of German analysts, under the auspices of the German Academic Exchange Service (DAAD), the German–Chinese Academy for Psychotherapy, and German psychoanalytic groups, including the German Psychoanalytical Association (DPV), the German Psychoanalytical Society (DPG), and the German Society for Psychotherapy and Depth Psychology (DGPT), has offered an intensive three-year training programme that has attracted leading Chinese psychiatrists and psychologists. They have also sponsored extensive training programmes covering basic to advanced training in psychodynamic psychotherapy. In the last training programme, from 2008 to 2010, 14 groups of participants were trained—144 psychiatrists and psychologists in the basic programme and 80 in the advanced programme. The curriculum consists of theoretical lectures, case supervision, and individual “self-experience” (short personal therapy) for beginners, and group psychoanalysis for advanced participants. Dr Teresa Yuan, an Argentine psychoanalyst with a Chinese background, has actively taught psychoanalytic psychotherapy in China since 1995. She offered seminars for the Child Psychiatric Institute at Beijing Medical University and courses and workshops with Anding Hospital. She started systematic training seminars in Bejing in 2002, in collaboration with Anding Hospital, under Professor Yang Yunping. These seminars have included European psychoanalysts Bien Filet (Netherlands), Sverre Varvin (Norway), and Julia Fabricius (UK). Dr Yuan, with the collaboration of Peter Loewenberg (USA), has also conducted psychodynamic training seminars for the Hospital Authority of Hong Kong in 2003, 2004, and 2005. This was followed in 2006 by training programmes in Beijing and Wuhan, arranged by the Norwegian Psychoanalytic Society under the leadership of Sverre Varvin, co-financed by the Norwegian Government and Anding Hospital/Beijing government. A basic goal of the Norwegian programme (and also of the German programme in Shanghai) has been to educate future teachers in psychotherapy in China—that is, to establish a “train the trainers” programme. The Norwegian programmes have had between 80 and 120 participants and are organized on two levels. An advanced group, who have benefited from several years of experience in the German programmes in Shanghai, learned psychotherapy and were also trained to do psychotherapy supervision. This group became the supervisors of the junior participants in the seminars and are now well-qualified teachers and supervisors in the advanced programmes as well as important teachers in hospitals and university settings in China. The three programmes arranged by the Norwegian group are organized as follows:


Intense weekly programmes twice a year with Norwegian instructors, also including German teachers in the first two programmes. There are lectures, supervisions in groups, and short intensive personal experiences, which are called “self-experience” and may be termed mini-therapies. Each participant is required to have two patients under regular supervision: one for 100 sessions or more, and one of 50 sessions or more. Supervisors have mostly been Chinese colleagues trained in the programmes, but also include supervision from Europe over Skype. Each participant has to write a paper to achieve qualification from the programme. Approximately 300 individuals have participated in the programmes.


The first programme was completed in 2008. Those who qualified—that is, those who fulfilled the requirements—which were the majority, received a diploma and were certified by the China Mental Health Association. They are now seen as leading figures in the present development of psychotherapy training in the Chinese mental health system. These three-year training programmes have, by and large, been a success. The importance of a three year training is demonstrated by the fact that among our students important developments in comprehension and competence to practise psychotherapy are evident at the end of the second year. Several Chinese trainees of these programmes were invited to spend time—usually between 3 and 12 months—for further training in psychoanalytic departments of universities and hospitals in Germany, or in research institutions like the Sigmund Freud Institute in Frankfurt. During these stays they also received periods of intensive personal psychoanalytic psychotherapy.


CAPA In 2008 the China American Psychoanalytic Association (CAPA) began a new initiative under the leadership of Elise Snyder. They organize two-year programmes with seminars, supervisions, and also psychotherapy or psychoanalysis. This is accomplished via Skype—which may be the main difference from the German and Norwegian programmes. These programmes have attracted considerable interest and have benefited from the work done, especially by the German analysts, in China over the last 20 years.


Psychoanalytic training Even though the German and the Norwegian programmes are not organized by nor have any formal connection with the International Psychoanalytical Association, there is no doubt that their efforts were crucial in establishing the possibility of proper psychoanalytic training in China. Full psychoanalytic training started in Beijing in 2008. An important precondition for this development was that Dr Irmgard Dettbarn, an IPA Interim Training Analyst, began working in China in 2007. There are now nine candidates in psychoanalytic training in China, all of whom were recruited from the Norwegian and/or the German programmes. Lectures and supervisions are offered by members of the IPA China Committee (Peter Loewenberg, Alf Gerlach, Maria Teresa Savio Hooke, Sverre Varvin) and other IPA analysts from Norway and Germany. The IPA China Committee initiated full psychoanalytic training in Shanghai in 2011 with the presence there of Dr Hermann Schultz, an IPA Interim Training Analyst.


The present situation Currently there are numerous activities related to psychoanalysis in China. In 2004, during the first “China Annual Meeting for Psychoanalysis”, the Psychoanalytic Association of China was founded with 33 members in Beijing, Chengdu, Guangzhou, Hangzhou, Harbin, Hefei, Shanghai, Suzhou, Wuhan, and Xi’an. In 2007, during the IPA Congress in Berlin, the first formal connection with the IPA was established with the formation of the IPA China Allied Centre, whose members are also members of the Psychoanalytic Association of China. There are currently several important centres of psychoanalytic work in China:


Beijing, Anding Hospital (Prof. Dr Yang Yunping, Director of the Department of Clinical Psychology). Shanghai, Shanghai Mental Health Centre (Prof. Dr Xiao Zeping, President of the SMHC). Wuhan, Tongji Medical University (Prof. Dr Shi Qijia, Head of the Psychotherapy Department).


Psychoanalytic authors translated into Chinese now include, among others, Sigmund Freud, Anna Freud, Joseph Sandler, Ralph Greenson, Anthony Bateman, and Jeremy Holmes. An ambitious project of translating Freud’s collected works into Chinese is lead by Dr Tomas Plänkers from the Sigmund Freud Institute in Frankfurt. Psychoanalytic papers are published in different journals, such as in the Chinese Psychotherapy in Dialogue, edited by Prof. Shi Qijia, or in the Shanghai Archives of Psychiatry (Shanghai Jingshen Yixue).


Asia conference and later development In 2009 the second Annual Meeting for Psychoanalysis was held in Shanghai, with the participation of IPA analysts from different regions. The theme of the conference was “Psychoanalysis in the Modernizing Society”, with lectures and workshops on theoretical, clinical, and empirical psychoanalytic research, cross-cultural psychoanalysis, as well as the challenges confronting psychoanalysis in a society in the process of modernization. In 2010 the IPA organised in Beijing its first Asia Psychoanalytic Conference, celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the IPA. The congress was held in cooperation with the Chinese Association for Mental Health and brought together psychoanalysts and psychoanalytic therapists from Asian Countries, but also from Europe, North and South America, and Australia. The conference, with over 500 participants, proved to be a highly successful event. There were papers of high quality from Chinese colleagues and from colleagues attending from other countries. Most importantly, the conference was an intercultural encounter where the IPA had the opportunity to show its face in Asia and where participants from the Asian part of the IPA family established contacts both with each other and with colleagues from the rest of the IPA world. It was, for example, significant that some reconciliation work between Chinese and Japanese colleagues could be achieved in connection with Shigeyuki Mori’s paper on Japanese war children (Mori, 2010).


How to develop psychodynamic psychotherapy and psychoanalysis in China Since its beginning in the 1990s, the long-term strategy of the German programmes has been to educate a core group of Chinese colleagues in basic psychodynamic psychotherapeutic principles. Basically it was a “teaching the teachers” programme. This line was also followed when the Norwegian programme was later implemented. The aim was not only that professionals working in the mental health field should gain this competence, but that they should then be able to organize themselves and develop as teachers in order to train younger colleagues. This strategy was developed in the Norwegian programme, where groups of senior colleagues who had had their basic training in the German programme underwent training as supervisors. We then had both participants at basic and advanced levels as well as supervisors in the same programme. The participants being trained in supervision could then, as part of their training, supervise younger colleagues attending the same course. This was further developed when training courses started in Wuhan, where those educated in the German programmes and those beginning the Norwegian programmes functioned as teachers in parallel with the Norwegian teacher, presenting lectures and providing short-term therapies during the course. In this way competence has been built on several levels with the explicit aim that Chinese colleagues will gradually oversee their own training. This development represented the background for the development of the IPA psychoanalysis training programme. The candidates in this training had all undergone a thorough basic training in psychodynamic psychotherapy in the earlier German and the Norwegian programmes. Several individuals who had trained in the German and Norwegian programmes have chosen to follow the CAPA programme, whose appeal reflects the in-depth work that had been done earlier to promote psychodynamic psychotherapy in China.


Conclusion Important work has been done by IPA analysts in developing psychodynamic psychotherapy and psychoanalysis in China over the last 27 years:


Training a significant number of psychiatrists and psychologists in psychoanalytically derived methods that have demonstrated clinical efficacy for several disorders. Many of these trainees have leading positions in Chinese psychiatric hospitals, psychotherapeutic hospital departments, faculties for clinical psychology at universities, and in health bureaus of the Chinese government. Qualification of trainers who can teach psychoanalytical understanding and treatment methods to new generations of clinicians. Perhaps most crucially, the psychoanalytic understanding of the therapist–patient relationship has proven to be of importance for any given psychiatric treatment. It has, for example, been shown that the efficacy of giving medication is highly dependent on the quality of the doctor–patient relationship. Here, an understanding of transference and countertransference is indispensable for giving medication in the right emotional atmosphere. The same holds true for any of the different treatments given in psychiatry. One could mention here that within other treatments paradigms, such as the cognitive–behavioural method, understanding of the importance of the unconscious interaction between therapist and patient—that is, transference and countertransference—has been increasingly valued. Psychoanalysis and therapies based on psychoanalysis are thus both forms of treatment and a way of understanding the relationship between therapists and patients. In this context it is pertinent to ask for evidence of whether it works, how it works, and what works for different patients. Research in China by Chinese researchers is therefore necessary. There is an increasing interest in research among psychoanalytically oriented Chinese colleagues. Psychoanalytic research projects, supervised by IPA colleagues, are already under way in China on trauma and therapeutic competence (Varvin, 2009). Training in China of future psychoanalysts who will establish psychoanalytic institutes and societies.


Thus, there have been considerable efforts during the last 27 years by IPA analysts to establish psychoanalytic psychotherapy in China on several levels:


to develop training in psychodynamic psychotherapy and supervision in psychodynamic psychotherapy; to aid the establishment of professional organisations; to help integrate psychoanalytic therapies and understanding in the care for the mentally ill; to help establish psychoanalytically relevant research; to establish psychoanalytic training with the aim of establishing psychoanalytic institutes for training in China.


This work is especially relevant as China is now working on mental health reforms that will include training in psychotherapy as part of the training of clinical psychologists and psychiatrists. IPA analysts Sverre Varvin and Alf Gerlach are consultants in this Chinese legal–administrative–educational process. In sum, the strength and vitality of current psychoanalytic activity in China is a testament to the nearly three decades of psychodynamic teaching, training, and clinical work carried out by many IPA and other psychoanalysts.

Bibliography Freud, S. (1921c). Group Psychology and the Analysis of the Ego. S. E. 18. Freud, S. (1925d [1924]). An Autobiographical Study. S. E. 20. Gerlach, A. (1995) China. In: P. 23Chile Psychoanalysis in Chile and the Chilean Psychoanalytic Association

Juan Pablo Jiménez

The history of psychoanalysis in Chile and of the Chilean Psychoanalytic Association (APCh) is well documented (Arrué, 1988, 1993; Davanzo, 1987; Fiorenzano, 1988; Ganzaraín, 1987; Gomberoff, 1990; Nuñez, 1981; Oyarzún, 1990; Prat, 1990; Whiting, 1980). It was Sigmund Freud himself who wrote about the first rapprochement between psychoanalysis and Chile. In 1911 (Freud, 1911g) he reviewed Dr Germán Greve’s paper “On Psychology and Psychotherapy of Some Anxiety States” (1910). Dr Greve [1869–1954] was a Chilean doctor who did his postgraduate studies in Germany in 1893. This paper, which was presented at an international congress in Buenos Aires in 1910, is the first reference to psychoanalysis in Latin America, and Freud considered it important enough to mention in his “On the History of the Psychoanalytic Movement” (1914d). However, Greve’s contact with psychoanalysis did not have a lasting effect. The spread of psychoanalysis in Chile did not actually begin until 1925, with the return of Fernando Allende Navarro [1890–1981], who had been trained in Europe as a doctor, psychiatrist, and psychoanalyst. In 1926, Allende Navarro ratified his M.D. licence with the dissertation, The Value of Psychoanalysis in the Polyclinic: Contribution to Chilean Psychology. This appears to be the first case of a psychoanalyst writing in Spanish. Allende Navarro was not involved in the academic sphere, restricting himself to the private practice of psychiatry and psychoanalysis. However, in that position he exerted significant influence, through his work as a psychoanalyst and training doctors who later would become major figures in Chilean psychiatry, most notably Carlos Nuñez Saavedra and Ignacio Matte Blanco [1908–95]. The career of the Jesuit priest Abdón Cifuentes [1878–1960], who became a professor of theology at the Catholic University, is especially noteworthy because it illustrates an unusually receptive attitude towards psychoanalysis among some sectors of the Catholic Church—an attitude that continues until today. After specializing in the study of psychiatry at the Maudsley Hospital in London, Ignacio Matte Blanco, now in his thirties, completed his psychoanalytic training at the Institute of the British Psychoanalytical Society. Upon his return to Chile in 1943, he gathered around himself an enthusiastic group of young psychiatrists, forming a Study Group, which was recognized on 17 August 1949 as a Member Society of the International Psychoanalytical Association. The first president of the newly constituted Chilean Psychoanalytic Association was none other than Fernando Allende Navarro. A few days earlier, on 3 August, Matte Blanco had been appointed to the Chair of Psychiatry at the Faculty of Medicine of the University of Chile. The period from 1949 to 1960 can be considered the golden age of psychoanalysis in Chile. It was firmly based at the university, with close ties to the field of psychiatry. Under the scientific leadership of Matte Blanco, several generations of psychiatrists, psychologists, and psychoanalysts were trained. Under the auspices of the Psychiatric Clinic of the University of Chile, initiated by its Director, Matte Blanco, a dynamic orientation towards psychiatry and psychology was established in the country. Among the psychiatrists and psychoanalysts trained during this period, the figure of Otto Kernberg stands out. At the same time, Nuñez Saavedra became Titular Professor of Psychiatry at the School of Medicine of the Catholic University, and Chile’s first two schools of psychology were founded at the National and the Catholic universities, each of which had a large number of psychoanalysts on its teaching staff. In 1960, after the Third Latin American Congress of Psychoanalysis, held in Santiago, internal conflicts began to appear within the Chilean Psychoanalytic Society. These eventually led to the resignation of a majority of the psychoanalysts from the programme directed by Matte Blanco and the establishment of a Psychoanalytic Institute independent of the university. In 1961, Otto Kernberg moved to the United States, the first of many analysts who emigrated during the 1960s. In 1967, Matte Blanco moved to Rome with his family, where he would write his most important works. He never returned to Chile. The Chilean Psychoanalytic Institute was thus deprived of its most active members, and it descended into stagnation, which lasted until the early 1980s. In a certain sense it can be said that the Chilean Psychoanalytic Association suffered the effects of the crisis that the country as a whole went through from the 1960s until the end of the 1980s. With the liberalization of higher education, begun during the military regime, the number of psychiatrists and psychologists seeking psychoanalytic training increased considerably. During the last years of the twentieth century, several institutes of psychoanalytic psychotherapy were born. The Association of Analytic Psychotherapists later evolved into the Chilean Society for Psychoanalysis (not a member of International Psychoanalytical Association, although it was formed by members who had left that body), and the Association of Group Analytic Psychotherapy. Also, the Lacanian movement appeared in the country. Interest began to be shown in spreading psychoanalysis to the provinces. All of these developments brought new challenges for the APCh. At the same time, within the APCh, a powerful movement for reform and openness developed, breaking with the previous stagnation. The statutes and bylaws were revised to be consistent with a more democratic atmosphere, and efforts were made to promote scientific activity. In 1999, five decades after the APCh was recognized by the IPA, the 41st IPA Congress was held in Santiago, attended by more than a thousand psychoanalysts from around the world. In the ensuing years the prestige of our Association grew, accompanied by a growing interest in psychoanalytic training. However, this reform movement ended after many of the changes proposed were approved by a majority of the members. In recent years a new insular tendency has developed, and the most progressive members have sought new horizons, especially in university-level work. Traditionally, one-third of the members and candidates have been involved in university teaching, following the tradition established by Matte Blanco. Today, there are signs that this number has been growing, due to the demand at the universities for professors capable of teaching psychoanalytic theory. In this process, the Association runs the risk of becoming dispersed into many sub-groups, each working in different universities, with a decrease of members involved in the organizational activities of the Association and of the Institute for Psychoanalysis. In fact, there are some graduate and postgraduate programmes in universities, such as MA, PhD, and post-doctoral programmes in psychoanalysis or in psychoanalytic psychotherapy, training psychoanalytic professionals outside the auspices of the APCh and the IPA. Historically, candidates trained in the Institute of Psychoanalysis have come from contact with psychoanalysts working in hospital or university settings; however, with the development of parallel training, there is a definite risk that the APCh may find itself without students. Therefore, the challenge that the APCh is now facing is to find ways to develop university-level activity without a deterioration in the psychoanalytic institution itself. Bibliography Allende Navarro, F. (1926).
