5Germany The history of psychoanalysis in Germany since 1950

Werner Bohleber


The way back to the psychoanalysis of Sigmund Freud The return to the psychoanalysis of Sigmund Freud by the group around Müller-Braunschweig was a long process of intellectual renewal and institutional reorganization. A more detailed understanding of this first requires a brief sketch of the intellectual world in which German analysts moved in the period between 1945 and the mid-1950s. The newly founded institutes in Berlin, Munich, and Stuttgart began by pursuing the programme at the “German Institute for Psychological Research and Psychotherapy”—namely, of grouping together the various schools in depth psychology under what was called synoptic psychotherapy. Once the constraints instituted by the National Socialist regime to amalgamate were no longer present, it became evident that in the years following the persecution of Jewish colleagues—a period during which German psychoanalysis was cut off from international developments—a clinical and theoretical reorientation had also been under way. The period in question witnessed a reassessment of the values inherent in German idealist thought and, more especially, those originating in the Romantic tradition. Moreover, after 1945 philosophical anthropology, which had at around this time become an important philosophical current within Germany, was to enter the arena.1 Many Freudian psychoanalysts found themselves concurring with the Romantic understanding of the unconscious as signifying the foundation of the self, whereby the superego was conceived as an instance of conscience [Gewissensinstanz] cautioning the human being not to neglect the self.2 By way of an example, I would like to cite three psychoanalysts who were to achieve positions of influence after 1945. Carl Müller-Braunschweig [1881–1958] sought to revitalise the work of Freud in Germany—above all, those aspects classified by others as “obsolete and of the past” (Müller-Braun-schweig, 1948). This effort was primarily directed at the neo-analysis of Schultz-Hencke. Müller-Braunschweig represented Sigmund Freud’s classic psychoanalytic conception, but he went on to supplement it by way of his anthropology of the human being as a self-consciously [geistig] committed entity (1947, 1954). In West Germany, Felix Schottlaender [1892–1958] was the sole IPA analyst after 1945. While opting not to rejoin the German Psychoanalytical Society (GPS) [Deutsche Psychoanalytische Gesellschaft (DPG)], he did renew his membership in the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society. Schottlaender was a protagonist of synoptic psychotherapy and cofounder of the Institute for Psychotherapy in Stuttgart. He elaborated his own conception of psychoanalysis, which he linked to existential philosophy and daseins-analysis. He managed to overcome the antitheses in the psychotherapeutic schools by means of the personalistic psychotherapy as developed by Ludwig Bins-wanger. During the war, Alexander Mitscherlich [1908–82]3 was assistant doctor at the Viktor von Weizsäcker Clinic in Heidelberg. Psychotherapeutically, Mitscherlich was an autodidact. After 1945, he advocated the programme of synoptic psychotherapy. His efforts at the time centred on formulating a psychotherapeutic anthropology. Like C. G. Jung, he drew a distinction between the ego and the self. What Mitscherlich valued so highly in Freud was that he had recognized the full significance of the phenomenon of transference and had made it the focal point of analytic methodology. Thus, the doctor was “introduced into therapy as a second subject” (Mitscherlich, 1948, p. 29), resulting in the abandonment of the old subject–object position of doctor as examiner and patient as object. Müller-Braunschweig, Schottlaender, and Mitscherlich, each in their own way, paradigmatically combined Freudian psychoanalysis with an anthropologically oriented depth psychology and an appreciation of C. G. Jung’s analytical psychology. Although Müller-Braunschweig, and later with increasing emphasis Mitscherlich, had each adopted Freud’s psychoanalysis as the foundation of their own thought, neither felt inclined at the time to abandon the positive achievements that had resulted from the collaboration with other psychotherapeutic directions. In spite of the lucidity with which Müller-Braunschweig determined Freud’s psychoanalytic research as representing the foundation of modern depth psychology, he nevertheless discovered in Jung’s comprehensive work “an abundance of valuable discoveries and aspects … that,—and to some extent already indicated in the approach of Freud’s works—may be easily incorporated into the organic, ongoing development of psychoanalytic research” (Müller-Braunschweig, 1949, V). This psychoanalytic-anthropo-therapeutic thought in the context of a depth psychology points towards the direction in which psychoanalysis had developed in Germany after its links had been severed from international psychoanalysis. It had evolved such that it had substantially shifted away from the direction taken by psychoanalysis in Vienna–Berlin prior to 1933, which was now centred in London and New York. It cannot be claimed that the entire development had simply led down a cul-de-sac, since there had indeed been several productive advances that were to be found in modern psychoanalytic development only decades later. Thus, in the 1950s, the term “encounter” was a central concept in philosophical anthropology—one that had, among others, already been introduced by Binswanger, Viktor von Weizsäcker, and others into psychotherapeutic theory formation, as first conceptualized in Martin Buber’s philosophy of dialogue as “I–Thou relationship”. All three psychoanalysts had extended the conception of transference to the concept of encounter, whereby Schottlaender (1952) was to give it central theoretical importance. Encounter, as an inter-subjective I–Thou relationship, went beyond transference, thereby facilitating within the therapeutic relationship the emergence of something new. And yet the speculative features this development acquired by way of an excessive philosophical–idealistic orientation are clearly evident. They also narrowed the clinical psychoanalytic basis of experience. Thus, above all else, the renewed encounter with international psychoanalysis introduced new advances not only in theory but in clinical practice as well—a fact impressively reflected in the autobiographies of the German analysts living at the time (Hermanns, 1992–97; Schröter, 1999). What course did this re-encounter with international psychoanalysis take?4 The IPA Congress in Zurich, in 1949, provided the first opportunity to establish contact once again with the association and its representatives. The acceptance of the German Psychoanalytical Association (GPA) [Deutsche Psychoanalytische Vereinigung (DPV)] as a Component Society of the International Psychoanalytical Association at the Amsterdam IPA Congress in 1951 forged connections at an institutional level. The Berlin GPA group, and the Berlin Institute of Psychoanalysis founded by them, dedicated itself first and foremost to the training of candidates, though it made less of an impact in the academic or public sphere. The founders of the GPA saw the organization’s chief task as the mediation of a genuine understanding for Freud’s work as a whole. The department of psychosomatics at the University of Heidelberg under the directorship of Alexander Mitscherlich and later, from 1960, the Sigmund Freud Institute evolved as a second centre of re-encounter and new orientation in German psychoanalysis. The publishing organ of this new direction was served by the journal Psyche, which Mitscherlich launched at the Ernst Klett Verlag in 1947, in collaboration with Felix Schottlaender and a Swiss anthropologist, Hans Kunz. Initially, the task they set themselves was to continue the “enriching encounter with the various schools of depth psychology” and to further develop an independent psychotherapeutic anthropology. Only gradually was it to develop into a journal of psychoanalysis.5 With funding provided by the Rockefeller Foundation, Mitscherlich succeeded in founding a department of psychosomatics at the University of Heidelberg in 1950. He rapidly integrated the works of Franz Alexander and his research group in Chicago, later going on to develop his psychosomatic theory of two-phase defence [zweiphasige Abwehr]. He aimed to affect a transformation of basic attitudes in medicine by integrating it with psychosomatics and depth-psychological medicine. An official observer of the West German Medical Association at the trials of doctors in Nuremberg, Mitscherlich subsequently became well known to a broader public following the publication of his report on the proceedings. As one of the few of his generation left untainted by any affiliations with National Socialism, Mitscherlich began to gain the trust of psychoanalysts living in emigration with whom he had established contact whom he later managed to persuade to return to Germany to hold lectures and seminars, and who had in some cases entrusted him with the publication of their work in Psyche. In 1951 Mitscherlich went on a four-month visit to the United States. He made the acquaintance of many important psychoanalysts there and was exposed to new impressions of training and clinical work. He considered ego psychology an integral development, though he remained critical of the form it took in America. He acknowledged the value of “strict analysis” and its training and practice. On his return to Europe, Mitscherlich became more and more an advocate of Freudian psychoanalysis and commenced work on his own version of ego psychology. He conceptualized a strengthened ego capable of critically withstanding social conformity and the old channelling of drives. It was in this context that the connection between psychoanalysis and critical social psychology began to take shape, which he then later went on to develop throughout the 1960s and 1970s. In the years that followed, Mitscherlich stepped up the number of publications of writings by authors of American ego psychology in Psyche, but also works from other psychoanalytic directions within Europe, among which were primarily writings by Michael Balint, Heinz Hart-mann, Jeanne Lampl-de Groot, Erik Erikson, Kurt Eissler, René Spitz, Melanie Klein, Helm Stierlin, and Paul Parin. Beginning with Erikson’s Growth and Crises of the Healthy Personality in 1953, on Mitscherlich’s recommendation Ernst Klett began publishing significant works in American ego psychology—those of Heinz Hartmann, Erik Erikson, René Spitz, David Rapaport, and Robert Waelder, among others. Almost all of Michael Balint’s works were also published. By way of these publishing activities and through the encounter with foreign psychoanalysts, it became possible in the years after 1955 to form a picture of the state of international psychoanalysis and to search for scientific connections. It was at this juncture that the isolation from psychoanalytic research around the world and the deficient development of local psychoanalysis became more acutely felt. Gerhard Scheunert (1956, 1959) was one of the chief critics of anthropological and synoptical psychotherapy. He succeeded Müller-Braunschweig—who had after 1954 developed an illness to which he finally succumbed in 1958—as President of the GPA. Inspired by Heinz Hartmann, Scheunert returned to a definition of the ego as an instance and substructure of the personality. This ego no longer bore any resemblance to the self of anthropological psychotherapy as a non-objectifiable core. The self–world relationship was no longer characterized existentially, and the ego became a specific organ in the assessment of reality and of the adaptation to the outside world. Ego psychology began to establish a foothold in German psychoanalysis. The self-understanding of ego psychology as an objective natural science aided a liberation from an otherwise diffuse terminology that permeated the German synoptic tradition. It was doubtless only by virtue of a clear break with this tradition that a further development in German psychoanalysis within an international context became possible. In the course of this development, Mitscherlich became increasingly estranged from Schottlaender, who, by contrast, proceeded in a direction away from ego psychology and towards daseins-analysis. Mitscherlich introduced strict standards of training into his department. He demanded that his assistants undergo a lengthy training analysis, to which end he sent them to Schottlaender, and to the Institute for Psychotherapy, in Stuttgart. Many of Mitscherlich’s assistants later became leading psychoanalysts and founders of psychoanalytic institutes in the Federal Republic. In 1955 relations soured between Mitscherlich and Schottlaender, and the latter consequently relinquished his function as training analyst for the department of psychosomatics as well as his editorial post at the journal Psyche. Mitscherlich, who had until then been a psychotherapeutic autodidact, applied for membership to the GPA and, after presentation of a case report at the Berlin Institute in 1956, was admitted as associate member on condition that he undergo his training analysis. Together with his wife, Margarete Mitscherlich-Nielsen, he spent a year in London in 1958. He took his training analysis with Paula Heimann; his wife took hers with Michael Balint. In an effort to re-establish Sigmund Freud’s psychoanalysis in Germany, Mitscherlich found key allies among Max Horkheimer and Theodor W. Adorno of the Frankfurt Institute of Social Research. In 1956, to mark the occasion of Freud’s 100th birthday, they organized an elaborate ceremony, which Theodor Heuss, then President of the Federal Republic of Germany, also attended. The aim was to revive a vital awareness of Freud and to encourage the breakthrough of psychoanalysis in the public sphere of post-war Germany. The success of the event was no less indebted to the foreign analysts who had taken up Mitscherlich’s invitation. Erik Erikson held the key lecture, and in the following weeks René Spitz, Michael Balint, Ludwig Binswanger, Gustav Bally, Frederick Wyatt, Franz Alexander, and Hans Zulliger also all gave lectures. A further step in the efforts to re-institute psychoanalysis in Germany was to make Freud’s works once again accessible within German-speaking countries. It was the S. Fischer-Verlag that was to assume responsibility for this task. In 1953 Gottfried Bermann Fischer had published the An Outline of Psychoanalysis and Civilization and Its Discontents as paperbacks. Additional editions followed in subsequent years. In 1960 the Fischer-Verlag took over the complete rights of the works of Sigmund Freud, along with the inventory of the Imago edition of the Complete Works [Gesammelte Werke]. Since the 1960s, the task of the historical–critical editing of the works of Freud at the Fischer-Verlag has been in the hands of Ilse Grubrich Simitis.6 The student edition [Studienausgabe], published between 1969 and 1975 and edited by Alexander Mitscherlich, Angela Richards, and James Strachey, was the first critical edition of Freud’s works to be published in the German language. In 1987, the appendix volume to the Gesammelte Werke was published with those of Freud’s as yet unpublished psychological–psychoanalytic writings.


The establishment and revival of psychoanalysis in the Federal Republic Whereas in 1956 the GPA numbered only 21 members, membership was to increase continually throughout the 1960s. By 1970, membership had swollen to 101. The “Hamburger Psychoanalytische Arbeitsgemeinschaft” [Hamburg Working Committee of Psychoanalysis] was founded in 1955; alongside Berlin, in 1959, it was to become the second training institute in Germany, with Gerhard Scheunert, Ulrich Ehebald, Wolf-Dietrich Grodzicki, and Hans-Jürgen Seeberger as leading members. Facing opposition from the faculties of medicine, though supported by the state of Hessen and especially State Premier August Georg Zinn, Mitscherlich succeeded in obtaining authorization for the founding of an “Institute and Training Centre for Psychoanalysis and Psychosomatic Medicine” in Frankfurt am Main. He managed to persuade politicians that the psychoanalysis of Sigmund Freud should return to Germany, an achievement that was to herald a new beginning. The Institute was funded entirely by the state of Hessen, a situation that amounted to nothing short of an extraordinary solution. Opening its doors in 1960, Mitscherlich established the institute strictly along the lines of Freudian analysis. What had been overlooked was the fact that until that time the Berlin Institute had been the central institution of the GPA. Like the clinic in Heidelberg, the institute in Frankfurt attracted foreign psychoanalysts who frequently attended lectures and seminars there. Jeanne Lamplde Groot, Piet Kuiper, Jaap van der Leuuw, from Holland, John Klauber, Willi Hoffer, Paula Heimann, and Michael Balint, were regular visitors from London. These, and many other analysts who visited from foreign countries, provided assistance in the form of “reconstruction”(Schröter, 1999), making it possible “to meet international standards in psychoanalysis within a relatively short period of time” (Loch, 1983, p. 343). The Institute drew many talented psychoanalysts as well as social psychologists. Above all through the person of Mitscherlich, the two succeeding decades witnessed extensive growth in training activity as well as a major output in clinical and social psychology that was to exert an enormous public impact. In 1964, the Institute moved into its own building and was renamed the “Sigmund Freud Institute”. From the 1950s through to the early 1970s, during the years of reform in the Federal Republic, Mitscherlich advanced to become one of the leading intellectuals in the country, a recognized social diagnostician and left–liberal champion of the young German democracy. He understood the work of psychoanalysis as the patient, albeit uncomfortable, work of enlightenment with respect to the repression that was exercised in connection with the National Socialist past in the form of the taboos, prejudices, and anti-democratic tendencies within German society. He was also the initiator of a psychoanalytic social psychology, which, during the 1960s and 1970s, was to have a far-reaching influence within the Federal Republic, also by way of its connection to the Critical Theory represented by the Frankfurt School of Social Research. In his work, above all in his book Auf dem Weg zur vaterlosen Gesellschaft [Towards a fatherless society] (1963), Mitscherlich sought to endorse the ego’s individual critical capacity in the conditions of a secularized mass society. In 1967 Alexander and Margarete Mitscherlich published their book Die Unfähigkeit zu trauern [The inability to mourn]. The work enjoyed enormous public success and, indeed, provided a plausible, comprehensive, and general explanation of the unresolved past in the debate within the Federal Republic. Aspects of the work were, however, later criticized, above all for the accusatory moral tone that permeated parts of the book. In 1961 Horst Eberhard Richter was appointed to the chair of psychosomatics and psychotherapy at the University of Gießen, where he built up an important centre for psychosomatics. He founded the local psychoanalytic institute there and succeeded in persuading Johannes Cremerius from Munich, among others, to come to Gießen. After having completed training at the Munich Institute of Psychological Research in the 1950s, Cremerius underwent a further course of psychoanalytic training in Zurich in 1960. He accepted the chair of psychosomatics and psychotherapy at the University of Freiburg in 1972. In addition to his work in psychosomatics, Richter achieved recognition for his development of a family therapeutic approach, for his work in social psychology as well as in social–political initiatives. He researched the influence of external factors on the psyche. For him, a psychoanalytic understanding of man was not possible without accounting for psycho-social conditions and the fate of the family in social processes. Together with Mitscherlich, Richter was one of the best-known psychoanalysts in Germany owing to his various positions and political engagement, as well as to his interventions in the public sphere. Due to a series of reform laws—family law, criminal law, and psychiatry, among others—and as a result of the investigation of National Socialism and its crimes, the Federal Republic underwent a process of modernization during the 1960s that, in turn, led to a fundamental liberalization of society. Political science, contemporary history, and a critical social theory brought about the preconditions for a transformation of identity within West Germany. It was in this connection that the Frankfurt School of Social Research around Max Horkheimer and Theodor Adorno was to play an influential role. They brought back the left-wing social theory dismissed in 1933—along with Freudian psychoanalysis as a means of the social enlightenment with which it was associated—into the context of West German discourse. All this led to the psychoanalysis of the 1970s achieving widespread recognition as a method of treatment and an approach to knowledge. Through the reform of medical studies, chairs in psychosomatics and psychotherapy were introduced into all university faculties of medicine, the majority of which were occupied by psychoanalysts. As early as 1967, psychoanalytically based methods of treatment had been introduced into statutory health insurance care for the general population, and in 1976 its indication was extended to all chronic neurotic disorders. Treatment via extended psychoanalytic psychotherapy, with high-frequency sessions (3–4 sessions per week) and with an upper limit of 240–300 sessions, financed by health insurance funds, was thus made possible. The introduction of psychoanalytic psychotherapy financed by health insurance funds was the subject of controversial discussions among psychoanalysts, especially since the possibility of admitting newly trained lay analysts to the health care system was dropped in 1976. This led to the virtual end of the training of lay analysts. From this point on, only medical doctors and psychologists could be admitted to analytic training. As reflected in the growing number of training candidates, it was through the student movement that psychoanalysis met with a great response among the younger generation. As early as 1980, the GPA had 230 members; this increased in the next decade to 611 members. Between 1967 and 1980, a series of further institutes were established in German cities and university towns: in Freiburg (1966), Ulm (1967), Stuttgart/Tübingen (1971), Munich (1973), Cologne (1974), Bremen (1974), Kassel (1978), and Heidelberg-Karlsruhe (1980). After the founding of these institutions, the GPA became more and more decentralized. Berlin had already lost its position as centre of the GPA by the 1960s.


A scientific zenith: from natural scientific ego psychology to hermeneutic psychoanalysis With respect to scientific method, new approaches to psychoanalysis began to establish themselves. This was facilitated by the foundation of scientific hermeneutics elaborated by Hans Georg Gadamer in his seminal work Wahrheit und Methode [Truth and method], published for the first time in 1960, as well as by the debate surrounding hermeneutics, which built upon hermeneutics and the critique of ideology in the social sciences as pursued by Jürgen Habermas and others. Gadamer’s leading idea centres on the dialogical character of understanding, which he explained phenomenologically as hermeneutic experience. This new understanding of hermeneutics prompted Wolfgang Loch to pursue theoretically investigations into the occurrence of transference and countertransference in the psychoanalytic process. Loch understood the psychoanalytic method as research aligned to the question of truth, which, precisely for this reason, exerts a therapeutic effect. For Loch, the use of the therapeutic relationship is on a par with interpretation as analytic method. Loch sought to synthesize the diverse theoretical approaches in psychoanalysis: ego psychology and theories of object relationships, explaining and understanding, as well as psychoanalysis as a natural science and as hermeneutics (Loch, 1965, 1972, 1975). Based on ego psychology, Hermann Argelander (1968, 1970) of the Sigmund Freud Institute worked out a psychoanalytic hermeneutics. Not looking for causal sources and motives concealed behind actions, he sought to comprehend the meaning of action. Argelander developed a special instrument of hermeneutic perception, namely, “scenic understanding”. Unconscious meaning does not represent itself solely in verbal exchange, but in the situation as a whole. Through this “situative information” the analyst is able to decode the unconscious fantasy patients represent in their interaction with him. It contains an infantile configuration, which, by means of the scenic function of the ego, is adapted to the present relationship between analyst and analysand. The scene is the mutually dynamic construction of analyst and patient—a joint creation, not just something of the past, which has to be discovered. In essence, scenic understanding involves a modern constructivist notion of transference, which was in advance of its time. In the mid-1960s there emerged within the social sciences a particularly inspiring and fruitful culture of discussion centring on a dispute that involved appropriate scientific method, scientific objectivity, and hermeneutic understanding. Along with the critical theory of Horkheimer and Adorno, psychoanalysis was also included in this dispute, more particularly in the form it took among social scientists and psychoanalysts at the Sigmund Freud Institute. Alfred Lorenzer was among the protagonists there. Unlike Argelander, he rejected ego psychology, since it falsifies the critical potential of psychoanalysis (Lorenzer, 1970, 1983). He sought to create a new meta-theory for psychoanalysis. Lorenzer developed a theory of the destruction of speech—something that occurs when the neurotic conflict is expelled and repressed from common speech such that it is only able to express itself in interactive scenes. It then reappears in the analytic relationship. Lorenzer (2002) later developed his approach into to a general theory of the forms of interaction. These are drive-bound interactive forms, scenically structured embodied conceptions of life, especially those originating in the early developmental phases, which language can only capture in part. They form a counter-system to the predominant consciousness of the linguistic community and to prevailing conditions. With the international pluralization of psychoanalysis in the 1970s, the various directions found their way to Germany, where the theory of object relations was to play a central role. Alongside interpretation and insight, the therapeutic relationship now gained more and more primacy over the technical rules of treatment. In addition to Wolfgang Loch, it was above all Johannes Cremerius and Helmut Thomä who, each in his own way, influenced and advanced clinical theory in Germany. All three were firmly rooted in the tradition of Michael Balint and the theory of object relations. Cremerius (1984) and Thomä (1981, 1983; Thomä & Kächele, 1985) had extended psychoanalytic thought on therapeutic relationship to include a dialectic and processing understanding of psychoanalytic technique. Both were concerned with liberating the analyst’s identification from the idealized classical technique of treatment. The rules of treatment were no longer an ideal instrument—they were embedded in the analytic configuration of the therapeutic relationship and directed at the aims of the analytic process. The therapeutic attitude of the analyst and his contribution to the creation of the therapeutic situation thus shifted to the focus of clinical theory. Both theoretically and clinically, each represented enormously fruitful approaches, and one might well characterize the period as the heyday of psychoanalysis in Germany. And yet, subsequent development shows that it did not unfold on a solid foundation, and that the resources drawn upon were inherently charged with conflict. Indications of this were already noticeable around the mid-1970s.


The return of the repressed: the debate on psychoanalysis during National Socialism Protracted upheaval in psychoanalysis in Germany erupted at the beginning of the 1980s following the investigation into National Socialism. It had already announced itself in 1977 at the IPA Congress in Jerusalem. In 1976, the GPA resolved to apply to hold the 1981 IPA Congress in Berlin, and it had been given the approval of the IPA Executive Council. However, at the IPA business meeting in Jerusalem, the application prompted fierce emotional reactions and rejection, making it clear to the German participants that for the Jewish colleagues the past was by no means past, and that the latter were not prepared to return to Germany—least of all to Berlin, the former centre of National Socialist extermination. Nevertheless, the GPA committee pursued its plans to hold the IPA Congress in Germany. By 1979, the IPA business meeting finally agreed to hold the 1985 Congress in Germany. The growing problem of the National Socialist past was then prised open at the Central European Psychoanalytic Societies Congress in Bamberg, in 1980. Affected by the events in Jerusalem, the Dutch psychoanalytic candidates at the Central European Congress in Bad Ischl, in 1978, proposed the “long-term effects of National Socialism and the Second World War in analytical practice and on the psychoanalytical movement” as the theme; this was reformulated by the GPA executive committee as “the return of war and persecution in psychoanalysis”—representing an attempt to focus on the clinical situation with patients and to avoid a discussion of the involvement of German psychoanalysts as a society during National Socialism. The discussion could, however, not be avoided, and vehement feelings of pain, anger, guilt, and shame flared up during the discussions at the Congress. It was no longer possible to maintain a stance of denial and concealment. Enlightenment was on the agenda, from which point on it comprised part of the programme of the GPA conferences. A short time later (1983) the journal Psyche republished an article written by Carl Müller-Braunschweig entitled “Psychoanalysis and World View”, previously published in a National Socialist gazette in 1933. This republication revealed the involvement of the founding father of the GPA in National Socialism. The notion of belonging to the GPA and association with a psychoanalytic tradition that had not been compromised by National Socialism thus revealed itself as an illusion no less than did the attempt to attribute to the GPS a National Socialist past by way of splitting. A group discussion consequently began in the GPA, and continued for years, about the involvement of German psychoanalysts in National Socialism: a painful enquiry into the question of collaboration as well as of guilt and shame. The investigation taught that in order to be able to recognize the suppressed and repressed problems and to be able to disclose defence strategies as well as projections and prejudices, feelings of guilt, and persecutory anxiety, group process, and reflection were required (Beland, 1988). In this connection, the exchange and confrontation with Jewish colleagues and their experiences proved necessary for the identification of blind spots and projections, as well as the awareness of unconscious identifications. The 1985 IPA Congress took place in Hamburg. Disputes about the main theme had already occurred in advance. The IPA programme committee was unable to decide as to whether to make its main focus National Socialism, and its crimes and consequences. Instead, “Identification and Its Vicissitudes” was opted for, limiting the theme of “Identification and the Nazi Phenomena” to one day—something that was to prove impossible, however. For many of the participants, the Congress was an important stage on the way to working through this horrible past, while still others felt disappointed that the questions had once again been denied. Some were critical of the fact that the Congress had barely left any room for the immediate experience and for the expression of feelings and reflections thereon (Moses & Moses-Hrushovski, 1986). In spite of the criticism, the international psychoanalytic community was to once again return to Germany, and the GPA was hence de facto, and not only formally, recognized as member. An essential contribution to this was the exhibition and documentary on the history of psychoanalysis in Germany, “Here Life Goes on in a Most Peculiar Way….” Psychoanalysis Before and After 1933 in Germany, as conceived and developed by a group of younger psychoanalysts and candidates—Karen Brecht, Volker Friedrich, Ludger Hermanns, Isidor Kaminer, Dierk Juelich, Regine Lockot (Brecht et al., 1985). This was later translated into English and shown in London, New York, and other major cities. The documentary signalled a first step towards historical research into the history of psychoanalysis under National Socialism, carried out in the period that followed by analysts of the GPA, but also of the GPS, as well as by historians, in a sustained manner.7 A further step in coming to terms with the past manifested itself in the setting up in 1990 of an Archive for the History of Psychoanalysis. The aim of the archive was to preserve and file the documents and literary remains of analysts. Furthermore, Alain de Mijolla founded the Association Internationale d’Histoire de la Psychanalyse at the Hamburg Congress.


Ruptures in the formation of identity among German psychoanalysts Traumatic experiences, memories and their defence, as well as the question of guilt and responsibility and its denial, set in motion a specific trans-generational dynamic in German society, which introduced to the concept of generation a specific meaning as category of memory and identification. In historical scholarship it proved to be a useful instrument since, with its aid, many of the after-effects of National Socialism can be explained with greater precision. In psychoanalysis, the concept of generation is more closely linked to the concept of trauma. The majority of leading psychoanalysts of the 1960s and 1970s belonged to a generation that had been recruited by the Wehrmacht as young soldiers during the Second World War, and who had been exposed to the cruelty of war, to injury and imprisonment. For members of this generation, born roughly between 1915 and 1925 [Loch in 1915; Brocher in 1917; Cremerius in 1918; Argelander in 1920; Ehebald and Thomä both in 1921; Richter in 1923], the new beginning after the total defeat of 1945 was perceived as a second chance. They personally identified with the construction of a new democratic society. In the early stages of their psychoanalytic careers they shared the view that here, at home, there was a great deal of catching up to do with respect to psychoanalysis, having been cut off from the developmental currents in international psychoanalysis. The generation of analysts who found their way to psychoanalysis during the 1950s felt that what had been conveyed to them, primarily by foreign colleagues, was something unique and highly valuable. By identifying with this valuable commodity and its idealization they accomplished something substantial for the reconstruction of psychoanalysis in Germany upon which the succeeding generation was able to build (cf. the autobiographies of psychoanalysts in Hermanns, 1992–97). But the majority of them also held the conviction that with the help of Mitscherlich and the foreign psychoanalysts, as well as their further training in psychoanalysis abroad (in London, the United States, Zurich, Amsterdam), they had brought back and represented an “undiminished and undiluted psychoanalysis” to Germany (Loch, 1986). To them it signified a “rebirth of psychoanalysis” (Loch, 1983) in the Federal Republic. They entered the universities during the 1960s and beginning of the 1970s and took up leading positions, founded institutes of psychoanalysis or else held key posts within such institutes, and trained the next generation of psychoanalysts. They had no relation to the psychoanalysis that had survived and been consequently compromised under National Socialism, and neither did they discuss it. This constellation of factors was something that was to weigh heavily on the subsequent generation of psychoanalysts and the formation of their psychoanalytic identity. The idealization of Freud’s genius and his psychoanalysis by the generation of teachers had an aspect of defence and was linked to a concealed sense of guilt fed by the expulsion of Freud and Jewish analysts, as well as by the Holocaust. The predominantly unconscious sense of guilt brought about the circumstance that these teachers transmitted Freud’s work in an idealized form characterized by idiosyncratic rigidity. Recognizing this rigidity was not always so easy, precisely because of this inclination to idealize. To the minds of successive generations, this connection induced a sense of self-doubt and self-devaluation in not being able to live up to the ideal (cf. also Jappe, 1995). While younger generations of analysts would not have uncritically identified with these inflexible, rigid attitudes but would have sought to liberate themselves from them, the hidden association with the sense of guilt of their teachers remained, for the most part, uncomprehended. During the 1980s, investigations by this young generation of analysts into the involvement of German society and their own parents under the National Socialist regime were carried over to the history of psychoanalysis. The repressed National Socialist past and its return in memory now overwhelmed the imagination of the young generation of psychoanalysts. The experience of disappointment attaching to the silence of their teachers with respect to the way in which psychoanalysis had been compromised under the National Socialist regime also encompassed what their analytic fathers had built up and achieved psychoanalytically. Their scientific achievements then fell victim to a lack of esteem and were largely forgotten. The psychoanalysis they had learned from their fathers was no longer the “undiminished” and “undiluted” psychoanalysis of Sigmund Freud but, rather, a teaching linked to the compromised psychoanalysis that had survived and had thus remained under the spell of National Socialism. With the republication of Müller-Braunschweig’s essay Psychoanalyse und Weltanschauung [Psychoanalysis and world view] in the journal Psyche in 1983, the illusion of GPA analysts being on the side the innocent, persecuted international psychoanalysis of Freud and of having had no involvement with the National Socialist legacy was broken. In the debates within the GPA on “psychoanalysis under Hitler: psychoanalysis today” one’s own motives for becoming a psychoanalyst were scrutinized and subsequently reflected upon—more specifically as to whether or not, with this choice of profession, one did in fact wish to abandon the legacy of one’s fathers and the “legacy of Hitler” by situating oneself in the tradition of the undistorted psychoanalysis created by Sigmund Freud. The significant growth of the GPA during the 1970s and 1980s was suspected of being subject to such a group illusion. There was a growing awareness within the GPA that there was still a great deal to make good and that the competence of German psychoanalysts had been diminished by a “collective burden of defence” [Abwehrbelastung] (Beland, 1986). The implications to be drawn from the situation were varied. Since the mid-1980s, psychoanalysis as developed by Melanie Klein and her students was to gain a foothold in the Federal Republic, earlier attempts during the 1950s having failed. One large group of GPA members affiliated themselves to this understanding of psychoanalysis. Furthermore, to many, the Kleinian concepts directed at the study of the destructive potential of the human being were more suited to understanding the grounds for the German people’s involvement in the criminal regime of National Socialism. Quite independently of this, contemporary German analysts are aware that the traumatic rupture of the Holocaust and the war continues to be a wound that has, until today, remained impossible to heal. A sense of guilt that has emerged as a result of this is conscious and has been the subject of reflection. Through this ongoing painful process of reflection it has also become evident that individuals’ identification with Freud and with psychoanalysis also brings them into contact with their deeply anchored identification with their own parents and with German history. One of the sites of this reflective process was the “Nazareth Conferences”, which took place for the first time in 1994 under the auspices of the IPA and the Israeli and German psychoanalytic societies (Erlich, Erlich-Ginor, & Beland, 2009). German psychoanalysis today is characterized by a reflected and, through history, ruptured self-consciousness. With its scientific achievements and high-level clinical standards, it is no longer obliged to conceal itself. The 45th IPA Congress took place in Berlin in 2007. The theme, “Remembering, Repeating and Working Through in Psychoanalysis and Culture Today” was charged with the memories and painful feelings among those participants associated with the expulsion of Jewish psychoanalysts from Berlin, and with the crimes of the Holocaust and its aftermath.


The conflict about high-frequency psychoanalysis in the public health care system In March 1993, the National Association of Statutory Health Insurance Physicians excluded from the catalogue of measures of SHI-physician care long-term psychoanalytic psychotherapy with four sessions a week as a form of psychoanalytically grounded method. One of the principal contentions was that the scientific proof of a specific indication and a more extensive therapeutic efficiency of this application had yet to be concluded. A protracted and at times heated dispute was sparked off, until the GPA executive committee finally reached a compromise in 1996. The focus of this controversy was not centred entirely on the necessary securing of high-frequency psychoanalytic treatment within the public health care system, but also on the self-understanding of the GPA itself. Many analysts perceived the dispute as an attempt to compel the GPA to act against their fundamental convictions and to review the essentials of psychoanalysis. The objective here was to remain steadfast and not to yield. The dispute took on the character of a generational conflict. Most of the analytic “fathers” of the generation that had come of age in the GPA had become leading psychoanalysts during the 1960s and 1970s and had fought for the recognition of psychoanalysis as a therapeutic method in the health care system of the Federal Republic. They now held influential positions as experts in the National Association of Statutory Health Insurance Physicians and perceived the GPA policy as a threat to their life achievements. Many GPA members, by contrast, interpreted their attitude in this dispute as representing a threat to their own psychoanalytic identity, and thus many of the “fathers”, along with the positions they held within the GPA, became isolated. In the controversy surrounding the specific indications and questions regarding the ongoing, substantial effectiveness of high-frequency long-term psychoanalysis, it became abundantly clear to many members of the GPA that much was yet to be achieved with respect to research. There was a growing awareness that it was not enough simply to demonstrate the effectiveness of psychoanalysis by means of case studies and clinical discussions: this must also be achieved by means of empirical research methods so as to hold up against other therapeutic methods and to provide proof of efficiency to the public. In 1997, the General Assembly of the GPA decided to carry out its own catamnesis study with the objective of determining more about the long-term efficacy of psychoanalytic treatment. To this end, all treatments carried out by its members between 1990 and 1993 were to be concluded. The survey, under the directorship of Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber, Bernhard Rüger, Ulrich Stuhr, and Manfred Beutel, continued until 2001 (Leuzinger-Bohleber, Stuhr, Rüger, & Beutel, 2003; Leuzinger-Bohleber et al., 2002) and was the first extra-clinical study to have been carried out by a psychoanalytic society as a whole.


The establishment of psychoanalysis in the East German states The Berlin Wall came down in 1989. Set in motion by Gorbachov’s political reforms, an immanent political and social process of transformation was initiated in Eastern Europe and the GDR. Within the EPF and IPA, the GPA participated in the mediation of psychoanalysis in the East European states and in the formation of psychoanalytic groups within these regions. East European doctors and psychologists likewise visited psychoanalytic institutes in the Federal Republic for training purposes. In the GDR, Stalinism had brought to an end the renewed attempt to forge links with psychoanalysis after the destruction wrought by National Socialism. At the Pavlov Congress in Leipzig, in 1953, the Pavlovian doctrine was introduced as a dogma to medicine and psychotherapy. Freud was replaced by Pavlov, and his writings were indexed. Those psychoanalysts living in the GDR, and members of the IPA Walter Hollitscher and Alexander Mette, as well as Dietfried Müller-Hegemann, who was not an IPA member but was close to the neo-analytic group around Schultz-Hencke, completed the transition and thereby excoriated Freud’s psychoanalysis.8 It was considered “an antihuman expression of imperialism” (Hollitscher), and one must “rank it as the ideology of Imperialism” (Mette). All three analysts were Communist Party members, and their Communist anti-fascism and party loyalties took precedence over their analytic convictions, which they abandoned. By the end of the 1970s, and with increasing intensity during the 1980s, a new interest in psychoanalysis began to assert itself among psychotherapists within the GDR (Geyer, 1992). After the fall of the Berlin Wall and the reunification of the two German States, many of them sought contact with the West German psychoanalytic societies. The GPA opened its congresses for them and organized East–West conferences and supervision groups. East German psychotherapists came to the West for psychoanalytic training. Through the creation of transitional regulations, those therapists from the East who fulfilled the criteria were able to obtain GPA membership. GPA working groups emerged in Leipzig and in Rostock/Greifswald. In 2008, the Leipzig working group of the GPA was recognized as its 14th institute. With the emigration of Therese Benedek from Leipzig to Chicago, in 1936, the erstwhile “Leipzig Working Group of the DPG” met its end. Through its institute, the GPA re-established links to the psychoanalytic tradition in Leipzig that had been destroyed by National Socialism.


The acceptance of the DPG into the IPA In 1951, once the DPG had been excluded from international psychoanalysis by the acceptance of the GPA into the IPA, it was above all the neo-psychoanalysis of Schultz-Hencke that was to further develop among its ranks. However, it thus discovered itself in a differently oriented national and international psychoanalytic environment. The ongoing advancement of psychoanalysis within the IPA societies exerted an increasing influence such that a “conceptual dualism in the self-understanding of the GPS” (Wellendorf, 2007, p. 405) began to take shape. And yet, the resulting self-understanding—in contrast to a GPA classified as an “orthodoxy”—of being a “liberal” society, “undogmatic” and open to many psychoanalytic theoretical traditions, did not last long (Schröter, 2006). Since the 1970s, younger members have increasingly come to regret having been cut off from international exchange with the IPA analysts. They took up concepts of a more evolved international psychoanalysis that had been the subject of discussions within the GPS. An increasing number of members began visiting British, French, and North American analysts for supervision. Clinical seminars with foreign analysts were organized. At the end of the 1980s the investigation of the history of psychoanalysis under National Socialism and the persecution of the Jewish psychoanalysts was carried out within the GPS. All this was to prompt ongoing processes of change. Official talks at committee level began with the GPA that also included possible acceptance into the IPA. Whereas in 1992 the proposal to establish contact with the IPA had still been rejected by the GPS in the wake of controversial debates, it was accepted in 1998 by its General Assembly with the objective of being accepted into the IPA. Negotiations extending over several years finally led to an agreement with which the GPA General Assembly was able to concur in the spring of 2001. At the 2001 IPA business meeting in Nice, the GPS proposal to be accepted as (Executive Council) Board Provisional Society was debated and approved. In the years that followed, the continuing development and reconstruction of the GPS into an IPA Society was steered by a Facilitating Committee, which, under the directorship of Anne-Marie Sandler, comprised five IPA members and five GPS–IPA members. The exchange and reciprocal recognition between GPA and GPS had made progress at several levels, to which the annual clinical-casuistic conference made a considerable contribution. The IPA accepted the GPS as a Component Society at its 46th Congress in Chicago, in 2009.

Notes Translated by Justin Morris. 1. Philosophical anthropology moved away from metaphysics and the philosophy of history and turned towards the life-world of the human being in an attempt to understand it through its position in nature. Results in biological, medical, psychological, and cultural scientific research are taken up in philosophical thought, though without recourse to the scientific objectification of human nature. 2. For a more detailed discussion, I refer the reader to my publications (Bohleber, 1989, 1995, 2003). 3. Within the last three years three biographies have been published on Mitscherlich (Dehli, 2007; Freimüller, 2007; Hoyer, 2008; cf. Bohleber, 2009). 4. See also Eickhoff, 1995; Hermanns, 2001; Kutter, 1992. 5. For a more detailed account, see Bohleber, 2009. 6. On the editorial history of Freud’s writings see Grubrich-Simitis, 1993. 7. The dissertation Psyche and Swastika by the American historian Geoffrey Cocks had already been published in 1983, which in the 1985 reviewed edition was published under the title Psychotherapy in the Third Reich: The Göring Institute. In 1985 Regine Lockot published Erinnern und Durcharbeiten. Zur Geschichte der Psychoanalyse und Psychotherapie im Nationalsozialismus, and in 1994, Die Reinigung der Psychoanalyse. 8. Here I draw on Bernhardt, 2000. Bibliography Argelander, H. (1968). Der psychoanalytische Dialog. Psyche. Zeitschrift für Psychoanalyse und ihre Anwendungen, 22: 325–339. Argelander, H. (1970). Gießen: Psychosozial. Bohleber, W. (1989). Psychoanalyse, romantische Naturphilosophie und deutsches idealistisches Denken. Psyche. Zeitschrift für Psychoanalyse und ihre Anwendungen, 43: 506–521. 100 Years of the IPA The Centenary History of the International Psychoanalytical Association 1910–2010 Evolution and Change Edited by Peter Loewenberg & Nellie L. Thompson Foreword byCharles Hanly











22Brazil History of psychoanalysis in Brazil

Regina Lúcia Braga Mota

The way in which the introduction of psychoanalysis was accomplished in Brazil can be traced back to the different physical and cultural traits of the peoples who contributed to the formation of the modern Brazilian society. A country over 8,000,000 km2 in size, with a population of around 180 million inhabitants, Brazil encompasses contrasting cultural and ethnical diversity as well as profound socioeconomic differences. It was initially populated by indigenous peoples, then colonized by the Portuguese after their official discovery in 1500 and ruled by them until Independence in 1822. In the centuries following the discovery, this mixture of indigenous peoples, Portuguese, Africans—brought to the country as slaves in the sixteenth century and emancipated only in 1888—also absorbed invasions by the Spanish, Dutch, and French. The sensuality of the Africans, the nudity of the indigenous people through the natural exposure of their bodies, sexually attracted the Europeans, resulting in master–servant intimacies and favouring, due to the tropical climate, a freer sexuality than found in European cultures. New groups of immigrants arrived in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, including Germans, Italians, Japanese, Arabs, Russians, Armenians, and Poles, especially in the south and southeastern regions. Instead of becoming a problem, the interracial marriages and the religious syncretism facilitated a harmonization of differences and the adoption of unlike cultures and prepared the ground for the future acceptance of Freud’s ideas on sexuality and of psychoanalysis. The history of the psychoanalytic movement in Brazil can be divided into three phases: the first begins in 1899 with a few Brazilians, notably Professor Juliano Moreira, an Afro–Brazilian from the state of Bahia, who read, studied, and wrote about the works of Freud. In the Medicine School of São Paulo, Dr Franco da Rocha had, since 1918, been teaching courses on psychoanalysis, based on Freud’s works. Such studies were intensified with the publication, in 1919, of the first translations of Sigmund Freud and the interest of important psychiatrists and intellectuals in the cities of São Paulo, Rio de Janeiro, and Porto Alegre. The second phase started in 1937 with the arrival, from Germany, of Adelheid Lucy Koch, who began to train psychoanalysts in São Paulo, according to the standards established by the International Psychoanalytical Association. At that time, the efforts of the pioneers were dedicated to the difficult task of establishing a training system, building sound institutions, and solidifying the presence of psychoanalysts in Brazilian society. Psychoanalytic ideas were introduced into the universities in the late 1940s, by three professors of psychiatry: Celestino Prunes in Porto Alegre, Durval Marcondes in São Paulo, and Danilo Perestrello in Rio de Janeiro. In the 1950s and 1960s the third phase began with the recognition, in 1951, of the Brazilian Psychoanalytic Society of São Paulo [Sociedade Brasileira de Psicanálise de São Paulo], followed, in 1955, by recognition of the Rio de Janeiro Psychoanalytic Society [Sociedade Psicanalítica do Rio de Janeiro], in 1959 of the Brazilian Psychoanalytic Society of Rio de Janeiro [Sociedade Brasileira de Psicanálise de Rio de Janeiro], and in 1963 of the Porto Alegre Psychoanalytical Society [Sociedade Psicanalítica de Porto Alegre]. The establishment of these societies marked the full institutionalization and development of psychoanalysis in Brazil and was accompanied by contributions to psychoanalytic theory and practice and an active participation in psychoanalytic congresses. There was also an intense interface with Brazilian cultural life, which inevitably transformed the scientific and institutional life of psychoanalysis. Freudian influence within the cultural movement of Brazilian Modernism gained visibility during the “Modern Art Week” of 1922 and through the modernist magazines. Freud frequently surfaces in the works of the principals of the movement, such as the writers Mario de Andrade and Oswald de Andrade, as well as the painters Tarsila do Amaral, Ismael Nery, Cícero Dias, and Flávio de Carvalho. The interfaces between psychoanalysis and our Modernism were deep and clear. Freudian thinking reached the Brazilian intelligentsia in the first decade of the twentieth century and spread among writers, thinkers, poets, and artists, supplying them with the basis for their ideological–aesthetic revolution. Because Freud’s ideas were libertarian, they were quickly assimilated. Psychoanalysis became popular in Brazil with the help of the modernist movement. In 1927, Durval Marcondes, whose efforts were pivotal in bringing psychoanalysis to Brazil, had founded the Brazilian Psychoanalytic Society [Sociedade Brasileira de Psicanálise] in São Paulo; he later founded a group in Rio de Janeiro. These first two associations, although acknowledged as provisional by the IPA in 1929, did not last long. In late 1929, the first edition of the Brazilian Psychoanalytic Journal was released in São Paulo. It was read and praised in a handwritten letter by Freud. But it was not until 1967 that the Revista Brasileira de Psicanálise [The Brazilian Psychoanalytic Journal] began to be published on a regular basis. It remains the most representative publication of psychoanalysis and the IPA in Brazil. The history of psychoanalysis in Brazil is described here beginning with the first associations created in the States of São Paulo, Rio de Janeiro, and Rio Grande do Sul. The first training analysis in Latin America took place in São Paulo, in October 1937, after Adelheid Lucy Koch was authorized by Ernest Jones, then President of the IPA, during the Marienbad Congress of 1936, to work as training analyst in Brazil. She was a training analyst from the Berliner Psychoanalytisches Institut (BPI) and had been analysed by Otto Fenichel. Her first patients were Virgínia Bicudo, Flavio Dias, Durval Marcondes, and Darcy Uchoa, followed by Lygia Amaral, Frank Phillips, Henrique Mendes, Isaias Melshon, Laertes Ferrão, and Judith Andreucci. The São Paulo Psychoanalytic Group was created at this time. In 1945, the São Paulo Study Group received permission from Ernest Jones to become a Provisional Society; it became a Component Society of the IPA in 1951, at the Amsterdam Congress. Many training analysts who had trained in São Paulo, along with analysts trained in other pioneer societies, later moved to other regions of the country and established psychoanalytic groups that eventually became new societies accredited by the IPA. The city of Rio de Janeiro currently hosts four societies accredited by the IPA. In 1944 the Juliano Moreira Study Center was founded in Rio by a group of psychiatrists, scholars, and enthusiasts of Freud’s ideas. It did not manage, however, to attract training analysts from Europe, especially not Jews, who wanted to escape Nazism but did not wish to settle in a country that was under a Fascist-inspired dictatorship. Thus, some members of the group—Alcyon Bahia in 1945, Danilo and Marialzira Perestrello in 1946, and Walderedo Ismael de Oliveira in 1947—went to Buenos Aires and became candidates of the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association [Asociación Psicoanalítica Argentina (APA)]. While these four doctors were in Argentina, a group started the Brazilian Institute of Psychoanalysis [Instituto Brasileiro de Psicanálise] with the aim of bringing training analysts to Rio. In February 1948, the English psychiatrist Mark Burke arrived in Rio, followed in December 1948, by the German analyst Werner Kemper, both sent by Ernest Jones, then President of the IPA. At first there was cooperation between the foreign analysts. In 1951, however, a schism developed, and Kemper left the Institute and founded the Psychoanalytic Study Center [Centro de Estudos Psicanalíticos]. Kemper’s group, whose analyses were carried out by analysts from São Paulo, was recognized as an IPA Study Group in 1953; in 1955 it became a Component Society of the IPA, the Rio de Janeiro Psychoanalytic Society [Sociedade Psicanalitica de Rio Janeiro (SPRJ–Rio 1)], during the Geneva Congress. Clear distinctions could be perceived among the three groups: the “Burke group”, the “Kemper group”, and the “Argentinian group”, the latter composed of the doctors who had qualified in Buenos Aires and had returned to Brazil in 1949 and 1950. After Burke left Brazil in 1953, some analysands concluded their studies in the Brazilian Psychoanalytic Society of São Paulo (SBPSP), while others went to the British Society. In the 1950s, Décio de Souza and Henrique Mendes, members of the British Society, came to Brazil to work. These two analysts and the “Burkeans” who returned from London were called “the British”. In 1957, the “Burkeans”, “Argentinians”, and “British” came together and were recognized as a Study Group by the IPA. In 1959 this Study Group became the Brazilian Psychoanalytic Society of Rio de Janeiro [Sociedade Brasileira de Psicanálise do Rio de Janeiro (SBPRJ–Rio 2)]. More recently, in 1997, the APRio 3 [Rio 3 Psychoanalytic Association] was created when a Study Group made up of members of the SBPRJ was recognized by the IPA, becoming a Component Society in 2005 at the Rio de Janeiro Congress. Another group—APERJ–Rio 4 (Rio de Janeiro Psychoanalytic Association–Rio 4)—was recognized as a Provisional Society by the IPA’s Council in 2002. It is a continuation of Pro-Ethics [Pró-ética], a group that broke with SPRJ–Rio 1 because of a disagreement concerning the resolution of ethical problems involving a candidate, which occurred during the military dictatorship in Brazil. This painful situation festered for 25 years (from 1973 until 1988), caused several IPA site visits to Rio 1, and prevented the IPA Congress from being held in Rio de Janeiro in 1983, as had been planned. The affair eventually came to a conclusion through the labours of the Ad Hoc Advisory Committee to Rio 1, set up by IPA’s Executive Council and made up of Latin-Americans. In a declaration published in the 2001 IPA Newsletter (Vol. 10, No. 1, pp. 14–15), the committee concluded that it found no crimes or ethical offences in Rio 1, and that it would be possible for those Rio 1 members who wished to remain as IPA members. It also concluded that Rio 1 should proceed with the reforms already started. It further suggested that the Pro-Ethics group should apply to the Executive Council for IPA membership, thus enabling it to become an independent organization. This happened eventually, as reported above. In the same newsletter, Rio 1 declared that its new managing board supported and respected the IPA code of ethics, reaffirming its position against all forms of dictatorship, authoritarian regimes, racism, and any form of attack against legitimate democratic states. It further recognized that it had paid a very high price for the difficulties in the past and that it considered itself an ethical society, able to proceed with its activities. Currently, a number of members of these four societies are professors of medicine and psychology in universities in the state of Rio de Janeiro. In the 1950s, Freudian ideas flourished in Porto Alegre, the capital city of the state of Rio Grande do Sul, and facilitated the development of psychoanalytic ideas by psychiatrists and medical professors. Mário Álvarez Martins, after his analytic training in Buenos Aires, where he was analysed by Ángel Garma, returned to Porto Alegre in 1947 with his wife Zaira Martins, a child analyst, and started the psychoanalytic movement in the state of Rio Grande do Sul. Encouraged by his fellow Argentinian analysts Mário Martins, Cyro Mar-tins, and José Lemmertz—trained in Buenos Aires—and Celestino Prunes, trained in Rio de Janeiro, they founded the Porto Alegre Psychoanalytical Society [Sociedade Psicoanalítica de Porto Alegre (SPPA)], accredited by the IPA in 1963. In 1989, a group of psychoanalysts belonging to the APA, APdeBA, SPPA, SBPSP, and SBPRJ initiated a movement for the creation of a new psychoanalytic society in the city of Porto Alegre. A Study Group was accredited by the IPA in January 1992, becoming a Provisional Society in 1997. In 2001, during the Nice Congress, the Brazilian Psychoanalytical Society of Porto Alegre [Sociedade Brasileira de Psicanálise de Porto Alegre (SBPdePA)] was accredited as an IPA Component Society. Since May 1987 the city of Pelotas, 258 km from the capital of the state of Rio Grande do Sul, has had a Nucleus sponsored by the Brazilian Psychoanalytic Society of Rio de Janeiro. The Nucleus was in fact a branch of the Institute, initially composed of psychoanalysts from the APA and SBPRJ. The first psychoanalytic institution in Brazil to be implemented outside a capital city, the Pelotas Psychoanalytic Society [Sociedade Psicanalítica de Pelotas (SPPel)], was accredited as a Component Society of the IPA in March 2004, during the New Orleans Congress. Thus the first four Brazilian psychoanalytic societies accredited by the IPA—the Brazilian Psychoanalytic Society of São Paulo [Socie-dade Brasileira de Psicanálise de São Paulo (SBPSP)], the Rio de Janeiro Psychoanalytic Society [Sociedade Psicanalítica do Rio de Janeiro (SPRJ)], the Brazilian Psychoanalytic Society of Rio de Janeiro [Sociedade Brasileira de Psicanálise do Rio de Janeiro (SBPRJ)] and the Porto Alegre Psychoanalytical Society [Sociedade Psicanalítica de Porto Alegre (SPPA)]—with the assistance and supervision of other societies, led to the creation of other Nuclei and Study Groups accredited by the IPA. In the early 1970s, excited by the 1960 inauguration of Brasilia, the new National Capital, a city built in the thinly populated Brazilian central plateau, Virgínia Leone Bicudo, having been invited to teach at the university there, moved from São Paulo to Brasília, a distance of 1,200 km. Here she had the initiative to begin the training analyses of six psychiatrists. It was the beginning of the Brasília Branch of the Psychoanalysis Institute of the SBPSP, which, infused with the pioneer spirit of the time, began to host professors and supervisors from São Paulo. The experience served as a model for beginning new nuclei to bring psychoanalysis to other distant locations. Accredited as a Study Group by the IPA in 1994, the Provisional Society (1999) became a Component Society at the New Orleans Congress in 2004. In 1975, the Rio de Janeiro Psychoanalytic Society and the Brazilian Psychoanalytic Society of Rio de Janeiro together formed a training subcommittee to bring psychoanalytic training to north-eastern Brazil, creating a Psychoanalysis Nucleus in the city of Recife, where two training analysts, Drs José Lins and Lenise Sales, had already been practising. The Nucleus was accredited as an IPA Study Group in 1988. With the qualification of new training analysts and intense scientific exchange, mainly with renowned French analysts, candidates from four states in the north-eastern region went to Recife to start their training. The Study Group finally became an IPA Component Society in 1999 during the Santiago Congress. Still in the 1970s, in Ribeirão Preto, a city in the state of São Paulo with a high level of economic and cultural development and a centre of academic excellence, some psychoanalysts qualified by SBPSP and one qualified by SBPRJ started a Nucleus that became a Study Group in 1993, becoming an IPA Component Society in 2005 at the Rio de Janeiro Congress. But the seed of psychoanalysis had been planted earlier in Ribeirão Preto with the foundation, in the 1950s, of the University of São Paulo Medical School, where from 1957 to 1965 the Psychoanalysis and Medical Psychology Department was headed by the Chilean psychoanalyst Hérnan Davanzo Corte. Dr Davanzo, of the Chilean Psychoanalytic Society [Associación Psicoanalítica Chilena], became a member of the Sponsoring Committee, nominated in 1993, to supervise the then Study Group. The psychoanalytic movement in the state of Mato Grosso do Sul began in 1971, in the medical and psychology colleges, with the arrival in Campo Grande of the SPRJ psychiatrist and psychoanalyst, Marila Teodorowic, and the initiative of Dr Fábio Leite Lobo, also of the SPRJ, who moved to the city in 1980. In 1989, with another two training analysts, they started psychoanalytic training in Campo Grande through the Rio de Janeiro Psychoanalytic Society. In 1997 they became a Study Group, and finally in 2007, at the IPA Congress in Berlin, the Mato Grosso Psychoanalytic Society became an IPA Component Society, the newest Brazilian society accredited by the IPA. Throughout Brazil this framework, the partnerships of societies and Nuclei, continues to generate a seemingly infinite number of new psychoanalytic societies. This success is due, however, to the efforts and determination of many of our members. Although past and current growth suggests that the future of psychoanalysis in Brazil is healthy, concerns with the quality of training and recognition of the need to improve and update our societies has led to the creation of an institution that could gather and organize the scattered nuclei. In 1967, the Brazilian Psychoanalytic Association (ABP)—since 2008 called Federação Brasileira de Psicanálise (FEBRAPSI [Brazilian Federation of Psychoanalysis])—was founded with the aim of gathering societies and Study Groups in Brazil accredited by the IPA, providing a forum, domestically and internationally, for scientific exchange and discussion of psychoanalytic topics. In 2009, the FEBRAPSI gathered 12 federated societies: four in the city of Rio de Janeiro, three in the state of Rio Grande do Sul (two in the city of Porto Alegre, and one in the city of Pelotas) and two in the state of São Paulo (one in the capital and one in the city of Ribeirão Preto), as well as the societies in Recife, Brasília, and Mato Grosso do Sul. It also gathered 10 Nuclei accredited to the FEBRAPSI (to become IPA Study Groups in the future): Curitiba, Marília, Campinas (sponsored by SBPSP); Florianópolis (SBPdePA); Santa Catarina (SPPA); Natal, Maceió, and Aracaju (SPR); and Espírito Santo and Salvador (SBPRJ). In July 2009, at the Chicago Congress, the Psychoanalytic Nucleus of Belo Horizonte (NPBH), initially sponsored by SPRJ, and the Fortaleza Nucleus, sponsored by SPR, became Study Groups of the IPA. And in 2010 the Nucleus of Goiânia (SPB) also became a Study Group. Together, these Brazilian societies, Nuclei, and Study Groups currently (2010) have approximately 1,200 members and 600 candidates in training. The official publication of the FEBRAPSI is the Revista Brasileira de Psicanálise [Brazilian Journal of Psychoanalysis], which has been publishing quarterly, since 1967, articles by Brazilian and foreign psychoanalysts and thinkers on contemporary issues in psychoanalysis. The journal is distributed to all members of the FEBRAPSI and to societies accredited by the IPA and enjoys a significant number of subscribers, nationally and internationally. Sigmund Freud is the most important authority in Brazilian societies. Melanie Klein remains very influential in São Paulo, Porto Alegre, and Rio de Janeiro. Wilfred Bion is currently one of the most studied authors in São Paulo and Porto Alegre. In Recife French psychoanalytic authors are influential. More recently, the study of post-Kleinian, Freudian, and Independent British authors has had a strong influence. Hanna Segal, Betty Joseph, John Steiner, Joseph Sandler, Peter Fonagy, Christopher Bollas, Donald Winnicott, and Heinz Kohut’s self psychology have also been of great interest. The French tradition, especially the works of Lacan, André Green, Jean Laplanche, Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel, and Joyce McDougall, is also part of our seminars, as are some American authors, such as Merton Gill, Harold Blum, Otto Kernberg, and Robert Wallerstein. The work of Sándor Ferenczi and W. R. D. Fairbairn have also been studied. It is worth mentioning important links with Argentinian authors, especially Heinrich Racker, David Liberman, Madeleine and Willy Baranger, as well as Italians Armando Ferrari and Antonino Ferro. The introduction of the study of Arminda Aberastury’s ideas on how to treat children via psychoanalysis has also been relevant. Brazilian psychoanalytic institutions are characterized by an intense and active cultural life. Beyond their own walls, there is an ongoing dialogue with universities, where research and doctorates in psychoanalysis have been attracting a number of students. Most of our societies have set up committees of community and culture to promote debates, aimed at the laymen, on such topics as psychoanalysis vis-à-vis cinema, theatre, literature, arts, philosophy, and sociology. Often the speakers and debaters at such events are renowned intellectuals and thinkers. The exhibition “Freud: Conflict and Culture”, organized by the Library of Congress in Washington, was displayed at the Museum of Modern Art of São Paulo (MASP) in October of 2001, together with one of Brazilian origin, named “Psychoanalysis and Modernism”. This latter exhibition reviewed the introduction of Freud’s ideas in Brazil through poets and artists in the 1920s and 1930s. It showed the history of the thoughts of Sigmund Freud and the influence of his work on Brazilian society in the first half of the twentieth century, especially in the modernist movement and in the ideas of some of its participants. Panels, films, and debates showed the historical events and movements that paved the way to Freudian ideas and promoted early converging paths between psychoanalysis, literature, and art. The tandem exhibitions were displayed at the Museum of Modern Art of Rio de Janeiro (MAM) in January of 2002 and at the Santander Cultural Center in Porto Alegre in April–May of the same year. In July 2005, Rio de Janeiro hosted the first International Congress of the IPA to be held in Brazil, at which the first Brazilian analyst to be elected President of the IPA, Cláudio Laks Eizirik, took office. The three models of training used in most IPA societies are currently the subject of ongoing discussion. Among key topics being debated is the controversial issue of the regulation of psychoanalysis as a profession by the government authorities. The main challenges facing psychoanalysts in Brazil are to give visibility to the relevance of psychoanalysis as an effective treatment and to maintain high standards for applicants for training in the face of economic constraints and competition from an increasing number of Lacanian and psychotherapy groups with less strict standards and cheaper training than that offered by IPA affiliates. Bibliography Eizirik, C. L. (2004). Psicanálise e psiquiatria no Brasil. Convergências e divergências. Mimeo, Paris. Eizirik, C. L., & Armesto, M. S. (2005). Psychoanalysis in Latin America. In: E. Person, A. Cooper, & G. Gabbard (Eds.), Textbook of Pychoanalysis. Arlington, VA: American Psychiatric Publishing. Gus, M. (2004). Psicoanalistas y psiquiatras, una vasta y compleja relación: Panel Historia del Psicoanálisis. Paper presented at the Asociación Latinoamericana de Historia del Psicoanalisis XXV Fepal Congress, Mexico. Haudenschild, T. R. L. (2007). Adelheid Koch: From Berlin to São Paulo in 1937 Poster presentation at the 45th IPA Congress, Berlin, July. Perestrello, M. (1992). A psicanálise no Brasil. In: Encontros &. Rio de Janeiro: Imago Ed. Roudinesco, E., & Pons, M. (1998). Dicionário de psicanálise. Rio de Janeiro: Jorge Zahar Ed. Wallerstein, R. S. (2000). The Lobo-Cabernite affair. Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, 48: 1030–1036.







Part IVAsia & Oceania







49The IPA administration from 2005 to 2009

Cláudio Laks Eizirik

From July 2005, the Rio Congress, until July 2009, the Chicago Congress, I had the honour and the responsibility of presiding over the International Psychoanalytical Association. Everything that can be achieved during each administration is necessarily the result of the joint work of several different groups, at different levels in the organization. In this chapter I will try to convey to the reader my view of the main trends, difficulties, challenges, and achievements of that period, highlighting the participation of the main committees and colleagues involved in different areas. Monica Siedmann de Armesto, the Secretary General during my term, and I began to work jointly in 2001, when we were Vice-presidents of the IPA; we intensified our partnership in 2003, when we were elected. We had previously, during the administration of Otto Kernberg and Robert Tyson, had the experience of taking part in the Executive Council, and this was a unique opportunity to see and participate in many innovations in the IPA. During the administration of Daniel Widlöcher, mainly in its two final years, our activities had increased, along with our responsibilities. We had already consulted with many friends and colleagues, with whom we shared our aim of increasing participation and introducing changes in the way the IPA was perceived by its members, as well as by the community at large. We found that, in spite of some extremely successful initiatives by previous administrations, many members still felt that the IPA was somehow a distant institution, with very little close contact with the membership and sometimes more concerned with collecting dues and establishing rules and procedures than with establishing a more live presence and addressing the concrete needs of the members. About the community at large, we felt that these initiatives should be strengthened, and that we needed to find new ways of having a greater presence in the outer world, for instance through a public information policy, a more effective website, and a more open relation with other international and local institutions, as real partners of all those who share an independent and critical thinking about human affairs. As the reader will perhaps see in this report, we tried to address as strongly as we could all these and other issues; in some areas we succeeded, in others we only achieved something or launched ideas and possibilities. In my opening speech at the Rio Congress, I expressed my conviction that psychoanalysis is a work in progress, a discipline undergoing transition: a number of its theoretical presuppositions are being critically discussed and revised and its various educational models re-examined. This transition has witnessed greater transparency and thoroughness in the formulation, presentation, and discussion of clinical material with the aim of stimulating and promoting a more objective evaluation of our clinical competence as analysts, as well as a greater openness to dialogue with other areas of knowledge. We then presented to the Board our strategic plan, approved immediately after the Rio Congress. In that document I wrote that, in my view, the central locus of progress in psychoanalysis, as science, treatment, and profession, is analytic work and the ability of our members and candidates to develop and maintain a psychoanalytic identity. The goal of the IPA would be to bring together from many countries the creative talents in our Association to further enable the development of best practice in a number of key areas.
