The following strategic goals were proposed in support of the IPA’s mission.


to encourage the development and improvement of good analytic practice; to strengthen the relationship of the IPA with its members, analysts in training, constituent organizations, and regional bodies; to expand the relationship with the society at large.


In order to achieve these goals, we established several new committees, kept some of the existing ones, and disbanded a few, as any new administration usually does. Among the new committees, special mention must be given to the Committee on Analytic Practice and Scientific Activities (CAPSA), which would stimulate an exchange of analytic clinical experience among the three regions, as well as stimulate the improvement of good analytic practice. This was an extremely successful committee; it sponsored meetings in three regions and was able to show how important it is to listen to colleagues from different analytic cultures and theoretical backgrounds, as well as work together with societies, federations, and other committees. A typical CAPSA meeting comprised the visit of an analyst from another region, who would discuss clinical material presented by members from the local region and also bring his/her own clinical material and explain theoretical ways of understanding how and why he/she worked that way. The visitor would both show himself at work and learn from what was being presented. CAPSA also funded invited colleagues to go to the regular regional meetings, with the same aims. In a sense, I would venture to say, CAPSA helped in several ways to bring about a change in our analytic community. Initially, it was mainly Latin American societies that applied to the programme, inviting colleagues from Europe and North America. But slowly the two other regions, Europe and North America, did the same, and in the final year of my term we had a nearly balanced situation among the three regions. Importantly, CAPSA helped to increase our ability to listen to one another and to learn from different analytic cultures. For us a primary goal was to make voices from some of the less traditional analytic centres—for instance, from Latin America, Italy, Germany, Northern Europe—be heard more, and this goal was achieved. That it was realized owes much to the excellent work of Elias Mallet da Rocha Barros, Glen Gabbard, Stefano Bolognini, and Abel Fainstein. As an example, we could mention several reports from colleagues from Europe and North America stating how impressed they were with the quality of the analytic skills of Latin American colleagues who visited their societies. A new Education Committee was constituted, and, thanks to its dedicated work and close collaboration with the Executive Committee and the Board, we were finally able to approve the three training models—Eitingon, French, and Uruguayan—and organize the structure and procedures to oversee them. This was one of the most demanding tasks during our term, and the Board worked hard at its July 2006 meeting in Berlin to arrive at this decision. It is interesting to review the main concerns that were voiced during these discussions, which had begun during Daniel Widlöcher’s term. On the one hand, some Board members felt that we were living as if we were blind to the reality existing in our psychoanalytic community—that is, the fact that we already had several training models, with many differences among them, and that to insist on retaining the current description of training was to remain in the past. Others, however, felt that any change would mean a loss in quality and potentially open the gate to all kinds of second-class and uncontrolled training. During the previous administration, a group chaired by Bob Pyles had broadly identified three training models: Eitingon, the French, and the Uruguayan. We appointed a new committee, chaired by Shmuel Erlich and composed of Fernando Weissman, Sander Abend, Aloysio dÁbreu, Marie-France Dispaux, and Daniel Jacobs, and their work led to a clearer and deeper view of each model, according to several criteria. This clarification made it possible for the Board to discuss, understand, listen to each other’s views, and finally to acknowledge that we did, indeed, have at least these three models. The Eitingon model was and is the prevailing one: its main features, briefly, are a training analysis with four to five sessions a week with a acknowledged training analyst, two supervisions of at least 80 hours each, a programme of four years of seminars covering the main analytic authors, but with Freud at the centre, as well as analytic technique, the presentation of clinical and theoretical papers, to be accepted as members. The French model has as its main locus for learning the supervision of analytic cases; the personal analysis of the candidate, which can be done with any member of his or another society, is done at a frequency of three sessions a week, and there is a more open system of seminars, but again with Freud as the main author; before entering training, the candidate is required to have a period of analysis in which he will try to work through his motivation to become an analyst. Clinical and theoretical papers are also required for membership in the Society. The Uruguayan is a mix of the other two models, with the frequency of sessions ranging from three to five, according to the need and the period of the process, an open system of seminars, including many optional ones, the organization of the training being divided between three groups of analysts—those in charge of the analyses of candidates, those in charge of the seminars (Freud is still the main author), and those in charge of supervisions. Graduation clinical papers are required. Looking back now, what we eventually achieved has an aura of “mission impossible” because of the heated debates, occasional harsh exchanges during and between Board meetings, and the warnings that any substantial change would run the risk of splitting the IPA. In my view, the outcome shows that human groups—even psychoanalytic ones—may sometimes be able to achieve rational goals and move forward. From a personal perspective, I feel that this was the riskiest issue we faced in the last years in our association, and it produced moments of great anxiety and fear in many of us, including myself, hand-in-hand with the sense that we had a historical task to accomplish and that we could not give up our responsibility. In the end, a feeling of pride remained for what we were able to achieve, as well as the reassuring knowledge that this was a remarkable contribution for the future of the IPA. A Candidates Loan Panel was established, very effectively chaired by Adriana Prengler de Benveniste. Thanks to it we were able to help many analysts in training. The History of Psychoanalysis and the IPA Committee, initially chaired by Daniel Widlöcher and now by Peter Loewenberg, has been actively working to produce this volume, to celebrate the centennial of the IPA. The Psychoanalysis and the University Committee, first chaired by Ricardo Bernardi and then by Adela Duarte, promoted meetings and exchanges in the three regions. There is a very disquieting reality in this regard: fewer analysts from the IPA than ever are working at universities, despite constant efforts to stimulate this participation. This committee addresses this problem, using several instruments, like meetings of analysts with university positions, offering of texts useful for teaching analytic concepts for undergraduate and graduate studies, and so on. But we are far from a reasonable positive answer, and much work has still to be done in this area. The Post-Graduate Center for Psychoanalytic Studies, chaired by Peter Fonagy, stimulated interchanges and proposed a new certificate for people who did relevant PhD studies in psychoanalysis. In order to stimulate interest in psychoanalysis and acknowledge the relevance of these studies, after a careful examination and in order to include these colleagues as friends of the IPA and psychoanalysis, but not as IPA members, this certificate was approved. The Committee on Psychoanalysis and Culture, chaired by Leopold Nosek, promoted meetings in the three regions. A new Prejudice Working Group was constituted and, under the effective chair of Janine Puget, produced papers and organized panels on this relevant issue. The first of these committees was in charge of organizing a programme of events or to sponsor existing ones that stimulated the relation of psychoanalysis with cinema, literature, the arts and so on, and several of these meetings were successfully held. The Committee on Prejudice was in charge of reflecting, from a psychoanalytic point of view, on various forms of prejudice, such as racism, anti-Semitism, prejudice against the women and homosexuals, and so on; their papers on these issues were circulated among the membership or presented at several events in the three regions and provoked much interest and discussion. We also launched the Public Information Committee, who worked hard under the inspiring leadership of Sergio Nick and helped to promote the Berlin Congress and the Chicago Congress, as well as designing and putting into action a strategy for more widely improving the image of the IPA. Despite some positive achievements, there is a long way to go in this area. In a joint venture with FEPAL, we established ILAP, the Latin American Institute of Psychoanalysis, which is effectively organizing psychoanalytic studies and training in several Latin American countries where the IPA had not been present; now, thanks to the dedicated work of Javier García (chair), Telma Barros, Juan Vives, Alicia Lustgarten, and Pedro Boschan, we have a concrete presence in several places, as well as regular summer schools. The Berlin Congress was a moving and successful meeting, whose programme committee was chaired by Georg Bruns, with Barbara Stimmel, Juan Pablo Jiménez, Paul Denis, and Anna Christopoulos. In my view, and in the view of many colleagues, it was a unique experience to return to Germany and to Berlin at a new and creative time for that country and for psychoanalysis, and to share experiences about the traumatic past as well as to jointly build new bridges for future achievements. The congress theme, “Remembering, repeating and working through” in psychoanalysis and culture aptly captured what we all had in mind. It was a very important moment to reflect on these three essential movements during an analytic process, according to Freud, but now through a contemporary perspective. At the same time, it was a unique opportunity to reflect and to share memories, facts, and insights about the meaning of Nazism and other traumatic situations brought about by brutal regimes in the past and in the present, to understand how these events produced lasting effects not only for their victims and perpetrators, but also in the following generations, and to see how psychoanalysis can help in the healing process. The Chicago Congress was also a rewarding one, and we owe our gratitude to Abel Fainstein, Glen Gabbard, Liliane Abensur, Monica Armesto, and Kate Schechter of the Programme Committee, as well as to Jorge Schneider and his colleagues in the Local Arrangements committees. Under its theme, “Psychoanalytic Practice: Convergences and Divergences”, and the many opportunities for small discussion groups and working parties meetings, the congress was a natural outcome of our administration’s emphasis on clinical discussions and exchanges. In 2010 the celebration of the IPA’s 100th anniversary was observed, and under the efficient and inspired leadership of Stefano Bolognini a committee organized events in the three regions: North America, Latin America, and Europe, as well as a special meeting in Beijing—the Asian meeting—in order to share experiences and to reflect on the relevance of the IPA and mainly in its future possibilities. We established a working group on Psychoanalytic Perspectives on the Ageing of Patients and Analysts and believe that Gabrielle Junkers and her colleagues will continue the relevant work they are already doing in Europe. The current evidence is that psychoanalysts work for longer than other professionals and that there are specific problems and features at each moment of the life cycle, and so better studies are needed on what it means to be an elderly analyst. On the other hand, more and more ageing people look for analysis, and we need to understand better what kind of analytic process, possibilities, and limitations are at stake in this kind of new analytic population. In the last months of the administration, after several frustrating attempts to improve our website, we established a new Website Editorial Team; now, thanks to Nadine Levinson, David Tuckett, Eike Wolff, and David Coe, we have a renewed and more attractive website. As for the already existing committees, we acknowledge the work done by Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber, Linda Mayes, Stuart Hauser, and Peter Fonagy as chairs of the Conceptual and Empirical Research Committee, Virginia Ungar for her continuous and dedicated work as chair of the Committee on Child and Adolescent Psychoanalysis (COCAP), David Sachs for his skilful and wise chairing of the Allied Centers Committee, Richard Lightbody for the balanced way of chairing the Constitution, Bylaws and Procedures Committee, Anne-Marie Sandler, Peter Blos, and Lee Brauer for their dedicated management of DPPT, Arthur Leonoff and Betty Dentzler for chairing the delicate and extremely relevant Elections Committee, Sara Zac de Filc and now Lynne Moritz for their important work as chairs of the Ethics Committee, Robert Pyles for chairing the Professional Practice Working Group, Robert Wallerstein, and then Linda Mayes, for their skilful management of the Research Advisory Board, Afaf Mahfouz for her wonderful ability to keep the IPA present and relevant in the United Nations and for organizing, with her colleagues, the most successful celebration of Freud’s 150 anniversary at the UN; Paolo Fonda and his colleagues for the marvellous work in progress in the Han Groen-Prakken Psychoanalytic Institute for Eastern Europe; Giovanna Ambrosio (Chair), Maria Teresa Flores, Gertraud Schlesinger-Kipp, Maria Teresa Lartigue, and her colleagues for their continuous and stimulating work in the Committee on Women and Psychoanalysis (COWAP); Nadine Levinson and Henk-Jan Dalewijk for chairing with expertise the Budget and Finance Committee; Doris Silvermann and Silvia Flechner for their tireless work on the IPA Newsmagazine, and, last but not least, Leticia Glocer de Fiorini (Chair), Salman Akhtar, Thierry Bokanowski, Alessandra Lemma, Sérgio Lewkowicz, and Mary Kay O’Neil for expanding, qualifying, and giving a stronger and really more international dimension to our Publications Committee. The International New Groups Committee plays a central role in the development of psychoanalysis, through the establishment, monitoring, and continuous work with new groups in places where the IPA is still not present, and the various liaison, facilitating, exploring, sponsoring, and visiting committees. Dozens of colleagues are devoted to this voluntary, demanding, and difficult work. During our administration, David Iseman was the first chair of the International New Groups Committee (ING), to which he dedicated all his energy and diplomatic skills. After his untimely death, David Sachs, with great generosity, undertook to chair it on a provisional basis; then we had Marilia Aisenstein as Chair, with her enormous capacity for work, the ability to face delicate situations and to look for solutions, and full commitment and love for psychoanalysis. As I mentioned at the beginning of this chapter, one of our priorities was to make the IPA more approachable in its relationship with individual members, societies, and regional bodies. We made it a point of honour for the officers to be present and actively engage in conversations with presidents of societies and of regional bodies in order to exchange points of view, explain the actions of the IPA, and get to know their specific realities, concerns, suggestions, and critiques. This took us to almost all regional meetings, as well as to many congresses of societies and regions, as well as to the French-speaking analysts’ meetings. We made it a point to work with total transparency, openly sharing information with members and societies. We were able to work in close contact with many distinguished and dear colleagues, among them Alvaro Rey de Castro, Juan Pablo Jiménez, Enrique Nuñez Jasso, Evelyne Sechaud, Peter Wegener, Lynne Moritz, Prudy Gurguerchon, Harriet Basseches, Arthur Leonoff, and Bob Pyles. These meeting allowed us to share information about the IPA’s plans and actions, to stimulate participation, to learn about each region’s specific needs, ideas, and suggestions, and to build a network of mutual collaboration, as well as to reduce misunderstandings that are so often seen in these huge organizations. In my view, one of our main achievements was the beginning of analytic training in China. For me personally, it was an unforget-table experience to take part in this special moment, in October 2008, in Beijing. This was another situation where we had to face some concerns and even opposition on the Board from members who felt it was too early for this step or that we did not have enough information. I felt that we needed to take decisive action on this issue, because I was and am deeply convinced that the IPA should be present in this fascinating and challenging country that is China, and I took a personal interest in making it happen. We were able to move forward, and we owe a great acknowledgement to the work of our China committee, wisely chaired by Peter Loewenberg, and drawing on the long experience and dedication of Alf Gerlach, Sverre Varvin, and Maria Teresa Hooke. In such a huge association as the IPA, with so many specific trends, cultural diversity, and enormous administrative strains, no one can pretend to lead the organization and work productively without a good staff. In our case we have had more than that, and I am extremely grateful for having had the chance to work closely with a man of the talent, the energy, the commitment, and the loyalty of Piers Pendred, then our Director General, with Jo Beavis, the very soul of our staff with her knowledge, sound judgement, and love for the IPA, with the continuous and dedicated work of Robert Stein and Simon Shutler, and with the enthusiasm and dedication of David Coe. I am particularly indebted to Petra Hasse, my personal assistant, with whom I worked daily during my term in office and whose good temper, effectiveness, patience, and tireless devotion to her task made it possible for me to face the demands of the job. During those four years I think we were fortunate to have a very active Board and Executive Committee, with whom all actions and decisions were discussed—sometimes fiercely, at other times more calmly. But in general it was possible to maintain a reasonable and civilized relationship, despite several disagreements and different positions on some controversial issues. For these years of close work, I feel grateful for having been able to count on the active presence of Peter Blos, Carmen Medici de Steiner, Jorge Canestri, Virginia Ungar, Nadine Levinson, and Marilia Aisenstein, and it was always a privilege and a true pleasure to be in continuous communication with Nadine Levinson, Henk-Jan Dalewijk, and, especially, Monica Siedmann de Armesto. From the beginning of our work together, we used to call each other, jokingly, “Jefe” and “Socia”, meaning “chief” and “partner”. I will forever be grateful to Monica for being such a wonderful partner on this journey. To conclude on a personal note: I was born, grew up, and undertook all my studies and analytic training in Porto Alegre, a lovely city in the south of Brazil, the capital of the state of Rio Grande do Sul, which borders Uruguay and Argentina. Since childhood I have had, in many respects, this feeling of living on the border: our State is part of Brazil, but different in the sense that we also have much in common with our neighbour countries. I am, at the same time, a Brazilian and a Jew, a psychiatrist and a psychoanalyst, a clinician and a researcher, a medical doctor and a lover of the arts, mainly literature and films, an inhabitant of my country but often feeling at home in so many different places. Despite some initial anxieties and doubts about what would happen in my position as IPA president, I was always able to count on constant love and support in my life, especially from my wife, Marisa, making my experience mainly a very positive and rewarding one. Now I can see that I became a better person and a better analyst for having met so many colleagues, cultures, and different ways of thinking and feeling, and perhaps understanding that, despite so many differences and bad feelings, it is really possible to work and live together and to jointly continue to build and maintain the vitality of psychoanalysis.







35India History of psychoanalysis in India

Suvra Chunder

The Indian Psychoanalytical Society was founded in Calcutta on 26 January 1922, long before many European and other Western countries thought of founding a society, and was immediately accepted as a constituent member of the International Psychoanalytical Association. Psychoanalysis in India had, indeed, an indigenous origin. As far back as the year 1911, Dr Girindrasekhar Bose, the founder of the Indian Psychoanalytical Society, was already experimenting with a psychological method of treatment of mental patients that was very much akin to Freud’s psychoanalysis. In those days in India, even among the men of medicine, psychology, or philosophy, few had heard of this new science of mind or even of Freud’s name. Obtaining his medical degree in 1910, Bose began his career as a medical man. But soon his earlier interest in the deeper workings of the mind and hypnotism, a technique that he had mastered, led him to psychiatric practice and to the study of psychology. He had heard of Freud’s startling discoveries, but Freud’s writings were not yet available in English. Simplistic articles, intended for the lay public, on psychoanalysis in the Indian press and in English newspapers and magazines were scarcely of much help to Bose in conducting the Freudian line of treatment of mental diseases. Working almost independently, Bose formulated his theory of opposite and opposing wishes, and by the middle of the second decade of the century he ventured to treat mental patients along psychoanalytic lines he himself had developed. In 1921, the University of Calcutta conferred on him the Doctor of Science degree for his dissertation The Concept of Repression, which has since been recognized as among the classics of psychoanalytic literature. In 1921 Bose began corresponding with Freud and Jones. These letters illustrate Bose’s bold and original thinking, as well as the distinctive nature of psychoanalysis in India. He was asked to be on the editorial board of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis. In some very important respects, Bose’s theory differs from Freud’s, although on many basic points there is a close correspondence between them. Bose recognized the difference between Eastern and Western mental functioning, which is dependent on the respective social structures. For example, he found the castration threat to be stronger in his European patients, while the desire to be female was more easily found in Indian male patients, a difference Bose possibly thought went “back to the image of the maternal deity”. The major point of difference was his theory of opposite wishes. Bose thought that his “theory of opposite wish could explain the occurrence in pairs in the same individual of such traits as sadism and masochism, observationism and exhibitionism, etc.”. Freud acknowledged: “I see that we did neglect the fact of the existence of the opposite wishes from the three sources of bisexuality (male–female), ambivalence (love–hate) and the opposition of active–passive” (Bose & Freud, 1956). In “A New Theory of Mental Life” (1933), Bose posited a “theoretical ego” to explain his theory of opposite wishes: “the ego that I would posit for the purposes of the theory of opposite wish may be called the ‘theoretical ego’…. It is the great reservoir of all wishes both conscious and unconscious. It includes within itself the Freudian ego, the id and the super-ego, in fact, all manifestations of mental life.” Some excerpts from the letters exchanged between Freud and Bose are quoted below:

26.01.1922 Dear Dr. Freud, Most likely you have received my last letter to which I am expecting a reply. You will be glad to learn that we have been able to start a psychoanalytical society in Calcutta. I am sending you herewith the proceedings of the inaugural meeting and I shall be very glad to have your suggestions regarding our society. I have applied for affiliation to the International Association … Yours sincerely, G. Bose


In the following letter, Freud responds to Bose’s request for a photograph and for an endorsement of his book, The Concept of Repression (1921).


20.02.1922 Dear Mr. Bose, At last I can send you the photograph you wished for—it will come to you from Hamburg—and write the few lines which you ask on behalf of your agent. As my English is very deficient you are invited to change my expressions so as to fit your purpose: “It was a great and pleasant surprise that the first book on a psychoanalytic subject which came to us from that part of the world (India) should display so good knowledge of psycho-analysis, so deep an insight into its difficulties and so much of deep-going original thought. Dr. Bose has singled out the concept of repression for his inquiry and in treating this theoretical matter has provided us with precious suggestions and intense motives for further study. Dr. Bose is aiming at a philosophical evolution and elaboration of our crude, practical concepts and I can only wish psychoanalysis should soon reach up to the level, to which he strives to raise it.” Having heard of your success in starting a psychoanalytic group in your country, I heartily congratulate you on it. May we meet one day not too far off, as I am rather old (66 years). Sincerely yours, Freud



27.10.1922 Dear Dr. Bose, I heartily congratulate you on the reception of your Society as one of the groups of the Internat. Ps. Association which occurred at the Berlin Congress a month ago. Now, as I am Editor of the German Zeitschrift fur Ps. As well as of the English Journal of Ps. A., I beg you to consent that your name may be printed on the cover of both journals as the leader and representative of the Indian group in the same way the other presidents are mentioned. If you agree it may first be done in the first number of year 1923. I hope to find soon some of your contributions in our journal. With kindest regards. Yours Freud



09.03.1929 Dear Prof. Bose, You directed my attention on the Oedipus wish especially and you were right in doing so. It made a great impression on me. Cordially yours, Freud


Thus, the Indian Psychoanalytical Society was founded on 26 January 1922 in a meeting convened by Dr N. N. Sengupta (Chairman), Dr G. S. Bose, Mr M. N. Banerjee, Mr H. K. Maity, and Dr S. Mitra. Others present were: Roy J. S. Bahadur, Mr G. Paul, Mr A. Majumdar, Mr R. C. Ghosh, Dr N. C. Mitra, Capt. (Dr) S. K. Roly, MD, IMS, Mr G. Bothra. From that time until the present the main branch of the Indian Psychoanalytical Society has been in Kolkata (then Calcutta), with other branches in Mumbai (then Bombay), Delhi, and Ahmedabad. Apart from training and treatment in psychoanalysis, the Society also extends affordable services to economically weaker sections of society at the centre itself. In 1938 Dr G. S. Bose founded the Lumbini Park Mental Hospital with 3 beds, which grew to an 80-bed hospital. It included psychoanalysis, apart from psychiatric medical treatment, for inpatients as well as outpatients. On 10 July 1970, the Girindrasekhar Clinic was established. This is a unique institution where psychoanalysts, psychiatrists, and psychologists work together in treating patients with psychological disturbances and disorders. Over the years, the Indian Psychoanalytical Society has had the honour of being visited by eminent personalities from the fields of psychoanalysis, psychology, and psychiatry from all over world—among them Dr Carl Gustav Jung, Prof. G. M. Carstairs of Edinburgh University, Dr W. H. Kiernan (visiting Professor of Psychiatry in WHO), Prof. Samuel Z. Orgel from Mount Sinai Medical School & Hospital, USA, and Prof. Shmuel Erlich (Israel). In Mumbai, beyond providing psychoanalytic training, treatment is provided for both adults and children. In Delhi, in collaboration with the University of Delhi (South Campus), the Society has started a Psychoanalytic Training Centre. Currently (2010) there are 31 psychoanalysts in India who are members of the IPA. In addition, there are 92 affiliate members, including both individuals who are interested in psychoanalysis and students who are undergoing psychoanalytic training. Finally, Samiksha [Analysis], the journal of the Indian Psychoanalytical Society, has been published since 1947. Bibliography Bose, G. S. (1921). The Concept of Repression (2nd edition). Kolkata: Vivekanda Book Centre, 2009. Bose, G. S. (1933). A new theory of mental life. Samiksha, 20 (1966, No. 1). Bose, G. S. (1945). Everyday Psychoanalysis (2nd edition). Kolkata: Vivekanda Book Centre, 2009. Bose, G. S., & Freud, S. (1956). Correspondence: The Beginnings of Psychoanalysis in India. Calcutta: Indian Psychoanalytical Society, 1999. Hartnack, C. (2001). Psychoanalysis in Colonial India. New Delhi: Oxford University Press. Orgel, S. Z. (1968). Freud–Bose: The development of psychoanalysis in India. Journal of the Hillside Hospital, 27: 230–237. Sinha, T. C. (1966a). Development of psycho-analysis in India. International Journal of Psychoanalysis, 47: 427–439. Sinha, T. C. (1966b). Psychoanalysis in India. In: Lumbini Park Silver Jubilee Souvenir Publication. Kolkata, 1966.







This book is dedicated to the next generation of IPA psychoanalysts on the frontiers: the International Psychoanalytical Studies Organization [IPSO], the Psychoanalytic Institute for Eastern Europe [PIEE], the Latin American Institute of Psychoanalysis [ILAP], and our colleagues in China training under the IPA China Committee In memoriam With sadness we record the death during the preparation of this volume of two of our contributors, who did not live to see their work realized in print Eddy de Klerk [1936–2010] Leo Rangell [1913–2011]








The Geography of the IPA Peter Loewenberg & Nellie Thompson For the past several decades the complex and anomalous politico-cultural geography of the IPA has been the subject of vigorous discussion by many IPA analysts and non-analytic cultural figures. Each discourse has reflected its particular time and revealed the presuppositions of its participants. For reasons of pragmatic utility in structuring the contributions to this book, we have, where feasible, followed the logic of geography rather than the logic of cultural affinity and the IPA’s historical development. Thus, the roster of psychoanalytic societies is intended as a reference list for ease in locating and finding psychoanalytic organizations. Thirty years ago the late Jacques Derrida [1930–2004] critiqued and deconstructed the “political geography of psychoanalysis” (Derrida, 1981, p. 70), adopting what he termed “‘a map-reading approach’ to psychoanalysis” (p. 67).1 He essayed a perceptive reading of the IPA “map” of the three divisions of the psychoanalytic world: North America, Latin America, and “the rest of the world”(p. 64). These three areas, he suggested, constitute “a tricontinental triangle.”(p. 87). Europe, the motherland of psychoanalysis, to the derisive amusement of Derrida, is subsumed with “all those parts of the world where psychoanalysis, to put it bluntly, has never set foot”(p. 65). Writing in 1981, he accurately observed “that there is practically no psychoanalysis in Africa, white or black, just as there is practically no psychoanalysis in Asia, or in the South Seas”(p. 69). Therefore, he argued,


It may be more accurate to say that there are in fact four areas. The “rest of the world” is divided into two: on the one hand, it covers Europe and all the places where analysis has taken firm root (broadly speaking, the cradle of psychoanalysis in the so-called democracies of the Old World); on the other hand, it also includes that immensity of territory where … Homo psychoanalyticus is unknown or outlawed … those areas of human settlement where psychoanalysis has made no inroads whatsoever—sometimes not even with the help of all the paraphernalia of colonization: almost all China, a good portion of Africa, the entire non-Judeo-Christian world…. The size of these psychoanalytically virgin territories, in terms both of their physical extension and of their (present and future) demographics, as well as their cultural and religious foundations, means that they constitute a vast problem for the future of psychoanalysis. [p. 87]


Today we recognize, as the chapters in this volume testify, that Homo psychoanalyticus has indeed begun to make inroads in the immense human and physical geography of Asia, which three decades ago Derrida described as “parts of the world where psychoanalysis … has never set foot”. Currently, in accord with the logic of their historical origins and socio-political reality, the psychoanalytic societies of Australia, Lebanon, India, Israel, South Africa and Turkey are members of the IPA European Region, and the psychoanalytic societies of Japan, Korea and Taiwan are members of the IPA North American Region. The organization of the chapters in this book takes cognizance of the legitimacy of Derrida’s condescension towards the IPA for subsuming “the rest of the world” under Europe. We have witnessed the emergence of psychoanalytic cultures and the promise of the future development of psychoanalysis in areas whose geographic coherence Derrida could not have foreseen. The IPA Asian Congress in Beijing in October 2010, attended by participants from throughout the world, including Psychoanalytic Institute of Eastern Europe (PIEE) representatives from Siberia, was a resounding indication of the development of psychoanalysis in China and testified to the viability of a potential Asian Region beyond China’s borders. The declared policy of current and past IPA administrations has been to facilitate and support the development of an Asian Region. We believe that the IPA’s second century will usher in a fourth IPA Asian Region, and we hope that this volume contributes to its realization. Note 1. We are indebted to John Forrester for guiding us to this Derrida essay. Bibliography Derrida, J. (1981). Geopsychoanalysis: … and the rest of the world. In: C. Lane (Ed.), The Psychoanalysis of Race (New York: Columbia University Press, 1998).








17Switzerland The Swiss

André Haynal & Ernst Falzeder

The Swiss were “psychoanalysts of the first hour” (Walser, 1976, p. 455). “Switzerland was the first country, and Zurich and Geneva were the first cities outside Vienna, in which psychoanalysis met with a broad response” (Weber, 2002, p. 87). The Swiss dominated the development of organized psychoanalysis during its first years. It was through them that psychoanalysis got a toehold in the doors of European psychiatry and academia. Zurich was the birthplace of modern psychodynamic psychiatry. The Swiss were chiefly responsible for editing the first psychoanalytic periodical, the Jahrbuch für psychoanalytische und psychopathologische Forschungen [Yearbook for Psychoanalytic and Psychopathological Studies]. Zurich was also the main recruiting centre for the nascent psychoanalytic movement, offering a unique possibility to teach and learn psychoanalysis, both at the Burghölzli and at Zurich University, at which Bleuler and Jung held posts as professor and assistant professor, respectively. The first generation of physicians who practised psychoanalysis as a profession came to Freud almost exclusively via Jung and Bleuler, among them Karl Abraham, Roberto Greco Assagioli, Ludwig Binswanger, Abraham Arden Brill, Trigant Burrow, Imre Décsi, Max Eitingon, Sándor Ferenczi, Otto Gross, Johann Jakob Honegger, Smith Ely Jelliffe, Ernest Jones, Alphonse Maeder, Hans Maier, Hermann Nunberg, Johan H. W. von Ophuijsen, Nikolai J. Ossipow, Franz Riklin, Hermann Rorschach, Tatiana Rosenthal, Leonhard Seif, Eugénie Sokolnicka, Sabina Spielrein, Fülöp Stein, Wolf Stockmayer, Johannes Irgens Strømme, Jaroslaw Stuchlík, and G. Alexander Young (cf. Falzeder, 1994, 2000). Indeed, for a few years, it was Zurich, not Vienna, that functioned as the organizational and training centre of psychoanalysis. Freud stated:


Nowhere else did such a compact little group of adherents exist, or could a public clinic be placed at the service of psycho-analytic researches, or was there a clinical teacher who included psychoanalytic theories as an integral part of his psychiatric course. The Zurich group thus became the nucleus of the small band…. Most of my followers and co-workers at the present time came to me by way of Zurich, even those who were geographically much nearer to Vienna than to Switzerland.” [Freud, 1914d, p. 27]


It was Eugen Bleuler [1857–1939], head of the Burghölzli, the renowned psychiatric clinic in Zurich, who had introduced psychoanalysis in his institution, recruiting a staff open to this new theory and method, among them Carl Gustav Jung. In September 1907, an informal “Freud Society” was founded, whose biweekly meetings “were exclusively devoted to a discussion of Freudian questions” (Jung to Meyer, 19 November 1907, in Meyer Archives, Johns Hopkins University). The first international meeting of psychoanalysts, on 27 April 1908, in Salzburg/Austria, organized by Jung, strengthened the bonds with Bleuler, who became, with Freud, the co-editor-in-chief of the newly founded Yearbook, whose managing editor was Jung. Apart from Freud’s study on “Little Hans” (Freud, 1909b), all contributions in its first issue, published in March 1909, were written by authors associated with the Burghölzli. Later in 1909, Jung was invited, together with Freud, to give talks on psychoanalysis at Clark University. When, at Freud’s instigation, Ferenczi founded the International Psychoanalytical Association at the International Congress of Psychoanalysis in Nuremberg in 1910, Jung was elected president, and he chose his relative and compatriot Franz Riklin as his secretary. The foundation of the Swiss branch society was somewhat delayed, however, mainly because of Bleuler’s reservations about the intended “exclusiveness” of the Society. As a compromise, Ludwig Binswanger agreed to act as its president, on condition that non-members were also invited to the meetings. Jung and Riklin were re-elected twice, as president and secretary of the IPA, respectively, by acclamation, in September 1911, at the IPA Congress in Weimar, and—foreshadowing the impending break—with only 30 of 52 votes at the Congress in September 1913 in Munich. In April 1914, Jung resigned from the presidency of the IPA, as a result of the well-known personal and theoretical differences with Freud and his inner circle, taking with him the majority of the members of the Swiss group. The early contributions of the Swiss psychoanalysts were crucial, as Jung’s well-known experiments on word associations (cf. Jung, 1973) represented a brilliant combination of two cutting-edge approaches of the time—psychoanalysis and experimental psychology in the tradition of Wundt, Aschaffenburg, and others—and demonstrated the existence of repressed, unconscious “complexes”. Jung’s concepts of complexes, of introversion of the libido, and of imagos were quickly adopted by Freud1 and other analysts and have become central concepts of psychoanalytic theory to this day, as have Bleuler’s notion of “ambivalence” and his term “schizophrenia” (Bleuler, 1908) for what had until then been called “dementia praecox”. Other concepts, controversial at the time, have also entered analytic theory and practice, such as Jung’s method of dream interpretation on the “subjective plane”,2 and his view of the compensatory function of the unconscious3 or of the importance of present conflicts, and not only of repressed childhood memories, in the psychoanalytic process. It should also be noted that Jung and the “Zurich school” were the first to call for the necessity of a training analysis of future analysts, as explicitly acknowledged by Freud (1912e, p. 116). The Burghölzli staff were the first to investigate psychoses from a psychoanalytic viewpoint. Psychotic cases were practically absent in the private practices of Freud and his dozen or so followers in Vienna, who, if they were medical doctors at all, mainly worked with so-called “nervous diseases”—that is, neuroses, and psychosomatic or functional disorders.4 Jung’s book, The Psychology of Dementia Praecox (1907), established his worldwide reputation. Ernest Jones hailed it as a “sensation” that would have heralded “a new era in psychiatry” (Jones, 1909), and its translator, Abraham A. Brill, still called it “the cornerstone of modern interpretative psychiatry” in 1936 (Brill, 1936, p. ix). Let us also mention the researches of Hermann Rorschach, which led to the development of his well-known projective test; the application of analysis to pastoral work and pedagogy by Oskar Pfister (an analysand of Jung’s; cf. Freud & Abraham, 2009, p. 413), Adolf Keller, Alphonse Maeder, Otto Mensendieck, Oskar Messmer, and Ernst Schneider (and later Hans Zulliger), as representatives of an early interest in child analysis and developmental psychology; the sanatorium of Dr Bircher-Benner (inventor of the popular Bircher muesli), where “regularity of life, simplicity of diet, and other therapeutic measures [could be] combined with psychoanalysis by Dr. Maeder” (Korrespondenzblatt, 1912, p. 2415); or the studies of Jung, Riklin, and others on psychoanalysis and religion and mythology. The crucial part played by Eugen Bleuler in these early years has long been underestimated. He was the head of an exceptionally creative scientific community (Loewenberg, 1995). Although he never was an unconditional supporter of “the Cause” and criticized parts of Freud’s theory as well as the politics of the IPA, in academic and psychiatric circles he was widely viewed as being chiefly responsible for the flourishing of the psychoanalytic movement. His colleague Alfred Hoche, an ardent critic of analysis, even called him the “figure-head of the sect” [Renommierstück der Sekte] (Bericht über die Jahresversammlung des Deutschen Vereins für Psychiatrie zu Breslau am 13. und 14. Mai 1913, pp. 795–796). Freud made great and repeated efforts to persuade him to stay within the IPA—something he had not done with any other vacillating supporter.6 When Bleuler finally did distance himself for good, for the above-mentioned reasons, and also after a failed analysis—by correspondence—with Freud (cf. Falzeder, 2004), this had probably more important consequences for the development of psychoanalysis than the dissensions of Adler, Stekel, or even Jung. Alexander and Selesnick (1965) even find “that without the dissension which led to Bleuler’s resignation, the future isolation of psychoanalysis from the universities and academic institutions never would have occurred” (pp. 1–2). When in 1914 Jung and his followers broke with the IPA and founded their own group, this caused an important caesur a in the institutional history of Swiss psychoanalysis and its contacts with the IPA. The page turns with the (re)foundation of the Swiss Psychoanalytical Society on 24 March 1919, on the initiative of Emil Oberholzer and Mira Oberholzer-Gincburg and Oskar Pfister. Oberholzer, a former analysand of Freud’s (cf. Falzeder, 2000; May, 2007), became its first president, and Hermann Rorschach vice-president. Among the first members we also find Arthur Kielholz and Raymond de Saussure. The IPA sent three “heavy-weight” representatives—Ernest Jones, Hanns Sachs, and Otto Rank—to be present and give talks on this occasion. (Did they fear a “Ver-Jungung”, as it was rumoured in Vienna?) None of these delegates had special ties with Zurich analysts or with the Swiss in general7 (in contrast to Abraham or Ferenczi, for example), and the Swiss took this as a sign of distrust. The idea that Hanns Sachs could even become a kind of supervisor (cf. Freud & Pfister, 1963, pp. 69–70) may also have been felt as a threat of an unfriendly foreign intrusion. During the following years, a generally peaceful evolution and good understanding developed between the Swiss group and the IPA, with the exception that the Swiss objected to the Viennese proposition that all members of the International be obliged to subscribe to the psychoanalytic journals. Interestingly, the Zurichers also protested against the publication, in the psychoanalytic Verlag, of Georg Groddeck’s novel Der Seelensucher (1921), as they considered it to be pornographic and wanted to prevent its distribution in Switzerland (Wittenberger & Tögel, 2001, p. 74; cf. Bos, 1994; letter from Freud and Rank in 1921 to Emil Oberholzer and the Swiss Psychoanalytical Society, in Pruyser, 1986). In the French-speaking part of Switzerland, in Geneva, Théodore Flournoy had already been interested in psychoanalysis around the turn of the century—and had been an important mentor and discussion partner for Jung. In 1919, Edouard Claparède, his pupil and successor as professor of psychology at the university, founded an open circle for people interested in psychoanalysis. In September 1920 the Société Psychanalytique de Genève was founded; however, it never became a member organization of the IPA and disappeared in the late 1920s (Quinodoz, 2005, p. 1747). Claparède was the head of the Institut Jean-Jacques Rousseau (named after the famous eighteenth-century thinker from Geneva), founded in 1912, where a small group of psychoanalytically oriented persons worked with children. When Sabina Spielrein moved to Geneva after the First World War, she became associated with this group and published papers on child analysis (six papers in 1920 alone) but also worked with adults (Richebächer, 2005). Among her analysands we find the later famous psychologist and epistemologist Jean Piaget, who in 1919 had just returned from Zurich, where he had studied with Bleuler, Jung, and Pfister. In 1922, Piaget gave a paper at the IPA Congress in Berlin. Although never a psychoanalyst sensu strictiori, he remained interested. Nevertheless, in 1971 he delivered, at the invitation of the American Psychoanalytic Association (APsaA), an important address about the relationship between his own researches and psychoanalysis. Théodore Flournoy and Pierre Bovet, also of Geneva, continued their work in developmental psychology but, like Claparède, kept their distance from organized psychoanalysis (“the … Geneva pseudo-analysts”, as Ernest Jones called them—Wittenberger & Tögel, 1999, p. 77). It was only the members of the next generation, such as Charles Odier and Raymond de Saussure (an analysand of Freud), who not only joined the Swiss Psychoanalytical Society and the IPA, but also participated in the development of psychoanalysis in France (where they both practised for a while), for example, in the foundation of the Société Psychanalytique de Paris, of the Revue Française de Psychanalyse, and the annual Congresses of French-speaking psychoanalysts. De Saussure’s book, La méthode psychanalytique [The psycho-analytic method] (1922), with a preface by Freud, was “especially well calculated to give French readers a correct idea of what psychoanalysis is” (Freud, 1922e, p. 283). Germaine Guex, the author of a book on “abandonnisme”, did important work in a medico-psychological consultation centre in the Valais, while John Leuba, from Neuchâtel, spent practically all his professional life, from the 1920s to the 1940s, in the French capital. Meanwhile, some tensions grew in the Zurich group in the 1920s, mainly between medical and non-medical analysts, particularly Pfister, who allegedly did not conduct “real” analyses but was more interested in (brief) psychotherapy. His rather unconventional ideas about the liberating power of love—with the implication of “free love”—were rather shocking for many in Zurich. “All the difficulties relating to non-physicians”, as Fritz Meerwein (1979) put it, “were basically linked with Pfister’s personality” (in Moser, 1992, p. 286). On the other hand, his loyalty to and special relationship with Freud made him an important figure. Against this background, Emil Oberholzer founded a purely medical psychoanalytic group in January 1928, thus causing a split (the IPA refused to grant the requested recognition as a branch Society). This “medical group” slowly lost its importance, however, especially after the emigration of Emil and Mira Oberholzer to the United States in 1938; it broke up for good after the Second World War. During the 1920s and 1930s, important work was done in German-speaking Switzerland. Hans Zulliger developed a psychoanalytic version of pedagogy and introduced a kind of play therapy, which abstained from practically all verbal interventions [“reine Spieltherapie”]. Hermann Rorschach’s projective test enjoyed great success and familiarized a larger public with psychoanalytic concepts. Arthur Kielholz became one of the important practitioners of psychoanalysis in the ascendant psychoanalytic life in Basel. Moreover, several German analysts, persecuted in their own country, emigrated to Switzerland. The most important of them, Heinrich Meng, moved to Basel in 1933, where he held a chair for “Mental Hygiene” at the university and was also a prolific writer. Hermann Nunberg and Frieda Fromm-Reichmann, too, were for some time members of the Swiss Society (Moser, 1992, p. 287). Philipp Sarasin, who had trained in Zurich with Bleuler, also settled in Basel and became a dominant figure in Swiss psychoanalysis, acting as president of the Swiss Society for 32 years (from 1928 to 1960). A descendant of an aristocratic, highly cultivated urban family, for many years he presided with serenity over the affairs of the Society. Apart from Sarasin, quite a number of key figures in the Society had also been analysed by Freud: Ernst Blum (Berne; cf. Pohlen, 2006), Charles Odier (Geneva and Lausanne), Henri Flournoy (Geneva), Raymond de Saussure (Geneva), and Emil Oberholzer (Zurich). By the 1940s, psychoanalysis had gained a foothold in the main urban centres of the country: Philipp Sarasin, Heinrich Meng, and Hans Christoffel in Basel; Hans Zulliger, Ernst Schneider, and Arnold Weber in Berne; in part (later) Charles Odier and Raymond de Saus-sure in Geneva and Lausanne; Rudolf Blum in Zurich, to mention only the best-known among them. The son of Théodore Flournoy, Henri, upheld the psychoanalytic tradition in Geneva and so ensured its continuation from the first blossoming of psychoanalytic thinking in the 1910s and 1920s up to the period after the Second World War. Charles Beaudoin, also in Geneva, tried to find a personal synthesis between Freudian and Jungian thinking and remained outside the mainstream and the IPA. At the IPA Congress in Lucerne in 1934, there were 300 Swiss participants (Moser, 2008). After the relative isolation of the Swiss during the Second World War, new developments soon emerged. The tradition of a “liberal” training system, the informal institutional atmosphere retained from the pioneering epoch, and perhaps also the old anti-authoritarian and democratic traditions of the country contributed to the Swiss Society developing specific idiosyncratic features. In contrast to the Berlin model, teaching was not based on a classroom-system and a rigid curriculum, but students could choose freely among the courses offered. Not only were, in principle, all so-called “ordinary”—that is, full—members allowed to conduct training analyses, but analyses by other persons, too, were generally accepted on a case-to-case basis by the training committee after personal interviews (with some fluctuations in these regulations). (Similar regulations were later adopted by other societies, such as the APF in France and some other groups.) While personal analysis, supervision, and seminars remained the core elements of the training system, the importance of additional auto-didactical work was heavily stressed. Owing to the political and linguistic structure of the country, the Society remained multi-centred. From 1967 onward, the length of the presidency was limited to three years, alternating between presidents from German and French Switzerland. The first organized training centres date from the end of the 1950s, such as the Psychoanalytisches Seminar Zürich (in 1958), and the Centre de formation de la Suisse Romande (called, from 1973 onward, the Centre Raymond de Saussure) in Geneva and Lausanne. There was a growing interest in psychoanalysis in psychiatric institutions and universities. Until then Switzerland had, as we have seen, succeeded in training competent and innovative psychoanalytic practitioners and thinkers with a benign neglect of the institutional strictures. Now a complementary evolution was starting, in part under the influence of other countries (such as that of the Société Psychanalytique de Paris on the French-speaking part of Switzerland). These influences were also transmitted by the then new president of the Swiss Society (1960–66), Raymond de Saussure, back from New York (where he had stayed from 1940 onward), who played an important role in the society. In Zurich, an influential group of a new generation of analysts—many of them analysands of Rudolf Blum—took over the leadership. Paul Parin [1916–2009] (research about psychoanalysis and society), Fritz Morgenthaler [1919–84] (interested in psychoanalytic technique, perversions, and later ethno-psychoanalysis), Jacques Berna (child analysis), Harold Lincke (ethology), and others formed a lively group, which raised interest and even enthusiasm. In French Switzerland there were lively discussions inspired by the controversies in Paris (pro and contra Lacan, among others), mainly along the lines of the French mainstream (Lebovici, Diatkine, Green, etc.), but also by the British (Hannah Segal), with their regular visits to Geneva. Interest in the psychopathology of psychoses was revived by Gaetano Benedetti in Zurich and in Basel, and by Marguerite Sechehaye [1887–1964] in Geneva. In general, there was a steady increase in interest in psychoanalysis, be it in private practice or in official teaching institutions, including the universities. The theoretical orientations of the Society became more diversified. Until the end of the Second World War, the German-speaking part of the Society was traditionally Freudian and close to the Vienna Society’s orientation. Slowly, an existentialist orientation gained influence, especially due to Ludwig Binswanger [1881–1966] who had, already from the 1920s onward, been intensely interested in philosophy (in phenomenology and hermeneutics, later in Husserl and somewhat in Heidegger, and always remaining in a mutually respectful relationship with Freud). The main representative of what came to be called Daseinsanalyse was Medard Boss in Zurich. Having had analysis with Freud, he founded a separate group and training institute outside the Swiss Psychoanalytical Society. He himself, however, remained a member of the Society. While some degree of interest in existentialist modes of thinking could be felt at that time in other places in Europe, too (in London, by Laing, in Germany, etc.), in Zurich explicit references to philosophers such as Heidegger were not welcome and were not to the liking of many psychoanalysts. Gradually the impact of this orientation diminished, to the point of nearly disappearing. An analyst who was close to this group for a while was Gustav Bally. A Swiss who lived in Berlin in the 1920s and early 1930s and later in Zurich, he took a firm stand in an article in the Neue Zürcher Zeitung against a paper of Jung’s suggesting a collaborative attitude with the Nazis. However, what really led to an institutional crisis in Zurich was the development of the Psychoanalytisches Seminar. Against the background of the youth movements and student revolts around and after 1968, and with the support of some members of the Zurich group, candidates put intense pressure on the Society, aiming to preside over the training institute. After bitter conflicts, this eventually led to the splitting-off, in 1977, of the Seminar, while the Swiss Society created a new institute, the “Freud-Institut Zürich”, under its own leadership. Attracted by the Seminar’s seductive offer of a training arrangement in which candidates could authorize themselves as psychoanalysts, and also due to the support of prominent analysts of the “old” group, the number of candidates—in part without any psychoanalytic experience—exploded to an average number of 200, peaking at 800 in the 1980s (Weber, 2002, p. 90). In the following years the situation stabilized. The Swiss Society established contacts with the then president of the IPA, Serge Lebovici, and its secretary, Daniel Widlöcher, and it has since then continued to function without the contribution of the now separate group of the Seminar. In summarizing, we can say that in general the Swiss Society fared rather well with its “liberal” traditions. It has enabled a good standard of psychoanalytic work proper and made creative contributions to psychoanalytic thinking and research in an atmosphere of intellectual freedom. Personal, institutional, as well as technical and theoretical differences, against a background of innovative studies in various fields, ranging from psychopathology and biology to the humanities and the social sciences, led to some splits. In any case, institutional unity has not been of utmost concern for the Swiss, who are used to living in 26 cantons with different laws and four languages (the famous Kantönligeist, also evoked by Freud—Kielholz, 1957, p. 401). In general, there has been a good and lively collaboration with the other European societies, leading, not least due to the efforts of the Swiss, to the creation of the European Psychoanalytical Federation, of which the Genevian Raymond de Saussure was one of the first presidents. Notes 1. It is probably no coincidence that Freud introduced his famous notion of the Oedipus complex in 1910 (Freud, 1910h, p. 171), at the height of his friendship with Jung, thus honouring a term made famous by the latter. Freud also took over the concept of the introversion of the libido, which he called “felicitous” [treffend] (1912b, p. 102; trans. mod.); he used it 19 times in his own works, for the last time in 1920 in Beyond the Pleasure Principle (1920g, p. 51; cf. Guttman, Parrish, Ruffing, & Smith, 1995). “Imago” is a term first introduced by Jung in 1911, a name he had taken from a 1906 novel by the Swiss writer Carl Spitteler, and from the ancient religious idea of “imagines et lares” (Jung, 1911/12, p. 164; also in Jung, 1967, § 62 and note 5). In 1912, Freud founded Imago. Zeitschrift für Anwendung der Psychoanalyse auf die Geisteswissenschaften [Imago: Journal for the Application of Psychoanalysis to the Humanities]. (To this day there exists the journal American Imago.) 2. That is, that “dream images do not reflect the relations between the dreamer and the persons seen in the dream, but they are an expression of tendencies within the dreamer” (Protokolle des Psychoanalytischen Vereins, our translation; cf. Maeder, 1913, pp. 11, 13). 3. Jung regards “the activity of the unconscious … as a compensation to the onesidedness of the general attitude produced by the function of consciousness” (Jung, 1921, p. 532). 4. Prior to his study on Schreber (Freud, 1911c)—to whom Jung had drawn his attention in the first place—Freud had published only one signifi-cant case history of a psychotic patient (Freud, 1896b), in which, however, he had introduced the important concept of “projection” as a defence mechanism (p. 184). 5. The report is by New York psychoanalyst and speech scientist Edward Wheeler Scripture [1864–1945] in his talk on “Psychoanalysis in Zurich” on 24 October 1911, before the New York Psychoanalytic Society. 30Uruguay Chronicle of a unique experience: the origins

Juan Carlos Capo & Javier García

The institutional features that characterize the Uruguayan Psychoanalytical Association (UPA) and that differentiate us from other institutes have developed within a society that has a democratic and participatory history, which at the outset of the twentieth century achieved electoral and labour rights and health care for women and children, enacted advanced legislation in the areas of labour conditions, family affairs, and divorce, and promoted free and universal education. At the end of the 1950s, legislation was approved by Parliament, establishing the autonomy of the state university with joint government by graduates and students. A vigorous student movement worked together with the workers’ movement in the following decade, the 1960s—also known as the second vanguard of the century—until these legal guarantees began to be abrogated in the early 1970s. In February 1956, at the 20th IPA Congress in Paris, the Uruguayan group was recognized as a Study Group of the International Psychoanalytical Association. In the same year a series of seminars began, led by Willy and Madeleine Baranger. Of French origin, both were psychoanalysts and teachers, with a Kleinian training. They were training analysts from the Argentine Psychoanalytic Association, having moved to Montevideo to carry out this work. In 1958 the first Uruguayan psychoanalysts to complete training with Willy and Madeleine Baranger began to teach seminars to a new generation. In 1961, at the 22nd IPA Congress in Edinburgh, the UPA was recognized as a Member Society of the IPA. The Uruguayan Psychoanalytical Association Institute was motivated by the same principles that had led Sigmund Freud, together with pioneering psychoanalysts such as Max Eitingon, to organize the first institute of psychoanalysis in Berlin. The two pillars of this training were, on the one hand, the Institute, which oversaw theoretical training and supervised clinical work, and, on the other hand, the personal analysis of the candidate. From an early stage until 1972–74, the training of psychoanalysts was directed by the Educational Director of the UPA and carried out by the teaching analysts. The three training functions—analysis of candidates; training through seminars and curricular supervision; and decision-making as to admittance of new candidates into the Institute—were thus combined:

An institutional change in course: laying the groundwork During the period 1972–74, a group—representative of all the levels of the Association and of the Institute—dedicated themselves to building the institutional structure of the UPA and the training of future psychoanalysts. This led to radical changes in training and teaching because the candidates and the graduates began an active, ongoing participation in these Institute functions. For example, the Education Commission was opened to include candidates, and the concentration of training functions in the hands of teaching analysts was replaced by the teaching affairs groups (analysts’ group, supervisors’ groups, and teachers’ groups of the Institute.)


Methods The teaching methods groups research, study, and organize the institutional functions, each from its respective area; thus each group contributes to the ongoing functioning of the institute. Members’ participation in a group is based not only on their professional titles, but on the quality of their contribution when their ideas are put to the test in discussions with colleagues as the group works together to resolve problems of teaching and supervision. The coordinator of each teaching function group serves as a member of the Education Commission.


Effects The document that laid the groundwork for the rules that arose out of the 1974 reforms states:


Access to each and every one of the functions in the Institute, and to each stage of training, must be the result of the combination of two factors: (A) An application by the candidate, declaring their interest in holding this post and assuming the responsibility for carrying it out; (B) The decision of the Institute, which, using procedures appropriate to each case, evaluates the applicant’s interests and capacity, reserving the right to either accept or reject the application. Both the applicant and the Institute must periodically review the performance of the task, upholding or rescinding the initial decision. The Institute does not authorize or evaluate the performance of an individual; rather, there is a parallel process: self-evaluation and appraisal by one’s peers.


Training is undertaken in three areas, and the separation of the teaching functions permits the formation of Work Groups, each of which researches theoretical questions in order to gain a deeper understanding of the practical and organizational problems inherent to its specific area. The expectation is that each such group will design its own area of research, admit and train new members, exchange experiences, and maintain a certain coherence as individuals in the group work together. Within this scheme, problems of a theoretical or normative nature may be adequately studied and resolved, thereby strengthening the essential functions of the Institute. The corresponding Work Group together with the Education Commission, upon reaching agreement on questions related to training, generate a converging overview from two complementary perspectives.


Non-intervention The other significant change consisted of separating the personal analysis of the candidate from institutional considerations through the policy of non-intervention of the analyst in the Institute at any point in the training process. Entrance into the Institute no longer depends on the candidate’s analyst. The aspiring student is evaluated by the Institute via an interview by a commission set up for that purpose.


Epistemological shift The theoretical training curriculum has also undergone significant changes, from an essentially Kleinian slant to a greater emphasis on studying the work of Sigmund Freud.


On-going review The process of review, in relation to the function of the Institute as well as to the plan for training psychoanalysts, is an on-going task of our group. At the Pre-Teaching Congress, held in conjunction with the FEPAL Congress in Mexico in 1978, the report of the Education Commission evaluated the risks of falling into dogmatism as well as the reinforcement of stereotypes, which can transform once creative ideas and rules into stagnant conventions. This can cause a slide into imaginary fantasies, possibly arising from the personal analyses of the participants, which then percolates through the theoretical activity of the seminars, resulting in the adoption of negative policies. This poses the necessity of the Institute assuming a mediating role.


The new training plan In the mid-1980s and the early 1990s, the Institute named a commission to study the analyst-training plan. Out of that report came a new Seminar Plan, which began to be partially enacted in 1992 and was fully implemented in 1994. This plan is presently in effect. It promotes the participation of candidates in defining their training programme and also ensures that candidates in seminars will not remain in the same class during their entire training; instead, the classes are re-organized every year. Furthermore, it specifies that teachers may offer seminars in accordance with their interests. This promotes a greater creative contribution on everyone’s part. The selection of seminars on the one hand, and on the other hand the required seminar load per year, are established and regulated in accordance with the Plan. The basis of this new Training Plan is the freedom of selection on the part of the candidate, the freedom of offering on the part of the teacher, and theoretical pluralism in the training courses.


Pluralism and democracy Our internal structure and institutional functioning are based on a dual authority. On the one hand, the officials are elected biannually by secret ballot; on the other hand, the Teaching Groups and the Commissions are formed by those who carry out each of these functions in a horizontal and democratic process. The only differences are those determined by differing personal experiences and backgrounds. Each group studies, discusses, and decides by consensus or by vote. This system of dual authority empowers the elected group and also gives independence to each group working in its respective area. This continuous participation in the Institute encourages and permits a pluralism of ideas, both in terms of institutional affairs and in the realm of psychoanalytic theories. These ideas are freely debated, as an inherent part of the Institute’s functioning.


The period of dictatorship, 1973–85 The military dictatorship interrupted this process, bringing with it persecution and exile. Psychoanalysis was prohibited, and many psychoanalysts were expelled from the area of health, education, and culture, where formerly psychoanalysis had developed with great energy and vigour. Some analysts were forced into exile; others took refuge within the UPA Institute and their individual consulting offices, in what became known as “insile”, or internal exile. The Institute, in the moments of state terror and military dictatorship, was forced to invest great effort to maintain itself and its members during this period of forced retreat. Thus it was not easy for those who had worked so hard to maintain and preserve the analytic function to “dis-invest” themselves. Nevertheless, the Institute was able not only to maintain itself, but to consolidate and grow. After the return to democracy, it was not easy for people to “de-exile” and again participate in the social, scientific, and cultural life of the country.


Psychoanalysis in the face of new challenges, or psychoanalysis and post-modern paradigms At the end of the twentieth and beginning of the twenty-first centuries, cultural life and the fields of health and education seem to have developed and strengthened a resistance to psychoanalysis. Thus psychoanalysis finds itself confronting a crisis—perhaps a necessary one—inherent in the analytic project, in which we are always in a state of “reinventing ourselves”, with each patient, in the institutional context, and in the scientific and social arenas. It seems inevitable that this creative re-invention must take place in both a subjective and a social context, combining the history through which we have lived, the present in which we carry out our work, and the future that we are building.


Necessarily non-harmonic perspectives The UPA Institute offers a workplace for psychoanalysts with varying degrees of experience but who are all understood to be still “in training” and candidates who are also, in a certain sense, “analysts in training”. In the context of the Institute, these currents come together, merge, and diverge. Differing configurations take shape in accordance with different experiences and the different functions that each carries out in the Institute, but all share a state of “unrest” in relation to that which is unknown and that which is yet to become known, of an “uncomfortableness” that gives flight to the fantasies of the analyst, comfortably stretched out in his easy chair. The UPA Institute does not exist in harmonic relation with psychoanalysis. The existence of the Institute does not facilitate the needs of psychoanalysis, even if an analytic association defines itself as psychoanalytic—but not “of psychoanalysts”. Fantasies that exercise strong influence on the atmosphere of the Institute can result in a powerful resistance to the process of psychoanalytic discovery. Nonetheless, in our group, the importance of psychoanalytic training has been very important, as one formative pillar among many. The transmission of desire in search of the unknown can flower in an institutional hothouse, in spite of affective conflicts. The conflictedness of this transmission has characterized psychoanalysis from its very origins with Freud. These imaginary fantasies—in Lacan’s sense that all “imaginary” behaviour and relationships are irremediably deceptive—may transfer from the analyst’s couch to the life of the Institute, very often in the form of narcissistic remnants that resist change, but also as wisps of transference of desires that provoke new thinking, out of a potentially creative discomfort. This type of conflict within the group can nourish the theoretical passions that tend to cause division, the allegiance to one teacher or another, and the complacency that can at times result from “knowing the answers”; or it can motivate theoretical and practical investigation and creativity. The so-called “psychoanalytic adventure” does not fit well with the predominant characteristics of present-day society: a dizzy acceleration and fragmentation, by developments in the neurosciences and planetary globalization; but these challenges make the “analytic adventure” both more difficult and more attractive. It is in the work on the critical frontiers, navigating extremely precipitous terrain, to some extent touching this vital root, this resiliency of the human spirit, which we share with those who seek us out, that the analytic adventure really begins—an adventure both unique and institutional.








39Korea The Korean Psychoanalytic Movement and the IPA (1980–2010)

Do-Un Jeong

There is a place for us now—a place for Korean psychoanalysis. On 8 January 2011, we opened the office for the Korean Association of Psychoanalysis (the integrated body of the Korean Psychoanalytic Study Group and the Korean Psychoanalytic Allied Centre of the IPA). For the past 30 years, members have made donations into a “psychoanalysis development fund”, and we now have the office! After two months of hard work of renovation, the place is beautiful. For the next 30 years, it will be the location for the training and education of Korean psychoanalysts and psychotherapists. The new office symbolizes the tangible presence of Korean psychoanalysis. Would you be surprised to hear that on 15 August 1939 Koreans were informed by a major daily newspaper that Dr Sigmund Freud was seriously ill in bed in London? This means he was already an influential international figure for the Korean public to know about. With the liberation in 1945 from Japanese colonial occupation (1910–45) and the following Korean War in 1950 (with the consequent influx of the influence of American medicine), Koreans were more exposed to psychoanalysis in terms of war medicine (combat-related mental disorders) and its literature. As a reflection of enthusiasm about psychoanalysis, the Sigmund Freud Symposium in commemoration of his 101st birthday was held in Seoul in 1957. In the 1960s, psychotherapy programmes and individual supervisions were initiated in hospitals. With Korea’s rapid economic growth in the 1970s and 1980s, psychotherapy training became a requirement for board-certification for psychiatrists. This acceptance of psychotherapies was in response to economic growth and its consequences, such as the change in lifestyle and the shift from the extended family system to a nuclear family system concomitant with industrialization and urbanization. Despite a growing interest in psychoanalytic theories and practice, originally Koreans had no systematic access to these. On 6 May 1980, six psychiatrists gathered and founded the Seoul Psychoanalytic Study Group, in commemoration of Dr Sigmund Freud’s birthday. It was an informal group led by Doo-Young Cho, with no international recognition. With its birth, this small, vulnerable group found itself in the midst of strong competitors, such as Jungians and Tao psychotherapists. Being unknown to the international community of psychoanalysis, it was a very difficult task to invite foreign psychoanalysts to Seoul for our education. The first psychoanalysts who came to give lectures included Alan Fraser, Myunghee Kim, and Masahisa Nishizono in 1984, and the first seminar by an invited analyst, Dr Normund Wong, was held in 1985. In the same year, some members attended the IPA Congress for the first time. In order to be able to attend it, each member needed a recommendation letter written by an IPA member. Koreans really felt like “orphans” in the international community of psychoanalysis. And they were even invisible. Despite local national efforts, there seemed to be no future waiting for us. Self-studies of psychoanalytic literature, with occasional external input from invited foreign analysts, were helpful but did not really drive us in the right direction. We felt the limitation to the depth of our hearts. No access to psychoanalytic training at all was available within Korea. The Korean Group encouraged a few members to obtain training in the United States. Disappointingly, after coming back to Korea, they withdrew from the Korean Group. In 1988, the first Korean student, Do-Un Jeong, was accepted to the San Diego Psychoanalytic Institute. Despite the hidden frustrations, the early group was able to expand its membership. Members came to domestic seminars from across the nation, with some members travelling for hours by train or bus. In 1989, the Seoul Psychoanalytic Study Group was renamed the Korean Psychoanalytic Society, but it still had no formal relationship with the IPA. Since a significant proportion of the members were university professors with hospital appointments, naturally they were reminded that it would enhance the identity of the group to publish a psychoanalytic journal. We knew that we would not need permission from the IPA for this. We consoled ourselves with being psychoanalytic “scholars” rather than psychoanalysts. The first volume of the journal Psychoanalysis appeared in 1990. It is published bi-annually and now is available on the web as well as in printed form. The Korean psychoanalytic movement, after its 10-year effort, drew positive international attention. In 1991, the IPA recognized the Korean group as its first Guest Study Group. Being recognized as a Guest Study Group by the IPA still remains an unforgettable memory among senior members. However, essentially, a guest is a guest: a guest is not a member of the family. Nothing really happened to change us from a Guest Study Group into a formal Study Group. We were losing patience. Years passed. In 1995, in response to a plea made by us, the Korean situation was discussed seriously at the Asian Group meeting during the IPA Congress by the International New Groups (ING) Committee. At the conclusion of the meeting, we felt that progress had been made in the IPA’s understanding of our situation. However, again years passed with no real progress for us.
