Our efforts to invite one or two training analysts to reside in Seoul for training Koreans did not succeed. Despite some loss of morale, we Koreans persevered. In 2000, the Korean Guest Study Group initiated the Advanced Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy Programme (APPP). This two-year psychotherapist training programme was the first one in Korea. As an outreach programme to general psychiatrists, our programme has turned out to be very successful. It is now a major source of new members admitted to the Korean Allied Centre. It is also a valuable source of new candidates applying to the psychoanalytic training programme by the Korean Study Group. In 2010, a total of 22 applicants were accepted into the APPP. After all those years of compliance with the IPA’s guidelines and regulations, our members were losing patience. Unfulfilled waiting is very frustrating, and it was tempting at least to some members when an easier way was offered to them. In 2001, it was discovered that the practice of “telephone analysis” was being done for a couple of members by an IPA-appointed interim training analyst. This was reported to the IPA because the Korean group was firmly against “telephone analysis”. The identity we had pursued for so many years was not “the Korean Telephone Analytic Society”. This was a major crisis in the Korean group. The result, unfortunately, was that we lost the three members involved in “telephone analysis”. Also, during the turmoil, the process of recognizing and accepting two Korean members as Direct Members of the IPA was put on hold. In May of 2002, the IPA sent an exploratory committee to Seoul, Korea, and the contentious situation was slowly resolved. However, this difficult challenge strengthened and consolidated our group identity. We owe something to the crisis, since it facilitated the further progress of Korean psychoanalysis, exemplified by the organization of the Korea Advisory Committee in 2003. In 2002, Korean efforts at developing and spreading psychoanalysis attracted international attention. Prof. Doo-Young Cho, the founding president of the Korean group, became the first Asian recipient of the Mary S. Sigourney Award. In the same year, the group was accepted to the Korean Academy of Medical Sciences as a member society. In 2003, on the basis of the recommendation of the IPA exploratory committee on Korea, the Korea Advisory Committee of the IPA was organized, and its members were appointed. In retrospect, it was the best decision made by the IPA for the development of Korean psychoanalysis. At the time, the committee and the Koreans expected that two Korean members who had successfully finished evaluation would be recognized as Direct Members at the 2003 IPA Congress. Life, however, is unpredictable: the 2003 Congress was cancelled due to the SARS epidemic. Finally, in March 2004, Do-Un Jeong and Tak-Yoo Hong were recognized as Direct Members of the IPA. Do-Un Jeong was then appointed to the Allied Centre (AC) Committee of the IPA. And in the same year, the Korea Seminar Weekend was carefully planned and generously supported by the Korea Advisory Committee in San Francisco. About 30 Koreans attended and enjoyed the serious, insightful case discussions in small groups with the series of supervisions offered pro bono by more than 10 training analysts from the United States. This was enthusiastically repeated in 2005. We plan to replicate it within Korea at the 31st-Anniversary International Congress of the Korean group, to be held in Seoul on 23–25 September 2011. With two Direct Members, Koreans were on the way to becoming a Study Group. In 2005, Jaehak Yu was recognized as the third Direct Member. He was followed in 2007 by Mee-Kyung Kim and Moo-Suk Lee. Now we were ready, and the IPA sent a Site Visit Committee to Korea in 2007. In January 2008, I was in New York City at the American Psychoanalytic Association winter meeting. At this time there was a combined meeting of the ING and the Allied Centre committees. I joined it as an AC member. It was a very challenging and historical moment. The IPA was enthusiastic about granting Study Group Status to Korea. Uncertainty was raised, however, about granting Allied Centre status to the rest of the Korean group. This concern was understandable because having the Study Group and the Allied Centre within one country was exceptional and apparently raised certain issues. However, considering the long history of the Korean group and its other 145 nonanalyst members, I gathered the courage to say that the IPA should not simply abandon more than one hundred Koreans who had been actively serving as the members of the Korean Guest Study Group—that is, the first Guest Study Group of the IPA. I was pleased that my opinion was generously accepted by the IPA President, Cláudio Eizirik. It was a very significant tipping point in Korea’s further development of psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Eventually, the IPA Board approved the recommendation. The Korean Study Group was newly formed, and the Korean Allied Centre inherited the Guest Study Group. I understand that this “Korean model” is now being considered positively by other countries. The recognition and approval meant much to me, as I had witnessed the development of Korean psychoanalysis during the period 1985–2008. It was a moment of calm, with reflections from the past passing by like a film strip. Reflections were of both personal and group memories. It seemed to be a very happy ending. What if the status of the Korean Allied Centre had not been recognized at that meeting? It might have been a total disaster for the Korean psychoanalytic movement; it might even have produced a group of 145 members dedicated to opposing psychoanalysis. Even with the acquisition of Korean Allied Centre status, those who would stay in the Allied Centre were not pleased enough. At least some of them considered the Allied Centre status simply as another name for Guest Study Group status. Despite the current administrative support of the Allied Centre by the appointed Allied Centre Committee, a partial truth may reside in the feeling of not belonging truly to the IPA, as long as the Allied Centre remains essentially a group of non-analysts. Korean members experienced and shared those years together, making efforts to bring psychoanalysis, IPA recognition, and psychoanalytic training to Korea. Then, with the long-awaited recognition of the Korean Study Group, the group identity fell on the verge of a split between the Study Group and the Allied Centre. It naturally created tension. Wisdom was needed. The resolution came in late 2008; the Korean Association of Psychoanalysis (KAPA) was organized as the integrated body of the Korean Study Group and the Korean Allied Centre. The Study Group is expected to support the educational and training activities of the Allied Centre, and the Allied Centre provides resources to the Study Group. For example, members of the Allied Centre who have completed the 2-year Advanced Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy Programme are an excellent pool of future psychoanalytic candidates. The year 2008 was an epoch-making year. The Korea Sponsoring Committee of the IPA was organized, and it conducted its first site visit in July. We happily realized that our isolation of decades from the international community of psychoanalysis had finally ended! Psychoanalytic training by Koreans within Korea had been our dream. The dream came true in 2009. And the “true and tangible reality” is progressing smoothly. We now have nine second-year candidates and three advanced candidates. We plan to have another class admitted very soon. We also have a few other members in training outside Korea, such as in San Diego and Seattle. On looking back now, despite the difficult experiences we have had, we feel that we have been blessed. On the way to the “here and now” of Korean psychoanalysis, we have too many names to mention. We sincerely appreciate all those friends of Korean psychoanalysis who have been a major part of our history. Due to limited space in this chapter, I would recommend those readers interested in Korean psychoanalysis to refer to the following papers: “Korea” by Doo-Young Cho (1995) and “Psychoanalysis in Korea” by Do-Un Jeong and David Sachs (2009). Korean psychoanalysis has grown from non-existence into a thriving community. Personally, I have observed the development of Korean psychoanalysis for the past 25 years, as well as being an active participant. One major issue I would like to raise in this chapter is the issue of speed. In this twenty-first century, everything changes so rapidly, whereas psychoanalytic training does not. In the process of development, Korean psychoanalysis has lost a substantial number of potentially excellent psychoanalysts. And the influential members are growing older. The younger generation needs more education, training, and experience. What if the development of Korean psychoanalysis by the IPA as well as by the Koreans had been more tailored and speedy? We might have lost fewer members and retained many more in our group. After many years, Korea has emerged from the unhappy side of the “psychoanalytic divide” and has successfully shifted to the other side. In the process, during the past thirty years we have been able to invite more than 50 analysts from abroad. We appreciate the economic growth of Korea during this period. It has also enabled us to attend congresses and to have personal training abroad. While there is much satisfaction in our achievement, we need to be vigilant to the emerging issues facing contemporary psychoanalysis. We have to find ways to deal with the realities of the powerful influences of biological psychiatry and neuroscience. Otherwise, we might lose ground in recruiting analytic thinkers and future analysts from the next generation. In Korea, we are continuing to lose the academic positions that our members have held in universities and general hospitals and consequently the opportunities to expose the trainees to psychoanalytic conceptions and practices. Since the foundation of the Korean group in 1980, we have travelled a long, winding road. Our history is a success story of which we are proud. Now we expect further developments over the next 30 years, free from the difficulties of the past. We also expect the expansion of psychoanalysis into disciplines other than medicine. It is an exciting journey. Bibliography Cho, D.-Y. (1995). Korea. In: Peter Kutter (Ed.), Psychoanalysis International: A Guide to Psychoanalysis throughout the World, Vol. 2: America, Asia, Australia, Further European Countries. Stuttgart-Bad Cannstatt: Frommann-Holzboog. Jeong, D.-U., & Sachs, D. (2009). Psychoanalysis in Korea. In: Salman Akhtar (Ed.), Freud and the Far East: Psychoanalytic Perspectives on the People and Culture of China, Japan, and Korea. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson.







6Greece The Hellenic Psycho-Analytical Society

Sotiris Manolopoulos

We chronicle the history of our Society by recording and revisiting the events that marked the developments of psychoanalysis in Greece. We also co-create its history with colleagues by repeating and reinterpreting, in the present, past events and experiences—rituals, procedures, rules, and regulations—that continue to influence our institutional culture. Events surrounding early attempts to found a psychoanalytic group in Greece have been recorded by Tzavaras (1984), Kontos, Manolopoulos, and Kouretas (1992), and Hartocollis (2000). Although the medical profession ignored Freud’s ideas, these found their way to Greek scholars through a paper, “The Beginning of Speech and Freudian Psychology”, by a linguist and educator, M. Triantafyllidis (1915). Between the two world wars and afterwards, it was women and men of letters who were interested in psychoanalytic ideas, which were perceived as a “bourgeois” ideology (Moraitis, 1930). A. Empeirikos [1907–75], a surrealist poet, returned to Athens in 1931, after his analysis in Paris with R. Laforgue, and began to practise as a lay analyst. D. Kouretas [1904–84], received his psychiatric training in France and began to lecture on psychoanalysis after returning to Greece in 1927. In 1940, G. Zavitzianos [1909–95], a psychiatrist, began to write on the psychoanalytic treatment of children. He joined the two others wishing training in psychoanalysis. The first attempt to establish a psychoanalytic group in Greece occurred in 1946 when Princess Marie Bonaparte, a Greek by marriage, spent time in her Athens home, where she held seminars and supervision sessions with A. Empeirikos, D. Kouretas, and G. Zavitzianos. Anna Freud’s visit and lecture at the Athens Medical School in January 1949 marked the official launching of the group. With her support and the help of E. Jones, the three Greek analysts became associate members of the Paris Society and the International Psychoanalytical Association. This group lasted only a year, perhaps because it was a gift that came from outside instead of being the result of internal processes (Potamianou, 1988). Hostility from the medical profession made Empeirikos leave for Paris, and he never practised again. Zavitzianos moved to the United States and Canada, where he became known for his writings on perversions. Kouretas became a professor of psychiatry in Athens and continued to practise and to write on the roots of psychoanalysis in ancient Greece. However, the Centre of Mental Health and Research, founded by A. Potamianou in 1956, invited prominent analysts from abroad—W. Niederland, R. Spitz, M. Mahler, J. Lacan, M. Schur, S. Lebovici, and R. Diatkine—to give lectures and hold seminars. In 1967 a second effort to establish psychoanalysis in Greece, called the “seminar of Thassos”, was planned as a regular meeting, with the intention of becoming a training activity from which an analytic group would be formed and eventually be recognized by IPA. However, this seminar was never realized as planned. In 1974 a third attempt was made when the IPA President, S. Lebovici, appointed a “Committee for the promotion of psychoanalysis in Greece and Yugoslavia”. P. Sakellaropoulos (Paris) and A. Alexandris (Canada) were approached with the plan to establish a Study Group consisting of six members, two of whom would be the training analysts, each with two candidates. In October 1974 the IPA committee (chaired by A. Limentani) visited Athens and interviewed potential candidates. Apparently the IPA proposed stricter criteria, which were rejected by the two Greek analysts. It became necessary to wait for other Greek analysts to complete their training abroad and return. From 1967 to 1974 the reign of the Greek junta forced clinicians to move abroad to seek training. During the 1970s and 1980s Greece moved towards democracy, joined the European Community, and began to become an open society. Four new departments of psychiatry were developed in general hospitals, and child psychiatry was recognized as a separate speciality. Given such favourable conditions, young clinicians trained abroad began to return to Greece.

The Study Group (1982–97) In 1980, P. Hartocollis, from the Menninger Clinic, took the Chair of Psychiatry at Patras University, and was soon joined by S. Beratis (New York Psychoanalytic Institute). He was approached in 1981 by S. Lebovici, who then wrote to the president of the IPA, A. Limentani, regarding the founding of a Study Group composed of Alexandris, Potamianou, Beratis, Hartocollis, and Sakellaropoulos. After a positive report following a fact-finding visit in June 1982 by J. Sandler, the Hellenic Group received a letter of recognition from the IPA on 11 October 1982. Under the guidance of the Sponsoring Committee (J. Sandler, J. de Saussure, S. Mentzos) the group began to accept its first candidates. This new effort to establish psychoanalysis in Greece was welcomed by young professionals, who had grown up in a culture searching for change, knowledge, and freedom of thought. After 1983, Fr. Carapanou [1909–2002], I. Kontos (Frankfurt), and others returned to become members of the group. The evolution from movement to organization brought conflicts inherent in the transmission of knowledge. In 1983 J. Sandler resigned, because he had undertaken other responsibilities within the IPA. J. Chasseguet-Smirgel joined the committee; she served until 1991, when she was replaced by D. Pines; A.-M. Sandler replaced J. de Saussure. These changes and the fact that the committee consisted of analysts from different psychoanalytic cultures contributed to the difficulties that had to do with the issue of who makes what decisions regarding educational and scientific activities. Four years later, in 1987, one member resigned and another also distanced himself from the group; they both disagreed about what was expected in order for them to move from the status of a provisional to a regular training analyst. The remaining three training analysts tried to respond to the needs of the candidates, and the group continued its activities with success. However, pressure remained on the group to have an adequate number of training analysts in order to become a Provisional Society. The worry was that the long delay in achieving this status might create barriers between members and create discouragement among students.


The Provisional Society (1997–2000) In 1996 the Sponsoring Committee approved the appointment of Th. Vergopoulo [1931–2007] (trained in Geneva) as a training analyst. Meanwhile one of the group’s members, N. Tzavaras, progressed towards becoming a training analyst, and his appointment was approved by the Sponsoring Committee in 1997. Finally, in July 1997, 15 years after the Study Group had been founded, it became possible to gain recognition as a Provisional Society, which was granted at the 40th IPA Congress in Barcelona. At that time, the Hellenic Psycho-Analytical Society (HPS) had 14 members (8 full and 6 associate), 8 corresponding members (IPA analysts of Greek descent practising abroad), 2 honorary members (S. Lebovici and H. Blum), and 30 candidates. The Society slowly developed, and in 1998 N. Kouretas (Boston) became a training analyst.


The Component Society (2000–) In 2001 the Hellenic Society became an IPA Component Society with the support of the IPA Liaison Committee (A.-M. Sandler, H. Leupold-Löwenthal). As the Society grew and developed, it became necessary to make changes in its constitution and bylaws in order to enable it to democratically govern itself. Accordingly, the bylaws were revised, and a Code of Ethics was drawn up. Changes were also made to help associate members to progress more easily towards becoming full members and training analysts. Soon new training analysts were appointed: G. Costoulas (Geneva) in 2002 and M. Aisenstein (Paris) in 2005. In 2003 S. Beratis, A. Potamianou, and S. Manolopoulos were recognized as child analysts by the IPA. Everything began to move quickly. New members became training analysts. The Education Committee gave full members the function of conducting training analyses when this was judged to be necessary (due to the lack of available training analysts). By the autumn of 2010 there were 22 full members, 12 of them training analysts, 16 associate members, and 3 honorary members (H. Blum, N. Nicolaidis, S. Mentzos). The Society had 41 candidates in training, 12 candidates who had completed their training and were soon expected to become associate members, and 10 new professionals who had been accepted and were waiting to begin their training. It cannot be said that there is a crisis in psychoanalysis in Greece.


Scientific activities Since 1983, the HPS has organized conferences, symposia, and lectures in an effort to introduce the specific research contributions of the speakers in order to further develop and enrich the clinical and theoretical work of Greek psychoanalysts and students. This effort to present a plurality of ideas is evident in the list of invited speakers (A. Green, O. Kernberg, J. McDougall, M. Fain, M. Parsons, H. Segal, J. Amati Mehler, M. and E. Laufer, C. and S. Botella, R. Rousillon, among others). Finally, along with monthly scientific meetings, a symposium is held annually, with an invited guest speaker. A committee undertakes the publication of monographs of the works presented at the scientific meetings and the symposia. In collaboration with international bodies, the HPS has organized, in Athens, the European Psychoanalytical Federation training analysts’ meeting (October 2001); the 19th Annual EPF meeting on “Transformations” (April 2006); and the 70th Congrès des Psychanalyse de Langue Française, with the theme, “Entre psychè et soma” (May 2010). In July 1984 the International Delphi Psychoanalytic Symposium was inaugurated by Greek and Canadian analysts. Held every four years, it is a prestigious gathering where psychic and social issues are explored by psychoanalytic authors and scholars of the ancient Hellenic spirit. During the 1996 Symposium, at the crossroads near Delphi, where Oedipus is said to have killed his father, a memorial inscribed with an excerpt from Sophocles’ Oedipus Rex was inaugurated by P. Hartocollis and the Governor of the Province of Boiotia.


Outreach activities Through its newsletter and website the HPS reaches out to mental health professionals. It also offers a course of introductory lectures on psychoanalysis for young clinicians. Annual joint meetings with scholars from other fields—history, journalism, politics—and with featured themes such as, “Violence in the city”, “Narcissism in societies”, “Xenos”, and “A discussion with actors and audience of a play in a theatre” are also organized. Some members of the HPS serve as professors and consultants in universities and in the National Health System.


The issues The members of the HPS have defined issues around which they find themselves in disagreement, in an effort to resolve them. For example, the relationships among the Education Committee, Executive Council, and General Assembly have been complex. Any changes in the psychoanalytic orientation of training need the approval of the General Assembly. Some conflicts around educational issues have become intense. “Fear” was the keyword in many meetings. Some felt that there was a fear in the candidates and members; some felt that the source of fear was the despotism of one senior member. The question was whether the Society could function as a “third” that represented and safeguarded impartial standards, or whether it became submerged in narcissistic problems. Procedures could be under pressure to lose their “externality” and become sites onto which idealizations were projected. At times an intense antagonism with regard to power has provoked repetitions of unmetabolized experiences that had not been worked through. As a result members were apprehensive about presenting their ideas and instead wrote “defensive” papers. For this reason “Greek” psychoanalysis has not begun to develop. A question has been raised regarding the pre-selection of candidates as required by the Eitingon model. Some members, trained in France, question this model. Metapsychology and the theory of drives are also a subject of controversy within the HPS. Research, qualitative vs. empirical, is another controversial issue; the relevance of neuro-science is also debated. Another contentious issue is psychotherapy. Some believe that as one learns psychoanalysis, so one also learns psychotherapy as a modification of its technique according to the requirements posed by specific psychic organizations. Others think that the HPS should organize formal training for psychotherapists in addition to undertaking training on psychotherapy for candidates. The HPS is not a member of any psychotherapy society. In Greece there are local national “societies” that have training programmes for the psychotherapy of adults, children, and adolescents. But there is neither public insurance covering the cost of psychotherapy, nor any legal regulation of the practice of psychotherapy. * * * In chronicling our history, I used Freud’s (1912–13) insights to help me reflect on the links between our primitive psyche and our political institutions. Since its inception, the psychoanalytic movement has needed to be organized because not all experiences could be symbolized and internalized. Administrative instruments—rules, procedures, and so forth—were invented in order to regulate relationships at the points where thinking and internalization processes could not adequately transform reality. Psychoanalysis’ two aspects, as a movement and an organization, are in conflict; this is especially true in Greece, a country with deficits in organization. These deficits are manifested in populism and corruption that have replaced the repressive tendencies of the rural society Greece used to be. They function as defences against the acceptance of the authority of whoever is responsible to make decisions. Analysts examine how their media (institutions) convey “messages” beyond verbal meanings, the shadows of heritage—unmetabolized elements expressed in institutional practices (EPF, 2008). Members’ characters are reflected in the culture of the institutions they organize with instruments they invent as an extension of their characters. In creating such “frames”, they effect a splitting that allows a space where they go on transforming reality: they use paradigms of thinking in order to understand what they do not know. Their institutions become sites where non-differentiated elements are split and deposited (Bleger, 1967). As analysts, we are “born” in the “middle of a discussion”: we undertake subjectively our heritage as we become subjects of our psychic realities. We try to construct an ethical public space that allows the tragic sense and recognition of otherness. However, we wonder whether our procedures are not an avoiding of real changes. By setting rules, a group closes its unelaborated experiences in an area of trauma (the institution). The question is whether this area is available to be visited from time to time for doing some work of elaboration. Institutional activities give us time and space in order to revisit such areas and wonder if at certain moments we could have transformed our realities in a symbolic way instead of taking various measures.

Bibliography Bleger, J. (1967). Psycho-analysis of the psycho-analytic frame. International Journal of Psychoanalysis, 48: 511–519. EPF (2008). The Shadows of Heritage. European Psychoanalytical Association, 21st Annual Conference, Vienna. Freud, S. (1912–13). Totem and Taboo. Standard Edition, 13. Hartocollis, P. (2000). Report from abroad. A letter from Greece. Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, 48: 675–679. Kontos, J., Manolopoulos, S., & Kouretas, N. (1992). Greece. In P. Kutter (Ed.), Psychoanalysis International: A Guide to Psychoanalysis Through the World, Vol. 2. Stuttgart: Frommann-Holzoboog. Moraitis, D. (1930). Individual Psychology and Its Applications on Psychotherapy and Education. Athens: Private publication. Potamianou, A. (1988). Episkepsis. Pensées autour de la visite d’Anna Freud à Athènes. Revue Internationale d’ Histoire de la Psychanalyse, 1: 247–254. Triantafyllidis, M. (1915). The beginning of speech and Freudian psychology. Bulletin of the Educational Society, 5: 219–231. [in Greek] Tzavaras, A. (Ed.) (1984). Psychoanalysis in Greece. Athens: The Society of Modern Greek Culture and General Education Studies. [in Greek]







15Sweden A short history of the Swedish Psychoanalytical Society

David Titelman

Freud’s thought was anticipated in Sweden in the work of the famous Swedish playwright and novelist August Strindberg. In the late nineteenth century he wrote penetrating dramas, novels, and short stories the themes of which were religious doubt, the relationship between the sexes, and the father and his position in the family. This antecedent notwithstanding, psychoanalysis was introduced in Sweden in an ambiguous way. Two independent pioneers, Emanuel af Geijerstam, who worked in Gothenburg between 1898 and 1928, and Poul Bjerre, active in Stockholm and its immediate vicinity during the first five decades of the twentieth century, were united in their ambivalent attitude towards psychoanalysis. Geijerstam was a scientist and psychotherapist who felt a kinship with Alfred Adler and Carl Gustav Jung. Poul Bjerre, who met Freud in 1910, introduced some of Freud’s ideas to the Swedish Society of Physicians, and translated some of Freud’s articles in 1924, was convinced that his own ideas were more important than those of Freud; he believed that Freud’s allegedly mechanistic views missed the essential points of his own, alternative school of psychotherapy. This ambivalence, combined with a massive anti-psychoanalytic campaign among Swedish psychiatrists at the beginning of the twentieth century (Johansson, 1999b, 1999c), was the likely background for the note that Freud added in 1923 to “On the History of the Psycho-Analytic Movement”: “At the present time Scandinavian countries are still the least receptive” (Freud, 1914d, p. 34). In August 1931, a group of clinicians interested in psychoanalysis met to discuss the establishment of a psychoanalytic society in Scandinavia. Alfhild Tamm, Sweden’s first woman psychiatrist and since 1926 a member of the Psychoanalytic Society in Vienna, became chairperson in 1934 of the newly established Finnish–Swedish Psychoanalytical Society. Ludwig Jekels, a student of Freud’s, resided in Stockholm for almost three years, from 1935 to 1938; he then returned to Vienna, only to face the Nazi invasion and the need to move again; he first moved first to Australia and then to the United States (Leupold-Löwenthal, 2009). During the same period, Scandinavians travelled to Vienna, Berlin, and Zurich to undergo analysis with, among others, August Aichhorn, Helene Deutsch, Paul Federn, Eduard Hitschmann, and Oskar Pfister. The Nordic psychoanalysts educated and organized themselves according to the standards of the International Psychoanalytical Association, while Bjerre and others started alternative psychotherapeutic organizations, which were usually antagonistic to psychoanalytic theories of sexuality and dreams. As a result of the growth of Nazism and the Second World War, the Dutch psychoanalyst René de Monchy settled in Stockholm. He had married the Swedish psychoanalyst Vera Palmstierna in Vienna, where she was in analysis with Freud. A key person in Dutch psychoanalysis with a personal contact with Freud, de Monchy played a dominant role during his eight years in Sweden. He was the analyst of Ola Andersson, whose 1962 dissertation, “Studies on the Prehis-tory of Psychoanalysis”, became an internationally recognized contribution to the history of psychoanalysis and the most influential psychoanalytic work from Sweden. Andersson uncovered the historical and philosophical context of the development of Freud’s ideas until 1896—that is, to the point when psychoanalysis began to take form as an independent discipline. He also carried out original research leading to the verification of the true identity of Freud’s patient Emmy von N. Another of de Monchy’s analysands was the Hungarian-born psychologist Lajos Szekely [1904–95]. Szekely, who had come to Sweden as a Jewish refugee via the Soviet Union and Finland with his wife Edith and his daughter Mirjam Wolff—both of whom are psychoanalysts—had started his analytic training in Holland and completed it in Stockholm. For five decades, Szekely played an important role in the training of Swedish psychoanalysts and published articles on creativity and the unconscious in English, French, German, Hungarian, and Swedish. Stefi Pedersen [1908–80] started her psychoanalytic training at the Psychoanalytical Institute in Berlin. Her first analysis was with Otto Fenichel, whom she followed to Oslo 1933. In 1943 she escaped from the Nazis over the high mountains between Norway and northern Sweden. She was active in what now became the Swedish Psychoanalytical Society (SPS)—the Swedish and Finnish psychoanalytic groups became separate societies in 1943—and took up a position of an independent outsider within the Swedish Society. Stefi Pedersen maintained an intellectual kinship and personal ties with such seemingly different psychoanalytic thinkers as Alexander Mitscherlich in Frankfurt and Margaret Little in London. She published articles and books in German, English, Norwegian, and Swedish on narcissism and humiliation and the psychological consequences of political persecution, anti-Semitism, and exile. In August 1943, Tor Ekman [1887–1971] returned to Sweden after almost twenty years in Leipzig and Berlin. In Leipzig, he had held a position as lecturer at the university. Ekman was trained by Therese Benedek and was close to Alfhild Tamm. Until his death he had an influential position in the Swedish Psychoanalytical Society. His publications, however, were few. A similarly prominent position in the Swedish Society was held by Carl Lesche, a philosopher of science who was born in Finland and had moved to Sweden in the early 1950s. Lesche was influenced by Husserl and Dilthey. A primary focus of his work was the nature of psychoanalysis as a scientific discipline. Lesche delineated what in his view made psychoanalysis a hermeneutical discipline in contrast to natural science. He also thought it was essential to differentiate psychoanalysis from psychotherapy. Anna-Stina Rilton is another clinician and teacher of the same generation as Szekely, Pedersen, and Lesche, who played an important role in the training of Swedish psychoanalysts in the post-war decades. After the Second World War, the second generation of psychoanalysts, represented by Bo Larsson and Andras Pöstenyi, included philosophically minded Swedish psychoanalysts influenced by Lesche, who were teachers of new generations of Swedish psychoanalysts. Larsson was the first editor of The Scandinavian Psychoanalytical Review, 1978–1979, a biannual English-language journal that continues to be published jointly by the Nordic psychoanalytic societies. Other influential psychoanalytic teachers and leaders of the Swedish Society and Institute belonging to the second post-war generation are Birgitta Ejve, Imre Szecsödy, and Ulf Tidén (who died in 1997). Throughout its history, many members of the Swedish Psychoanalytical Society have been engaged in psychiatry, public health, and preventive medicine. At the child psychotherapeutic clinic, Ericastiftelsen [The Erica Foundation], founded in 1934, a psychoanalytically oriented training programme in child psychotherapy was established and continues to be offered today. The first medical leader of the clinic was Gunnar Nycander, who was trained in the Swedish–Finnish Psychoanalytic Institute. His successors, beginning with Gösta Harding in 1945, have usually been members of the Swedish Psychoanalytical Society. In the 1950s, a group of psychoanalysts led by Thorsten Sjövall worked as consultants to an abortion clinic in Stockholm. The needs of the social workers and nurses at the clinic led to the establishment of a psychoanalytically oriented training programme in psychotherapy. Eventually the clinic’s name was changed, first to The Bureau for Mental Health, and subsequently to the Stockholm County Council Institute of Psychotherapy. The training programme was eventually integrated with a corresponding programme offered by the Department of Clinical Neuroscience at the Karolinska Institutet medical university. It is a sad sign of the times—and to some extent perhaps also of a lasting, strong anti-psychoanalytic sentiment in Swedish psychiatry and, today, neuroscience—that this psychoanalytically oriented programme was closed in 2008 and the Institute of Psychotherapy dismantled in 2009. From the late 1960s to about 1990, the membership of the Swedish Psychoanalytical Society grew steadily. In 1982 and 1986, in response to problematic group dynamics and to the Society’s authoritarian culture, which generated a collective sense of deadlock and diminished creativity, the two presidents of the Society during this period, Bo Larsson and Birgitta Eijve, respectively, asked the IPA to provide consulting visits. These interventions led to a strengthening of the Society’s democratic organization and to a more open intellectual climate. Since its inception, the orientation of the Swedish Psychoanalytical Society has been mainstream Freudian. However, in the late 1970s, a group of Swedish psychoanalysts invited Herbert Rosenfeld of London to lead clinical seminars in Stockholm, which inspired a wave of interest in Kleinian thought among Swedish psychoanalysts. The only previous significant link between Melanie Klein and Sweden was the fact that in 1920, after the establishment of a Communist dictatorship in Hungary, she had accompanied her husband, Arthur Klein, to the provincial town of Säffle in western Sweden, where he worked for the large paper mill and corporation, Billeruds AB (Grosskurth, 1986). In 1988, Lars Sjögren and Ludvig Igra (both recently deceased) translated a selection of texts by Melanie Klein into Swedish. Sjögren subsequently published a biography of Freud. Together with Clarence Crawford and Bengt Warren, a member of the Swedish Psychoanalytical Association (see chapter 16), Sjögren in 1996 launched the largest psychoanalytic publication project in Sweden to date—an authorized, carefully edited new translation of Freud’s collected works, published by Natur och Kultur in Stockholm. In 1968, one of the members of the Society, Margit Norell, who was critical of the way the psychoanalytic training was organized, left the Society and formed the alternative Swedish Society for Holistic Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy. The “Holistic Society” initially found ideological support in the work of North American neo-Freudians; subsequently, it oriented itself towards the British object-relations schools, Klein, Bion, and then back to Freud. It also changed its name to the Swedish Psychoanalytical Association, and is today recognized by the IPA as a second, independent Society and Institute in Stockholm. During the last decade the two societies have collaborated closely, and plans have advanced to merge the two groups into one psychoanalytic organization. A training programme in child and adolescent psychoanalysis, initiated by the late Johan Norman, a previous president of the Swedish Psychoanalytical Society, is an example of one such collaboration and a centre of creativity in the Swedish Society. Professor Emeritus Rolf Sandell, a member of the Swedish Psychoanalytical Society and an internationally recognized research methodologist in clinical psychology, is a sought-after consultant for research into psychoanalysis and psychoanalytically oriented psychotherapy. He is one of the internationally best-known currently active Swedish psychoanalysts. In light of the history of psychoanalysis in Sweden and of the continued attacks against it in Swedish academia and in the media, and in light of Freud’s own downhearted thoughts about psychoanalysis in Scandinavia in 1923, the strength of the Swedish Psychoanalytical Society and the endurance and creativity of its members is striking. Whereas earlier difficult periods were characterized by miniscule size and vulnerability, stalemates in the climate of the Society, dissent and splintering, and, in recent years, a gradual decline of the membership from about 230 to fewer than 200 members, the immediate challenge today is to reintegrate with the other IPA Society in Stockholm (currently The Psychoanalytic Association). This project too—it is a marriage of sorts—will require all the creativity there is to maintain a united and robust psychoanalytic organization and to strengthen the standing of psychoanalysis itself in Sweden. Note This study leans heavily on the research by Per-Magnus Johansson, who is a Professor of the History of Ideas and Science at Gothenburg University as well as a psychoanalyst (Johansson, 1999a, 1999b, 1999c). A longer text on psychoanalysis in Sweden was published in The Freud Encyclopedia (Johansson & Titelman, 2002). Bibliography Andersson, O. (1962). Studies on the Prehistory of Psychoanalysis: The Etiology of Psychoneuroses and Some Related Themes in Sigmund Freud’s Scientific Writings and Letters 1886–1896. Stockholm: Svenska Bokför1aget/Norstedts. Freud, S. (1914d). On the history of the psycho-analytic movement. Standard Edition, 14: 7–66. Grosskurth, P. (1986). Melanie Klein: Her World and Her Work. New York: Knopf. Johansson, P. M. (1999a). Freuds psykoanalys, Vol. 1: Utgångspunkter [Freud’s psychoanalysis, Vol. 1: Points of departure]. Gothenburg: Daidalos. Johansson, P. M. (1999b). Freuds psykoanalys, Vol. 2: Arvtagare i Sverige [Freud’s psychoanalysis, Vol. 2: Inheritors in Sweden, part 1]. Gothenburg: Daidalos. Johansson, P. M. (1999c). Freuds psykoanalys, Vol. 3: Arvtagare i Sverige [Freud’s psychoanalysis, Vol. 3: Inheritors in Sweden, part 2]. Gothenburg: Daidalos. Johansson, P. M., & Titelman, D. (2002). Sweden and psychoanalysis. In: E. Erwin (Ed.), The Freud Encyclopedia (pp. 553–557). New York: Routledge. Leupold-Löwenthal, L. (2009). Ludwig Jekels (Jekeles). Retrieved 1 December 2009 from: www.answers.com/topic/jekels-jekeles-ludwig







48The IPA administration from 2001 to 2005

Daniel Widlöcher

In July 2001, the situation of the International Psychoanalytical Association did not seem to present any real difficulties. The recent creation of the House of Delegates ensured a new structure that was considered almost more democratic than before. Still, were we going to follow a road towards regionalization? And what should we do about the request of Latin American societies to authorize analyses under supervision at a frequency of three sessions a week? Was the development of psychoanalysis in Asia and in Eastern Europe still on the agenda? All these were questions that we were going to be able to consider in all serenity! So, as was the custom, in Nice, at the end of July 2001, the new Council met. This was the last Council that convened, in the traditional way, half a dozen vice-presidents and the Executive Board, composed of a president, a secretary general and a treasurer, assisted by three associate secretaries chosen by the president. But at that time we were supposed to “govern” with a House of Delegates—one that was quite determined to control everything, without quite knowing what there was to control! And the next era was already beginning—that is, two houses would become a single assembly, carefully controlled by regional leaders, fully determined to exert their double authority on both a central power, which would be reduced to a consulting and co-ordinating function (a kind of UN general secretary), and on local societies. During Otto Kernberg’s presidency I had written a report on the role of regional structures, and while I remained strictly ultramontane I supported the notion that it was a good idea to give a greater representation to societies, the representation of members then being indeed very formal and limited to votes in elections meant to choose international authorities, which took place at one general assembly every second year. In fact, it was with the intention to develop intermediary structures that Serge Lebovici had created a presidents’ meeting during the congresses. Until then, component societies had had no “official” avenue of expression in the life of the IPA! From one excess, we were in danger of falling into the other. It is true that only the American Psychoanalytic Association (APsaA) had been recognized as a regional society, because since Freud the IPA authorities had very much wished to stay in direct contact with their members. In my report I pleaded for a dual movement—democratic on the one hand, concerning the definition of the goals we wished to reach, and centralizing on the other, for the accomplishment of these goals. In other words, it was necessary to listen to the societies’ presidents and to the regions regarding the decisions to be taken, but also to let the central power have a free hand to carry them out. But this position did not take into account the forces that were hostile to internationalism. When I stood as a candidate for the IPA presidency, I had no idea what was simmering. I did know that there was a political and an ideological conflict about all this—briefly, it was called “research in psychoanalysis”. Those of us who were concerned about the role of psychoanalysis in hospitals and in universities were more and more aware of the strenuous efforts that awaited us if we wanted to maintain the legitimacy of psychoanalytic practice and knowledge in a scientific world devoted to a naturalistic and quantifiable approach. There were many colleagues, especially in France, who were determined to place themselves outside the field of positivistic science and at the service of pure individual subjectivity. Joseph Sandler made the decision to organize an annual meeting in London, within the university framework, on the issue of research in psychoanalysis, and he immediately invited me to take part in it. He knew that at the Salpêtrière hospital I had launched empirical research that took into account a psychopathological perspective and the action of anti-depressive agents and investigated a few hypotheses that had also been taken up by Sandler and Walter Joffé, inspired by Edward Bibring and George Engels, on the concept of basic depressive inhibition. Psychoanalysts in American or European universities were immediately enthusiastic, and these meetings soon became very successful. I think here of Sidney Blatt, of R. Sandell’s group in Stockholm, of Marianne Bohleber-Leuzinger in Frankfurt, of the group in Zürich. Kernberg, by inviting me to succeed Wallerstein, anticipated that I would pursue action in favour of scientific politics. This is what I did, having at my disposal quite large funds, while at the same time watching budgetary limits, thereby circumventing critics and tricky questions as to the general programme. I thought it best to underline that research should not be limited to an objectifying and quantifying approach, but that a place should be left to critical conceptual research in the perspective announced by Sandler. In the general framework of research (and of its budget!), I suggested that we should create two different committees, one devoted to empirical and quantitative studies, which would remain under the authority of Peter Fonagy and in the spirit of the White Book he had just published, the other aimed more towards critical studies and to reflective and historical studies, under the authority of Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber. This last committee has, from that time on and until today, devoted a great number of meetings to this historical and conceptual reflection. Let me add to these programmes those concerned with the study of psychoanalytic training and with treatment requests, started by my second secretary, Don Campbell. But all these great debates came to life only during the international conventions, where general decisions were taken. During the weekly course of work with the general secretary and secretaries dispatched between Paris and London, only common matters could be treated with the 60 or so constituent societies and their 3,000 members! So, first I did all this work with Alain Gibeault and during the two following years with a British colleague, Don Campbell. Very naturally, I had first wished to choose a secretary who would be French, like me, but a member of the “other Society”. André Green recommended Gibeault, whom I had known for a long time because of my relationship with the Centre de Santé Mentale of the XIIIth district in Paris. I had appreciated the way in which he had, before me, taken up the role of associate secretary of the IPA, and his presidency of the European Federation. Since he was warm and naturally kindly, I imagined I would renew the team I had shared with Serge Lebovici long ago. But he was hesitant and did not answer until the last moment. Later I understood that his unwillingness was due do his work load, which he did not want to make any lighter in order to face new tasks. At half mandate, I suggested to him that he step down, which he accepted with understanding and with much generosity. The choice of Don Campbell was inspired by completely different reasons. Delicate missions, which I will mention further on, prompted me to look for a secretary outside the French environment. I needed to know the sensitivities of the very influential British Society and to go hand in hand with it in order to arrive successfully at the end of my term of office. English friends recommended Don Campbell, whom I did not know, but very quickly our work together seemed easy. It is true that on important issues we negotiated our agreement. Don Campbell wished to give a more clinical touch to research politics and to launch a research project dedicated to the causes and effects of the decrease in candidates and in cases for analysis for candidates in supervision. The goal was to invite societies to make local enquiries on these important demographic matters. I agreed to this project and left him all the freedom he needed to accomplish it; this he did successfully, and it was taken up by the administration that succeeded us. On the other hand, I needed his neutrality in the discussion about rules of training, and the acknowledgment of some flexibility in this area—an attitude that hurt the very orthodox convictions of a staunch Briton like himself. But let us return to everyday life. I had to be informed of the scientific activities in the whole world and to take part in them as much as possible. There was not a month when I did not travel abroad, outside France. I also had to prepare and chair activities that were specific to the IPA, mainly the bi-annual convention. The 2003 Congress had been planned in Toronto, in order to respect the rotating order of the three continents. With Gibeault I wanted to give this convention a clinical aspect and have the greatest number of our colleagues talk about their clinical practice, about the diversity of their methods. Ricardo Bernardi of Montevideo was preparing a thesis on the history of the debates between psychoanalytic schools, based mainly on the discussions that had brought together Kleinians and Lacanians on the Rio de la Plata in the 1960s. At the same time as our group in Paris was giving up Lacan, students who had remained true to him were starting a campaign of propagation, particularly in Latin America. With Ricardo, we therefore decided to dedicate a congress to a pluralistic debate centred on unique clinical material. Our friends from Ulm, Helmut Thomä and Horst Kächele, agreed to submit for an intensive discussion some fragments of a clinical case that they had just published. But then the SARS flu arrived—not the avian flu but the porcine flu, with its host of alarms in the media and in the newspapers. Were we going to face a viral epidemic catastrophe, like the one still in our memories: the Spanish flu of 1918? Should we be reminded of the sanitary and social chaos of the war and of its consequences? The times were not the same, but the panic spread out on all front pages made it look that way. Was Canada immune? We suddenly hear that two cases are registered at Toronto airport! Panic is here to stay. I receive letters, complaints, and threats, particularly from the United States and from Europe, from countries most exposed to information on television. The Toronto convention has to be cancelled, since this a country, as anyone can see, that is terribly contaminated. We are heading towards a fiasco. Of course, great expense had been incurred, but what does that amount to in view of the death of hundreds of analysts? I am slow in answering, also, because my feeble medical knowledge makes me think that the April and May sun, even in Canada, will have silenced the virus for some time. This is indeed what happened in the end, but unfortunately I had to make a decision. My American legal advisers, whom I had consulted, were wise enough to explain to me that in the case of an epidemic among psychoanalysts, the latter (or at least the survivors!) could accuse me personally of carelessness and sue me. I did not have to hear any more. I gave up. We cancelled the Toronto Congress (the first cancelled congress since 1912, with the exception of the First and Second World Wars!) And now I had to pay the money back, not with my pocket money, but with IPA funds (many reservations, working capital funds, expenses already incurred). And what about my convention! The one at half-term is really one’s own. The one before was organized by the preceding administration and the last is “offered” by the following administration. When should I hold the replacement convention? And where? I considered that we should stay in North America and within a few months’ time. It had to take place on the same continent, but six months later! We needed the South. We thought of New Orleans. It is a place of transition, summer during winter, it has something European about it. For the French it was a marvellous opportunity to visit the old French quarter and taste the spicy Creole cuisine. But psychoanalysts from the United States do not much like the South—something I discovered right then: New Orleans is not meant for them. Fortunately, this city attracts psychoanalysts from the South, and in March of the following year, 2004, the convention was a great success, even if the numbers were not there. Why the numbers? A convention’s general expenses are tremendous (simultaneous translation, hotel reservations). A great number of reservations are necessary in order to redeem the costs. It was a foregone conclusion that in these exceptional circumstances the balance sheet of the “New Orleans” Congress would show a deficit. On the other hand, the scientific dimension was respected. Thomä and Kächele’s clinical observations led to a rich discussion from the floor: and it is too bad that such a way of operating is so seldom used. We even had the opportunity to hold discussions with our Lacanian friends, Patrick Guyomard and Alain Vannier from Paris, and with American colleagues. This was the first encounter of this sort in the framework of the IPA. I do not think it would have been possible with other Lacanian-minded analysts. Unfortunately, there wasn’t enough time to have a considered reflection on the conditions that are necessary to make such a discussion possible. True, what would have been the most interesting would have been to study the negative conditions that do not allow this discussion: a dialogue with those with whom we cannot discuss. Another innovation was to invite a neurobiologist, Antonio Damasio, to give a talk. It was planned for one morning at 8:00 AM. I warned him the preceding evening that the hour proposed for his speech might explain the small number of participants. The room was full, questions broke out from all corners, and we had to hurry the end of the discussion. In short, neurobiological “wolves” were not missing in the sheep-fold, and I was happy to see that our usual colleagues welcomed them very heartily. Then we had the third convention—the one that was to close my presidency—which took place in Rio de Janeiro. Here, my mandate was ending, and Claudio Eizirik’s succession to the presidency was made very easy. Perhaps one of the most stimulating parts of my activity as president was to contribute to the development of many groups of psychoanalysts, particularly in Europe and in Asia. Of course, in the United States, old groups that were outside the IPA came to join us, and in Latin America, mainly through splits, we welcomed many groups originating from existing institutes. In these situations, as in similar ones in Europe, we had to admit that separation is better than a permanent state of fighting. Here, also, we had to keep an eye on the quality of clinical practice (and, therefore, on the good of the patients). The extension of our member societies also took another form: the return to the IPA of societies that had separated from it three or four decades earlier, generally immediately after the war. In fact, I found in the presidency of the IPA what I had experienced when the Association Psychanalytique de France (APF) had joined the IPA. These separations of old were in fact due to various causes, to Nazism in Germany and in Austria, but elsewhere to simple rivalries that had long been forgotten, as in Holland or in Sweden. During these years, the development of psychoanalysis in the former Soviet Union and satellite countries was perhaps the most notable event. Since that time, and for many years, I have kept in close contact with the societies of Central Europe. In Moscow itself, the situation was not simple. Some of “our” candidates had come to train in Paris at the Paris Psychoanalytical Society [Société Psychanalytique de Paris (SPP)], others had gone to Germany. Between one country and the next, objective rules for training were not quite the same, nor the standards of selection, nor, of course, the theoretical climate of the courses taught. Therefore, unavoidable rivalries came out into the open, leading to an unappealing collision between “French-style” psychoanalysis and psychoanalysis closer to the British atmosphere! Despite all our efforts to facilitate a respectful institutional pluralistic cooperation, I had to acknowledge the existence of two rival groups in a still difficult context. In order to facilitate a homogeneous support to all these Russian groups, I decided to create a federation that would assemble all the newly born—or newly reborn—psychoanalytic movements in the countries originating from the former Soviet Union and from some satellite countries that did not yet have well-formed groups. This Institute, which later came to be called the “Han Groen-Prakken Psychoanalytic Institute for Eastern Europe (PIEE)” (after a colleague from Amsterdam who played a very active part with candidates from Eastern Europe), still exists today. Difficulties began when the President of the European Federation (which played an active part with these groups in a scientific way) decided to manage the Institute, including its training functions. I saw this as a manoeuvre meant to entrust to the Federation a role in the training of candidates in this part of Europe, to take away this responsibility from the IPA, and then extend it to the whole of Europe. If successful, this would permanently transform the IPA into a federation of regional structures. I had to maintain with the greatest firmness the principle of a double structure: leaving the scientific life to the Federation but letting the IPA decide how to train candidates. This was in accordance with the IPA’s statutory principles, transgression of which would have opened the way to a regionalization of European psychoanalysis, at least in Eastern countries. In the Middle East, the situation was as complex, but for totally different reasons. In Turkey, it seemed that a competition was taking place between clinicians from Izmir, who had been taught by members of the APsaA, and psychoanalysts from Istanbul, trained by the Paris SPP. The latter—a few of them, at least—claimed that they had qualifications as members of an official society, and they challenged what they thought was an Americanization “made in USA” and, even worse, under the rule of a Turkish academic. So those from Istanbul had to split, although with the same origin for their training and despite all my efforts. The Training Committee I had created also had to split in order to support two rival groups. In Beirut, almost the opposite structure existed. There was only one Society—the Lebanese Society of Psychoanalysis—composed of clinicians from diverse trainings, most of them close to the Catholic University of Saint-Joseph, who were divided between orthodox Freudians trained at the SPP and “Lacanians” of various persuasions. Among the latter, some, of Muslim origin, wished to spread to surrounding Muslim countries (Syria, Jordan, Egypt) a psychoanalysis very much linked to the Lacanian way of thinking. Others wanted mainly to stay independent in the existing societies, particularly in French societies. Yet others asked for my help in order to create a group clearly linked to the IPA. At the start, all wished to maintain their unity. Faced with such opposite goals, was I to favour compromise or to facilitate clear and positive separations? This latter solution prevailed. All these local questions, and many others, required a great deal of time and many journeys to the region. I had to achieve, as best I could, a compromise that respected the wishes and the possibilities of each group—not to mention that from these new centres I had to keep an eye on what was transpiring locally in Central Asia, in the Caucasus, and around the Mediterranean! These were questions for tomorrow, but which I already had to consider today. Despite contacts already established, I am sorry that I was not able to give more help to what was taking shape in Alma Ata or in Erevan! Then there was the issue of the Far East. Between Japan and small groups in India, psychoanalysis did not seem to take its place very much in South East Asia. Nevertheless, a small group had formed in South Korea, with the support of American psychoanalysts from the West Coast. A minor scandal broke out when one of their colleagues who lived in Europe wanted not only to do supervisions but also conduct training analyses by telephone! Under pressure from the IPA Council, she had to give up, with grinding teeth. But that posed one question: what part would long-distance communication tools—telephone, television, the Internet, and so on—be able to play in the extension of analytic practices? Could they help take care of a patient in need? Yes, but could they give a candidate the means to practice by him- or herself? We needed to discuss this issue and initiate careful steps, which were at first criticized by most but which seem today to be more favourably accepted, following some “experiments” that were progressively attempted. It was in this context that some clinicians from Taipei, the capital of the island of Taiwan, first approached me. Some had personal experience of psychoanalysis, which they had acquired in Paris, London, or on the West Coast of the United States. But while the IPA Bylaws give a clear definition of the terms that allow a group to become an IPA Study Group, nothing is said about the way in which those terms should be fulfilled. Should we require either that a senior psychoanalyst should come to psychoanalyse a whole group (as was the case before the Second World War, particularly in Central Europe, despite totalitarian censorship) or that a group of young sympathizers find a way to emigrate to other countries for several years of training (which was the case after the war)? During that time, what was happening locally? Several projects had already been launched to fill this void. I suggested that the status of “Centre associated to the IPA” be open to groups interested in psychoanalysis, which may or may not include psychoanalysts, and that may or may not have the intention to evolve towards becoming a Study Group. These groups came to be successful IPA Allied Centres. The case of Taiwan exemplified this possibility. With my wife and a few colleagues from the APF, we got together with colleagues from other countries in order to support the Taiwanese’s interest in psychoanalysis. I myself have firmly insisted that the Taiwan Centre of Psychoanalysis should maintain close links with different psychoanalytic milieus and also keep its initial openness to international contacts and influences. Continental China presented a very different situation. Taiwan benefits from a Western economy, the Taiwanese are “rich”, there is a free market ideology. Universities and hospitals are as independent as they are in Europe. When I found myself confronted with the psychoanalytic situation in continental China, it seemed to me an extraordinarily confused situation. I found that psychoanalysis is everywhere and nowhere at the same time. Many hospitals and university centres seem to have contacts with a large number of analysts of all faiths, coming from many countries, from Buenos Aires to Oslo. It is difficult to understand why, from the North to the South of China, these institution refer to one group of theoreticians rather than another group. Everything, of course, depends on the generosity of the missionaries, since apart from lodging, the Chinese have no other means. And the ideological surveillance of the Party remains vigilant, without one clearly knowing what the Party encourages or tolerates. A few words remain to be said about what may be the most important step taken by my administration. During the preceding administration, we had received a request from the Latin American societies, which asked us to authorize supervisions of training analyses at a frequency of three rather than four sessions a week. Had the moment come to clearly call into question the apparent unity of the training rules? The decision that was taken at my request did not answer the concern about changing the written rules, but it did address the issue of acknowledging that a certain “hypocrisy” existed inside the IPA. By recognizing some variability in the rules, we acknowledged a reality, and therefore the possibility of discussing it instead of hiding it under the carpet. It had to be made possible to talk frankly in the IPA about this issue. Otto Kernberg had opened the discussion about the “training” functions, showing how controversial it was to bind these functions to the unique status of the training analyst. The issue of the frequency of sessions was more delicate and touched the heart of our practice. Therefore, I wrote a short memorandum that I wanted to submit to the IPA authorities, so that they would be able to express themselves and would do so. Also, I began to have private encounters with many members of the Association, with the aim of exploring this issue. I was struck by the gap that existed between the silent uneasiness of interviewed groups and the much more open attitude of colleagues seen on an individual basis. This or that colleague, who had the reputation of being quite attached to maintaining the rules of the setting, would admit in private that a greater openness was necessary. This was quite obvious when I talked with British or North American friends, members of institutions that had a very “orthodox” reputation. I had to decide that we should talk about it, and then that we should consult together collectively. Here, I came up against a resistance that made me feel this ambivalence: the opponents to change never wanted to be exposed to votes but held up any decision and took refuge in guerrilla fighting behind the lines. This strengthened my conviction that drastic measures had to be taken and that therefore a greater flexibility of methods should be acknowledged and, hence, tolerated. But the opponents, who were mainly worried about their own conditions of existence, fearing that their treatments at four sessions a week would not be reimbursed or that candidates might go to a competing society, tried to put off confronting the issue rather than veto measures that did not directly concern them. I felt, little by little, that the views of analysts were following or resigning themselves to be open to change. My American friends trusted me on an individual level, even more so because, personally, I had supported the principle of four weekly sessions. So finally, the principle of flexibility was accepted, with the reservation that we would have to discuss individually the choices that this one or that one made.







44The IPA administration from 1969 to 1973: Rome, Vienna, Paris—a peak and a turn

Leo Rangell

I am pleased to contribute this segment, known to me from the most intimate experience, to the history of the International Psychoanalytical Association over its first century of existence. The years of my presidencies spanned from 1969 to 1973, which included the historic Vienna Congress of 1971 and the important following one in Paris in 1973. When I ascended to the presidency of the IPA at the Rome Congress in 1969, I had no clear idea of the place this event would occupy on the curve of IPA history—neither a clear regard for all that had preceded it, nor how it would fare or fit in with what lay ahead. Perhaps this is characteristic of all history: that at any historic moment, the past is known selectively and the future can only be the subject of speculation. What I was searingly clear about in Rome was the historic significance of the immediate surrounding events. Once the drama of the election had settled down—this turned out to have its own historic effects—I was acutely intent about the nature of the moment at which it had come, the period we had just lived through, and the agenda that lay ahead. The world was in turmoil, and psychoanalysis was at a peak. I had been unaware of the strength of the gathering students’ revolution until I saw their parades in Rome. The day before I was to be elected president at the Business Meeting, as I walked in the street with my adolescent son, marchers carrying banners “Down with the AP$A” invited me to join them. I told my son that tomorrow they will point at me as the enemy. Deconstruction, of every intellectual discipline, was in the air; psychoanalysis was not to be exempt. The over-all title of the scientific programme of the Congress was “New Developments in Psycho-Analysis”. An innovation was that all seven major presentations were made in plenary rather than simultaneous sessions for discussion of previously published papers. One of these was my paper (1969) on “The Intrapsychic Process”, which was an expansion of Freud’s signal process of the testing for anxiety. I have written elsewhere that the history of psychoanalysis is a combination of ideas and people, which at every juncture of change is a fusion of the two. Experiences at this congress led me to the opinion that the election that took place in Rome in 1969 played a significant role in providing the soil for a theoretical revolution, the advent of the future self-psychology as a competitive theoretical paradigm, a major step towards a pluralism of theories. The same events at that congress were contributory to the entry of Kleinian theory from Great Britain to the United States via the window of Los Angeles, and the significant changes in the ambience of theory that ensued. A number of historical studies have dealt with these movements, as well as the London–Los Angeles connection, from different vantage points (Hopkins, 2006; Kirsner, 2000; Rangell, 2004). A prescient issue related to this progression of events to come made its way into the Rome Congress when the Dutch Society announced its intention at the Business Meeting to graduate candidates in child psychotherapy only into membership in their Society. Notice was thus being given that this would involve the IPA, since according to the Constitution and By-Laws of the latter, membership in a Constituent Society brought with it membership in the IPA. As the discussion progressed, a sense of unease became apparent, as various discussants recognized that this might become a precedent for a questionable development. Could various specialities not follow, such as geriatrics, adolescent problems, psychosomatic illness, and so on, that might lead to a claim for membership in the IPA as psychoanalysts by individuals only partially trained? A number of British analysts, among them Drs Limentani, Balint, and Hanna Segal, expressed concern from their Society’s position, having in mind the well-known Hampstead Child Therapy programme in their midst. Other discussants spoke of the desirability of including child training along with adult training, as well as vice versa. Psychoanalysts should be trained in both, across the life cycle. The result was the recommendation to appoint a committee to investigate the question under broad lines and to report back to the membership at the next congress. Before the Congress came to an end, another decision of major historical consequence was passed unanimously. The IPA voted to accept the invitation of the Vienna Society to hold its next congress in Vienna, for the first time in the city where the new science had been born and from which it had suffered its infamous rejection. Anna Freud had given her assent to return. Between the two congresses, another event occurred in the history and development of psychoanalytic science. In May 1970, Heinz Hartmann, who was President of the IPA from 1953 to 1959 and its Honorary President from 1959 until the time of his death, died. At his Memorial, I said the following in the name of the IPA:


In 1939, Sigmund Freud died. That same year, Dr Hartmann published a small monograph, on the ego and its adaptive function, which announced a new phase in the understanding of man and defined the direction of the next three decades…. Subsequent history showed that in that year the baton had been passed between the two men…. His capacity—no, his very nature—to integrate, build, bridge and organize has become synonymous with his name. He unified the past with the present, the child with the adult, the inside with the outside, the special with the general, the normal with the most disturbed. [Rangell, 1970]


The decline of Hartmann’s influence in recent years, and the demise of the Hartmann era declared today (Bergmann, 2000), is on a level with the decreased supremacy of Freudian thought itself. A year later, the Vienna Congress opened, amidst great expectations. At that moment in time, the present was an indelible ambience of burning historical impact, centring in the universal resolve, “We shall not forget”. The post-War era of exuberance and reparation had reached a high point—we hardly knew that a decline was next—and plans were taking shape for the return of a Freud to Vienna, the first since Freud’s escape 30 years earlier. Those still here who were present at the opening of the Vienna Congress held in the Hapsburg Palace the last week in July will never forget the scene—Anna Freud rising to speak—the hush in the audience—her first words delivered in unexpected German—the burst of applause—the remainder of her speech in English. My own presidential address (1972) at that opening session was on Aggression and the Oedipus Complex. My focus and intent was on the theoretical core of our science, which I always felt was intertwined with interpersonal issues. I had chosen “the theory of aggression” as the scientific theme of the congress (the seed for this decision having come from my wife). I was here introducing the idea that the two theoretical pillars of behaviour as of that time, libido and aggression, love and hate, were not far apart—that not one, but both were dominant and irrefutable within the central conflict, the Oedipal complex of psychoanalytic theory. I envisioned that this would be strongly meaningful from a number of standpoints to Anna Freud as well at this historical moment. The spirit and content of that historic meeting make any summary of it pale; such were the depths as well as contradictions of that week’s activities. At one layer—no, more than a layer, one large segment of the proceedings—were the heights of celebration and joy, of nostalgia, tears, and poignancy, as psychoanalysis unfolded in that glorious setting day after day. It was history being turned back. Both scientific and social, one event and session after another were accompanied by mellow thought and affective participation. Over 2,300 were in attendance, more than double the highest number ever before. A symbolic and meaningful moment was when I presented a medallion of an old Roman coin of Pallas Athene to Miss Freud, with the inscription on one side, “Auf meine treue Anna–Antigone gestützt. Sigmund Freud.” Much excitement was in the air. But there was a dark side to that same congress going on underneath, of which I was only partially aware. I did have a premonition as to the affective weight of the issue that had been introduced by the Dutch Society. My first presidential statement, when called upon by Dr van der Leeuw at the Business Meeting in Rome, was: “It has been my fortune during my entire career that at every position at which I arrive I seem to have been asked to sit on top of a volcano. This does not seem to have changed now, except that this time it looks like an international volcano.” But although I had some sense of its potential impact, I was far from sufficiently aware of the magnitude of its understructure. In the interval since the previous congress, Anna Freud had sent in an application for Society status in the IPA for the Hampstead Child Therapy Clinic. This entire sequence, moreover, could be understood in the context of the 1941–45 discussions of the Hamp-stead child training issue in which the Kleinians politically prevailed (King & Steiner, 1991). In 1944 Freud and Glover resigned from the Training Committee; in 1951 Anna Freud founded the Hampstead Child Therapy Clinic outside of the British Society. The Rome/van der Leeuw/Anna Freud move confronted at the Vienna Congress was an attempt to revise that loss. This was not unexpected, considering the previous action of the Dutch Society. The Executive Board received this request and, with full appreciation of the difficulties in this path, treated it with a prompt and straightforward response. I had already appointed as chairman of the committee to look into this issue Dr Samuel Ritvo, an analyst devoted and friendly to Anna Freud in every respect. The Council voted immediate Study Group status for the proposed Society, which was the route for any applying new group, yet of course more rapid in this instance than any other. Further deliberations, following dissemination of the committee’s report, would carry the matter further. What went on, however, behind all the celebrations, was a series of meetings of the Hampstead group in the face of the group’s—and, most importantly, Anna Freud’s—severe disappointment at the Study Group status. Perhaps I can portray the nature and intensity of these private sessions most sharply by reporting that in the heat of one of these discussions, a senior Hampstead analyst, Dr Lothar Rubinstein, collapsed and died of a heart attack. At the Business Meeting, the action of the Board was brought to the membership and was uncontested. During the interval before the next congress, however, the IPA succeeded in mediating between the Hamp-stead and the British Society using the services and good grace of William Gillespie, who worked out an agreement with Anna Freud that graduates of the Hampstead programme could analyse the required number of adults through the British Society and thus qualify for acceptance into the Society and the IPA. At that point Miss Freud withdrew the application of the new Study Group. Unique to the programme of the special congress in Vienna was a major contribution on the main topic given by Anna Freud (1972) as the final paper.
