In a brilliant essay, “Comments on Aggression”, integrating the subject of aggression into the theoretical framework of psychoanalysis, Miss Freud started her paper by citing the scientific achievements of previous congresses, and then stating that this one “did no more, obviously, than to demonstrate some of the limits of scientific group efforts of this nature”. Here a subtle beginning of some negativism made its appearance, the full significance of which would not become overt until more and new material became available years later. Her one reference to the preceding presidential address was: “However, members of the congress were warned at the outset by their President to keep their expectations at a modest level.” This view and negative assessment made no mention of the effort to assimilate aggression into the developmental heart of psychoanalytic theory, leaving things much as before. Beside my own dimly taking note of this negative choice of statements, this was independently observed and commented upon to me by William Gillespie. The Ritvo Report, which was still to be acted upon, recommended a compromise by requiring only one adult case for a new child analyst category; the designation as such to be in the Roster. This was discussed with passion, earnestness, and thoroughness at the Business Meeting of the next congress, in Paris, and was received but its proposal not accepted. Had this been the end, this entire experience might have been absorbed into future history unrecognized as to its complete nature. However, 25 years later a spate of books appeared referring to and re-reporting these events that led to further elaboration and a deeper insight into its role in history. The series of events in Rome and Vienna presented itself again in several retrospective studies, revealing data previously unavailable and conclusions made that illustrate how history often settles for distorted versions that may remain permanent views. As the oldest of the six living past-presidents of the IPA at this writing, I am the one who can see the furthest back by direct observation—or, at least, the one who has lived through the longest past. While I am presenting as many issues as I can based on documents and other concrete evidence now made available, a limitation on others will, of course, be the state of my memory. From a series of subsequent historical studies, by revelations of the correspondence of Heinz Kohut (Cocks, 1994), personal interview (Wolf, 1994), psychobiography (Strozier, 2001; Young-Bruehl, 1988) and psychohistories (Kirsner, 2000; Rangell, 2004), retrospective accounts of that era have provided evidence of new data and distorted beliefs that indicate a serious hidden aspect of the historical events of those times. In a definitive psychobiography of Anna Freud (Young-Bruehl, 1988), the author reported that Anna Freud not only resented the decision of the IPA during the Vienna Congress, but felt specifically that the action taken in conferring Study Group status and awaiting further discussion of the Committee report represented “his [Rangell’s] own victory at the Executive Council”, and that “training and membership in the IPA for child analysts was being so obviously blocked” (pp. 494–405). My specific role throughout the congress, subjected to repeated criticisms, was reported in a chapter on how Anna Freud acted “In the Face of Enemy Forces”. My shock at reading this was followed by astonishment and then sadness as further new assessments of the past came to light, which recorded history in reverse. Based on my history and scientific activity, this purported “enmity” on my part directed at Miss Freud came from one of the main friends and supporters of child analysis in the theoretical battles of the time. Another item: in an interview of a former analysand and then colleague of Heinz Kohut, Wolf (1994) stated that Kohut lost the election in Rome because Anna Freud and her close circle of friends became aware of his turn to self-psychology and withdrew their support. I (Rangell, 2004) have recorded evidence of the opposite sequence—that Kohut became a self-psychologist after the election—and have given reasons why I felt, from close, direct contact, that this had taken place at least partially because of his disappointment. Indeed, from letters between Kohut, Anna Freud, and others that were also published years later (Cocks, 1994), Miss Freud’s surprisingly active campaigning for Heinz Kohut to be elected President at the Rome Congress was evidenced and documented. Not only was she not against him at the time, but her and her group’s support of his coming book was noted in the acknowledgments when it came out two years later (Kohut, 1971). It was only six years later, when his second book (1977) was published, that Anna Freud felt, according to Young-Bruehl (1988), that Kohut, once a member of their circle, “had become anti-psychoanalytic” (p. 440). Without agreeing with Anna Freud’s re-evaluation, in her own terms her judgement did not always turn out right. Many other gross distortions came to light: it was not only my actions that were questioned, but also my motives. “Anna Freud was just bemused [when Rangell] piously presented her with a medallion”, her biographer wrote; “… there was a feeling of estrangement about it all … Most of the analysts who found the Vienna Congress so moving … had no idea that their Pallas Athene … felt personally betrayed” (p. 404). When, at the next congress in Paris, I announced Anna Freud’s election as Honorary President, which I had proposed, to succeed Heinz Hartmann, who had died in 1970, I did this, her biographer explained, “to keep her from making a public protest” (p. 406). Miss Freud did not attend that congress, according to Young-Bruehl, because of her displeasure over the preceding one. Both the biographer and the biographed were describing different congresses than I knew or had presided over. These views persisted. Referring to a eulogy I gave to Anna Freud in San Diego 12 years later, in 1983, “Colleagues like Leo Rangell did not fail to register … her self-sufficiency … without disparagement. His public statements never disguised his attitude” (p. 407). In my history of this era, from Anna Freud’s correspondence, I traced this otherwise enigmatic attitude of Anna Freud to a London–Los Angeles connection between Miss Freud and Ralph (“Romi”) Greenson that long preceded the Vienna Congress, having its roots in local rivalries in the 1950s. I have traced (Rangell, 2004) the complex relationships of various personal, affective links between the British and the Los Angeles Societies to the otherwise serendipitous entry of the British Klein-ians onto the American scene via the window of Southern California psychoanalysis beginning precisely in the late 1960s. This historical scientific development played a major role in the course of the development of scientific theory, ultimately leading, by labyrinthine paths, to the establishment of a pluralism of theories at the expense of a steady growth of the Freudian theoretical tree. The interpersonal phenomena of which I became aware at the level of the IPA between the 1960s and 1970s played their part in the intersubjective network that brought about this scientific development. Ironically, Anna Freud played a surprising and unexpected role in the gradual diminution of the theory she stood for and loved. For more detailed accounts of these relationships and events, I refer the reader to my book (Rangell, 2004). The following congress, held in Paris in July 1973, was, perhaps in reaction to the angst of the previous one, one of great warmth, pleasure, and collegiality throughout. The social events were quite sparkling, and the ambience uniformly engaging. There was no central theme, but a certain diversity of content, as several topics were introduced and discussed. There were two panels, one on Transference, the other on Hysteria Today (with particular reference to the visit by Freud to Charcot in Paris). In my outgoing presidential address, however, I (Rangell, 1974) introduced what for psychoanalysis was a new theme—the moral issue of integrity. This valedictory presentation drew its affect as well as content from my intense experiences during four years of leadership of a large mass of individuals during challenging and stressful times. Both transference and hysteria were relevant issues, but so was this new element, usually considered outside the domain of this discipline. This turned out to be a major developing subject of interest, as I went on to describe the syndrome of the compromise of integrity (C of I) as being on a par with neurosis in human affairs. My sources came from multiple directions, clinical, social, and organizational, and also from the national political scene. By serendipity, and no small source of data, concomitant with our congress, a committee was meeting in Washington to investigate that famous or infamous break-in into the Watergate headquarters of the Democratic Party. A voluminous future output of literature was to pour out, of which my psychoanalytic reflections were to become a part. The aim of this new inclusion of observable data, which are not usually a source of theorizing or psychoanalytic action, is high—but, in my opinion, necessary—and the means complex. My new thoughts were to amend Hartmann’s stance of a distance from moral issues, to which I had adhered before, by retaining the analytic position—that is, Anna Freud’s equidistant stance—between all three psychic structures, addressing ego–superego conflicts analytically in the same manner and spirit as the more conventional ego–id conflicts of the past. This contribution was received enthusiastically by the audience, but its eventual general absorption into our science remained to be seen. To merge this report into the next phase of IPA history—the subsequent presidency of Serge Lebovici—my four years of intense leadership of the IPA were followed by my representing half the analytic world at the next congress, held in London in 1975. There, at the Opening Plenary session, I debated against André Green, each of us representing differing views on the subject “Changes in Psychoanalytic Practice and Experience”. Anna Freud was the discussant. Green (1975) argued that new observations and data demanded new psychoanalytic theories, while I (1975) believed that new data did indeed emerge but could and should be incorporated into the steadily evolving Freudian theory, grafted upon all elements that were considered enduring. Over 30 years later, Martin Bergmann (1999) referred to this debate as a landmark in the history of psychoanalysis, placing it in a series with the Marienbad symposium and the Controversial Discussions of the British Society. Stating that Anna Freud had supported my position, Bergmann (1999) wrote: “Rangell and Anna Freud carried the day”. Yet, he went on, “the era they represented had already passed”. Anna Freud’s (1976) suggestion of restricting psychoanalysis to the treatment of neuroses “contributed to the demise of the Hartmann era … The analytic center, so eloquently defended by Rangell and Anna Freud, could not turn back the tide of change that André Green had represented.” Without agreeing with Bergmann about the doomsday of Hartmann, or Freud, the splintering and fragmentation of psychoanalytic theory had already become strongly rooted and was to stamp the trend for the rest of the analytic century. The field had reached a peak and turned downward. * * * With the death of Leo Rangell in 2011, the IPA lost a leading statesman and psychoanalysis lost a major theoretical and institutional contributor.
